Combined with evidence that TsCatL facilitates invasion of epithelial cells, these observations support the epidemiological/biological inference that TsCatL likely contributes to extracellular matrix degradation to aid parasite migration through host tissues.

32.Option: Incubating rTsCatL2 with Caco-2 cells resulted in the loss of intercellular junctions, entry of cell junction proteins into the cytoplasm and cell autophagy, leading to disruption of the intestinal epithelial barrier.
Evidence: When rTsCatL2 was added to Caco-2 cells, the cells gradually shrank and became rounded with increasing incubation time. The tight junctions between the cells disappeared and some of the cells even fell off.

Immunofluorescence with anti-rTsCatL2 antibody showed that rTsCatL2 bound mainly around the cell periphery at the beginning and gradually entered the cytoplasm and nucleus with increasing incubation time;

After 60 min, immunofluorescence showed that claudin 1, e-cadherin, occludin and zo-1 entered the nucleus of the cells, the fluorescence of claudin 1 had disappeared in some cells, the fluorescence of e-cadherin, occludin and zo-1 was still present, and occludin and zo-1 also started to cluster (Fig 7A).
When rTsCatL2 was co-cultured with Caco-2 cells, the expression of claudin 1 decreased by 43.23% after 30 min incubation and 58.65% after 60 min incubation (*P < 0.05),
The results indicated that rTsCatL2 induced autophagy in Caco-2 cells.
32.Rationale: The passage documents that rTsCatL2 exposure causes loss of tight junctions and cell detachment, internalization/nuclear entry of junctional proteins (including substantial loss of claudin 1), and induction of autophagy in Caco-2 cells. These combined cellular effects are consistent with disruption of the intestinal epithelial barrier and are directly supported by the experimental observations in the study.

32.Option: The binding of rTsCatL2 to the cell periphery and its subsequent internalization into the cytoplasm and nucleus indicate that the protein functions as a transcriptional regulator in host cells.
Evidence: Immunofluorescence with anti-rTsCatL2 antibody showed that rTsCatL2 bound mainly around the cell periphery at the beginning and gradually entered the cytoplasm and nucleus with increasing incubation time;
The result showed that rTsCatL2 interacts with laminin and collagen I but not with claudin 1, e-cadherin, occludin or fibronectin.
The results indicated that rTsCatL2 induced autophagy in Caco-2 cells.
32.Rationale: While the study shows rTsCatL2 can be internalized into the cytoplasm and nucleus, there is no experimental evidence that it acts as a transcriptional regulator (no data on changes in host gene transcription, DNA binding, or recruitment of transcriptional machinery). The observed interactions with extracellular matrix proteins and the protease activity, together with effects on junctional proteins and autophagy, better support enzymatic and disruptive roles rather than a role as a host transcriptional regulator. Thus the specific inference that rTsCatL2 functions as a transcriptional regulator is not supported by the passage.

33.Option: Patient-reported preferences for oral and topical formulations are primarily driven by aesthetic concerns related to scarring and social stigma, rather than by treatment convenience or burden.
Evidence: In general, patients preferred oral and topical treatments due to the easier administration and being perceived as ‘fitting’ the disease.
This in addition to the perceived contrast between the treatment and the perceived disease severity, i.e., a long parenteral treatment for a ‘pimple’.
When discussing parenteral formulations, patients often reported ‘fear of needles’—an aversion, or even a trauma, against injections.
Efficacy was understood as clinical cure in relation to the lesion, the absence of sequelae, scar formation and the prevention of disease relapse/reinfection.
patients discussed the preference for an effective treatment in healing the wound, regardless of scar appearance, potential side effects or treatment duration.
33.Rationale: The passage explicitly attributes preferences for oral/topical formulations to easier administration, perceived fit with a local disease, fear of needles, and trade-offs between efficacy and other attributes. It also notes patients valued clinical cure 'regardless of scar appearance.' While scarring and stigma are mentioned elsewhere as concerns of the disease, the text does not state that aesthetic concerns are the primary drivers of formulation preference. Therefore the option overstates the role of aesthetics and is not supported by the quoted evidence.

33.Option: Incorporating patients' preferences regarding treatment duration, formulation, and cost into Target Product Profiles (TPPs) for cutaneous leishmaniasis can improve the design of new treatments and enhance their real-world effectiveness and accessibility.
Evidence: TPPs are a valuable instrument to include these requirements and optimise the design and development of medicinal products.
We present a methodological and analytical approach as to involve patients in the identification of preferred characteristics of a new drug, and how this could inform TPPs.
Themes were then assessed for potential incorporation into a TPP, and for being considered during drug development in general.
In general, patients preferred oral and topical treatments due to the easier administration and being perceived as ‘fitting’ the disease.
Treatment regimen was found to be a TPP category rich in themes and trade-offs. Patients perceived long treatment durations as burdensome...
Costs were frequently mentioned by patients, especially those related to procedures such as administration of injections, consultations, diagnostic tests, and transport.
33.Rationale: The passage frames TPPs as tools to optimize product design and explicitly describes the study as assessing patient-preferred characteristics to inform TPPs. It documents patient concerns about formulation (oral/topical), treatment duration (burdensome), and cost (frequently mentioned). From an epidemiological and product-development perspective, integrating these documented preferences into TPPs is a logical inference to improve design, acceptability, and real-world accessibility of new treatments.

33.Option: The lack of shared decision-making in case management was apparent, with interviewees seeing injections as the only option to get cured, suggesting that patient preferences are often overridden by clinical protocols.
Evidence: Notably, one Brazilian patient with contraindications described moments of shared decision making with the treating medical team, which was not observed in other groups.

In contrast, some patients perceived injections as working faster and more effective than alternative modes of administration. While others, such as two Colombian patients, reported injections as the only treatment option.
Patients often saw AEs as an inevitable part of the treatment, and accepted them.
33.Rationale: While the passage notes that shared decision-making was described by one patient and 'was not observed in other groups,' and that some patients reported injections as the only option, it does not provide direct evidence that patient preferences are 'often overridden by clinical protocols.' The available quotes show variability in decision-making and some patients accepting injections or perceiving them as the only option, but they do not document the systematic overriding of preferences by protocols. Thus the stronger causal claim is not supported by the passage.

33.Option: The findings suggest that patients in high-income countries are more likely to prioritize treatment efficacy over cost, while those in low-income settings prioritize affordability, indicating a need for region-specific TPPs.
Evidence: We took the socioeconomic context into consideration by including participants from a high-income country (Austria) and low- and middle-income countries (Brazil and Colombia).
Costs were frequently mentioned by patients, especially those related to procedures such as administration of injections, consultations, diagnostic tests, and transport.

In a high-income setting, an Austrian patient describes paying a 140 EUR (154 USD) fee for specialized care to shorten waiting times, while a Brazilian patient described the challenges trying to access specialized care in their town, both due to the costs of the appointment and limited of availability of specialists.

In line with the exploratory nature of the study, we did not want to prioritize; therefore, we did not count the number of times a specific aspect was mentioned (frequency analysis) and did not present themes within a TPP category in a particular order.
33.Rationale: Although the study included participants from different socioeconomic settings and documents that costs are an issue for many patients, the authors explicitly avoided frequency-based prioritization and did not present comparative quantitative findings by region. There is no presented evidence that high-income patients prioritized efficacy over cost or that low-income patients prioritized affordability in a comparative sense. Therefore the regional prioritization claim and consequent recommendation for region-specific TPPs cannot be directly derived from the passage.

33.Option: The study demonstrates that discrete-choice experiments (DCEs) are the most effective method for capturing patient preferences in NTD drug development, surpassing qualitative interviews.
Evidence: We report the methods and findings of a qualitative study assessing preferences for treatment of CL patients from Brazil, Colombia, and Austria, who were infected with New-World Leishmania species and received different types of treatment.
Studies using exploratory approaches (e.g., using qualitative semi-structured interviews or focus group discussions) of patient involvement are scarce [20].

Adepoyibi et al. [18] conducted a survey among laboratory personnel, national tuberculosis control program managers, donors, technical experts, patients and researchers, and asked them to rank the items in a TPP for tuberculosis diagnostic tools by their perceived importance.
33.Rationale: The passage describes the use of qualitative semi-structured interviews and references other methods (surveys, ranking), but it does not mention discrete-choice experiments (DCEs) nor provide any comparative assessment concluding that DCEs are the most effective method. The study demonstrates qualitative methods can yield insights for TPPs, but it does not support the specific claim about DCEs surpassing qualitative interviews.

34.Option: The simulation of parasite development in the SMT suggests that P. vivax parasites spend an average of 25.3 hours as trophozoites and schizonts before termination of the assay, which may explain the variability in EC50 values across different isolates.
Evidence: The mean λ was 19.1 hours (95% CI 11, 29) for rings, 23.1 hours (95% CI 11, 36) for trophozoites, and 2.2 hours (95% CI 0, 7) for the schizonts.
The total duration of the P. vivax erythrocytic cycle was estimated as 44.5 hours (95% CI 29, 62).

The diversity in parasite life cycle stages in P. vivax infections creates a significant confounding factor, particularly since there is an apparent marked variation of drug susceptibility in different erythrocytic life cycle stages.
In previous work, it has been demonstrated that the trophozoite stages of P. vivax are almost completely resistant to CQ, and continue to mature no matter how high the concentration of drug.
34.Rationale: The passage provides the mean durations for each stage: rings 19.1 h, trophozoites 23.1 h, schizonts 2.2 h; trophozoites + schizonts thus average 23.1 + 2.2 = 25.3 hours. The authors explicitly state that variation in life cycle stage composition is a significant confounder and that trophozoites are essentially CQ-resistant. Therefore a lengthy period spent as trophozoites/schizonts (i.e., ~25.3 hours) before harvest can contribute to variability and apparent increases in EC50 across isolates. The combination of the stage-duration estimates and statements about stage-specific susceptibility supports the option's conclusion.

34.Option: Because P. falciparum isolates are more synchronous and predominantly contain ring stage parasites, the SMT is more reliable for P. falciparum than for P. vivax, and thus should be prioritized in drug susceptibility testing.
Evidence: The SMT was initially developed for testing drug susceptibility in P. falciparum [13], [14], where almost all parasites in the peripheral circulation are at the immature ring stage.

There was a significantly higher mean proportion of ring stage parasites in samples at the start of the assay (0 hours) for P. falciparum (0.922) compared to P. vivax (0.588) (Wilcoxon rank sum test, p<0.001).
87.5% (189/216) of P. falciparum isolates contained 100% ring stage parasites at 0 hours, compared to only 2.8% (4/141) of P. vivax isolates.

The diversity in parasite life cycle stages in P. vivax infections creates a significant confounding factor, particularly since there is an apparent marked variation of drug susceptibility in different erythrocytic life cycle stages.
34.Rationale: While the passage documents that P. falciparum samples are more synchronous and predominantly ring stages and that the SMT was developed for P. falciparum (supporting greater applicability in that species), it does not explicitly state that SMT is "more reliable" for P. falciparum in the sense of a formal comparison of reliability, nor does it recommend that P. falciparum testing "should be prioritized." The normative recommendation to prioritize P. falciparum testing is not directly supported by the passage. Therefore the inferential leap to prioritization is not derivable solely from the presented evidence.

34.Option: The emergence of highly chloroquine-resistant P. vivax in Southeast Asia has created an urgent need for improved understanding of drug resistance mechanisms and the development of new antimalarial agents.
Evidence: Further, the emergence of highly chloroquine (CQ) resistant P. vivax in Southeast Asia (CQ remains a front-line treatment for vivax malaria as it is affordable, well tolerated and safe, and its long half-life ensures protection from early relapses [7], [8]) has created an urgent need for an improved understanding of the mechanisms of drug resistance in these parasites, the development of robust tools for defining the spread of resistance, and the discovery of new antimalarial agents.
34.Rationale: This option is a direct restatement of text from the passage. The passage explicitly links the emergence of highly CQ-resistant P. vivax in Southeast Asia to an urgent need for better understanding of resistance mechanisms and for development of new agents; thus the conclusion is directly supported.

34.Option: The use of mixed-effects modelling in the dose-response analysis for CQ data ensures that inter-isolate variability is accounted for, which improves the accuracy of EC50 estimates in P. vivax isolates.
Evidence: The sigmoid Emax dose-response curve (equation 1) was fitted to all available data simultaneously, with mixed-effects modelling used for CQ data.
Inter-isolate variability was included for Emax, EC50 and γ.
Predicted values for EC50 for each isolate were calculated from Empirical Bayes estimates.
34.Rationale: The passage states that mixed-effects modelling was used for CQ and that inter-isolate variability was explicitly included for key parameters (Emax, EC50, γ), with EC50 estimates obtained via Empirical Bayes. These methodological choices are intended to account for between-isolate differences and produce isolate-specific parameter estimates, which is the basis for the option's claim that such modelling accounts for inter-isolate variability and thereby improves the accuracy of EC50 estimation. The assertion that accounting for variability improves accuracy is a reasonable, directly supported methodological inference from the quoted modeling approach.

34.Option: Assay duration and the proportion of ring stage parasites in the initial sample are critical factors that influence the accuracy of chloroquine susceptibility testing in P. vivax, and insufficient exposure of ring stage parasites to the drug can lead to artificially inflated EC50 values.
Evidence: For the results of the SMT to be valid, a sample, irrespective of the drug resistance phenotype of the parasites, must have had sufficient exposure to the drug to affect a response.

The diversity in parasite life cycle stages in P. vivax infections creates a significant confounding factor, particularly since there is an apparent marked variation of drug susceptibility in different erythrocytic life cycle stages.
In previous work, it has been demonstrated that the trophozoite stages of P. vivax are almost completely resistant to CQ, and continue to mature no matter how high the concentration of drug.
There was a significant negative association between the CQ assay duration (hours) and the ln (EC50) values for P. vivax (r2 = 0.219, p<0.001; Figure 1a).

The proportion of rings at the start of the assay was also significantly negatively associated with the ln(EC50) values for both P. vivax (r2 = 0.245, p<0.001) and P. falciparum isolates (r2 = 0.206, p<0.001; Figure 2).

It follows that, if a drug only acts on ring stage parasites, but the sample contains a majority of trophozoites and schizonts, the parasite is likely to be erroneously categorised as resistant simply because there were no susceptible life cycle stages present in the assay.
34.Rationale: The passage explicitly identifies both assay duration and initial stage composition as significant predictors of EC50 in P. vivax and emphasizes that valid SMT results require sufficient exposure of the drug to the relevant target stages. It further explains that trophozoites are largely CQ-resistant and that absence of susceptible stages (rings) can lead to incorrect classification of resistance. Together these statements directly support the option's assertion that assay duration and ring proportion are critical and that insufficient exposure of rings can artificially inflate EC50 values.

35.Option: Although the over-expression of detoxification enzymes in resistant populations is often associated with gene copy number variations, regulatory variations in upstream regions of these genes also contribute to their up-regulation, as evidenced by allele frequency correlations with transcription levels and predicted effects on transcription factor binding.
Evidence: Comparing the transcription profile of detoxification genes over-transcribed in resistant populations with their copy number variations (CNVs) previously inferred from targeted DNA-seq revealed that most detoxification genes differentially expressed in resistant populations belong to genomic clusters affected by CNV variations associated with resistance [28]. Among them, seven showed a significant positive correlation between their transcription level and their CNV (Fig 2 and S1 Fig).

Because CNV did not fully explain the over-expression of all detoxification genes associated with resistance, their upstream regions were sequenced in order to identify promoter variations potentially associated with their up-regulation.

The sequencing of 1 Kb upstream regions of all detoxification genes using targeted DNA-seq identified 188 variations located upstream detoxification genes over-transcribed in resistant populations (S3 Table). Among them, 23 variations showed a significant correlation between their allele frequency and the transcription level of the downstream gene (r ≥ 0.7 and p ≤ 0.05).

Seven variations were potentially favoring the binding of transcription factors previously identified as enhancers of detoxification genes in resistant populations (Fig 3 and S4 Table). These included two variations likely to have a major effect on the binding of enhancers: the variation at position -642 (respective to ATG) affecting the gene CYP6P12-like AAEL014891 favoring the binding of the HNF-1A element and the variation at position -410 affecting the gene CYP6CB1-like AAEL009018 favoring the binding of the HNF-3 element.
35.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports that many over-transcribed detoxification genes are located in genomic clusters with CNVs and that seven genes showed a significant positive correlation between transcription level and CNV, indicating gene dosage effects. It also states that CNV did not fully explain all over-expression, prompting sequencing of upstream regions; this revealed 23 upstream variations whose allele frequencies significantly correlated with gene transcription and seven variants predicted to alter transcription factor binding, with two strong examples given. Together these findings support the inference that both CNVs and regulatory (upstream) variations contribute to up-regulation of detoxification enzymes.

35.Option: The over-expression of detoxification enzymes in resistant populations is primarily driven by non-synonymous mutations in the coding regions of detoxification genes, as these mutations are significantly enriched in resistant strains and may directly enhance insecticide metabolism.
Evidence: High quality RNA-seq reads allowed calling a total of 749,596 substitutions against the reference genome, with an average of 300,000 substitutions per population (Table 3). Among them, 244,211 (32.58%) were considered polymorphic (allele frequencies variation > 5% across all populations). Among them, 12,087 were considered associated with resistance (Diff SNPs) as they showed an allele frequency variation > 40% between at least one resistant population and all susceptible strains together with a variation occurring in the same direction in populations from the same region (S5 Table). Nearly half of them (5,857) were located within annotated genes with 625 being non-synonymous (NS Diff SNPs) affecting 436 annotated genes.
Diff SNPs were significantly enriched among detoxification enzymes such as P450s (2.5 fold), CCEs (9.5 fold) and UGTs (1.9 fold).

Among all NS Diff SNPs affecting detoxification enzymes, none were located in the potential substrate binding sites of CCEs, GSTs and UGTs while seven were located in P450 substrate recognition sites (SRS).

Comparing the transcription profile of detoxification genes over-transcribed in resistant populations with their copy number variations (CNVs) previously inferred from targeted DNA-seq revealed that most detoxification genes differentially expressed in resistant populations belong to genomic clusters affected by CNV variations associated with resistance [28].
35.Rationale: Although the passage documents enrichment of non-synonymous (NS) differential SNPs among detoxification genes (supporting that coding changes exist and may be functionally relevant), it also shows that many over-expressed genes are explained by CNVs and by upstream regulatory variations. Furthermore, most NS Diff SNPs are not located in substrate/active sites for CCEs, GSTs and UGTs (only a few P450 SRS changes were identified). The passage therefore does not support the stronger claim that NS coding mutations are the primary driver of over-expression; rather, multiple mechanisms (CNV, regulatory variation, and some coding changes) contribute.

35.Option: The presence of kdr mutations in resistant populations is sufficient to explain the observed over-expression of detoxification enzymes, as these mutations are consistently found in all resistant strains and are known to confer pyrethroid resistance.
Evidence: Resistance of mosquitoes to pyrethroid insecticides can be the consequence of various mechanisms, such as non-synonymous mutations affecting the voltage-gated sodium channel targeted by these insecticides (knock down resistance mutations, known as kdr mutations), a lower insecticide penetration, its sequestration, and its biodegradation (metabolic resistance) [16, 17]. Knock down resistance mutations and metabolic resistance are known as the two main pyrethroid resistance mechanisms in mosquitoes.

Previous genotyping of kdr mutations revealed that resistant populations from South-America carried the V1016I and F1534C mutations at high frequencies (V1016I from 77 to 96% and F1534C from 75 to 100%) while the S989P mutation was not detected. Asian populations carried the S989P, 1016G and F1534C mutations at medium to high frequencies (14 to 68%; 21 to 58% and 22 to 75% respectively) [28].

Comparing the transcription profile of detoxification genes over-transcribed in resistant populations with their copy number variations (CNVs) previously inferred from targeted DNA-seq revealed that most detoxification genes differentially expressed in resistant populations belong to genomic clusters affected by CNV variations associated with resistance [28].
35.Rationale: The passage distinguishes kdr (target-site) mutations and metabolic resistance as separate mechanisms and documents that kdr mutations are present at varying frequencies across populations. However, it also shows that over-expression of detoxification enzymes is associated with CNVs, upstream regulatory variants, and some coding SNPs. The passage does not claim—and does not provide evidence—that kdr mutations alone are sufficient to explain the observed over-expression of detoxification enzymes; hence the option's sufficiency claim is not supported by the presented data.

35.Option: The identification of copy number variations (CNVs) in detoxification gene clusters provides a reliable and universally applicable method for monitoring metabolic resistance in Aedes aegypti populations across all geographic regions.
Evidence: Comparing the transcription profile of detoxification genes over-transcribed in resistant populations with their copy number variations (CNVs) previously inferred from targeted DNA-seq revealed that most detoxification genes differentially expressed in resistant populations belong to genomic clusters affected by CNV variations associated with resistance [28]. Among them, seven showed a significant positive correlation between their transcription level and their CNV (Fig 2 and S1 Fig).

Because CNV did not fully explain the over-expression of all detoxification genes associated with resistance, their upstream regions were sequenced in order to identify promoter variations potentially associated with their up-regulation.
studies performed in different geographical areas often highlighted different candidate genes [20, 22], questionning the universality of genes associated with metabolic resistance.
35.Rationale: While CNVs are shown to explain the over-expression of many detoxification genes (and several genes showed significant transcription-CNV correlations), the passage explicitly states that CNV did not fully explain all over-expression and that upstream regulatory variations and other mechanisms also play roles. Moreover, the authors note that different geographic areas often implicate different candidate genes, which questions universality. Therefore, the claim that CNV detection alone is a reliable and universally applicable monitoring method across all regions is not supported by the passage.

35.Option: The up-regulation of detoxification enzymes in resistant populations is solely due to the selection of transcription factor binding sites in upstream regions, as the identified variations show strong correlations with gene expression and are predicted to enhance binding of known enhancer elements.
Evidence: The sequencing of 1 Kb upstream regions of all detoxification genes using targeted DNA-seq identified 188 variations located upstream detoxification genes over-transcribed in resistant populations (S3 Table). Among them, 23 variations showed a significant correlation between their allele frequency and the transcription level of the downstream gene (r ≥ 0.7 and p ≤ 0.05).
Seven variations were potentially favoring the binding of transcription factors previously identified as enhancers of detoxification genes in resistant populations (Fig 3 and S4 Table).

Comparing the transcription profile of detoxification genes over-transcribed in resistant populations with their copy number variations (CNVs) previously inferred from targeted DNA-seq revealed that most detoxification genes differentially expressed in resistant populations belong to genomic clusters affected by CNV variations associated with resistance [28]. Among them, seven showed a significant positive correlation between their transcription level and their CNV (Fig 2 and S1 Fig).

Because CNV did not fully explain the over-expression of all detoxification genes associated with resistance, their upstream regions were sequenced in order to identify promoter variations potentially associated with their up-regulation.
35.Rationale: The passage demonstrates that upstream promoter variations with effects on predicted transcription factor binding correlate with expression for some genes, supporting a regulatory contribution. However, it also documents that CNVs explain over-expression of many genes (with significant transcription-CNV correlations) and that non-synonymous SNPs are present in detoxification genes. The text explicitly indicates that CNV alone did not fully explain expression differences and so upstream regulatory changes are part of a multi-mechanism picture. Thus the assertion that up-regulation is solely due to selection on TFBS is not supported by the evidence.

36.Option: The upregulation of CCDC168 in all infection comparisons suggests that this gene may play a central role in the host's immune response to S. mansoni, regardless of infection intensity.
Evidence: One (1) DEG, CCDC168 was upregulated in the three comparisons (IU, HU and HL).
36.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that CCDC168 was upregulated in all three pairwise comparisons (infected vs uninfected, high vs uninfected, and high vs low). Consistent upregulation across comparisons indicates an association of CCDC168 with S. mansoni infection irrespective of intensity. The cautious phrasing "may play a central role" is a reasonable inference from this consistent pattern of differential expression and is directly supported by the quoted statement.

36.Option: Children with low S. mansoni infection intensity exhibit a transcriptional profile that is intermediate between uninfected and high-intensity infected individuals, suggesting a dose-dependent response.
Evidence: D No significant DEGs were identified among children with low S. mansoni infection intensity compared to the uninfected.

We identified 33 significant DEGs (S2 Table and Fig 3C) among individuals with high infection compared with those with low infection (HL) of which 30 (90.9%) were upregulated and 3 (9.1%) downregulated.
36.Rationale: The passage reports that there were no significant DEGs when comparing low infection to uninfected children, which indicates the low-intensity group does not show a distinct intermediate transcriptional profile relative to uninfected under the study's significance criteria. Although high vs low comparisons do show differences, the absence of DEGs in the low vs uninfected contrast contradicts the claim that low-intensity infection produces an intermediate (dose-dependent) transcriptome; thus the option is not supported by the passage.

36.Option: There is no significant difference in gene expression between individuals with low levels of S. mansoni infection and those who are uninfected, suggesting that low-intensity infection may not elicit a detectable host transcriptional response.
Evidence: D No significant DEGs were identified among children with low S. mansoni infection intensity compared to the uninfected.

Genes were defined as significantly differentially expressed between conditions using the following criteria: adjusted p-value <0.05; fold change (FC) Log2 (FC) > 1.0 for up-regulated genes and Log2 (FC) < -0.8 for down regulated genes.
36.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports that the low infection versus uninfected comparison yielded no significant differentially expressed genes under the stated statistical thresholds. That directly supports the inference that, in this dataset and by the study's detection criteria, low-intensity S. mansoni infection did not produce a detectable transcriptional signature compared with uninfected children.

36.Option: The absence of significant DEGs in the low infection vs. uninfected comparison may be due to the use of POC-CCA, which cannot differentiate between Schistosoma species and may have introduced misclassification bias.
Evidence: POC-CCA was used in the field to classify participants by infection intensity to select and collect blood samples that were sequenced for RNAseq.
Samples were later classified by infection intensity using CAA in the laboratory as described below.

POC-CCA is available in a cassette format to detect the Cathodic Circulating Antigen (CCA) secreted by schistosomes in urine within 20 minutes [9]. It is a qualitative test and has been recommended to be used to screen for S. mansion infections in the field.
36.Rationale: The option attributes the lack of DEGs to misclassification from POC-CCA. However, the passage states that although POC-CCA was used in the field for initial selection, samples "were later classified by infection intensity using CAA in the laboratory," and the gene expression analyses were based on CAA-defined infection categories. The passage does not state that POC-CCA cannot differentiate species nor that misclassification by POC-CCA affected final classification; moreover, the final laboratory CAA assay (UCP-LF CAA) was used to classify infection intensity. Therefore the specific causal claim about POC-CCA–driven misclassification causing the absence of DEGs is not supported by the text.

36.Option: The low number of differentially expressed genes in S. mansoni infected children may be due to the parasite's ability to suppress inflammatory responses, leading to a relatively weak effect on the host transcriptome.
Evidence: We found that 14 genes were significant differentially expressed between infected and uninfected children of which 9 (64%) were upregulated and 5 (36%) were down regulated among infected individuals compared to uninfected (IU) (Tables 2 and 3 and Fig 3A).
A total of 56 (43 upregulated and 13 downregulated) DEGs between children with high S. mansoni infection intensity and the uninfected were observed.

Animal studies have shown upregulation of immune genes early in the infection and of metabolic related genes later in the infection [11–13] as well as differences in expression profiles between susceptible and less susceptible hosts [14].
36.Rationale: While the passage documents relatively small numbers of DEGs in some contrasts, it does not provide evidence that this is due to parasite-mediated suppression of inflammatory responses. The passage instead notes patterns observed in other studies (e.g., upregulation of immune genes early in infection) but does not report immunosuppression as the mechanism in this human dataset. The causal explanation offered in the option (parasite suppression) is speculative and not directly supported by text, so it cannot be derived from the passage alone.

37.Option: The divergence between the Paraguayan and Argentinean T. trivittatus populations is due to recent climate change and urbanization, which altered venom composition and gene expression.
Evidence: has been predicted to increase in response to ongoing global climate change [6].
Our study revealed significant toxinological and genetic divergence between the Paraguayan samples and T. trivittatus from Argentina, indicating that they probably comprise unique cryptic species.

Divergence time estimates (using calibration data from [41]) indicate that the T. trivittatus populations from northern Argentina and Paraguay diverged between the middle Miocene and early Pliocene (~15–5 Million years ago (Ma)).
37.Rationale: The passage does mention that range shifts have been predicted in response to ongoing global climate change, but it does not attribute the observed toxinological and genetic divergence to recent climate change or urbanization. In fact, divergence time estimates indicate the populations split between ~15–5 Ma (middle Miocene to early Pliocene), which is far older than 'recent' changes. The passage therefore does not support the causal claim that recent climate change and urbanization produced the divergence or altered gene expression.

37.Option: The Argentinean and Paraguayan populations of T. trivittatus are genetically identical but differ in venom composition due to environmental adaptation.
Evidence: Our study revealed significant toxinological and genetic divergence between the Paraguayan samples and T. trivittatus from Argentina, indicating that they probably comprise unique cryptic species.

In the time-calibrated phylogeny generated with BEAST [40] (Fig 7), the Paraguayan T. trivittatus is 8.14% divergent (at the nucleotide level) from T. trivittatus from Argentina, and 8.97% from T. confluens.

Banding patterns in silver-stained SDS-PAGE gels differed between T. trivittatus venoms from Paraguay and Argentina, notably with proteins of 17, 19, 25, and 40 kDa being only present in the Argentinean venom, together with an intense 30-kDa component, and a fraction migrating around 10 kDa only found in the Paraguayan population (Fig 2, left panel).
37.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports significant genetic divergence (8.14% nucleotide divergence) between the Paraguayan and Argentinean populations, so they are not genetically identical. Although the passage documents differences in venom composition, it does not support the assertion that the populations are genetically identical and that differences are solely due to environmental adaptation. The data indicate both genetic and toxinological divergence.

37.Option: The Paraguayan population of T. trivittatus is a subspecies of the Argentinean population due to shared venom components and similar antivenom recognition.
Evidence: Both populations shared two components in this mass range (± 2 Da), 6606.1 and 6941.1.

A comparison of these values (Paraguay: 4.16 ± 0.15, 95%CI: 3.95–5.01; Argentina: 3.97 ± 0.06, 95%CI: 3.86–4.16) rendered the difference nonsignificant (F = 1.71, p = 0.198), implying that T. trivittatus venoms from Paraguay and Argentina were similarly recognized by the anti-T. trivittatus (INPB) AV.
Our study revealed significant toxinological and genetic divergence between the Paraguayan samples and T. trivittatus from Argentina, indicating that they probably comprise unique cryptic species.
In the time-calibrated phylogeny generated with BEAST [40] (Fig 7), the Paraguayan T. trivittatus is 8.14% divergent (at the nucleotide level) from T. trivittatus from Argentina
37.Rationale: While the passage documents some shared venom components and a lack of significant difference in one ELISA half-maximal recognition value (indicating some similar antivenom recognition), it also reports substantial toxinological and genetic divergence (including an 8.14% nucleotide divergence) and explicitly suggests they 'probably comprise unique cryptic species.' The data do not support labeling the Paraguayan population simply as a subspecies of the Argentinean population.

37.Option: The Paraguayan population of T. trivittatus exhibits significant toxinological and genetic divergence from the Argentinean population, suggesting it may represent a distinct species.
Evidence: Our study revealed significant toxinological and genetic divergence between the Paraguayan samples and T. trivittatus from Argentina, indicating that they probably comprise unique cryptic species.

In the time-calibrated phylogeny generated with BEAST [40] (Fig 7), the Paraguayan T. trivittatus is 8.14% divergent (at the nucleotide level) from T. trivittatus from Argentina, and 8.97% from T. confluens.

Divergence time estimates (using calibration data from [41]) indicate that the T. trivittatus populations from northern Argentina and Paraguay diverged between the middle Miocene and early Pliocene (~15–5 Million years ago (Ma)).

Banding patterns in silver-stained SDS-PAGE gels differed between T. trivittatus venoms from Paraguay and Argentina, notably with proteins of 17, 19, 25, and 40 kDa being only present in the Argentinean venom, together with an intense 30-kDa component, and a fraction migrating around 10 kDa only found in the Paraguayan population (Fig 2, left panel).

In the NaTx mass range (6–8 kDa), peptides unique to the Paraguayan population were components with m/z 6726.6, 6916.5, and 7263.5 Da. Whereas peptides unique to the Argentinean population were 6630.0, 6754.5, 6787.5, 7047.9, 7324.1, and 7598.4 Da.
The main proteolytic component unique to the Argentinean population venom migrated at 37 kDa, whereas the main component of the Paraguayan population was close to 110 kDa.
37.Rationale: The passage directly reports both significant toxinological (venom composition, mass-spectra, proteolytic profiles) and genetic divergence (8.14% COI nucleotide divergence, ancient divergence time estimates) between the Paraguayan and Argentinean T. trivittatus populations, and explicitly states these populations 'probably comprise unique cryptic species.' These pieces of evidence support the inference that the Paraguayan population may represent a distinct species.

37.Option: The Paraguayan population of T. trivittatus is more toxic than the Argentinean population due to higher venom lethality and greater proteolytic activity.
Evidence: Medium lethal dose in NIH Swiss mice intraperitoneally injected with T. trivittatus venom (a pool from female and male specimens) was estimated as 1.19 mg/kg (95% CI: 0.89–1.71).

To test the neutralizing capacity of therapeutic anti-Tityus antivenoms towards venom from the Paraguayan population of T. trivittatus, Swiss mice were injected i.p. with 0.2 mL amounting to 3×LD50s pre-incubated with 100 μL of antivenoms produced in Brazil, and Argentina.
The Brazilian (Instituto Vital Brazil) anti-T. serrulatus showed the highest protection (100% survival), followed by the Argentinean anti-T. trivittatus (INPB thereinafter) AV (50% survival).

several bands with proteolytic activity were identified, which were distinct between T. trivittatus populations. The main proteolytic component unique to the Argentinean population venom migrated at 37 kDa, whereas the main component of the Paraguayan population was close to 110 kDa.
37.Rationale: The passage provides an LD50 for the Paraguayan venom but does not provide a comparable LD50 for the Argentinean population, so a direct quantitative comparison of lethality is not possible from the presented data. Zymography shows different proteolytic components between populations (different molecular weights), but does not quantify overall proteolytic activity or demonstrate that Paraguayan proteolysis is greater. Therefore the specific claim that the Paraguayan population is more toxic due to higher lethality and greater proteolytic activity is not supported by the passage.

38.Option: The zDALY value of 55 years per 100,000 persons per year in Morocco indicates that the disease burden of CE is significantly higher than in other countries, primarily due to differences in livestock size and GNI per capita.
Evidence: The mean (min-max) estimated zDALY per year was 18,330 years (17,775–19,074) (i.e. 55 years per 100,000 persons).
ALE is the time lost to compensate for the monetary losses of livestock (i.e. the number of years of individual work needed to produce the amount lost).
ALE was estimated by dividing the monetary value of livestock losses by the GNI per capita [17] obtained from the World Bank database.
ALE, which includes animal productivity losses, thus accounted for almost all of the estimated zDALY value.
38.Rationale: The passage provides the zDALY value (55 years/100,000) and explains that ALE (based on livestock losses divided by GNI) composes most of zDALY. However, the passage does not present any direct comparison of zDALY or CE burden with other countries, nor does it provide evidence that differences in burden are "primarily due to differences in livestock size and GNI per capita." Therefore the comparative and causal claims about other countries and primary drivers cannot be derived from the passage.

38.Option: Despite the national control programme launched in 2005, CE-linked economic losses did not significantly decrease in any region, and in some regions, such as Laayoune Boujdour Sakia El Hamra Guelmim Essmara, losses tended to increase during the 2011–2014 period.
Evidence: In 2004, Morocco has set up a national programme of CE control by creating an inter-ministerial CE control committee that involved the Ministry of Agriculture, the Ministry of Health and the Ministry of the Interior. In 2005, a monitoring guide was developed and the programme was launched in 2007, when CE notification became mandatory [3].

the implementation of integrated control measures has been extremely difficult due to inter-ministerial collaboration issues, and only the notification system of patients undergoing surgery for CE has been put in place, which does not fit the goal of early detection [5].
In most Moroccan regions, losses varied from year to year, but no significant trend of loss reduction was observed in any region from 2011 to 2014 (Fig 7).
Laayoune Boujdour Sakia El Hamra Guelmim Essmara (LSBGE) has the lowest losses, but losses tended to increase from 2011 to 2014, especially losses caused by CE in animals (Fig 8).
38.Rationale: The passage documents the establishment and launch timeline of the national programme and explicitly states implementation difficulties. It also explicitly states that no region showed a significant trend of loss reduction from 2011–2014 and that the LSBGE region's losses tended to increase. From these statements it is a valid epidemiological inference that despite the programme, CE-linked economic losses did not significantly decrease regionally and some regions saw increases.

38.Option: The increase in economic losses in the Laayoune Boujdour Sakia El Hamra Guelmim Essmara region, despite its low human incidence, suggests that the national control programme is more effective in high-incidence areas than in low-incidence ones.
Evidence: Laayoune Boujdour Sakia El Hamra Guelmim Essmara (LSBGE) has the lowest losses, but losses tended to increase from 2011 to 2014, especially losses caused by CE in animals (Fig 8).
In most Moroccan regions, losses varied from year to year, but no significant trend of loss reduction was observed in any region from 2011 to 2014 (Fig 7).
Marrakech Tensift Al Haouz Tadla Azilal (MTATA) and Meknes Tafilalet (MT) were the regions with the highest economic losses.
38.Rationale: While the passage notes that LSBGE had the lowest losses but experienced increasing losses, it provides no data comparing programme effectiveness between high- and low-incidence regions or evidence that the programme performed better where incidence was higher. The claim that the programme is more effective in high-incidence areas is an attribution not supported by the provided data, so this inference cannot be drawn from the passage.

38.Option: The lack of data on decreased fertility in camels and the poor implementation of the control programme in the LBSGE region suggest that the national programme has failed to address the root causes of CE transmission.
Evidence: Decreased fertility was not estimated for camels due to the lack of data.

the implementation of integrated control measures has been extremely difficult due to inter-ministerial collaboration issues, and only the notification system of patients undergoing surgery for CE has been put in place, which does not fit the goal of early detection [5].

The causes of CE persistence in Morocco are manifold, including the slaughterhouse practices, and the poor understanding of the parasite life cycle by the population that leads to risky behaviors [13,14].
Laayoune Boujdour Sakia El Hamra Guelmim Essmara (LSBGE) has the lowest losses, but losses tended to increase from 2011 to 2014, especially losses caused by CE in animals (Fig 8).
38.Rationale: The passage does state that decreased fertility in camels was not estimated due to lack of data and documents poor implementation of integrated measures nationally. It also lists persistent root causes (slaughterhouse practices, poor public understanding). However, the option links lack of camel fertility data and "poor implementation of the control programme in the LBSGE region" to conclude the programme has failed to address root causes. The passage does not provide evidence of poor programme implementation specifically in LSBGE, nor a definitive statement that the programme has wholly 'failed' to address root causes. Therefore the specific causal conclusion about failure based on LSBGE data and camel fertility is not fully supported by the passage.

38.Option: The high economic burden of CE in Morocco, particularly in rural populations, underscores the need for improved surveillance and public awareness to reduce transmission and prevent disease progression.
Evidence: For the 2011–2014 period, the estimated mean for annual total economic losses caused by CE ranged from 70 to 74 million USD (73 million USD per year on average).

The mean (min-max) estimated DALY per year at the national level was 160 years (106–238) (i.e. 0.5 years per 100,000 persons). The mean (min-max) estimated zDALY per year was 18,330 years (17,775–19,074) (i.e. 55 years per 100,000 persons).
The risk is particularly high for rural populations, where dogs are kept for herding and people have low standards of living.

ministerial data are underestimating the true prevalence of infection, because many infected individuals do not present symptoms, or because part of people with symptomatic disease will not be treated by surgery, and therefore will not be recorded in the Ministry of Health registry [5].

the implementation of integrated control measures has been extremely difficult due to inter-ministerial collaboration issues, and only the notification system of patients undergoing surgery for CE has been put in place, which does not fit the goal of early detection [5].

The causes of CE persistence in Morocco are manifold, including the slaughterhouse practices, and the poor understanding of the parasite life cycle by the population that leads to risky behaviors [13,14].
38.Rationale: The passage documents a substantial annual economic burden (~73 million USD/year), quantifies human and combined burden (DALY, zDALY), and identifies rural populations at particular risk. It also describes shortcomings in surveillance (underestimation of prevalence, surveillance based mainly on surgical case recording) and notes poor public understanding and risky behaviors contributing to persistence. From these pieces of evidence, it is a reasonable epidemiological inference that improved surveillance and public awareness are needed to reduce transmission and prevent disease progression.

39.Option: The fact that B. pseudomallei has been isolated from pediatric patients with pneumonia in Mexico, but melioidosis was not diagnosed, indicates that the clinical presentation of melioidosis in children is distinct from that in adults.
Evidence: B. pseudomallei has been isolated from pediatric patients with pneumonia in Mexico, but melioidosis was not diagnosed at any time [12].
Yet, these events are not reported, and melioidosis has been described in only a few cases [17,19].
39.Rationale: The passage states that B. pseudomallei was isolated from pediatric patients but that melioidosis was not diagnosed; it also notes underreporting of melioidosis cases. However, there is no information comparing clinical presentations in children versus adults or any statement that pediatric disease manifests differently. The observation of isolates without diagnosis could reflect under-recognition, limited diagnostics, or reporting issues rather than a distinct pediatric clinical presentation. Therefore the claim that children's clinical presentation is distinct cannot be derived from the passage.

39.Option: The high genetic similarity between B. pseudomallei strain 294H and a strain isolated from a patient in Illinois, US, who had traveled to Mexico suggests that melioidosis is primarily imported into Mexico from the United States.
Evidence: A dendrogram based on maximum parsimony from analysis of core SNPs for strain 294H was carried out comparing with a number of B. pseudomallei strains that were isolated from patients with melioidosis living in different locations [41].

The result showed that strain 294H clustered with B. pseudomallei strains that were isolated from people living in the US who had a history of travelling to Mexico and other countries in the American continent (Fig 3).
Strain 294H was close to a strain isolated from a person with melioidosis living in Illinois, US, who previously had travelled to Mexico.
39.Rationale: The passage shows genetic relatedness between the Mexican clinical strain 294H and strains from US patients who had traveled to Mexico; specifically, a US isolate from Illinois whose patient had traveled to Mexico is closely related. This pattern is consistent with transmission linked to Mexico (e.g., travel-acquired infections diagnosed in the US), not with importation into Mexico from the United States. The passage provides no evidence that melioidosis is primarily imported into Mexico from the US; inferring that directionality (importation into Mexico) is not supported by the provided data.

39.Option: The presence of B. pseudomallei in Mexico, particularly in non-tropical regions like Sonora, suggests that the bacterium may be more widely distributed than previously recognized, and its detection in clinical cases may be underreported due to limited awareness and diagnostic capabilities.
Evidence: In North America, the highest incidence of melioidosis is predicted in Mexico, with 550 cases per 100,000 people each year [18]. Yet, these events are not reported, and melioidosis has been described in only a few cases [17,19].
B. pseudomallei has been isolated from pediatric patients with pneumonia in Mexico, but melioidosis was not diagnosed at any time [12].

We recently related environmental B. pseudomallei to two fatal cases of melioidosis in Sonora, Mexico [20]. Although Sonora is not tropical (located in northwest Mexico), the presence of B. pseudomallei in stressful conditions has been documented previously [21], including in desert regions [22].
Greater awareness of Burkholderia spp. in Mexico and more effective tools to correctly detect these species may help to provide accurate treatment of infections in clinical settings.
39.Rationale: The passage documents environmental and clinical detection of B. pseudomallei in Mexico, including in a non-tropical region (Sonora), and notes predicted high incidence but few reported cases. It also explicitly calls for greater awareness and better detection tools. Together these statements support the epidemiological inference that B. pseudomallei may be more widely distributed in Mexico than recognized and that clinical detection is likely underreported because of limited awareness/diagnostic capacity.

39.Option: The identification of a novel B. pseudomallei strain with a new ST (ST 1872) in Mexico suggests that the bacterium is undergoing rapid evolution and diversification in the country.
Evidence: B. pseudomallei strain 294H also represents a new ST, which was assigned ST 1872 (S2 Table).
These results showed that new STs were identified in some of the strains in this work.
39.Rationale: The passage documents the identification of a novel sequence type (ST 1872) for a Mexican B. pseudomallei isolate, which indicates genetic diversity and that new STs are present. However, there are no data on the rate of change, temporal sampling, or comparative diversity over time to support the claim of 'rapid evolution and diversification.' Therefore the stronger assertion of rapid evolution is not derivable from the provided evidence.

39.Option: The presence of B. pseudomallei in desert regions of Sonora, Mexico, confirms that the bacterium is adapted to arid environments and can survive under extreme stress, which may explain its widespread distribution across diverse climates.
Evidence: We recently related environmental B. pseudomallei to two fatal cases of melioidosis in Sonora, Mexico [20]. Although Sonora is not tropical (located in northwest Mexico), the presence of B. pseudomallei in stressful conditions has been documented previously [21], including in desert regions [22].
39.Rationale: The passage reports detection of B. pseudomallei in Sonora and notes prior documentation of its presence in 'stressful conditions' and desert regions. This supports that the bacterium can be found in such environments. However, claiming that this 'confirms' adaptation to arid environments and survival under extreme stress, and that this explains widespread distribution across climates, is stronger than the evidence allows. Presence in deserts demonstrates occurrence but does not by itself prove physiological adaptation or the mechanism behind distribution. Thus the causal and confirmatory language is not supported by the passage.

40.Option: The absence of D. nuttalli and H. concinna on horses indicates that these tick species are not capable of infecting non-ruminant hosts, limiting their potential to contribute to rickettsial transmission in mixed livestock settings.
Evidence: The same five tick species were captured from cattle and goats while three of the five tick species were found on horses, with the absence of D. nuttalli and H. concinna (Table 3).
40.Rationale: The passage reports an observational absence of D. nuttalli and H. concinna on horses in this sampling (a descriptive result), but does not provide evidence that these species are biologically incapable of infesting non-ruminant hosts. An absence in these data can reflect sampling scope, host exposure patterns, or ecological factors rather than innate host incapacity. Therefore the stronger causal claim that these ticks 'are not capable of infecting non-ruminant hosts' and that this 'limits their potential to contribute to rickettsial transmission in mixed livestock settings' cannot be derived from the passage.

40.Option: The co-feeding of domestic animals increases the risk of rickettsial infection in ticks, which in turn implies that reducing livestock density in rural areas would significantly lower the incidence of human rickettsioses.
Evidence: Among the ticks, 563 were captured from different domestic animals, while 723 were collected from different types of vegetative covers (Table 3).

DNA of R. raoultii was detected in the ticks from all the different sources but its positive rate in ticks from the domestic animals (32.0%, 180/563) was significantly higher than that from the vegetative covers (10.5%, 76/723) (P = 91.42, P<0.05).

The total positive rate of both R. raoultii and “Ca. R. tarasevichiae” in ticks from the domestic animals (40.7%, 229/563) was significantly higher than that from the vegetative covers (19.5%, 141/723) (P = 69.24, P<0.05).
40.Rationale: While the passage documents higher Rickettsia-positive rates in ticks collected from domestic animals compared with vegetative covers, it does not describe 'co-feeding' as a mechanism, nor does it present data linking livestock density to human incidence of rickettsioses. The step from observed higher tick infection rates on animals to the policy implication that reducing livestock density would 'significantly lower the incidence of human rickettsioses' requires causal evidence and human incidence data not provided in the study. Thus the compounded causal and policy inference is not supported by the passage.

40.Option: The higher prevalence of R. raoultii in ticks from domestic animals suggests that these animals are the primary reservoir hosts for this pathogen, and thus should be targeted for vaccination or culling to control rickettsiosis outbreaks.
Evidence: DNA of R. raoultii was detected in the ticks from all the different sources but its positive rate in ticks from the domestic animals (32.0%, 180/563) was significantly higher than that from the vegetative covers (10.5%, 76/723) (P = 91.42, P<0.05).

The total positive rate of both R. raoultii and “Ca. R. tarasevichiae” in ticks from the domestic animals (40.7%, 229/563) was significantly higher than that from the vegetative covers (19.5%, 141/723) (P = 69.24, P<0.05).
40.Rationale: The data show higher R. raoultii prevalence in ticks collected from domestic animals, but do not demonstrate that domestic animals themselves are infected reservoirs (i.e., maintain and amplify the pathogen independent of ticks), nor do they measure pathogen prevalence in the animals' blood or tissues. Reservoir status requires direct evidence of infection and transmission competence in the vertebrate host. Further, recommending vaccination or culling is a management decision that requires additional evidence on host reservoir competence, feasibility, and effectiveness—information not present in the passage. Therefore the strong conclusions about primary reservoir status and suggested interventions are not supported by the provided data.

40.Option: The higher positive rate of Rickettsia-infected ticks in domestic animals compared to vegetative covers suggests that domestic animals play a significant role in the transmission of rickettsioses in the Harbin area.
Evidence: Among the ticks, 563 were captured from different domestic animals, while 723 were collected from different types of vegetative covers (Table 3).

DNA of R. raoultii was detected in the ticks from all the different sources but its positive rate in ticks from the domestic animals (32.0%, 180/563) was significantly higher than that from the vegetative covers (10.5%, 76/723) (P = 91.42, P<0.05).

The total positive rate of both R. raoultii and “Ca. R. tarasevichiae” in ticks from the domestic animals (40.7%, 229/563) was significantly higher than that from the vegetative covers (19.5%, 141/723) (P = 69.24, P<0.05).
40.Rationale: The passage provides statistically significant evidence that ticks collected from domestic animals have higher rates of Rickettsia infection than ticks from vegetative covers. From an epidemiological perspective, this pattern reasonably supports the inference that domestic animals are important in local tick infection dynamics and therefore likely play a significant role in transmission cycles in the Harbin area. The option uses cautious language ('suggests' and 'play a significant role') and does not overstate causality beyond what the data support, making it a justified inference from the presented results.

41.Option: The high prevalence of HTLV-1 infection in São Paulo's Japanese-descendant community is primarily driven by horizontal transmission through blood transfusions and injection drug use, as these are common risk behaviors in this population.
Evidence: Some risk behaviors were less reported in this population, such as history of tattooing (7.2%), piercing (4.1%), having been in prison (0.3%), injection drug use (0.1%) and multiple sexual partners (4.2%).
Nearly 7% of the participants reported having received a blood transfusion, and of these, almost half was before 1993, when compulsory HTLV-1 screening was implemented for Brazilian blood donors.

In the multiple logistic regression model HTLV-1 infection was associated with age ≥45 years (p = 0.025), female sex (p = 0.040) (Fig 2), being first and second-generation Japanese immigrants (p = 0.002 and p = 0.017, respectively) and with report of having sexual partners with history of blood transfusion (p = 0.014).
41.Rationale: The passage states that injection drug use was extremely rare (0.1%) and only ~7% reported having received a blood transfusion, so these behaviors are not common in this population. The multivariate model associated HTLV-1 with age, sex, generational status and having sexual partners with history of blood transfusion, not with personal injection drug use or personal transfusion as primary drivers. Therefore the claim that prevalence is primarily driven by blood transfusions and injection drug use is not supported by the passage.

41.Option: The high prevalence of HTLV-1 infection among Japanese immigrants and their descendants in São Paulo, Brazil, is likely due to a combination of vertical transmission during prolonged breastfeeding and sexual transmission within long-standing relationships, particularly among older generations.
Evidence: Although most people infected by the HTLV-1 remain asymptomatic, they are viral reservoirs and maintain the HTLV-1 transmission chain, transmitting it both to their children and to their sexual partners, once HTLV-1 is horizontally transmitted through close contact with fresh blood and unprotected sexual intercourse, as well as vertically through prolonged breast feeding [3–5].

In the majority of these 12 families (75%), at least two members tested anti-HTLV-1 positive. Genetic distance between viral isolates of each family group was 0.0%. ... All of them reported having been breastfed.
Although all tested OKSP106 offspring were breastfed for more than 6 months, only one (OKSP380) was HTLV-1-infected and presented 100% identity with her mother, suggesting vertical transmission.
In contrast, in families 6, 7, 8, 9 and 10, genetic distance between viral isolates of index cases and their couples showed 100% identity, evidencing sexual transmission.
HTLV-1 infection was associated with age ≥45 years (p = 0.025) ... being first and second-generation Japanese immigrants (p = 0.002 and p = 0.017, respectively)
41.Rationale: The passage explicitly describes both vertical transmission via prolonged breastfeeding and sexual transmission, with molecular evidence (100% identity within mother-child and couple pairs) supporting these routes within family clusters. The multivariate association with older age and being first/second-generation immigrants supports that transmission has occurred over longer-standing relationships and across older cohorts. Thus a combined role of breastfeeding-related vertical transmission and sexual transmission among older generations is directly supported by the data.

41.Option: The fact that most HTLV-1-infected individuals remain asymptomatic suggests that transmission dynamics in this population are not influenced by clinical symptoms or disease progression.
Evidence: Although most people infected by the HTLV-1 remain asymptomatic, they are viral reservoirs and maintain the HTLV-1 transmission chain, transmitting it both to their children and to their sexual partners...
Of all those infected, 61.5% have attended the clinic to date and the majority remain asymptomatic (97.3%)
41.Rationale: The passage states that most infected persons are asymptomatic yet remain viral reservoirs who transmit HTLV-1 to children and sexual partners. This supports the inference that transmission continues irrespective of symptomatic disease, i.e., clinical symptoms or progression do not appear necessary for ongoing transmission dynamics in this community.

41.Option: The presence of both HTLV-1aA and HTLV-1aB subtypes in São Paulo suggests that the virus was introduced independently multiple times through different waves of Japanese immigration, rather than through sustained transmission within families.
Evidence: The phylogenetic analysis of the HTLV-1 5’LTR region revealed that all isolates were classified as Cosmopolitan (1a) subtype. Of these, 47.8% HTLV-1 isolates were classified as belonging to the Transcontinental (A) subgroup and 52.2% to the Japanese (B) subgroup (Fig 3).

With the beginning of Japanese immigration to Brazil in 1908, mostly from provinces of Kumamoto, Hokkaido, Kagoshima, and Okinawa, Brazil is the country with the largest number of Japanese and their descendants outside of Japan.
genetic distance between viral isolates of index cases and their couples showed 100% identity, evidencing sexual transmission.
All of them reported having been breastfed...only one (OKSP380) was HTLV-1-infected and presented 100% identity with her mother, suggesting vertical transmission.
41.Rationale: While the presence of two subgroups could be consistent with multiple introductions, the passage also provides direct molecular evidence of recent intrafamilial transmission (100% identity among couples and mother-child pairs) and documents vertical and sexual transmission within families. The option asserts an exclusive interpretation ('rather than through sustained transmission within families'), which is not supported: the data show clear evidence of sustained familial transmission. Therefore the claim cannot be derived solely from the passage.

41.Option: The higher prevalence of HTLV-1 infection among first and second-generation Japanese immigrants is likely due to increased exposure to environmental factors in São Paulo, such as air pollution or urban living conditions, rather than transmission from infected family members.
Evidence: In the multiple logistic regression model HTLV-1 infection was associated with age ≥45 years (p = 0.025), female sex (p = 0.040) (Fig 2), being first and second-generation Japanese immigrants (p = 0.002 and p = 0.017, respectively) and with report of having sexual partners with history of blood transfusion (p = 0.014).
Although all tested OKSP106 offspring were breastfed for more than 6 months, only one (OKSP380) was HTLV-1-infected and presented 100% identity with her mother, suggesting vertical transmission.
In contrast, in families 6, 7, 8, 9 and 10, genetic distance between viral isolates of index cases and their couples showed 100% identity, evidencing sexual transmission.

Although most people infected by the HTLV-1 remain asymptomatic, they are viral reservoirs and maintain the HTLV-1 transmission chain, transmitting it both to their children and to their sexual partners...
41.Rationale: The passage links higher prevalence to generational status and provides molecular and epidemiologic evidence for vertical and sexual transmission within families. It does not present any data or discussion implicating environmental exposures (e.g., air pollution or urban living conditions) as causes of higher prevalence. Therefore attributing higher prevalence to environmental factors rather than family transmission is not supported by the passage.

42.Option: Although PQ8W is well tolerated in most patients, significant falls in haemoglobin can occur after the first dose highlighting the importance of clinical monitoring to identify vulnerable patients with early signs of hemolysis, before clinical deterioration.
Evidence: Overall, 92% (46/50) patients completed all 8-doses of primaquine; the median total mg/kg dose administered was 6.0 mg/kg (range 4.3 − 7.8). In total, 40 (80%) patients completed 12 months of follow up.
The median Hb concentration fell after starting treatment with the lowest concentration reported on D7 (12.2 g/dL, range 8.3–16.6).
The median fall in Hb was -1.5 g/dL (range -4.3 to 2.5) on D3 and -1.6 g/dL (-5.1 to 3.2) on D7.
The median maximal change in Hb was -1.8 g/dl (range -5.6 to 0), representing a median maximal fractional fall in Hb of -13.0% (range -33.3 to 0%).
Two patients had falls in Hb exceeding 5 g/dL (Fig 4).

There was one SAE reported in a 16-year old male with the Kaiping variant, whose Hb fell from 13.5 g/dL at baseline to 9.7 g/dL (28.1%) on D3, and 9.0 g/dL (33.3%) on D7 associated with general malaise, anorexia and reduced oral intake requiring hospital admission for intravenous fluids.
42.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence that most G6PDd patients completed PQ8W and that the regimen was generally tolerated (92% completed all doses). It also documents measurable and sometimes large falls in hemoglobin occurring early after treatment initiation (notably by D3–D7), including two falls >5 g/dL and one SAE requiring hospitalization for IV fluids. These facts support the epidemiological inference that while PQ8W is tolerated by most, significant hemolysis can occur early in some patients and clinical monitoring is therefore necessary to detect early signs of hemolysis before clinical deterioration.

42.Option: The 8-week primaquine regimen is ineffective in preventing relapses in patients with severe G6PD deficiency, as evidenced by the high rate of recurrent parasitaemia in patients with known G6PD variants.
Evidence: Two Vietnamese patients had recurrent symptomatic P. vivax parasitaemia on D68 and D207. Both were males with confirmed hemizygous G6PDd; they had completed their PQ8W and received total primaquine doses of 5.5 and 5.9 mg/kg, respectively.
The incidence rate of P. vivax parasitaemia was 46.8 recurrences/1000 p-y (95% CI: 11.7 − 187.1), and the 12-month incidence risk of recurrence was 5.1% (95% CI: 1.3–18.9%).
The corresponding incidence risks and rate of P. vivax parasitaemia were higher in G6PD normal patients enrolled into all arms of the randomised controlled trial (Table 3).
42.Rationale: The passage shows a low overall 12-month recurrence risk (5.1%) and only two documented recurrences among the 50 G6PDd patients; it does not report a high recurrence rate in patients with known G6PD variants. Moreover, recurrence rates were reportedly higher in G6PD normal patients in the main trial. Therefore the claim that PQ8W is ineffective in preventing relapses in patients with severe G6PD deficiency is not supported by the presented data.

42.Option: The 8-week primaquine regimen should be avoided in patients with G6PD deficiency due to the high risk of life-threatening hemolysis, as demonstrated by the need for hospitalization in one patient.
Evidence: There was one SAE reported in a 16-year old male with the Kaiping variant, whose Hb fell from 13.5 g/dL at baseline to 9.7 g/dL (28.1%) on D3, and 9.0 g/dL (33.3%) on D7 associated with general malaise, anorexia and reduced oral intake requiring hospital admission for intravenous fluids.
Two patients had falls in Hb exceeding 5 g/dL (Fig 4). ... The lowest recorded Hb concentration was 8.2 g/dL.

Overall, 92% (46/50) patients completed all 8-doses of primaquine; the median total mg/kg dose administered was 6.0 mg/kg (range 4.3 − 7.8). In total, 40 (80%) patients completed 12 months of follow up.
No patients required a blood transfusion.
42.Rationale: While one patient required hospitalization for IV fluids because of clinically significant hemolysis, the study data do not indicate widespread life-threatening hemolysis: most patients completed therapy, only two had Hb falls >5 g/dL, the lowest Hb recorded was 8.2 g/dL, and no transfusions were needed. The single hospitalization does not support the categorical conclusion that PQ8W should be avoided in all G6PDd patients due to high risk of life-threatening hemolysis; such a strong recommendation is not derivable from these data alone.

42.Option: The use of the 8-week primaquine regimen in patients with G6PD deficiency is associated with a higher risk of severe anemia compared to G6PD normal patients treated with daily primaquine regimens.
Evidence: The median maximal change in Hb was -1.8 g/dl (range -5.6 to 0), representing a median maximal fractional fall in Hb of -13.0% (range -33.3 to 0%).

The median maximal fall in Hb was -2.0 g/dL (range -5.6 to 0) in the 25 confirmed hemizygous males, -2.0 g/dL (range -4.1 to -1.1) in the 5 confirmed heterozygous females, and -1.7 g/dL (range -3.7 to 0) in the 16 patients without confirmed G6PD deficiency.

The corresponding median maximal fall in Hb by D28 in G6PD normal patients enrolled into the randomised controlled trial were: -0.9 g/dL (range -4.9 to 0) in the placebo arm, -0.8 g/dL (range -5.6 to 0) in patients treated with 7 day primaquine regimen (1mg/kg/day) and -1.0 g/dL (range -5.5 to 0) in patients treated with the 14 day primaquine regimen (0.5 mg/kg/day);

48.Option: Modest use of insecticide-treated cattle by livestock farmers in areas of relatively high cattle density can reduce the role of cattle in T. b. rhodesiense transmission despite the presence of high tsetse densities in adjacent wildlife areas.

Chemical analyses of hair samples collected at the time of the survey showed that 18% of 176 individual cattle and 27% of 48 herds had detectable levels of alpha cypermethrin or cypermethrin (ranging from 1.6 μg/g to 1278 μg/g), indicating treatment within approximately 7 days previously.

If we assume a three-day feeding cycle, and that 16% of cattle are treated weekly, tsetse probability of mortality from insecticide-treated cattle would be approximately 0.05 per day. If we assume a two-day feeding cycle and that 27% cattle are treated, the probability of mortality from insecticide would be approximately 0.14 per day.
Using the parameter values in Table 1, the best fit additional daily probability of adult mortality (μF) was 0.15 per day (S1 Table, Fig 2).
Of the 750 cattle sampled in 2016, none was positive for T. b. rhodesiense DNA...

Importantly, no cattle were PCR-positive for T. b. rhodesiense in 2016. Together these observations support the inference that modest use of insecticide-treated cattle can reduce the role of cattle in T. b. rhodesiense transmission even while tsetse persist at higher density in adjacent wildlife.

By contrast, concerns about community acceptability (fear of sampling, not receiving individual results) attracted substantially lower agreement (around 42–51%).
