Evidence: During the campaign, individual cards were scanned and manually date-stamped for each dose received; temporary cards were provided for anyone not bringing a vaccine card. A vaccination registry was created based on this documentation (18).

Overall, OCV coverage by respondents’ recall was 91% (95% CI: 88%‒93%) for the first dose and 85% (95% CI: 81%‒89%) for the second dose, among the 1,102 individuals aged ≥1 year in households surveyed at first follow-up.

Vaccination cards were available for 443 (40%) individuals. OCV coverage by card, among individuals with available documentation, was 93% (95% CI: 89%‒96%) for the first dose and 84% (95% CI: 78%‒89%) for the second dose (Table 6).
121.Rationale: The passage states that a vaccination registry was created from scanned and date-stamped cards, but it does not present registry-derived coverage estimates or document a discrepancy between registry data and survey estimates. Without explicit registry coverage numbers or evidence of registry errors, one cannot infer from the passage that the registry was poorly maintained or inaccurate. Therefore this conclusion is not supported by the provided text.

122.Option: The cytosolic phospholipase A2 (PLA2c) is the sole enzyme responsible for prostaglandin synthesis in mosquitoes, as its knockdown completely abolishes PG production and immune function.
Evidence: The silencing of PLA2s significantly decreased in 70% the production of PG in the midgut, whereas the silencing of PLA2c did not affect PGs production (Fig 5C).
The knockdown of PLA2c increased the Dengue viral loads (DV2) in the midgut over 5-fold compared to LacZ, whereas PLA2s silencing had no effect on viral loads (Fig 5D).

we show for the first time that, blocking of PG production compromises humoral immune responses in the mosquito Ae. aegypti, downregulating Toll and IMD pathways. As a result, mosquitoes become more susceptible to bacteria and Dengue virus infections. We also characterized one more component of PG synthesis in mosquitoes, describing that a cytosolic phospholipase A2 (PLA2c) induced by the microbiota is involved in PG synthesis in the midgut. Knockdown of this PLA2c increases mosquito susceptibility to bacteria and Dengue infections.
122.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that silencing PLA2s (a secreted PLA2) reduced PG production by ~70%, while silencing PLA2c did not affect PG production (first quote). Thus PLA2c is not the sole enzyme responsible for PG synthesis. Although PLA2c knockdown increased viral loads (second quote) and the authors describe PLA2c as 'involved' in PG synthesis, the experimental data contradict the claim that PLA2c knockdown 'completely abolishes PG production.' Therefore the option is not supported by the passage.

122.Option: The inhibition of prostaglandin synthesis leads to a complete failure of the mosquito immune system, rendering it incapable of mounting any defense against pathogens.
Evidence: ASA pretreatment hampered the expression of defensin A, attacin and cecropin G, while the transcript levels of gambicin were unaltered (Fig 2A, S3 Fig and S1 Dataset).

In vehicle-pretreated Aag2 cells, Ec-challenge induced the upregulation of 361 genes, whereas 822 were down-regulated (Fig 2A, S2 Fig and S1 Dataset). In contrast, in ASA-pretreated, Ec-challenged Aag2, 1008 genes were up-regulated and 1267 genes were down-regulated.
Treatment of mosquitoes with ASA compromised anti-bacterial defense by increasing the mortality rate from 28% to 49%, in the case of Serratia marcescens (Fig 3F).
122.Rationale: The data show that inhibition of PG synthesis (e.g., ASA treatment) suppresses many immune-related genes and increases susceptibility, but does not abolish all immune responses. Some antimicrobial transcripts (e.g., gambicin) were unaltered by ASA, and many genes were still upregulated even with ASA pretreatment (second quote). Mortality increased but was not complete (28% to 49%), indicating partial impairment rather than total failure (third quote). Thus the passage does not support the absolute statement of complete immune failure.

122.Option: Prostaglandins modulate the amplitude of the immune response in mosquitoes, rather than being responsible for its activation.
Evidence: In invertebrate immune responses work on an on/off switch model, where the recognition of pathogen-associated molecular patterns (PAMPs) turns the switch on, and its clearance turns it off. In mosquitoes, besides PAMPs, no other molecules are known to directly regulate the activation and the amplitude of immune responses.

blocking of PG production compromises humoral immune responses in the mosquito Ae. aegypti, downregulating Toll and IMD pathways. As a result, mosquitoes become more susceptible to bacteria and Dengue virus infections.
ASA pretreatment hampered the expression of defensin A, attacin and cecropin G, while the transcript levels of gambicin were unaltered (Fig 2A, S3 Fig and S1 Dataset).
In contrast, in ASA-pretreated, Ec-challenged Aag2, 1008 genes were up-regulated and 1267 genes were down-regulated.
122.Rationale: The passage frames insect immune activation as initiated by PAMP recognition (first quote), implying PAMPs are the primary activators. Experimental results show that blocking prostaglandin production downregulates key immune pathways (Toll and IMD) and reduces AMP induction and pathogen control (second and third quotes), indicating PGs influence the magnitude of the response. The fact that many genes remain upregulated even after ASA treatment (fourth quote) supports that activation can still occur (via PAMPs) but its amplitude is modulated by prostaglandins. Therefore the inference that PGs modulate amplitude rather than being the primary activators is supported.

122.Option: Prostaglandins function as primary signaling molecules that directly trigger the Toll and IMD pathways in response to microbial infection in mosquitoes.
Evidence: In invertebrate immune responses work on an on/off switch model, where the recognition of pathogen-associated molecular patterns (PAMPs) turns the switch on, and its clearance turns it off.
Activation of Toll and IMD leads to the production of AMPs and other effector molecules that participate in the pathogen killing [2].

blocking of PG production compromises humoral immune responses in the mosquito Ae. aegypti, downregulating Toll and IMD pathways. As a result, mosquitoes become more susceptible to bacteria and Dengue virus infections.
122.Rationale: While the passage shows that prostaglandin production influences Toll and IMD pathway output (third quote), it also states that PAMP recognition is the mechanism that turns the immune 'switch' on (first quote) and that activation of Toll/IMD leads to AMP production (second quote). There is no experimental evidence presented that PGs are the primary triggers that directly initiate Toll or IMD activation; rather, PGs appear to modulate pathway activity after activation. Thus the claim that PGs function as primary direct triggers is not supported.

122.Option: Prostaglandins are essential for the initial activation of immune pathways in mosquitoes, as their absence prevents the induction of immune genes in response to bacterial challenge.
Evidence: In invertebrate immune responses work on an on/off switch model, where the recognition of pathogen-associated molecular patterns (PAMPs) turns the switch on, and its clearance turns it off.
In contrast, in ASA-pretreated, Ec-challenged Aag2, 1008 genes were up-regulated and 1267 genes were down-regulated.
ASA pretreatment hampered the expression of defensin A, attacin and cecropin G, while the transcript levels of gambicin were unaltered (Fig 2A, S3 Fig and S1 Dataset).
122.Rationale: The passage indicates PAMP recognition triggers immune activation (first quote). Experimentally, even when prostaglandin synthesis was inhibited (ASA pretreatment), many genes (1008) were still upregulated upon bacterial challenge (second quote), and at least one AMP (gambicin) was unaffected by ASA (third quote). These findings show that initial induction of immune genes can still occur without prostaglandins, although the magnitude of induction of some genes is reduced. Therefore prostaglandins are not shown to be essential for initial activation.

123.Option: The use of a stable emulsion containing GLA, a synthetic glucopyranosyl lipid A molecule similar to the naturally derived MPL®, ensures consistent and universal protection across all Leishmania species and transmission routes.
Evidence: KSAC and L110f recombinant proteins were prepared as previously described [11], [12], [21] and mixed at the time of injection with a stable emulsion (SE) formulation of the pure, synthetic hexa-acylated TLR4 agonist glucopyranosyl lipid A (GLA) [22].

With the exception of two vaccine candidates, a synthetic glycovaccine [9] and autoclaved L. major+CPG [10], all Leishmania vaccines tested to date were challenged with needle inoculation of the Leishmania parasite.

To assess the relevance of the parasite strain used in challenge, we carried out a preliminary experiment using a recent field isolate of L. major (WR-2885)... KSAC-immunized mice were protected while L110f-immuinized mice lost the partial protection displayed against the Friedlin V1 laboratory attenuated parasite strain (Figure S1).
123.Rationale: The passage describes the use of GLA-SE in the experiments but does not claim that this formulation ensures consistent or universal protection across all Leishmania species or transmission routes. On the contrary, the text notes that most prior vaccines were tested by needle inoculation rather than natural sand fly transmission (so different routes matter) and provides data showing differential vaccine performance against different parasite strains (KSAC protected but L110f lost partial protection against a field isolate). Therefore the universal protection claim is not supported by the passage.

123.Option: The robust antibody response and positive IgG2a∶IgG1 ratio induced by KSAC+GLA-SE vaccination are sufficient to confer protection against vector-transmitted L. major infection, independent of T cell responses.
Evidence: Immunization of mice with KSAC+GLA-SE induced a robust antibody response following the first immunization and afterwards maintaining a positive IgG2a∶IgG1 ratio (Fig. 1 A, B).

KSAC+GLA-SE vaccination induced the production of KSAC-specific IFN-\u03b3+ CD4+ T cells, and the relative size of this cell fraction was maintained after a challenge with L. major-infected sand flies (Fig. 3A).
Following sand fly-challenge, only KSAC+GLA-SE immunized mice showed a significant reduction of parasite number (P<0.01) compared to GLA-SE immunized mice (Figure 2D).
Antigen-specific IFN-\u03b3 predominates over IL-10 in vaccinated mice challenged with L. major (Friedlin V1)-infected sand flies
123.Rationale: While the passage shows KSAC+GLA-SE induces a robust antibody response and a positive IgG2a:IgG1 ratio, it also provides multiple lines of evidence that KSAC vaccination elicits strong cell-mediated (Th1) responses—KSAC-specific IFN-γ+ CD4+ T cells, dominant IFN-γ over IL-10, and higher effector memory T cell frequencies—which are correlated with protection. The passage does not present evidence that antibodies alone (independent of T cell responses) are sufficient for protection; in fact, the T cell data indicate a key role for cell-mediated immunity. Thus the claim of sufficiency and independence from T cell responses is not supported.

123.Option: The partial protection observed in L110f-vaccinated mice following either needle or fly challenge with the Friedlin-V1 strain was abrogated when the mice were challenged with sand flies carrying the virulent WR-2885 strain, indicating that the L110f+GLA-SE vaccine is ineffective against field isolates of L. major.
Evidence: Animals immunized with either vaccine also controlled L. major infection post-sand fly challenge through 6 weeks, although the level of protection with the L110f-containing vaccine was reproducibly lower than that for the KSAC-containing vaccine (Figure 2C).

Following sand fly-challenge, only KSAC+GLA-SE immunized mice showed a significant reduction of parasite number (P<0.01) compared to GLA-SE immunized mice (Figure 2D). The number of parasites in L110f+GLA-SE immunized mice were intermediate, but showed no significant difference from controls (Figure 2D).

To assess the relevance of the parasite strain used in challenge, we carried out a preliminary experiment using a recent field isolate of L. major (WR-2885)... The four families of SPMs, lipoxins, maresins, protectins, and resolvins, have been shown to act as initiators of the resolution of acute inflammation; therefore, they limit polymorphonuclear neutrophil infiltration, counteract the production of cytokines and chemokines, and enhance macrophage-mediated actions [14].

130.Option: AT-RvD1 reduces parasite burden by increasing the production of IFNγ, which enhances the activation of cytotoxic T cells and promotes parasite elimination.

131.Option: The DENV/ZIKV IgG-NS1 ratio in mothers is a reliable predictor of ZIKV infection during pregnancy, independent of the clinical outcome in the child.

Therefore the claim that the ratio is a reliable predictor of ZIKV infection independent of child outcome is not supported by the presented data.

131.Option: Pre-existing immunity to DENV, as measured by IgG-NS1 levels, is associated with a reduced risk of all forms of congenital Zika syndrome, including neurodevelopmental and ocular alterations.

131.Option: The presence of anti-ZIKV NS1 IgM antibodies in children with microcephaly suggests a long-lasting immune response that may contribute to protection against severe congenital Zika syndrome.

131.Rationale: While the passage documents a higher seropositivity rate (interpreted as a long-lasting IgM response) among children with microcephaly, it does not provide evidence that this IgM response confers protection. In fact, microcephaly occurred in children with this IgM signal. The authors characterize the association as minor and note low absolute antibody levels. Therefore inferring a protective role from the observed IgM persistence is not supported by the presented data.

131.Option: The development of microcephaly in children with congenital Zika syndrome is associated with low plasma levels of placentally transferred, pre-existing DENV IgG-NS1 antibodies.

131.Rationale: The option proposes a mechanism (ADE) that both contradicts observations and is not supported by the study. The passage states that anti-ZIKV NS1 antibodies lack risk of antibody-dependent enhancement, and prior animal data suggest pre-existing DENV antibodies can enhance vertical transmission (which would worsen, not mediate protection). The study observed that low, not high, DENV IgG-NS1 levels are associated with microcephaly. No evidence is presented that protection (if present) is mediated via ADE; thus this mechanistic claim is unsupported and inconsistent with cited findings.

132.Option: The high density of mCherry presented on the surface of CHIKV 37997-mCherry virions likely contributes to the induction of strong and long-lasting antibody responses against mCherry, which can be achieved with a single infection and surpasses the response elicited by conventional protein immunization with boosting.

In adult mice, infection with 10^4 or 10^6 PFU of CHIKV 37997-mCherry induced high antibody responses against mCherry when assessed at day 14 post-infection and the high levels of antibody lasted for at least 34 days (Fig 10A).

Immunization with 100 μg of purified mCherry protein in complete Freud’s adjuvant induced antibodies against mCherry at more than 20 fold lower level than induced by CHIKV 37997-mCherry infection at day 14 (Fig 10A). Boosting with 50 μg of mCherry enhanced antibody responses to the level induced by CHIKV 37997-mCherry infection in only 2 of 7 mice (Fig 10A).

132.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that mCherry is presented on the virion surface in a repetitive pattern and quantifies this as 240 copies per virion, and it reports that a single infection with CHIKV 37997-mCherry induced high anti-mCherry antibody titers that persisted for at least 34 days. It also reports that conventional protein immunization with purified mCherry produced antibody titers more than 20-fold lower and that boosting only matched the viral presentation response in a minority of mice. Together these observations support the inference that the high-density virion presentation of mCherry contributes to stronger and longer-lasting antibody responses achievable with a single infection, surpassing conventional protein immunization with boosting.

132.Option: The repetitive display of mCherry on CHIKV 37997-mCherry virions likely enhances B cell activation due to the high density of antigen, which may explain the strong and sustained antibody responses observed after a single infection.
Evidence: The resulting chimeric virus presents mCherry on the virion surface in a repetitive pattern and induces strong antibody responses against both CHIKV and mCherry.

134.Option: Individuals with a history of eye worm migration, even in the absence of detectable microfilaremia, are significantly more likely to report a range of clinical symptoms, including neurological manifestations such as paresthesia and transient paralysis, compared to those without any signs of loiasis infection.

In addition, individuals in the EW category reported myalgia and severe headache more often compared to LN (1.36 [1.01–1.82] and 1.33 [1.15–1.54], respectively).

134.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports that both groups with a history of eye worm (EW and EWMF) had significantly higher adjusted prevalence ratios for multiple symptoms compared to LN, including paresthesia and transient paralysis. The summary sentence reiterates that infection states with eye worm history were significantly associated with clinical complaints while microfilaremic individuals did not report more complaints than uninfected persons. Therefore the statement is directly supported by the data.

134.Option: Individuals with a history of eye worm migration are more likely to experience symptoms due to the presence of adult worms in the bloodstream, which can be detected through repeated blood sampling during peak migration periods.
Evidence: Adult L. loa filariae can live more than 15 years in their definitive hosts, continuously migrating through the soft tissue and reproducing sexually.
Female worms release microfilariae (mf) which can be found in the peripheral blood of patients, in sometimes very high numbers (up to 300,000 mf/mL).
Loiasis without detectable microfilaremia is commonly referred to as “occult loiasis”.
5 mL of peripheral blood were collected from all participants between 10 am and 3 pm in an EDTA tube.
Two Giemsa-stained thick blood smears of 10 μL each were examined under a microscope and, if no mf were found, additional examination of 1 mL whole blood was performed after lysis and centrifugation.
134.Rationale: The passage describes adult L. loa as migrating through soft tissue (subcutaneous), not circulating as adults in the bloodstream; blood contains microfilariae, not adult worms. While the study used timed and concentrated blood sampling to detect microfilariae, there is no evidence in the passage that adult worms are present in the bloodstream or that repeated blood sampling during 'peak migration periods' would detect adults or is the reason for symptoms. Thus the mechanistic claim and the diagnostic implication about adults in blood are not supported by the provided text.

134.Option: The clinical burden of loiasis is underestimated in endemic regions because individuals with microfilaremia are more likely to be diagnosed and treated, while those with only a history of eye worm are often overlooked despite reporting more severe symptoms.
Evidence: The majority of clinical data in scientific literature stem from reports on travelers and temporary residents of endemic regions [1,7–11].

In summary, the infection states comprising participants with history of eye worm were significantly associated with the occurrence of clinical complaints. Contrarily, microfilaremic individuals reported complaints in a similar proportion as persons without signs of loiasis infection.
134.Rationale: While the passage documents that individuals with a history of eye worm report more symptoms than microfilaremic individuals, it does not provide evidence about diagnosis or treatment patterns (who is more likely to be diagnosed or treated) or explicitly state that the clinical burden is underestimated for this reason. The claim about underestimation due to diagnostic/treatment bias is an inference beyond the data presented and is not directly supported by the passage.

134.Rationale: The passage shows that Calabar swelling-like manifestations and urticaria were reported more often by individuals with a history of eye worm (EW), and that EWMF sometimes reported fewer Calabar swelling-like events than EW. Microfilaremic-only subjects (MF) did not report more complaints than uninfected persons. Although the introduction states high microfilarial densities are associated with risk of severe manifestations in general, the presented comparative symptom data do not support the specific claim that higher microfilarial density is directly linked to increased Calabar swelling or urticaria; in fact, some analyses suggest the opposite (EW > EWMF or MF). Therefore the specific causal linkage asserted is not supported by the passage.

Evidence: All intergroup comparisons are adjusted to sex, age and positivity of Mansonella PCR.

LP had higher total WBC counts (median [inter quartile range]: 7.4 [5.9–8.9] vs. 6.7 [5.4–8.4] x103 leucocytes/μL, p-adj.<0.001), as well as higher absolute eosinophils/μL (1.1 [0.5–1.8] vs. 0.6 [0.25–1.3] x103, p-adj.<0.001) and relative eosinophil counts (15.0% [8.0–22.0] vs. 9.2% [4.1–17.0] of total WBCC, p-adj.<0.001) compared to LN.

Comparing all individuals with microfilaremia (MF and EWMF) to patients with history of eye worm only (EW), the two former categories were characterized by higher absolute eosinophil (1.3 [0.8–1.85] and 1.4 [0.8–2.0] vs. 0.8 [0.34–1.6] 103/μL; p-adj. = 0.076 and 0.003) and relative eosinophil counts (18.0% [11.0–23.0] and 17.0% [12.0–24.0] vs. 13.0% [6.0–21.0]; p-adj. = 0.016 and 0.002).
HYMF group had higher absolute eosinophil counts than HMF and LMF (1.70 [0.60–2.70] vs.1.40 [0.90–1.90] and 1.30 [0.80–1.90] 103/μL; p-adj. = 0.034 and p-adj. = 0.021), respectively.

In summary, loiasis was associated with absolute and relative eosinophilia. Extent of eosinophilia was higher in microfilaremic individuals in comparison to only eye worm positives. The level of eosinophilia was associated with the quantity of L. loa microfilaremia.
134.Rationale: The analyses were adjusted for Mansonella PCR, yet loiasis-positive individuals (LP) had significantly higher absolute and relative eosinophil counts compared to LN, and higher microfilaremia levels correlated with greater eosinophilia (HYMF > HMF > LMF). These findings indicate that eosinophilia is associated with L. loa infection and increases with L. loa microfilarial density; there is no evidence in the passage that eosinophilia is primarily driven by M. perstans co-infection. Therefore the option is not supported.

135.Option: Intradermal immunization of the OSP:rTTHc conjugate vaccine induced OSP-specific IgA antibody secreting cells in intestinal lamina proprial tissue, suggesting that this route may elicit mucosal immune responses.
Evidence: We found significant IgA secreting cell responses (P<0.05) in the intestinal lamina propria of mice immunized intradermally with OSP:rTTHc in the presence or absence of alum compared with responses in mice receiving alum only (Fig 7).
We did not find this response in animals receiving OSP:rTTHc intramuscularly.
135.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports significant OSP-specific IgA secreting cell responses in the intestinal lamina propria after intradermal immunization (with or without alum) and notes that the same response was absent after intramuscular immunization. Induction of antigen-specific IgA-secreting cells in intestinal lamina propria is direct evidence of a mucosal immune response; therefore the inference that intradermal immunization may elicit mucosal immunity is supported by the data.

135.Option: The use of alum as an adjuvant significantly enhanced anti-OSP and anti-TT serum IgG responses in mice immunized with the OSP:rTTHc conjugate vaccine, regardless of the administration route.
Evidence: These responses were comparable at different doses and molar ratios of OSP to TThc, with no major increase afforded when the vaccine was administered in the presence of alum.
We found a significant increase in OSP (Fig 3A) and tetanus (Fig 3B)-specific IgG responses in serum of mice who received Inaba OSP-rTTHc vaccine intramuscularly or intradermally.
135.Rationale: The passage states that serum IgG responses were comparable across doses and molar ratios and explicitly notes that there was "no major increase afforded when the vaccine was administered in the presence of alum." Thus the claim that alum significantly enhanced anti-OSP and anti-TT IgG responses regardless of route is not supported by the reported data.

135.Option: Intradermal immunization with the OSP:rTTHc conjugate vaccine resulted in higher systemic IgG responses compared to intramuscular immunization, indicating superior overall immunogenicity.
Evidence: We found a significant increase in OSP (Fig 3A) and tetanus (Fig 3B)-specific IgG responses in serum of mice who received Inaba OSP-rTTHc vaccine intramuscularly or intradermally.

In general, immune responses were comparable across all the vaccine cohorts, although anti-OSP responses rose more rapidly in mice immunized with 20 μg of OSP (5:1) compared to responses in mice immunized with 10 μg or 50 μg.
Anti-TT responses were highest in mice immunized intradermally, with a significant boosting increase following the third immunization (Fig 3B).
135.Rationale: The passage reports that serum IgG responses to OSP were observed after both intramuscular and intradermal immunization and that immune responses were generally comparable across cohorts. Only anti-TT responses were specifically noted as highest after intradermal immunization. There is no statement that intradermal immunization gave higher systemic anti-OSP IgG overall or superior overall immunogenicity; therefore the option overstates what can be inferred.

135.Option: The conjugation method using squaric acid chemistry enabled the induction of both systemic and mucosal immune responses regardless of the administration route.
Evidence: Conjugation of OSP to rTTHc was performed via squaric acid chemistry as previously described [16,19].

We found significant IgA secreting cell responses (P<0.05) in the intestinal lamina propria of mice immunized intradermally with OSP:rTTHc in the presence or absence of alum compared with responses in mice receiving alum only (Fig 7).
We did not find this response in animals receiving OSP:rTTHc intramuscularly.
135.Rationale: While the vaccine was prepared using squaric acid chemistry and the study shows that systemic responses occurred and that mucosal IgA responses were induced, those mucosal responses were route-dependent (observed after intradermal but not intramuscular immunization). The phrase "regardless of the administration route" is contradicted by the reported absence of lamina propria IgA responses after intramuscular immunization; therefore the option is not supported.

135.Option: The absence of IgA-secreting cell responses in the intestinal lamina propria after intramuscular immunization suggests that the vaccine is ineffective at inducing mucosal immunity regardless of route.
Evidence: We found significant IgA secreting cell responses (P<0.05) in the intestinal lamina propria of mice immunized intradermally with OSP:rTTHc in the presence or absence of alum compared with responses in mice receiving alum only (Fig 7).
We did not find this response in animals receiving OSP:rTTHc intramuscularly.
135.Rationale: The passage shows that intramuscular immunization did not elicit intestinal IgA-secreting cells, but intradermal immunization did induce significant lamina propria IgA responses. Thus the vaccine is capable of inducing mucosal immunity when given via the intradermal route; concluding it is ineffective regardless of route is contradicted by the data.

136.Option: The greater allelic diversity observed in the Australian B. pseudomallei population compared to the Southeast Asian population suggests that the Australian strain is more virulent and responsible for higher mortality rates in clinical cases.
Evidence: Differences in mortality rates also differ greatly between the two endemic regions, with mortality rates of approximately 50% in Thailand compared with <20% in Australia [5]. The difference in mortality rates could reflect differences in virulence but is probably more likely to be due to differences in intensive care provisions between the two regions [6].

Previous studies have demonstrated that B. pseudomallei populations from the melioidosis-endemic regions in Southeast Asia and Australia are not only geographically distinct but exhibit differences in clinical presentation and genetic features [2], [3], [4].
136.Rationale: The passage notes differing mortality rates between regions and states that differences "could reflect differences in virulence but is probably more likely to be due to differences in intensive care provisions." It does not state that Australian isolates have greater allelic diversity, nor does it link allelic diversity directly to higher virulence. Therefore the specific causal claim that greater allelic diversity in Australia explains higher mortality cannot be derived from the passage.

136.Option: The use of Bayesian clustering algorithms like Structure and BAPS is justified because they can detect population structure even when recombination rates are low and genetic diversity is high, making them ideal for species with strong clonal inheritance.
Evidence: Due to relatively low levels of sequence diversity and high levels of lateral gene transfer among B. pseudomallei isolates [8], [11], sequence data from only seven genes are insufficient for robust phylogenetic discrimination [11], [12].
The program Structure [13] is a Bayesian-based clustering algorithm that has been used to infer population structure within genetically diverse bacteria such as Helicobacter pylori [14].
we further evaluate and update the previous population assignments [11] by comparing these results with commonly used assignment algorithms.
136.Rationale: This option asserts that these algorithms are justified for situations with low recombination and strong clonality. The passage actually emphasizes the opposite: B. pseudomallei has "high levels of lateral gene transfer" (high recombination) and "relatively low levels of sequence diversity," conditions that make traditional phylogenetic methods insufficient and motivate the use of population genetics/Bayesian clustering approaches. Thus the premise of low recombination/strong clonality is not supported by the passage.

136.Option: The high rate of recombination relative to mutation in B. pseudomallei necessitates the use of allele frequency-based methods, such as Structure and BAPS, for reliable population assignment, as traditional phylogenetic approaches are insufficient due to genetic mixing.
Evidence: Due to relatively low levels of sequence diversity and high levels of lateral gene transfer among B. pseudomallei isolates [8], [11], sequence data from only seven genes are insufficient for robust phylogenetic discrimination [11], [12].

Pearson et al. therefore used a population genetics approach to determine that B. pseudomallei STs form two distinct populations, conforming to the geographic regions of Southeast Asia and Australia [11].
The program Structure [13] is a Bayesian-based clustering algorithm that has been used to infer population structure within genetically diverse bacteria such as Helicobacter pylori [14].

We used both BAPS and Structure results to assess population assignments [18]. BAPS (version 4) is another free software package for Bayesian inference of genetic structure within a given dataset [19], [20], [21], [22].
GenAlEx ... requires a priori population designations to define population allele frequencies and subsequently calculate the likelihood of population assignment for each ST.
136.Rationale: The passage states that high levels of lateral gene transfer (recombination) and low sequence diversity undermine the utility of phylogenetic methods based on limited loci. It then describes the use of population-genetic, allele frequency–based Bayesian clustering methods (Structure, BAPS) and allele-frequency analyses (GenAlEx) to assign populations. This supports the principle that recombination/genetic mixing makes allele-frequency and clustering approaches more appropriate than traditional phylogenetics.

136.Option: The identification of a fourth population by BAPS, consisting of three STs including a novel human isolate from the USA, suggests that B. pseudomallei has undergone recent and widespread global dispersal beyond its endemic regions.
Evidence: The number of populations supported by Structure and BAPS are two and three respectively.

The database consisted of 641 B. pseudomallei STs from 1802 isolates collected over 89 years from 35 countries. Approximately 44% of these isolates were collected in Southeast Asia and 53% in Australia and Papua New Guinea.
This organism is found sporadically in other equatorial regions such as South and Central America, Africa, and the Indian subcontinent [1].
136.Rationale: The passage states that Structure supported two populations and BAPS supported three; it does not report a fourth population or a novel USA isolate as described in the option. While the passage notes sporadic findings outside the main endemic regions, it does not provide evidence of a recent, widespread global dispersal or of the specific BAPS-identified fourth population claimed. Therefore this inference cannot be drawn from the passage.

136.Option: The high rate of recombination in B. pseudomallei leads to frequent misclassification of isolates into incorrect populations, which undermines the reliability of both Structure and BAPS for epidemiological attribution.
Evidence: Due to relatively low levels of sequence diversity and high levels of lateral gene transfer among B. pseudomallei isolates [8], [11], sequence data from only seven genes are insufficient for robust phylogenetic discrimination [11], [12].
We further evaluate and update the previous population assignments [11] by comparing these results with commonly used assignment algorithms.

To be conservative in our assignment of STs to a population, we suggest that a ST only be considered to be from a given population if Structure or BAPS assigned it to that population ≥95% of the time.
136.Rationale: Although the passage acknowledges high lateral gene transfer and the challenges it poses for phylogenetic discrimination, it describes using multiple population-assignment methods (Structure, BAPS, GenAlEx) and conservative thresholds (≥95%) to assess and increase confidence in assignments. The passage does not state that high recombination causes frequent misclassification that undermines reliability of these methods; rather, it outlines strategies to evaluate and improve assignment robustness. Thus the strong negative claim about undermining reliability is not supported by the passage.

137.Option: The presence of multiple PvDBP copies in Ethiopian P. vivax isolates is a direct result of intense transmission and high rates of polyclonal infections, which promote gene duplication through genetic drift.
Evidence: P. vivax infections are also much more likely to contain multiple parasite strains in areas where transmission is intense and/or relapse is common [10, 15–18].

The moimix analyses showed that 24 of the 44 isolates from Ethiopia had Fws values of more than or equal to 0.90 ... Eight of the isolates from Ethiopia were shown to have low Fws values ranging from 0.4 to 0.7.

Compared to the Ethiopian isolates, a larger number of erythrocyte binding gene regions including DBP1, the MSP3 and RBP gene families were detected with high-order copies in the Southeast Asian isolates (Fig 5; S5 Table).

Though fewer erythrocyte binding genes were detected with copy number variation in the isolates from Ethiopia, the range of copy number e.g., in the DBP1 gene was higher in isolates from Ethiopia than in isolates from Southeast Asia (S5 Table).
137.Rationale: The option asserts a causal link from intense transmission and high polyclonality to the multiple PvDBP copies in Ethiopia. The passage actually indicates that intense transmission and higher polyclonality are characteristic of regions like Southeast Asia, not necessarily Ethiopia, and that many Ethiopian isolates were monoclonal (Fws ≥ 0.90). Moreover, the passage shows that more erythrocyte binding loci with high-order copies were detected in Southeast Asia, whereas Ethiopia had fewer CNV loci though a wider DBP1 copy-number range. There is no direct evidence in the passage that intense transmission or high polyclonality in Ethiopia caused PvDBP duplication via genetic drift; therefore the causal claim is not supported by the provided data.

137.Option: The phylogenetic clustering of P. vivax isolates with multiple PvDBP copies in Ethiopia suggests that gene duplication events occurred only once in the evolutionary history of the parasite population.
Evidence: An unrooted whole genome phylogenetic tree of the 44 Ethiopian samples showing the evolution of (A) PvDBP and (B) PvMSP3. The Ethiopian isolates were divided into three subclades.
No distinct clusters were observed between isolates with single and multiple PvDBP and PvMSP3 genes.
These patterns suggest that these gene regions could have expanded multiply among samples at different locations.
137.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports that isolates with single and multiple PvDBP copies are intermingled on the tree and states that the patterns "suggest that these gene regions could have expanded multiply among samples at different locations." That observation contradicts the assertion that duplication occurred only once; rather the evidence supports multiple independent expansion events. Thus the option is not derivable from the passage.

137.Option: The expansion of the DBP1 gene in P. vivax isolates from Ethiopia is likely due to selection pressure from antimalarial drugs, as evidenced by the high copy number of resistance genes in the same population.
Evidence: high copy number observed in Pvcrt and the multidrug resistant gene (Pvmdr1) has also been shown to be associated with increased antimalaria drug resistance [21,22].

Among the 43 erythrocyte binding genes, duplications were observed in DBP1 on chromosome 6 and MSP3.11 on chromosome 10. DBP1 ranged from one to as high as five copies, and MSP3.11 ranged from one to as high as three copies among our samples (Fig 5), consistent with previous findings [19, 20, 79].

Compared to the Ethiopian isolates, a larger number of erythrocyte binding gene regions including DBP1, the MSP3 and RBP gene families were detected with high-order copies in the Southeast Asian isolates (Fig 5; S5 Table).
137.Rationale: While the passage notes that high copy number in genes such as Pvcrt and Pvmdr1 has been associated with drug resistance (background literature), it does not present data showing high copy number of these resistance genes in the Ethiopian sample set or any direct association between antimalarial drug pressure and DBP1 expansion in Ethiopia. The observed DBP1 duplications in Ethiopia are reported, but no evidence in the passage links those duplications to drug selection. Therefore the specific causal inference that DBP1 expansion is due to antimalarial drug pressure is not supported by the provided data.

137.Option: The high level of genetic diversity in erythrocyte binding genes among P. vivax isolates from Ethiopia is primarily driven by frequent recombination and gene conversion events between paralogous genes.
Evidence: Recent genomic studies have indicated that some highly polymorphic genes in the P. vivax genome are associated with red blood cell invasion and immune evasion [10, 12, 19, 23].

Members of the erythrocyte binding gene family, including reticulocyte binding proteins (RBPs), Duffy-binding proteins (DBPs), and merozoite surface proteins (MSP3 and MSP7) have been previously shown to exhibit high sequence variation in P. vivax [20, 30].
The PvMSP multigene family has the highest number of polymorphic sites when compared to the others, with PvMSP3 and PvMSP1 the highest number of nonsynonymous and synonymous mutations.
These patterns suggest that these gene regions could have expanded multiply among samples at different locations.
137.Rationale: The passage documents high genetic diversity and polymorphism in erythrocyte binding genes and describes duplication/expansion events, but it does not provide direct evidence that recombination or gene conversion among paralogs is the primary driver. No analyses or specific results (e.g., recombination detection statistics, gene conversion signals) are presented to support recombination/gene conversion as the principal mechanism. Thus the mechanistic claim is not derivable from the passage.

137.Option: Despite lower overall transmission intensity in Ethiopia compared to Southeast Asia, P. vivax isolates from Ethiopia exhibit higher copy number variation in the DBP1 gene, suggesting that host-parasite interactions, particularly related to Duffy phenotype diversity, may drive gene expansion rather than transmission intensity.
Evidence: Compared to P. falciparum, P. vivax isolates from Southeast Asia (e.g., Thailand and Myanmar), Pacific Oceania (Papua New Guinea), and North and South America (Mexico, Peru, and Colombia) have significantly higher nucleotide diversity at the genome level. This could be the result of frequent gene flow via human movement, intense transmission, and/or variation in host susceptibility [10–14].
Compared to Southeast Asia and South America where P. vivax is highly endemic, data on polymorphisms in erythrocyte binding gene candidates of P. vivax from Africa is limited.

Though fewer erythrocyte binding genes were detected with copy number variation in the isolates from Ethiopia, the range of copy number e.g., in the DBP1 gene was higher in isolates from Ethiopia than in isolates from Southeast Asia (S5 Table).

It was previously thought that most African populations were immune to P. vivax infections due to the absence of DARC gene expression required for erythrocyte invasion. However, several recent reports have indicated the emergence and potential spread of P. vivax across Africa [32, 48–50].

Functional analyses indicated that region II of this gene bound to both Duffy-positive and Duffy-negative reticulocytes, although at a lower frequency compared to PvDBP, suggestive of its role in erythrocyte invasion [40].
137.Rationale: The passage establishes that Southeast Asia is characterized by higher endemicity and intense transmission, whereas data from Africa (including Ethiopia) are from a region with different transmission context. Despite this, the authors observed that the DBP1 gene in Ethiopian isolates showed a wider range of copy numbers (up to five copies) even though fewer loci overall had CNV. The passage also emphasizes the relevance of Duffy (DARC) status for P. vivax invasion and notes emergence of P. vivax in Africa and genes (e.g., PvEBP) that can bind Duffy-negative reticulocytes. Taken together, these observations support the epidemiological inference that factors related to host-parasite interactions (for example, adaptation to Duffy phenotype diversity) could be driving DBP1 expansion in Ethiopia, rather than simply transmission intensity. The statement is cautious ("may drive") and is consistent with the data and discussion presented in the passage.

138.Option: The variation in drug efficacy estimates across diagnostic methods indicates that the WHO-recommended single Kato-Katz method is no longer valid for monitoring drug efficacy in STH control programs.
Evidence: Therefore, in this study we compared the equivalence in ALB efficacy measured by duplicate Kato-Katz thick smear, Mini-FLOTAC, FECPAKG2 and qPCR in a head-to-head comparison with a single Kato-Katz.
The (G)ERR point estimates were used to classify drug efficacy as ‘satisfactory’, ‘doubtful’ or ‘reduced’ following the WHO criteria recommended for a single Kato-Katz
In summary, a duplicate Kato-Katz provided drug efficacy significantly or marginally equivalent to a single Kato-Katz for the three STH species in all study sites.

For A. lumbricoides infections, efficacy of a single-oral dose of 400 mg ALB estimated by single Kato-Katz was high (ERR >95%) across the different study sites (Table 3). This high drug efficacy was confirmed by the three microscopic methods as well as by qPCR.
138.Rationale: The passage treats the single Kato-Katz as the reference standard for equivalence comparisons and reports that duplicate Kato-Katz (and in many cases other methods) provided equivalent or confirming results. It also uses WHO criteria tied to single Kato-Katz to classify efficacy. These points support continued utility of single Kato-Katz rather than proving it 'no longer valid.' The observed variation does not by itself justify concluding the method is invalid; the study explicitly compares other methods to the single Kato-Katz and finds equivalence for at least duplicate Kato-Katz and confirmation for some species/methods.

138.Option: FECPAKG2 is the most reliable method for assessing drug efficacy due to its high agreement with single Kato-Katz in classifying individual responses, despite its lower sensitivity.
Evidence: FECPAKG2 was previously evaluated as being less sensitive than Kato-Katz, while qPCR was found to have superior sensitivity for all STH [16, 26, 39].

we noticed high numbers of false negative test results at baseline and follow-up for FECPAKG2. When applying FECPAKG2, drug efficacy could not be measured in 438 (32.3%) of the 1,354 individuals with STH infection, because of false negative results at baseline (A. lumbricoides: 148/441, T. trichiura: 135/456 and hookworms: 155/457).
For FECPAKG2 (κFleiss = 0.65, p = 0.01) there was a substantial agreement (0.61 ≤ κFleiss <0.81),
For both duplicate Kato-Katz (κFleiss = 0.81, p <0.001) and qPCR (κFleiss = 0.84, p <0.001) there was almost a perfect agreement (κFleiss ≥0.8).
138.Rationale: The passage reports that FECPAKG2 has lower sensitivity and produced a large number of false negatives (drug efficacy could not be measured in 32.3% of infected individuals). Its agreement with single Kato-Katz (κ=0.65) is only 'substantial' and is lower than duplicate Kato-Katz and qPCR (both κ≥0.81). Therefore the claim that FECPAKG2 is the most reliable method is not supported by the data presented.

138.Option: The use of qPCR for monitoring drug efficacy is not recommended due to its high sensitivity, which leads to false positive results at follow-up and inflates the estimation of drug failure.
Evidence: FECPAKG2 was previously evaluated as being less sensitive than Kato-Katz, while qPCR was found to have superior sensitivity for all STH [16, 26, 39].

When applying qPCR, individual drug efficacy could not be measured in only 56 (4.1%) of the 1,354 individuals because of false negative results at baseline (top graphs of Figs 2–4: A. lumbricoides: 22/441, T. trichiura: 11/456 and hookworm: 23/456).
For both duplicate Kato-Katz (κFleiss = 0.81, p <0.001) and qPCR (κFleiss = 0.84, p <0.001) there was almost a perfect agreement (κFleiss ≥0.8).

For the other diagnostic methods, the proportion of samples that were positive at follow-up but negative at baseline did not exceed 1.3% (single Kato-Katz: 1.2%; duplicate Kato-Katz: 1.3%; Mini-FLOTAC: 1.0%; qPCR: 1.2%)
138.Rationale: The passage describes qPCR as having superior sensitivity and relatively few false negatives at baseline. It also reports almost perfect agreement between qPCR and single Kato-Katz in classifying drug efficacy and shows that the proportion of cases positive at follow-up but negative at baseline for qPCR (~1.2%) is low and comparable to other methods. The passage provides no evidence that qPCR causes false positives at follow-up that systematically inflate estimates of drug failure; on the contrary, qPCR shows strong agreement with the reference in classification, so the blanket recommendation against qPCR is not supported by the presented data.

138.Option: The efficacy of single ALB against STHs was lower in sites where drug pressure has been high, suggesting that anthelmintic resistance is likely emerging in Pemba (Tanzania) and other high-exposure areas.
Evidence: Based on the reported national coverage of drug administration to both PSAC and SAC for the last 5 years (2009–2014; Preventive Chemotherapy Database of the WHO), the site in Ethiopia was considered to have experienced a low drug exposure, the site in Lao PDR a medium drug exposure and the site in Pemba (Tanzania) a high drug exposure prior to the start of the trials [35] (Table 1).
For T. trichiura infections, estimations obtained by single Kato-Katz were 52.9% in Ethiopia, 36.7% in Lao PDR and -11.2% in Pemba (Tanzania).
For hookworm infections (Table 5), the drug efficacy measured by single Kato-Katz was high (>95%) in both Ethiopia (96.3%) and Lao PDR (96.1%), but moderate in Pemba (Tanzania) (84.2%).
The downside of these increased control efforts is that resistance to anthelmintic drugs, such as ALB and MEB, is likely to develop.
138.Rationale: The data do show lower ALB efficacy estimates in Pemba (the site with high historical drug exposure) for some species (notably T. trichiura and to some extent hookworm). However, the passage does not present direct evidence that anthelmintic resistance (AR) is present or 'likely emerging'—there are no resistance markers or causal analyses provided. While the observed lower efficacy is consistent with a possible concern about AR (and the passage elsewhere notes AR is a theoretical downside of large-scale PC), concluding that AR is likely emerging goes beyond the direct evidence in the Passage Body. The study documents an association (lower efficacy in the high-exposure site) but does not establish causation or definite emergence of resistance.

138.Option: The results suggest that despite differences in diagnostic sensitivity and fecal egg counts across methods, there was good agreement in classifying drug efficacy according to WHO guidelines, indicating that each method holds promise for assessing drug efficacy in STH control programs.
Evidence: Generally, these figures highlight three important findings. First, they confirm the distinct differences in sensitivity across the diagnostic methods.
For both duplicate Kato-Katz (κFleiss = 0.81, p <0.001) and qPCR (κFleiss = 0.84, p <0.001) there was almost a perfect agreement (κFleiss ≥0.8).
For FECPAKG2 (κFleiss = 0.65, p = 0.01) there was a substantial agreement (0.61 ≤ κFleiss <0.81), for MiniFLOTAC (κFleiss = 0.60, p = 0.03) there was a moderate agreement (0.41 ≤ κFleiss <0.61).

In summary, a duplicate Kato-Katz provided drug efficacy significantly or marginally equivalent to a single Kato-Katz for the three STH species in all study sites. For the other methods, the equivalence of drug efficacy varied by STH species and study site.
138.Rationale: The passage documents that diagnostic methods differ in sensitivity, but also reports substantial-to-almost-perfect agreement with the single Kato-Katz reference for classifying drug efficacy (κ statistics: duplicate Kato-Katz and qPCR almost perfect; FECPAKG2 substantial; Mini-FLOTAC moderate). The authors summarize that duplicate Kato-Katz was equivalent to single Kato-Katz across species/sites, and that other methods' equivalence varied by species and site. Taken together, these findings support the inference that, despite methodological differences, multiple methods can reliably classify drug efficacy per WHO criteria and therefore each has potential utility (i.e., 'holds promise') in monitoring programs — with the caveat (explicit in the Passage Body) that performance varies by species and context.

139.Option: The functional conservation of cathepsin-like cysteine proteases between B. malayi and C. elegans is complete, as demonstrated by the ability of O. volvulus CPL-1 to rescue the C. elegans cpl-1 mutant.
Evidence: The function(s) of CPLs in filarial nematodes during embryogenesis, however, was only predicted indirectly by immunoelectron microscopy (IEM) [39],[40], while detailed studies of the CPLs in C. elegans [40],[41] have provided more direct proof of their function in the free living nematodes.

However, their predicted essential function during development may not always be conserved in both filarial nematodes and C. elegans, as has been shown for the roles of CPL and CPZ in the molting of O. volvulus and C. elegans [33].
139.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that CPL functions in filarial nematodes were only predicted indirectly and also cautions that predicted functions “may not always be conserved” between filarial nematodes and C. elegans. There is no mention of any rescue experiment (O. volvulus CPL-1 rescuing a C. elegans mutant) or demonstration of complete functional conservation. Therefore the specific claim in the option is not supported by the passage.

139.Option: The use of siRNA in filarial nematodes has been shown to be effective in reducing microfilariae release and inducing embryonic abnormalities, suggesting that siRNA may be a more efficient RNAi tool than dsRNA for targeting filarial genes.
Evidence: In comparison to the control worms, adult worms treated with dsRNA or siRNA corresponding to the Bm-cpl genes showed a persistent and significant reduction in the release of Mf into the culture medium after 1, 3 and 5 days (P<0.05 for all) (Fig. 3).

While the dsRNA Bm-cpl-5 fragment (approx. 800 bp) was taken up by the adult worms and was seen in the hypodermal regions only after 48 h, the siRNA appeared in these regions earlier and was clearly already seen after 24 h (Fig. 2B).

Analysis by qRT-PCR on RNA isolated from the RNAi treated worms... showed that the Bm-cpl transcript levels were reduced by 51.0% in Bm-cpl-5 dsRNA treated worms and by 48.9% in the siRNA treated worms...
The Bm-cpl transcript levels were also reduced in Bm-cpl Pro treated worms; 66.5% and 37.4% in dsRNA and siRNA treated worms respectively...
139.Rationale: The passage shows that siRNA is effective (siRNA treatments produced significant reductions in Mf release and transcript levels) and that siRNA can be taken up faster in some assays. However, the data do not support the general claim that siRNA is more efficient than dsRNA. qRT-PCR results show comparable reductions for Bm-cpl-5 (51.0% dsRNA vs 48.9% siRNA) and actually greater reduction for Bm-cpl Pro with dsRNA (66.5% dsRNA vs 37.4% siRNA). Thus while siRNA is effective, the assertion that it is more efficient than dsRNA is not supported by the experimental results in the passage.

139.Option: The B. malayi cathepsin-like cysteine protease genes belonging to group Ia are essential for embryogenesis and development in adult female worms, as demonstrated by RNAi-mediated silencing leading to reduced microfilariae release, embryonic developmental abnormalities, and decreased transcript levels.
Evidence: dsRNA-mediated silencing of the B. malayi cathepsin-like cysteine protease genes, Bm-cpl-1, Bm-cpl-5, and Bm-cpz results in the reduction of Mf secretion in vitro
This reduction in the secretion of Mf corresponded to a 71.4% (Bm-cpl-1), 92.8% (Bm-cpl-5) and 61.3% (Bm-cpz), inhibition (average) of Mf release 48 h after RNAi treatment.

In comparison to the percentage of intrauterine pre-Mf in both the medium control (21.2%) and the negative control, Ov-cpz-int2 (22.1%) adult female worms treated with Bm-cpl-5 and Bm-cpl Pro dsRNA or siRNA showed significant reductions in pre-microfilariae (pre-Mf); Bm-cpl-5 dsRNA; 6.8% ... Bm-cpl Pro dsRNA; 9.0% ...
Phenotypic changes and structural abnormalities in developing embryos were also observed following RNAi treatment (Fig. 5).

The option frames this as a plausible implication ('may be involved'), which follows from the documented co-incidence in the data.

145.Option: The up-regulation of CCL7 and CCL12 during fibrosis is consistent with the role of the related chemokine CCL2 in other liver diseases [44]. CCL2 is a chemoattractant for HSCs [44] and its expression is associated with fibrogenesis in both human cholestatic liver disease and in the bile-duct-ligated rat model of cholestatic liver injury [45]. CCL2 expression is important for the development of granulomas and fibrosis in schistosomiasis mansoni [46]. In contrast, there was no significant change in its expression during S. japonicum infection; instead, CCL21, CXCL1, CCL12 and particularly CCL7, may promote the initiation of S. japonicum induced fibrosis by recruiting fibrogenic effector cells and by directly inducing fibrogenic signalling pathways.
Evidence: Genes within clusters 2 and 4 also exhibited functional association with leukocyte extravasation signalling. Several genes with chemotactic activity for eosinophils (CCL11), neutrophils (S100A8, S100A9 [19]) and monocytes/macrophages (CCL6, CCL8, CCL12, CXCL14) occur within these clusters and correlated well with the expression of genes encoding markers for eosinophils, neutrophils and alternatively activated macrophage markers, respectively (Table 2).
Expression of CCL7 was correlated with that of alternatively activated macrophage markers and fibrosis associated genes (Table 2).

CCL7 was absent from uninfected livers but was present in infected livers from 6 weeks p.i and was significantly elevated above control levels at 7 weeks p.i. Staining occurred predominantly in granulomas at the periphery of the liver (Figure 5e,f) and localised to the fibrotic zone of these lesions (Figure 5g) resembling the distribution of HSCs (Figure 5h).
145.Rationale: Parts of the option are supported by the Passage Body: CCL7 is shown to be absent in uninfected liver, present and elevated at 6–7 weeks p.i., and localised to fibrotic zones; CCL12 is listed among chemokines with monocyte/macrophage chemotactic activity that are present in late (fibrosis-associated) clusters. The Passage Body also notes correlations between CCL7 and markers of alternatively activated macrophages and fibrosis-associated genes. However, the option introduces several statements not present in the passage (e.g., the role of CCL2 in other liver diseases, the assertion that there was "no significant change" in CCL2 during S. japonicum infection, and the stronger causal claim that these chemokines "directly induc[e] fibrogenic signalling pathways"). Those specific comparative and causal claims are external to the Passage Body and are not documented therein. Because the option combines supported observations with external assertions that are not in the passage, it cannot be fully derived from the Passage Body and is therefore Incorrect.

145.Option: The temporal expression of distinct chemokine subsets regulates the recruitment of specific immune cell types during different phases of S. japonicum-induced granuloma formation and fibrosis.
Evidence: Most striking was the temporal expression of different chemokine subsets (Table 1).

Hierarchical clustering identified seven distinct clusters representing genes that were consistently up-regulated during infection (Cluster 3); up-regulated to a greater extent later in infection (Cluster 2 and Cluster 4); up-regulated to a greater extent earlier in infection (Cluster 5 and Cluster 7); and down-regulated during infection (Cluster 1 and Cluster 6).

Genes in clusters 2 and 4 exhibited peak expression at 6–7 weeks p.i paralleling increased collagen deposition and hepatic fibrosis. Several genes within this cluster are associated with hepatic fibrosis or HSC activation (Figure 3) [18].

Several genes with chemotactic activity for eosinophils (CCL11), neutrophils (S100A8, S100A9 [19]) and monocytes/macrophages (CCL6, CCL8, CCL12, CXCL14) occur within these clusters and correlated well with the expression of genes encoding markers for eosinophils, neutrophils and alternatively activated macrophage markers, respectively (Table 2).

Analysis of the cellular composition of the liver revealed a significant increase in CD4+ T-cells, CD8+ T-cells and B-cells at 4 weeks p.i, compared with uninfected mice where the numbers of these cells in the liver declined thereafter. The number of macrophages in the liver was significantly increased from 4 weeks p.i. compared with uninfected controls and peaked at 6–7 weeks p.i (Figure 4d).
145.Rationale: The Passage Body repeatedly documents temporally distinct chemokine expression patterns (clustered by time), links chemokine subsets to chemotactic activities for specific cell types, shows correlation of chemokine expression with cell-type marker genes, and provides flow cytometry/histology data demonstrating phased recruitment of T-cells, B-cells, macrophages, eosinophils and neutrophils during granuloma development and fibrogenesis. Together these findings directly support the inference that temporally distinct chemokine expression regulates recruitment of specific immune cells across different phases of S. japonicum-induced granuloma formation and fibrosis. The option is therefore derivable from the Passage Body and is Correct.

146.Option: The exchange of deeply buried water molecules in the ATP-binding pocket during the simulation suggests that these waters are essential for maintaining the catalytic competence of TbREL1.

146.Option: The N-terminal domain of TbREL1 retains catalytic activity because it contains a C-terminal OB-fold domain that functions as an RNA-binding module, similar to KREPA2.

Interestingly, an oligonucleotide binding (OB-fold) domain, usually associated with DNA ligases and capping enzymes in cis, appears to be provided by KREPA2 in trans and has been predicted to act as a conformational switch regulating various steps in the editing process [13].
As the N-terminal domain alone has shown to be capable of RNA ligation activity, further studies characterizing its functional dynamics are relevant.
146.Rationale: The passage explicitly states the OB-fold domain is provided by KREPA2 in trans, not that the N-terminal domain contains a C-terminal OB-fold. Although the passage does state the N-terminal domain alone can exhibit ligation activity, the option's causal wording ('retains catalytic activity because it contains a C-terminal OB-fold') misstates the structural arrangement described. Thus the option contradicts the passage and is not supported.

146.Option: The presence of ATP induces significant stabilization of the D210-R288 salt bridge and pi-stacking interaction between F209 and the adenine ring of ATP, which are critical for maintaining the active conformation of TbREL1.

The option asserts specific residue interactions and their critical role, but those specifics are not present in the passage and therefore cannot be derived from it.

146.Option: The conserved peripheral loop (F262-A282) is predicted to be involved in RNA binding due to its high flexibility and proximity to the ATP-binding site, as revealed by principal components analysis.
Evidence: The loops predicted to be involved in RNA binding and interdomain signaling are highlighted in blue and yellow, respectively, and the residues suggested for mutation are highlighted in green.
A comparison of the principal components for the apo and ATP-bound systems illustrates large local and global differences in the enzyme motion.
Ensemble-averaged electrostatics calculations from the MD simulations reveal a putative RNA binding site near the ligase active site, a finding that has previously eluded scientists.

However, it does not name or number a specific 'conserved peripheral loop (F262-A282)', nor does it state that this loop has high flexibility or is proximal to the ATP-binding site. 146.Option: The putative RNA binding site of TbREL1, predicted by ensemble-averaged electrostatics calculations, overlaps with the DNA-binding region of a homologous human DNA ligase, suggesting that TbREL1 may use a similar mechanism for substrate recognition despite its distinct biological function.

147.Option: The high degree of genetic similarity between human and animal Salmonella serovars, despite the absence of genotypically similar strains in the same household, suggests that transmission may occur through indirect environmental routes or across broader community networks rather than direct animal-to-human contact.
Evidence: None of the compounds showed similar serovars in both humans and animals (table 1).
All Salmonella genotypes had at least 80% similarity and the majority varied between 99% and 100%.
Salmonella genotypes causing diarrhoea in children were always clustered with animal genotypes.
147.Rationale: The passage shows two key facts: (1) human and animal isolates are genetically very similar and cluster together by MLST, and (2) similar serovars were not found within the same household compounds. Combining these observations using epidemiologic reasoning supports the inference that transmission is not predominantly direct, within-household animal-to-human contact; instead, genetically related strains circulating across the community or via indirect/environmental routes (e.g., contaminated food, shared environments) are plausible transmission pathways. The passage provides the genetic similarity and the absence of same-household matches that underpin this inference.

147.Option: The high proportion of Salmonella serovars in the chicken population compared to sheep and goats suggests that chickens are the primary reservoir for NTS transmission to humans in this rural Gambian community.
Evidence: The proportion of Salmonella isolated was higher in the chicken population than in other species: 66.6%, 28.6% and 4.8% in chickens, sheep and goats, respectively.

147.Rationale: The passage reports substantially higher isolation of Salmonella from chickens than from sheep or goats, and states that domestic animals can play an important role in maintenance and transmission of NTS. From an epidemiologic perspective, a species with markedly higher carriage prevalence is plausibly the primary reservoir for human exposure in this setting. While the study does not conclusively prove direct transmission from chickens to humans, the observed higher chicken carriage supports the inference that chickens are the most important animal reservoir in this community.

147.Option: The presence of multiple Salmonella serovars in the same household, especially in chickens, indicates that mixed infections and intraspecific recombination are common and may drive the emergence of new pathogenic clones in this community.
Evidence: In the same compound, a diversity of Salmonella serovars were circulating at animal level especially in chickens; the number varying from 2 to 3 different serovars: in compound 1 (C1), S. Moualine, S. Tornow and S. Poona; in compound 6 (C6), S. Schwarzengrund and S. Stanleyville and in compound 7 (C7), S. Offa, S. Give and S. Poona (table 1).
Four new alleles were discovered: hemD (152), hemD (153), hisD (256) and purE (226) and eighteen novel sequence types (ST) (table 1).
147.Rationale: The passage documents multiple serovars circulating within households (particularly in chickens) and reports discovery of novel alleles/sequence types. However, it does not provide evidence that mixed infections (simultaneous infection of a single host) are common, nor does it present data demonstrating intraspecific recombination as the mechanism generating new clones. The conclusion that recombination is common and is driving emergence is a mechanistic claim not supported by the presented data. Therefore this inference goes beyond the evidence in the passage.

147.Option: The significant association between salmonellosis and co-diagnosis with malaria suggests that malaria is a primary driver of NTS transmission in this community, likely through immunosuppression that increases susceptibility to infection.
Evidence: All children who presented with diarrhoea except one were secondarily diagnosed with another disease such as malaria (4 children) or other disease symptoms including fever, or cough (3 children) (table 2).
There was a significant association (p-value<0.01) between expressing clinical signs of salmonellosis, i.e. diarrhoea and being co-diagnosed with a secondary disease (table 3).
147.Rationale: The passage reports an association between diarrhoea due to salmonellosis and having a secondary diagnosis (including malaria in some children). However, an association does not establish causation or identify malaria as a primary driver of NTS transmission. The data do not demonstrate temporal sequence, immunosuppression mechanisms, or that malaria increases exposure or transmission; only co-occurrence is shown. Therefore the causal claim that malaria is a primary driver via immunosuppression is not supported by the passage.

147.Option: The fact that all Salmonella genotypes causing diarrhoea in children were clustered with animal genotypes in the phylogenetic tree indicates that direct animal-to-human transmission occurs within the same household.
Evidence: Salmonella genotypes causing diarrhoea in children were always clustered with animal genotypes.
None of the compounds showed similar serovars in both humans and animals (table 1).
Nevertheless, one serovar namely S. Moualine was simultaneously found in a diarrheic child and in a chicken but in different compounds: C1 in Banico Allunhare and C8 in Koina (table 1, figure 1).
147.Rationale: While phylogenetic clustering shows genetic relatedness between human and animal isolates, the passage explicitly states that no compounds had similar serovars in both humans and animals and the one instance of the same serovar occurred in different compounds. Thus the evidence does not support the specific claim of direct within-household animal-to-human transmission. Clustering alone cannot demonstrate the location or direction of transmission; the absence of same-household matches contradicts the option's assertion.

148.Option: The clustering of ompA types within households suggests that mass azithromycin distribution may have led to the emergence of antibiotic-resistant strains of C. trachomatis.
Evidence: All 21 communities had received mass azithromycin distribution at baseline, with a mean antibiotic coverage of 89% (95% confidence interval [CI] 86–92%) in the 20 communities with available coverage data.
Of these 36 households, 35 contained only a single ompA type across all children and/or timepoints (Fig 3A).
The ompA types of a given community were statistically significantly more similar within the same household than between different households (PERMANOVA P<0.001).
148.Rationale: The passage documents both mass azithromycin distribution and strong within-household clustering of ompA types. However, it provides no data or statements about antibiotic resistance, selection of resistant strains, or changes in antimicrobial susceptibility. Household clustering is more directly explained by localized transmission or exposure to the same strains rather than by emergence of antibiotic resistance. Therefore the claim that clustering suggests emergence of antibiotic-resistant strains is not supported by the passage.

148.Option: The high rate of re-infection with the same ompA type suggests that natural protective immunity to Chlamydia trachomatis is serovar-specific and therefore effective against heterologous strains.

We hypothesized a lack of genotype-specific protective immunity, which would result in a relatively high proportion of re-infections with the same ompA type as the original infection.
Of these 21 children, 14 (67%, 95%CI 43–85%) had the same strain at both timepoints, which was significantly more than would be expected by chance (permutation P<0.001; Fig 3B).
148.Rationale: The option claims that high same-type re-infection indicates serovar-specific natural immunity and effectiveness against heterologous strains. This is the opposite of what the passage implies. The authors hypothesized that a high proportion of same-type re-infections would indicate a lack of genotype-specific protective immunity, and the observed 67% same-type re-infections supported that hypothesis. Thus the passage suggests limited or absent serovar-specific protection, not that such immunity is effective against heterologous strains.

148.Option: The high concordance of ompA types in re-infections implies that the MOMP antigen is highly conserved across C. trachomatis strains, making it a reliable target for universal vaccine development.
Evidence: A total of 15 different ompA types were identified, ranging from A1 to A11 and from B1 to B4.
Each ompA type had at least one amino acid difference relative to the other sequences.
Of these 21 children, 14 (67%, 95%CI 43–85%) had the same strain at both timepoints, which was significantly more than would be expected by chance (permutation P<0.001; Fig 3B).
The major outer membrane protein (MOMP), coded by the ompA gene, is an immunodominant antigen of human Chlamydia trachomatis infections, and a primary target for vaccine development.
148.Rationale: The passage explicitly documents sequence diversity among ompA types and states that each type had at least one amino acid difference, so ompA is not strictly highly conserved. The observed concordance of ompA types in re-infections is presented alongside evidence of household and community clustering of strains and was interpreted as indicating lack of genotype-specific protective immunity rather than conservation of MOMP. Therefore the claim that concordance implies high conservation and that MOMP is a reliable universal target is not supported by the passage.

148.Option: The lower rate of same-ompA-type re-infection in genital chlamydia studies indicates that children with trachoma are more likely to develop long-term immunity than adults with urogenital infections.
Evidence: Studies of genital infections in humans have found some supportive evidence of serovar-specific immunity, since re-infections have usually been found to be caused by different serovars than the initial infection.
We hypothesized a lack of genotype-specific protective immunity, which would result in a relatively high proportion of re-infections with the same ompA type as the original infection.
Of these 21 children, 14 (67%, 95%CI 43–85%) had the same strain at both timepoints, which was significantly more than would be expected by chance (permutation P<0.001; Fig 3B).
148.Rationale: The passage contrasts prior genital infection studies (which often found re-infections caused by different serovars) with the current ocular study that observed frequent same-type re-infections. However, the passage does not provide direct comparative data on long-term immunity between children with trachoma and adults with urogenital infections, nor does it claim children develop greater long-term immunity. In fact, the current study's high same-type re-infection rate argues against durable serovar-specific protection in these children. Thus the specific comparative inference in the option is unsupported by the passage.

148.Option: Chlamydia vaccines targeting only the MOMP may be less likely to confer protective immunity than vaccines containing multiple antigenic epitopes.
Evidence: The major outer membrane protein (MOMP), coded by the ompA gene, is an immunodominant antigen of human Chlamydia trachomatis infections, and a primary target for vaccine development.

Animal studies suggest that natural protective immunity mediated through MOMP may be serovar-specific, since experimental re-infection with the same serovar causes less clinical inflammation than the primary infection, whereas re-infection with a different serovar produces a similar degree of inflammation as the first infection.
We hypothesized a lack of genotype-specific protective immunity, which would result in a relatively high proportion of re-infections with the same ompA type as the original infection.
Of these 21 children, 14 (67%, 95%CI 43–85%) had the same strain at both timepoints, which was significantly more than would be expected by chance (permutation P<0.001; Fig 3B).
A total of 15 different ompA types were identified, ranging from A1 to A11 and from B1 to B4.
Each ompA type had at least one amino acid difference relative to the other sequences.
148.Rationale: The passage establishes MOMP as the immunodominant vaccine target but also documents considerable ompA (MOMP) diversity and presents data consistent with limited genotype-specific protective immunity (high rate of same-type re-infections). From an epidemiological and vaccinology perspective, these findings support the inference that a vaccine targeting only a single antigenic region of MOMP might not provide broad protective immunity across diverse ompA types or may fail to induce durable protection; including multiple antigenic epitopes could broaden coverage and improve the likelihood of protection. This inference is directly supported by the documented antigenic variation and the observed re-infection patterns in the passage.

149.Option: The inability of Shuni virus to replicate in Culex pipiens pipiens after thoracic injection indicates that the species lacks the cellular machinery necessary for arbovirus replication, rendering it incapable of supporting any stage of the virus life cycle.
Evidence: Direct injection of SHUV into the thorax resulted in high infection rates of 68% for Cx. p. pipiens (N = 50), and 100% for Ae. aegypti (N = 50; Fig 3A).
Transmission rates of 32% (16/50) were found for Cx. p. pipiens and 8% (4/50) for Ae.aegypti.

Mean titers of virus-infected Cx. p. pipiens body samples were almost all below the detection limit of 103 TCID50/ml of the endpoint dilution assay (Fig 3C). This indicates that even when SHUV is injected into the thorax, there is no productive virus replication.
Taken together, SHUV is able to disseminate in mosquitoes, but both the midgut and salivary glands form a barrier for SHUV.
149.Rationale: The option asserts a complete inability of Cx. p. pipiens to support any stage of the virus life cycle. The passage, however, reports that intrathoracic injection produced substantial infection rates (68%) and measurable transmission rates (32%) in Cx. p. pipiens, demonstrating that the virus can infect and reach saliva in this species. Although body titers after injection were largely below detection (indicating limited productive replication), the presence of infected bodies and saliva shows Cx. p. pipiens is not completely incapable of supporting stages of the virus life cycle.
