The passage further states that both midgut and salivary gland barriers exist, rather than an absolute absence of cellular machinery for replication.

149.Option: Shuni virus transmission by Culex pipiens pipiens is limited by a strong salivary gland barrier, as evidenced by the low transmission success despite high infection rates after thoracic injection.
Evidence: Direct injection of SHUV into the thorax resulted in high infection rates of 68% for Cx. p. pipiens (N = 50), and 100% for Ae. aegypti (N = 50; Fig 3A).
Transmission rates of 32% (16/50) were found for Cx. p. pipiens and 8% (4/50) for Ae.aegypti.
This corresponds to transmission success of 47.1% (16/34) for Cx. p. pipiens and 8% (4/50) for Ae. aegypti.
This indicates a relatively weaker salivary gland barrier in Cx. p. pipiens compared to Ae. aegypti mosquitoes which had 100% infection rate, but relatively low transmission success.
149.Rationale: The option claims transmission is limited by a strong salivary gland barrier in Cx. p. pipiens. The passage explicitly reports that transmission success in Cx. p. pipiens after thoracic injection was relatively high (47.1%) and interprets this as indicating a relatively weaker salivary gland barrier in Cx. p. pipiens compared to Ae. aegypti. Thus the evidence contradicts the statement that a strong salivary gland barrier limits transmission in Cx. p. pipiens.

149.Option: The midgut barrier is the main barrier preventing infection of Culex pipiens pipiens with Shuni virus, as evidenced by the absence of infection following oral exposure despite successful virus replication and transmission after direct thoracic injection.
Evidence: No SHUV infection was observed in the Cx. p. pipiens mosquitoes (N = 211) following oral exposure.
To this end, Cx. p. pipiens and Ae. aegypti mosquitoes were intrathoracically injected with SHUV, to bypass the potential midgut barrier.
Direct injection of SHUV into the thorax resulted in high infection rates of 68% for Cx. p. pipiens (N = 50)...
Taken together, SHUV is able to disseminate in mosquitoes, but both the midgut and salivary glands form a barrier for SHUV.
149.Rationale: The passage reports no infection after oral exposure of Cx. p. pipiens, whereas intrathoracic injection (which bypasses the midgut) produced substantial infection and measurable transmission. The experimental design explicitly used thoracic injection to bypass a potential midgut barrier. These lines of evidence support the inference that the midgut barrier is the principal obstacle preventing oral infection of Cx. p. pipiens with SHUV under the tested conditions.

149.Option: The high transmission success of Shuni virus in Culex pipiens pipiens after intrathoracic injection suggests that this mosquito species is a competent vector capable of sustaining and spreading the virus in natural settings.
Evidence: No SHUV infection was observed in the Cx. p. pipiens mosquitoes (N = 211) following oral exposure.
Direct injection of SHUV into the thorax resulted in high infection rates of 68% for Cx. p. pipiens (N = 50)...

Mean titers of virus-infected Cx. p. pipiens body samples were almost all below the detection limit of 103 TCID50/ml of the endpoint dilution assay (Fig 3C). This indicates that even when SHUV is injected into the thorax, there is no productive virus replication.
Taken together, SHUV is able to disseminate in mosquitoes, but both the midgut and salivary glands form a barrier for SHUV.
149.Rationale: Vector competence in natural settings requires the mosquito to become infected via an infectious blood meal and subsequently transmit the pathogen. The passage shows Cx. p. pipiens did not become infected after oral exposure (natural route), even though thoracic injection (artificially bypassing the midgut) produced infection and some transmission. Additionally, body titers after injection were largely below detection, indicating limited productive replication. Therefore, the experimental finding of transmission after injection does not justify concluding that Cx. p. pipiens is a competent natural vector capable of sustaining and spreading SHUV in the field.

149.Option: The low infection rate of Shuni virus in Culex pipiens pipiens following oral exposure is primarily due to insufficient viral load in the blood meal, rather than biological barriers within the mosquito.
Evidence: both mosquito species ingested low amounts of SHUV that were below the detection limit of 103 TCID50/ml of the endpoint dilution assay.
No SHUV infection was observed in the Cx. p. pipiens mosquitoes (N = 211) following oral exposure.
To this end, Cx. p. pipiens and Ae. aegypti mosquitoes were intrathoracically injected with SHUV, to bypass the potential midgut barrier.
Taken together, SHUV is able to disseminate in mosquitoes, but both the midgut and salivary glands form a barrier for SHUV.
149.Rationale: While the passage notes that mosquitoes ingested low amounts of SHUV (below the detection limit), the investigators explicitly tested and demonstrated that bypassing the midgut barrier via thoracic injection yielded infections in Cx. p. pipiens. The authors conclude that both midgut and salivary gland barriers exist. Thus, although low blood-meal viral load could contribute, the data support biological barriers (especially the midgut barrier) as a key reason for the lack of oral infection. The claim that insufficient viral load is the primary cause is not directly supported by the provided evidence.

150.Option: IL-32γ is associated with the induction of microbicidal molecules that contribute to control Leishmania species infection in human macrophages.
Evidence: This cytokine can induce production of tumor necrosis factor alpha (TNFα), IL-8, IL-6, and IL-1β in THP-1 and RAW264.7 macrophages cell lines [3,4], with IL-32γ being the most active isoform [5].
Fig 5C shows a significant decrease of iNOS and nitrite production after 24 h of infection with both Leishmania species in cells silenced for IL-32 compared to control cells.
In addition, cathelicidin and β-defensin 2 mRNA expression as well as antimicrobial peptide LL-37 concentration were strongly reduced in THP-1-derived macrophages depleted of IL-32 (Fig 5D and 5E).

In IL-32γ overexpressing cells, we observed a significant decrease in the percentage of infected cells and infection index after 4 h and 24 h with both L. (L.) amazonensis and L. (V.) braziliensis (Fig 6A and 6B).
In parallel, we observed a significant increase of iNOS mRNA expression and nitrite production after 24 h of infection with both Leishmania species in IL-32γ-transfected cells (Fig 6C).

Moreover cathelicidin and β-defensin 2 mRNA expression as well as LL-37 peptide production were strongly increased when IL-32γ was overexpressed, especially in infection with L. (V.) braziliensis (Fig 6D and 6E).
150.Rationale: The passage directly shows that IL-32γ is the active isoform and that modulating IL-32 levels alters macrophage microbicidal effectors (iNOS/nitrite, cathelicidin/LL-37, β-defensin 2). Silencing IL-32 decreased these microbicidal molecules and increased infection, whereas IL-32γ overexpression increased these molecules and reduced infection indices. Therefore it is epidemiologically and experimentally supported that IL-32γ is associated with induction of microbicidal molecules that contribute to control Leishmania infection in human macrophages.

150.Option: The induction of IL-32γ by L. (L.) amazonensis is primarily due to LPS contamination in the parasite culture, which confounds the interpretation of the results.
Evidence: Here we showed that in THP-1 cultures Leishmania, in contrast to LPS, only induced IL-32γ (Fig 1A and S1A Fig—right panel).
Parasites were washed three times with sterile phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) pH 7.4 (1,000 g, 10 min, 10°C).
100 ng/mL of E. coli LPS (O111:B4 Sigma-Aldrich) was used as a control. This commercial LPS was further purified based on [37].
150.Rationale: The option claims IL-32γ induction is due to LPS contamination. The passage explicitly contrasts Leishmania-induced IL-32γ with LPS, noting that Leishmania (not LPS) only induced IL-32γ in these cultures. The methods also describe washing parasites multiple times and using purified commercial LPS as a control, which argues against simple LPS contamination explaining the observed induction. The passage provides no evidence of LPS contamination causing the IL-32γ signal; instead, it presents data that differentiate Leishmania effects from LPS.

150.Option: IL-32γ overexpression in THP-1 cells causes an increase in TNFα, IL-6, and IL-8 production, which is linked to enhanced cell death and reduced parasite control.
Evidence: This cytokine can induce production of tumor necrosis factor alpha (TNFα), IL-8, IL-6, and IL-1β in THP-1 and RAW264.7 macrophages cell lines [3,4], with IL-32γ being the most active isoform [5].

Overexpression of IL-32γ (Fig 4A) resulted in significant increases in TNFα and IL-8 mRNA expression and protein production after infection with either Leishmania species in comparison with egfp transfected control cells (Fig 4B).

In IL-32γ overexpressing cells, we observed a significant decrease in the percentage of infected cells and infection index after 4 h and 24 h with both L. (L.) amazonensis and L. (V.) braziliensis (Fig 6A and 6B).
Increased intracellular IL-32γ concentrations are associated with cell death [41]. However we observed LDH concentrations remained stable following Leishmania infection (S1C Fig).
150.Rationale: While the passage supports that IL-32γ overexpression increases TNFα and IL-8, the specific claim that this causes increased IL-6 in these experiments is not demonstrated (IL-6 is cited as inducible by IL-32 in other contexts but was not reported here). Crucially, the option asserts that overexpression is linked to enhanced cell death and reduced parasite control; the data show the opposite for parasite control (overexpression decreased infection indices) and report no increase in LDH (a cell death marker). Therefore the causal claim of enhanced cell death and reduced parasite control is not supported by the passage.

150.Option: IL-32γ regulates the balance between pro- and anti-inflammatory mediators in a way that promotes chronic infection by suppressing immune activation.
Evidence: During Leishmania infection, macrophages can produce proinflammatory cytokines (TNFα, IL-1β, IL-8) and regulatory (IL-10, IL-1Ra) molecules [29–31]. Thus a balance between pro- and anti-inflammatory mediators during the immune responses is critical to control inflammatory diseases [32,33].

Silencing of endogenous IL-32γ decreased TNFα mRNA expression at 24 h (p < 0.05), but not TNFα protein levels ... Both IL-8 mRNA and IL-8 protein were strongly reduced after infection with either Leishmania species when IL-32 was silenced (Fig 3B).
Fig 5C shows a significant decrease of iNOS and nitrite production after 24 h of infection with both Leishmania species in cells silenced for IL-32 compared to control cells.

In IL-32γ overexpressing cells, we observed a significant decrease in the percentage of infected cells and infection index after 4 h and 24 h with both L. (L.) amazonensis and L. (V.) braziliensis (Fig 6A and 6B).
150.Rationale: The option claims IL-32γ promotes chronic infection by suppressing immune activation. The experimental data instead indicate that IL-32γ promotes proinflammatory mediators (TNFα, IL-8) and microbicidal effectors (iNOS, antimicrobial peptides), and that loss of IL-32 increases infection whereas overexpression decreases infection. Thus IL-32γ appears to enhance, not suppress, immune activation and to facilitate parasite control in these in vitro experiments, contradicting the option's assertion.

150.Option: The higher production of IL-10 and IL-1Ra induced by L. (L.) amazonensis can be responsible for further control of inflammatory process and immunosuppression in in vivo infections caused by this species.
Evidence: Both L. (L.) amazonensis and L. (V.) braziliensis induced significant amounts of TNFα, IL-8, IL-1Ra and IL-10.
L. (L.) amazonensis induced considerably higher levels of TNFα, IL-1Ra and IL-10 than L. (V.) braziliensis (Fig 1C).

During Leishmania infection, macrophages can produce proinflammatory cytokines (TNFα, IL-1β, IL-8) and regulatory (IL-10, IL-1Ra) molecules [29–31]. Thus a balance between pro- and anti-inflammatory mediators during the immune responses is critical to control inflammatory diseases [32,33].
No differences were found between the two Leishmania species on IL-10 mRNA expression and proteins levels (Fig 4D).
150.Rationale: The passage documents that L. (L.) amazonensis induces higher IL-10 and IL-1Ra in these macrophage cultures and that IL-10 and IL-1Ra are regulatory molecules involved in balancing inflammation. However, the option asserts a causal in vivo effect (these higher levels being responsible for control of inflammation and immunosuppression in in vivo infections). The experiments here are in vitro macrophage studies and do not provide direct evidence for in vivo outcomes or causation. Therefore the specific in vivo causal inference is not derivable solely from the passage.

151.Option: Serological tests are less sensitive than qPCR in detecting chronic Chagas disease, making PCR a more reliable gold standard for diagnosis.
Evidence: Infected individuals then enter a chronic phase where parasites are rarely seen in the blood and diagnosis relies on the use of serological tests.

PCR sensitivity during the chronic phase is highly variable depending on several factors such as volume and processing of specimens, characteristics of the analyzed population, primers and PCR methods. Negative PCR results do not prove that infection is absent.
The diagnosis of Chagas disease in human samples was based on serological assays.
151.Rationale: The passage does not provide evidence that serology is less sensitive than qPCR for chronic Chagas disease or that PCR should be considered the definitive gold standard. It states that diagnosis in the chronic phase "relies on the use of serological tests" and explicitly notes that "PCR sensitivity during the chronic phase is highly variable" and that "Negative PCR results do not prove that infection is absent." Thus the passage does not support the strong conclusion that serology is less sensitive and that PCR is a more reliable gold standard.

151.Option: The higher detection rate of T. cruzi DNA in clot samples compared to whole blood samples is primarily due to the elimination of cross-contamination through the use of individually packed FastPrep Lysing Matrix.
Evidence: Conventional PCR has several drawbacks, especially cross-contamination. For this reason, quantitative PCR (qPCR) has now been more widely used and accepted.
Clot was initially disrupted using the FastPrep technique and then DNA was extracted using the recommended methodology of the Roche extraction kit.
Lysing Matrix E was chosen because the results were more reproducible.

Among the Chagas seropositive samples, the qPCR tested positive in 31.3% (47/150) ... of GEB samples ... and in 40.0% (60/150) ... of clot samples ... the difference in the proportions was statistically significant (P = 0.0002).
151.Rationale: While the passage mentions cross-contamination as a drawback of conventional PCR and describes use of the FastPrep technique and selection of Lysing Matrix E for reproducibility, it does not state or provide evidence that elimination of cross-contamination via individually packed FastPrep lysing matrices is the primary reason for the higher detection rate in clot samples. The passage attributes improved detection to clot as a source of DNA and to the extraction approach (FastPrep + Roche kit) and reports higher DNA concentration and lower Cq values in clot samples, but it does not link the improved detection primarily to reduced cross-contamination from the lysing matrix.

151.Option: The improved method for DNA extraction from clot samples increases the sensitivity of qPCR detection of Trypanosoma cruzi, as evidenced by significantly lower Cq values compared to DNA extracted from whole blood with guanidine hydrochloride.
Evidence: The objective of the present study was to improve and develop a more sensitive method to recover parasite DNA from clot samples for the diagnosis of Chagas disease. Clot was initially disrupted using the FastPrep technique and then DNA was extracted using the recommended methodology of the Roche extraction kit. DNA extracted from clot proved to have a higher sensitivity and presented a lower Cq value compared to DNA extracted from whole blood-GE (GEB) samples.

Among the Chagas seropositive samples, the qPCR tested positive in 31.3% (47/150) ... of GEB samples ... and in 40.0% (60/150) ... of clot samples ... the difference in the proportions was statistically significant (P = 0.0002).

Similarly, when the mean Cq values obtained by the qPCR were compared, the mean Cq value was significantly lower, when qPCR was performed using DNA extracted from clot samples, than using DNA extracted from GEB samples 27.32 [CI: 26.69–27.95] and 29.02 [CI: 28.36–29.67], respectively (P < 0.0003).
The mean DNA concentration was higher on clot samples than in GEB samples (S1 Table).
151.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports that the new clot-based extraction method "proved to have a higher sensitivity and presented a lower Cq value" versus GEB (whole blood with guanidine/EDTA). It provides quantitative support: a higher qPCR positivity rate in clot (40.0% vs 31.3%), a statistically significant difference (P = 0.0002), and significantly lower mean Cq values for clot DNA (27.32 vs 29.02, P < 0.0003). These statements directly support the inference that the new clot extraction method increases qPCR sensitivity.

151.Option: The new method's improved sensitivity is due to the washing of clot samples with erythrocyte lysing buffers, which enhances parasite DNA recovery.
Evidence: Recently we have demonstrated, by conventional PCR, that clot samples are superior as a source of DNA for Chagas diagnosis [14]. However, DNA extraction was performed using the phenol–chloroform method, which has several disadvantages: the extraction process is complex, the clot needs to be washed, the reagents are toxic, and the quantification cycle (Cq) values of qPCR assays are variable when the assay is repeated.

Clot samples were initially homogenized using the FastPrep machine followed by extraction using the High Pure PCR Template Preparation kit. Briefly, 300 μl of clot, 300 μl of Guanidine/EDTA (GE), 40 μl of Proteinase K (20 mg/ml) and 5 μl of IAC (40 ρg/μl) were place in a Lysing Matrix E tube (MP Biomedicals, Santa Ana, CA). The mixture was FastPrep processed ... and then centrifuged ... A total of 450 μl of supernatant was transferred to a new tube and 150 μl of binding buffer ... was added. The mixture was incubated at 70 °C for 10 min and the extraction process was performed using High Pure PCR Template Preparation kit (Roche Diagnostics).
151.Rationale: The passage describes that in the older phenol–chloroform method the clot "needs to be washed," but the new method is described as homogenization with FastPrep, addition of GE and Proteinase K, centrifugation, and extraction with the Roche kit. There is no mention that the new method includes washing with erythrocyte lysing buffers, nor does the passage attribute improved sensitivity to such washing. Therefore the specific causal claim that improved sensitivity is due to washing with erythrocyte lysing buffers is not supported.

151.Option: The use of guanidine hydrochloride for DNA preservation and sample shipping is no longer necessary due to the development of this new clot-based DNA extraction method.
Evidence: It has been recommended that when blood samples are drawn they should be immediately mixed with one volume of 6M guanidine hydrochloride– 0.2 M EDTA, pH 8.00 (GE) to stabilize DNA for shipping [12,13]. However, shipping and handling of guanidine is troublesome and the logistics for specimen collection with GE are complex.
Clot was initially disrupted using the FastPrep technique and then DNA was extracted using the recommended methodology of the Roche extraction kit.
Briefly, 300 μl of clot, 300 μl of Guanidine/EDTA (GE), 40 μl of Proteinase K (20 mg/ml) and 5 μl of IAC (40 ρg/μl) were place in a Lysing Matrix E tube (MP Biomedicals, Santa Ana, CA).
151.Rationale: Although the passage notes that shipping and handling guanidine is troublesome, it does not claim that guanidine hydrochloride is no longer necessary. In fact, the clot extraction protocol used in this study still includes adding Guanidine/EDTA (GE) to clot material prior to processing ("300 μl of clot, 300 μl of Guanidine/EDTA (GE), ... were place in a Lysing Matrix E tube"). Additionally, GEB (blood mixed with guanidine/EDTA) was used as the comparator. Therefore the passage does not support the inference that guanidine preservation and shipping are no longer necessary.

152.Option: Glucose levels in male Ae. albopictus mosquitoes increase during recovery from chilling, and this increase is directly responsible for the maintenance of mating competitiveness after short-term chilling.
Evidence: As shown in Fig 3E, no significant difference was observed on the glucose levels between IHC5°C-0h and IHC5°C-3h males (Dunnett’s multiple comparisons test, P > 0.05), while the glucose levels of males which have been chilled for over 3 h was significantly decreased (Dunnett’s multiple comparisons test, 6 h: P < 0.01; 12 h: P < 0.0001; 24 h: P < 0.0001).
An average of 51.3% reduction in glucose levels was observed in individual males which were chilled for 24 h compared to non-chilled male (5.89 μg vs 8.91μg).
There was no statistical difference on C index between IHC5°C-3h and IHC5°C-0h males (Dunnett’s multiple comparisons test, P > 0.05)
152.Rationale: The option claims glucose increases during recovery and that such an increase is directly responsible for preserved mating competitiveness after short-term chilling. The passage provides no evidence of an increase in glucose during recovery; instead it reports no significant change in glucose between 0 and 3 h and significant decreases after >3 h, including a 51.3% reduction at 24 h. The passage also shows maintained mating competitiveness after short-term chilling (≤3 h), but it does not attribute that effect to an increase in glucose during recovery. Therefore the causal claim in the option is not supported by the provided data.

152.Option: The optimal chilling temperature for Ae. albopictus males ranges between 5 and 10°C, with minimal effects on post-chilled male survival rate, and this range is consistent across all mosquito species used in vector control programs.
Evidence: we selected 5 and 10°C as the chilling temperatures to immobilize IHC males and the effects of chilling duration and feeding regimes on the longevity of post-chilled males were further assessed.
our results indicate that chilling at 5 or 10°C for short periods has no impact on the survival and longevity of Ae. albopictus male adults under both laboratory and semi-field conditions.
Previous studies have indicated that chilling is suitable to handle Anopheles arabiensis and Ae. aegypti male adults if the handling conditions are optimized [42, 43].
152.Rationale: The passage supports that 5–10°C are acceptable chilling temperatures for Ae. albopictus males for short durations with minimal impact on survival. However, the statement extends this conclusion to 'all mosquito species used in vector control programs,' which is not supported by the Passage Body. The paper mentions other species in prior studies but does not provide evidence that the 5–10°C range is optimal or consistent across species. Thus the cross-species generalization is unsupported.

152.Option: The reduced mating competitiveness observed in males chilled for 24 h is primarily due to the physical stress of prolonged compaction during transportation, rather than the duration of chilling itself.
Evidence: a decreased C index (~43% reduction) was observed in IHC5°C-24h males when compared to IHC5°C-0h males (Dunnett’s multiple comparisons test, P < 0.05).

Similar results were obtained in the HC10°C males, with a significantly decreased C index (~57% reduction) in HC10°C-24h males (Dunnett’s multiple comparisons test, P < 0.05), but not in HC10°C-3h males (Dunnett’s multiple comparisons test, P > 0.05), when compared to non-chilled males (Fig 3D).

Taken together, our results indicate that a short duration (e.g. less than 3 h) of chilling at 5 and 10°C does not affect the mating competitiveness of IHC/HC males. In addition, the reduced mating competitiveness of males after chilling may associate with the reduced glucose level.

When males were compacted at different heights during chilling, we found that the compaction height had a significant impact on the survival of IHC males ... reduced survival was found for IHC males when compacted at a height of 8 cm (Dunnett's multiple comparisons test, 5°C-3 h: P < 0.05; 5°C-24 h: P < 0.05; 10°C-3 h: P < 0.05).

a slight reduction of survival of the IHC males was observed after transportation following the three independent experiments, however, the reduction was not statistically significant between non-transported and transported males ... An average of 95.1% of the IHC males survived after the three transportation experiments.
152.Rationale: The experimental results link reduced mating competitiveness to 24 h of chilling and to reduced glucose levels after prolonged chilling. While extreme vertical compaction (8 cm) reduced survival, the study found no evidence that compaction during transportation was the primary cause of reduced competitiveness; transportation produced only a slight, non-significant reduction in survival. The passage attributes decreased competitiveness to prolonged chilling (and associated glucose depletion) rather than to packing/transport compaction. Therefore the option's assertion that compaction during transportation is the primary cause is not supported.

152.Option: The use of drones for aerial release of sterile male mosquitoes is not feasible in areas with high ambient temperatures, as it would lead to immediate mortality regardless of chilling conditions.
Evidence: This aerial release method has recently been adapted for immobilized and packed mosquitoes, showing high release efficiency and homogenous dispersal of the sterile males [36].
During recovery, mosquitoes should avoid direct sunlight to reduce mortality due to the heat absorption (personal observation).
The mean temperature in the field site was 30°C (± 2°C).

a slight reduction of survival of the IHC males was observed after transportation following the three independent experiments, however, the reduction was not statistically significant between non-transported and transported males ... An average of 95.1% of the IHC males survived after the three transportation experiments.
152.Rationale: The passage describes successful adaptation of aerial release methods for immobilized and packed mosquitoes and gives no experimental evidence that drones are infeasible in high ambient temperatures or that high temperatures would cause immediate mortality regardless of chilling. The authors note that direct sunlight/heat can increase mortality and advise avoiding direct sunlight during recovery, but that is not equivalent to the absolute infeasibility claimed in the option. Therefore this strong, general negative conclusion is not supported by the presented data.

152.Option: A short duration (e.g. less than 3 h) of chilling at 5–10°C does not affect the mating competitiveness of irradiated male Ae. albopictus mosquitoes, despite a significant reduction in glucose levels after 24 h of chilling.
Evidence: Two- to three-day-old IHC male adults were chilled in refrigerators set up at different temperatures and for different durations to determine the optimal handling conditions for the transportation of IHC males.

There was no statistical difference on C index between IHC5°C-3h and IHC5°C-0h males (Dunnett’s multiple comparisons test, P > 0.05), however, a decreased C index (~43% reduction) was observed in IHC5°C-24h males when compared to IHC5°C-0h males (Dunnett’s multiple comparisons test, P < 0.05).
Taken together, our results indicate that a short duration (e.g. less than 3 h) of chilling at 5 and 10°C does not affect the mating competitiveness of IHC/HC males.

As shown in Fig 3E, no significant difference was observed on the glucose levels between IHC5°C-0h and IHC5°C-3h males (Dunnett’s multiple comparisons test, P > 0.05), while the glucose levels of males which have been chilled for over 3 h was significantly decreased (Dunnett’s multiple comparisons test, 6 h: P < 0.01; 12 h: P < 0.0001; 24 h: P < 0.0001).
An average of 51.3% reduction in glucose levels was observed in individual males which were chilled for 24 h compared to non-chilled male (5.89 μg vs 8.91μg).

Male pupae of the HC strain were collected and then irradiated using the X-ray irradiator (Wolbaki, Guangzhou, China) at a dose of 45 Gy [14]. The irradiated HC (IHC) male pupae were then allowed to emerge in stainless steel cages with access to a 10% sugar solution.
152.Rationale: The passage directly reports that irradiated IHC males chilled for short durations (≤3 h) at 5–10°C showed no significant change in mating competitiveness compared to non-chilled controls. It also reports that glucose levels were significantly reduced after longer chilling durations (over 3 h, especially 24 h). Thus the statement accurately reflects the study's findings and is derivable from the Passage Body.

153.Option: The high prevalence rates of E. canis infection observed in other regions of the world, such as up to 30%, suggest that Malaysia should also have a similarly high prevalence, indicating that the current findings may be due to underreporting or inadequate surveillance.
Evidence: To date the presence of ehrlichial agents in dogs in Malaysia has not been investigated using molecular techniques and therefore, this study was undertaken to detect E. canis DNA and to determine the prevalence of the disease caused by this pathogen in dogs in Malaysia.

Five hundred blood samples (323 clinic cases and 177 stray dogs) were evaluated using PCR in this study out of which ten were identified as E. canis positive, giving an overall prevalence rate of two (2.0%) percent.
This is the first confirmed detection of E. canis DNA from dogs in Malaysia.
153.Rationale: The passage provides the observed low prevalence (2.0%) from this Malaysian study and states this is the first confirmed molecular detection in Malaysia. It does not cite or present any data about very high prevalence rates (e.g., up to 30%) in other regions, nor does it provide evidence that underreporting or inadequate surveillance is the reason for the low observed prevalence. Therefore, the claim that Malaysia "should" have similarly high prevalence and that current findings are due to underreporting is not derivable from the passage.

153.Option: The low prevalence of E. canis detected by PCR in this study may be due to the limitations of PCR in detecting subclinical or chronic infections, which are not as readily identified as acute infections when using blood samples alone.
Evidence: Conversely, polymerase chain reaction (PCR) is a sensitive method of detection of acute monocytic ehrlichiosis in dogs; in fact it is designed to aim for the organism itself which makes PCR an invaluable technique capable of detecting traces of pathogen even before the onset of clinical signs [14].

Therefore, the advantages of molecular detection of Ehrlichia include diagnosis before the development of antibodies in early stages of disease and identifying new species and also closely related species of Ehrlichia using species-specific primers and sequencing [15].
153.Rationale: The passage characterizes PCR as a sensitive method that can detect the organism even before clinical signs and before antibody development. It does not state that PCR is limited for detecting subclinical or chronic infections when blood samples are used. On the contrary, PCR is presented as particularly valuable for early/acute detection. The claim that low PCR prevalence is due to PCR limitations for subclinical/chronic infections is not supported by the text and therefore cannot be derived solely from the passage.

153.Option: Serological tests may have been more effective than PCR in detecting E. canis in this study, as they are more sensitive in identifying chronic or past infections, which could explain the discrepancy between clinical signs and PCR results.
Evidence: Currently serological tests are the most commonly practiced method for diagnosis of E. canis infection. These serological tests reflect the quantity of antibodies present in the serum and therefore indicate exposure but not the severity of disease and the duration of infection [11].
Furthermore, antibodies are usually absent during the first two weeks of onset [11].

Conversely, polymerase chain reaction (PCR) is a sensitive method of detection of acute monocytic ehrlichiosis in dogs; in fact it is designed to aim for the organism itself which makes PCR an invaluable technique capable of detecting traces of pathogen even before the onset of clinical signs [14].
153.Rationale: The passage states that serology indicates exposure (i.e., past or chronic infection) while PCR is particularly useful for detecting acute infection and early infection prior to antibody development. Thus, when clinical/hematological abnormalities are present but PCR is negative, a plausible and text-supported explanation is that serology (detecting antibodies from prior or chronic infection) might detect infections that PCR (which targets current circulating organism in blood) does not. This inference is directly supported by the passage's comparison of the diagnostic roles of serology and PCR.

153.Option: The absence of E. canis DNA in dogs with typical clinical signs of CME may be due to the fact that these dogs were infected with other ehrlichial species, such as E. ewingii or A. phagocytophilum, which are known to cause similar hematological manifestations.
Evidence: Some closely related pathogens, including Ehrlichia ewingii, Ehrlichia chaffeensis, Anaplasma phagocytophilum and Neorickettsia risticii, are shown to cause similar clinical and hematological manifestations in dogs as well [2], [5].

Because rickettsiales are able to infect a broad range of hosts, and multiple pathogens can co-exist in both vertebrate and invertebrate hosts, the availability of a rapid, highly sensitive, and specific test that can diagnose one or more pathogens, including co-infections, in a test sample will be valuable for timely diagnosis and treatment [8], [9].

Currently serological tests are the most commonly practiced method for diagnosis of E. canis infection. These serological tests reflect the quantity of antibodies present in the serum and therefore indicate exposure but not the severity of disease and the duration of infection [11].
153.Rationale: The passage explicitly notes that several related organisms can cause similar clinical and hematological signs in dogs and that multiple pathogens can co-exist. Therefore, dogs with clinical signs consistent with CME but negative for E. canis DNA by PCR could plausibly be infected with other ehrlichial or related agents. This explanation is directly supported by the documented overlap of pathogens and similar manifestations in the passage.

153.Option: The low PCR detection rate may reflect a lack of transmission of E. canis in Malaysia, as the brown dog tick Rhipicephalus sanguineus may not be present or infected in the region.
Evidence: E. canis is transmitted by the brown dog tick Rhipicephalus sanguineus [3], [4].

Five hundred blood samples (323 clinic cases and 177 stray dogs) were evaluated using PCR in this study out of which ten were identified as E. canis positive, giving an overall prevalence rate of two (2.0%) percent.
This is the first confirmed detection of E. canis DNA from dogs in Malaysia.
153.Rationale: While the passage states the vector (R. sanguineus) for E. canis, it provides no data on the presence, absence, or infection rate of this tick species in Malaysia. Moreover, the study did detect E. canis DNA (albeit at low prevalence), which demonstrates transmission does occur. There is no evidence in the passage that the low PCR detection rate is due to absence of the tick vector or lack of transmission in the region, so this causal claim cannot be derived from the provided information.

154.Option: The national health information system in Ethiopia is fully capable of capturing and tracking noma cases, as evidenced by the inclusion of noma-related data in the HMIS for all health facilities.
Evidence: The Ministry of Health has also put in place HMIS to gather data from all health facilities in the country. The system is in its scale-up phase and need to be expanded to address diseases of the poor. Currently the system does not include the indicators to acute cases and sequel of noma.

Many cases remain undetected and unreported due to various factors, including limited diagnostic skills among health care providers, the rapid disease progression and high acute-phase mortality, a lack of routine surveillance and inclusion health information systems, and family stigma that leads to hiding affected children [10].
154.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the HMIS does not include indicators for acute noma cases and sequelae and that the system needs expansion to address diseases of the poor. It also notes many cases remain undetected and unreported. Therefore it cannot be inferred that the national HMIS is fully capable of capturing and tracking noma cases; the text provides direct evidence to the contrary.

154.Option: The declining trend in the number of noma cases receiving care from 2015 to 2020 reflects a genuine reduction in the incidence of noma in Ethiopia, likely due to improved public health interventions and increased awareness.
Evidence: In the last 10 years, I have seen about 300 cases, but since 2020, we are seeing fewer noma cases. However considering the country situation with war and drought, it is expected that noma cases will increase in the coming years. We have not operated on noma patients for the past two years due to COVID 19 pandemic movement restriction.
The retrospective identified 69 reported case of noma from January 2015 to December 2020, with 97% of cases documented by two NGOs supporting surgical mission (Fig 3).

Many cases remain undetected and unreported due to various factors, including limited diagnostic skills among health care providers, the rapid disease progression and high acute-phase mortality, a lack of routine surveillance and inclusion health information systems, and family stigma that leads to hiding affected children [10].
154.Rationale: The passage notes fewer cases seen since 2020 but immediately attributes reduced case numbers to factors such as COVID-19 movement restrictions and limited surgical activity, and highlights substantial under-detection and under-reporting. The retrospective data are overwhelmingly NGO-derived, indicating reporting biases. Thus a decline in cases receiving care does not permit the inference of a genuine reduction in incidence driven by improved public health interventions from the data presented.

154.Option: The high number of noma cases reported from Amhara and Somali regions indicates that these regions have the most advanced health systems and best access to specialized care for noma patients.
Evidence: The retrospective identified 69 reported case of noma from January 2015 to December 2020... The recorded patient address indicated that 31 patients are from Amhara region, 9 from Somalie, 8 from Oromia, 5 from Afar, 5 from SNNPR, 4 from Gambella and 3 from Addis Ababa (Fig 4).
97% of cases documented by two NGOs supporting surgical mission (Fig 3).
The selection of hospitals and NGOs was based on convenience, with focus on institutions that provide plastic surgery, oral care, and dermatology services in each region.
Project Harar: ... the organization has developed a unique outreach model to find patients from even the most remote rural areas, and to transport them to Addis Ababa for the surgery.
154.Rationale: The clustering of reported cases in certain regions is documented, but the passage indicates that most cases were captured by NGO surgical missions and that hospital/NGO selection was by convenience and outreach patterns. The data therefore reflect detection and referral practices (and NGO outreach), not that those regions have the most advanced health systems or best access to care. The passage does not provide evidence that higher case counts mean superior regional health system capacity.

154.Option: The integration of noma into existing non-communicable disease and nutrition programs would be sufficient to ensure timely detection and management of acute cases across Ethiopia’s primary health care system.
Evidence: Eighty-five percent of the participant stated that there is no oral health program in the country’s health system.
Currently the system does not include the indicators to acute cases and sequel of noma.
there is no program for training of dental hygienist/therapist or dental practitioner/nurse.
The number of dental professional including dentists and dental hygienists working in the country health system was only 270.
154.Rationale: The passage documents major gaps in oral health programming, workforce shortages, lack of training programs, and absence of HMIS indicators for noma. Although some respondents suggested possible integration pathways (NTD versus NCD/nutrition), the documented deficits indicate that mere integration into existing NCD/nutrition programs, without addressing workforce, training, surveillance, and service gaps, would not be sufficient to ensure timely detection and management across primary care. The text does not support the claim of sufficiency.

154.Option: The absence of acute noma case records in the national health information system underscores the urgent need for routine surveillance programs and improved data collection at the primary health care level.
Evidence: The Ministry of Health has also put in place HMIS to gather data from all health facilities in the country. The system is in its scale-up phase and need to be expanded to address diseases of the poor. Currently the system does not include the indicators to acute cases and sequel of noma.

Many cases remain undetected and unreported due to various factors, including limited diagnostic skills among health care providers, the rapid disease progression and high acute-phase mortality, a lack of routine surveillance and inclusion health information systems, and family stigma that leads to hiding affected children [10].
Identifying cases is also a challenge that I encountered during my time on the project.

All participant agreed on the limitation of on the availability of human resource for oral health and poor capacity of the health institution for integrating of oral health program in to the primary health care unit.
154.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the HMIS lacks noma indicators, that many cases are undetected and unreported, and that identifying cases is a known challenge. Respondents also agree the primary health care system lacks oral health capacity. These statements directly support the epidemiological inference that routine surveillance and improved primary-level data collection are urgently needed to monitor and respond to acute noma cases.

155.Option: The limited reactivity of antivenom with disintegrins and the DC fragments of SVMPs, which are recognized as inhibitors of platelet aggregation, suggests that these components are not critical in the pathophysiology of envenoming.
Evidence: MAJar-3 antibodies are IgG1 isotypes and recognize conformational epitopes located on the jararhagin disintegrin-like domain. MAJar-3 neutralizes jararhagin collagen binding and hemorrhagic activity and cross-reacts with hemorrhagins from venoms of different species of viper snakes [52].

Furthermore, the antivenoms reacted equally with the toxins from the same protein family, regardless of snake phylogeny or the presence of the venom in the immunization pool used for antivenom production, highlighting new priorities when considering the selection of venoms to be used in the production of antivenoms.
155.Rationale: The passage does not report limited reactivity of antivenom with disintegrins or DC fragments; on the contrary, it describes a monoclonal antibody (MAJar-3) that recognizes a disintegrin-like domain and neutralizes hemorrhagic activity and cross-reacts across species. Moreover, the authors state that antivenoms reacted equally with toxins from the same protein family regardless of phylogeny or inclusion in the immunization pool. There is no evidence in the passage that limited reactivity to these components occurs or that such limited reactivity implies they are not critical to pathophysiology. Therefore the claim cannot be derived from the passage.

155.Option: The antivenom's efficacy in neutralizing B. jararacussu venom is lower than that for B. jararaca venom due to the absence of B. jararacussu venom in the immunization pool, despite similar toxin profiles.
Evidence: The antibothropic serum (SAB) was produced at the Instituto Butantan, São Paulo, Brazil in horses immunized with a mixture of the following venoms: B. jararaca (50%), B. neuwiedi (12.5%), R. alternatus (12.5%), B. moojeni (12.5%) and B. jararacussu (12.5%).
The venom analysis showed that phylogenetic classification per se is not directly linked to venom composition.
155.Rationale: This option asserts that B. jararacussu venom was absent from the immunization pool; however, the passage explicitly lists B. jararacussu (12.5%) as included in the immunization mixture. Thus the premise of the option is false. The passage also does not provide data showing lower efficacy against B. jararacussu compared to B. jararaca; instead it notes phylogeny is not directly linked to venom composition. Because the fundamental factual claim about absence from the pool is incorrect and no supporting neutralization data are provided, the inference cannot be derived from the passage.

155.Option: Despite the phylogenetic distance of the snake and the fact that the venom is not included in the immunization pool used to produce SAB, the antivenom is efficient in neutralizing the most important effects of B. atrox venom.
Evidence: The efficacy of the antivenom was then assessed for the neutralization of relevant symptoms of experimental envenomings by (a) B. jararaca, which accounts for 50% of venom composition in the immunization pool and is prevalent in the southeastern Brazil, and (b) B. atrox, which is not present in the immunization pool although representing a common cause of snakebite in the Amazon.

Furthermore, the antivenoms reacted equally with the toxins from the same protein family, regardless of snake phylogeny or the presence of the venom in the immunization pool used for antivenom production, highlighting new priorities when considering the selection of venoms to be used in the production of antivenoms.
155.Rationale: The passage states that B. atrox venom was specifically tested even though it was not included in the immunization pool and that antivenoms reacted equally with toxins from the same protein family regardless of snake phylogeny or inclusion in the pool. From these statements it is epidemiologically reasonable to infer that the antivenom shows cross-reactivity and can neutralize the major toxic effects of B. atrox venom despite its exclusion from the immunization mixture. Thus this option is supported by the passage.

155.Option: The cross-reactivity of the antivenom with P-III SVMPs across species is due to the high sequence homology of these toxins, which is confirmed by the structural similarity of their catalytic domains.
Evidence: The venom analysis showed that phylogenetic classification per se is not directly linked to venom composition.

Furthermore, the antivenoms reacted equally with the toxins from the same protein family, regardless of snake phylogeny or the presence of the venom in the immunization pool used for antivenom production, highlighting new priorities when considering the selection of venoms to be used in the production of antivenoms.

the variability in venom composition is notable and can be correlated with phylogeny [36], [37], age [38], [39], sex [40], geographical distribution [13], [40], [41] and diet [42]–[44] of the snake. However, venom variability is mostly related to the expression level of each group of toxin rather than to the presence or absence of major families of venom proteins.
155.Rationale: While the passage documents cross-reactivity across toxin families, it does not provide molecular sequence alignment data, nor does it state that cross-reactivity is attributable to high sequence homology or structural confirmation of catalytic domains. The authors note that venom variability is mainly in expression levels rather than presence/absence of families, and they do not present direct evidence confirming sequence or structural homology as the mechanism. Therefore the specific causal claim about high sequence homology and catalytic domain structural similarity is not supported by the passage.

155.Option: The antivenom's neutralization of hemorrhagic activity is less effective in B. atrox venom than in B. jararaca venom due to differences in the expression levels of P-III SVMPs.
Evidence: For the neutralization of hemorrhagic activity, doses of 10 µg B. jararaca or B. atrox venom were incubated with SAB at ratios of 1, 2 or 4 times the SAB volume required to neutralize 10 µg of reference venom, according to the manufacturer.
The venom analysis showed that phylogenetic classification per se is not directly linked to venom composition.

Furthermore, the antivenoms reacted equally with the toxins from the same protein family, regardless of snake phylogeny or the presence of the venom in the immunization pool used for antivenom production, highlighting new priorities when considering the selection of venoms to be used in the production of antivenoms.
However, venom variability is mostly related to the expression level of each group of toxin rather than to the presence or absence of major families of venom proteins.
155.Rationale: The passage describes that hemorrhagic neutralization experiments were performed for both B. jararaca and B. atrox, and it emphasizes that antivenoms reacted equally with toxins from the same protein family regardless of inclusion in the immunization pool. The passage does not report results showing that hemorrhagic neutralization is less effective for B. atrox, nor does it link any difference in neutralization efficacy to expression levels of P-III SVMPs. Although expression-level variability is mentioned as a general phenomenon, no data are provided in the passage to support the specific causal claim that differences in P-III SVMP expression explain reduced neutralization of hemorrhage in B. atrox. Therefore this inference cannot be derived from the passage.

156.Option: The experimental model demonstrates that ST313 infection in chickens leads to prolonged gastrointestinal inflammation, mirroring the chronic enteropathy seen in human iNTS patients.
Evidence: Infection with ST313 D23580 elicited a mean 11- fold increase in CXCLi1 gene expression and 20- fold increase CXCLi2 gene expression in the ileum at 3 days post-infection (Figure 2B).
At 7 days post-infection, little intestinal inflammation was seen in the D23580 infected group and the inflammation-associated architectural disturbance had largely resolved
156.Rationale: The passage describes a strong, rapid intestinal inflammatory response to ST313 at day 3 but explicitly states that by day 7 intestinal inflammation in the D23580-infected group had largely resolved. Thus the data indicate a transient, not prolonged, gastrointestinal inflammation in chickens infected with ST313. The passage does not present evidence of a chronic enteropathy analogous to a long-term human condition, so the claim of prolonged inflammation mirroring chronic human enteropathy cannot be derived from the experimental findings.

156.Option: Chickens are the primary natural host of ST313, as evidenced by the high levels of systemic invasion and intestinal colonization observed in the experimental model.
Evidence: Both ST313 isolates were found to colonize the ceca (Figure 1A) and invade the spleen (Figure 1B) and liver (data not shown) at 3 days post-infection with Salmonella detected in all birds in these groups.
Consequently, it has been suggested that ST313 is restricted to human infection, or at least adapted to a niche of immunosuppressed HIV or malaria-infected patients [1], [3].
potential domestic or wild animal sources of infection have been incompletely investigated.
156.Rationale: While the experimental model demonstrates that ST313 can colonize and systemically invade chickens under experimental conditions, the passage explicitly notes that epidemiological data are sparse and that it has been suggested ST313 may be restricted to humans. The experimental ability to infect chickens does not by itself establish chickens as the primary natural host in nature. The authors also state that animal sources have been incompletely investigated, so the passage does not provide sufficient epidemiologic evidence to conclude chickens are the primary natural host.

156.Option: The absence of epidemiological evidence for a zoonotic source of ST313 in Africa confirms that the pathotype is strictly human-adapted and incapable of sustained transmission through animal reservoirs.
Evidence: epidemiological investigation has been unable to determine an environmental or zoonotic source of iNTS, with transmission being considered likely to be through direct or indirect human-to-human routes and with asymptomatic carriage potentially playing a role [1], [3], [6].
Consequently, it has been suggested that ST313 is restricted to human infection, or at least adapted to a niche of immunosuppressed HIV or malaria-infected patients [1], [3].
potential domestic or wild animal sources of infection have been incompletely investigated.

Both ST313 isolates were found to colonize the ceca (Figure 1A) and invade the spleen (Figure 1B) and liver (data not shown) at 3 days post-infection with Salmonella detected in all birds in these groups.
156.Rationale: The passage states there is currently no epidemiological identification of an animal source and notes the suggestion that ST313 may be human-restricted, but it also explicitly acknowledges that animal sources have not been fully investigated. Moreover, experimental infection shows ST313 can colonize and invade chickens. Thus the absence of epidemiological evidence does not 'confirm' strict human adaptation or inability to transmit via animal reservoirs; that stronger conclusion is not supported by the passage.

156.Option: Although ST313 isolates show a reduced ability to thrive in the intestinal tract, the level of colonization is sufficient for chickens to act as a potential source of zoonotic infection.
Evidence: ST313 colonizes the intestinal tract but at a lower level than ST19
All ST19 and ST313 isolates colonized the ceca of the chicken by 3 days post-infection and remained colonized at 7 days post-infection (Figure 1A).
Both ST313 isolates colonized at significantly lower levels than either F98 (P = 0.033 for Q456, P = 0.002 for D23580 at 7 days post infection) or 4/74 (P = 0.036 for Q456, P = 0.015 for D23580),
Although there is currently no epidemiological evidence that birds are a source of ST313 infection, they are perhaps the most likely zoonotic source of Salmonella.

Infection of the chicken with pathovars of S. Typhimurium like ST19 can persist within the gastrointestinal tract leading to fecal shedding into the environment, contamination of meat and fecal contamination of eggs
156.Rationale: The experimental data show that ST313 reliably colonizes the chicken ceca (though at lower levels than ST19) and persists through day 7, and prior text notes that chickens commonly live in close contact with humans and that S. Typhimurium in chickens can lead to fecal shedding and contamination. While the passage acknowledges no current epidemiological proof that birds are the source of ST313 in Africa, these combined findings support the epidemiological inference that chickens could act as a potential zoonotic source despite lower intestinal colonization levels.

156.Option: The rapid systemic invasion and intense inflammatory response observed in chickens infected with ST313 suggest that this pathotype is evolving toward a 'stealth infection' strategy similar to S. Gallinarum and S. Pullorum.
Evidence: genomic analysis of D23580, an MDR ST313 isolated in Blantyre, Malawi, revealed that ST313 has undergone genomic degradation including pseudogene formation and chromosomal deletion that is a feature of host-adapted serovars such as S. Typhi and S. Gallinarum, that cause systemic typhoidal disease in their hosts [4], [5].
ST313 S. Typhimurium shows a phenotype of rapid invasion in the chicken
Infection with ST313 D23580 elicited a mean 11- fold increase in CXCLi1 gene expression and 20- fold increase CXCLi2 gene expression in the ileum at 3 days post-infection (Figure 2B).

Histological examination of the ileum as early as day 3 revealed that D23580 infection led to a rapid influx of heterophils to the ileum, leading to flattening and fusion of the villi, consistent with the levels of CXC chemokine expression (Figure 3 and Table 2).
156.Rationale: Although genomic degradation similar to host-adapted serovars is described, the experimental findings demonstrate rapid systemic invasion and a pronounced, early inflammatory response (heterophil influx, chemokine upregulation) rather than a muted or 'stealthy' infection phenotype. The data therefore do not support the specific inference that ST313 is evolving toward a stealth infection strategy like S. Gallinarum/Pullorum; the passage provides mixed signals (genomic signs of adaptation but overt inflammation in this model), so that evolutionary claim cannot be directly derived.

157.Option: PORT is more likely to occur in patients with systemic immunosuppression, suggesting that immunosuppressed individuals should be prioritized for routine screening with multimodal imaging during posterior uveitis evaluations.
Evidence: Atypical presentations are more frequent in patients with systemic immunosuppression.

The aim of the study is to describe clinical findings, serology, multimodal imaging, and visual prognosis in patients with PORT to facilitate further recognition of this pathology and thereby decrease the frequency of irreversible complications leading to legal blindness.
Patients were evaluated using available images including OCT, fundus photography, and fundus autofluorescence (FAF).
PORT is characterized by multifocal grey-white lesions at the outer retinal layers and retinal pigment epithelium associated with minimal vitreous inflammation.
157.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that atypical presentations (which include PORT) are more frequent in patients with systemic immunosuppression. The study goal and methods emphasize the role of multimodal imaging (OCT, fundus photography, FAF) for recognition of PORT. From these statements it is epidemiologically reasonable to infer that immunosuppressed individuals—being at higher risk for atypical forms—are an appropriate group to prioritize for careful evaluation using multimodal imaging during posterior uveitis assessments. The recommendation for prioritized screening follows directly from the documented higher frequency in immunosuppressed patients and the described utility of multimodal imaging in detecting PORT.

157.Option: Since PORT lesions are often located in the macula and can lead to legal blindness, all patients with posterior uveitis should receive immediate intravitreal anti-VEGF therapy to prevent choroidal neovascularization.
Evidence: Most lesions have been reported in the macula, but this may be because macular lesions are visually the most symptomatic.
Complications encountered include choroidal neovascularization, serous retinal detachment, [4] and macular cysts[7] that may convert to what is known as huge outer retinal cystoid space (HORC).
Due to this, an anti-vascular endothelial growth factor (aflibercept) intravitreal injection was prescribed for her OD.
157.Rationale: While the passage notes macular predilection of lesions and that choroidal neovascularization can occur (and gives an example where anti-VEGF was used therapeutically), it does not state that all patients with posterior uveitis should receive immediate prophylactic intravitreal anti-VEGF. The evidence supports that anti-VEGF is used to treat identified choroidal neovascularization in specific cases, not as a universal immediate intervention for all posterior uveitis patients. The blanket recommendation in the option is not supported by the passage.

157.Option: PORT represents a diagnostic challenge; therefore, clinical suspicion and multimodal imaging are crucial for recognizing PORT lesions.
Evidence: PORT is characterized by multifocal grey-white lesions at the outer retinal layers and retinal pigment epithelium associated with minimal vitreous inflammation.
Most lesions have been reported in the macula, but this may be because macular lesions are visually the most symptomatic.

The aim of the study is to describe clinical findings, serology, multimodal imaging, and visual prognosis in patients with PORT to facilitate further recognition of this pathology and thereby decrease the frequency of irreversible complications leading to legal blindness.
Patients were evaluated using available images including OCT, fundus photography, and fundus autofluorescence (FAF).

PORT diagnosis was made by a uveitis specialist based on clinical findings, positive serology, and adequate treatment response (800 mg sulfamethoxazole and 160 mg trimethoprim every 12 hours, clindamycin 300 mg every 6 hours and tapering steroids initiating with 0.5 mg/kg/day of prednisone).
157.Rationale: The passage describes PORT as lesions in outer retinal layers with minimal vitritis (making them less overt clinically) and emphasizes the study's use of multimodal imaging and serology for recognition. The diagnostic approach described (clinical suspicion, serology, OCT/FAF/fundus photography, and specialist assessment) supports the inference that PORT is diagnostically challenging and requires high clinical suspicion plus multimodal imaging for recognition.

157.Option: The high rate of recurrence in PORT (25%) compared to classical OT (up to 79%) indicates that patients with PORT require more aggressive long-term immunosuppressive therapy to prevent reactivation.
Evidence: Recurrences when they appear, usually involve the adjacent retina.

PORT diagnosis was made by a uveitis specialist based on clinical findings, positive serology, and adequate treatment response (800 mg sulfamethoxazole and 160 mg trimethoprim every 12 hours, clindamycin 300 mg every 6 hours and tapering steroids initiating with 0.5 mg/kg/day of prednisone).
Treatment with pyrimethamine, sulfadiazine, and corticosteroids often results in full visual recovery, yet retinochoroidal scarring is often seen with optical coherence tomography (OCT).
157.Rationale: The passage does not provide any numeric recurrence rates for PORT or classical OT, so the specific figures (25% and 79%) are not supported by the text. The described management of PORT in the study involves antiparasitic therapy with adjunctive steroids and occasional local treatments (e.g., anti-VEGF when CNV is present). There is no evidence presented that patients with PORT require more aggressive long-term immunosuppressive therapy; in fact, immunosuppression is noted as a risk factor for atypical presentations. Therefore the causal recommendation in the option is not derivable from the passage.

157.Option: The similarity between PORT and occult macular dystrophy (OMD) on OCT imaging suggests that patients with suspected posterior uveitis should undergo full-field and multifocal electroretinography to differentiate between the two conditions.
Evidence: PORT is characterized by multifocal grey-white lesions at the outer retinal layers and retinal pigment epithelium associated with minimal vitreous inflammation.
Patients were evaluated using available images including OCT, fundus photography, and fundus autofluorescence (FAF).

PORT diagnosis was made by a uveitis specialist based on clinical findings, positive serology, and adequate treatment response (800 mg sulfamethoxazole and 160 mg trimethoprim every 12 hours, clindamycin 300 mg every 6 hours and tapering steroids initiating with 0.5 mg/kg/day of prednisone).
157.Rationale: Although the passage documents OCT and other multimodal imaging as part of PORT evaluation, it does not mention occult macular dystrophy (OMD), any similarity between PORT and OMD, or the use of full-field or multifocal electroretinography for differential diagnosis. The recommendation to perform ERG testing to distinguish PORT from OMD is not supported by any statement in the passage and therefore cannot be derived from the provided material.

158.Option: The induction of autophagy promotes T. cruzi metacyclogenesis, as evidenced by increased metacyclic trypomastigote formation and higher infection rates in Vero cells when autophagy is stimulated by rapamycin or spermidine.
Evidence: As shown in Fig 3A, the treatment of parasites with 50 ng/μl Rap under control conditions (Diamond media at 28°C) for 2 h, induced a significant increase in the percentage of parasites with more than two Atg8.1 positive vesicles, as compared to non-treated parasites.
Our results showed that the presence of either Spd or Spm significantly increased the detection of Atg8.1 (Fig 5A).

Our results showed that the percentage of MT differentiated up to 48 h was higher in both TAU medium at the first (TAU-F) and the total (TAU-T) period of metacyclogenesis than in controls (12.1 +/- 0.9% and 16.4 +/- 1.7% vs. 3.9 +/- 0,6%, Fig 6A).
Results showed that around 45% of cells were infected by TAU differentiated parasites, whereas 10% of the cells were infected by control parasites.
Conversely, Rap was able to increase the generation of MT when added to control medium at the first period of differentiation (8.3 +/- 0.2% vs. 3.9 +/- 0.6%).
158.Rationale: The passage provides direct experimental evidence that rapamycin (Rap) induces autophagy (increased Atg8.1 vesicles) and that Rap increases metacyclic trypomastigote (MT) generation and infection rates. It also shows that spermidine (Spd) and spermine (Spm) increase detection of Atg8.1, indicating they induce autophagy. However, the passage does not present direct experimental data that spermidine (or spermine) stimulation of autophagy leads to increased MT formation or higher Vero cell infection. The metacyclogenesis and infection assays linking autophagy stimulation to increased MT/infectivity are explicitly reported for rapamycin (and TAU starvation), but not for spermidine. Because the option asserts both rapamycin and spermidine produce the MT/infection increase, and the passage lacks direct evidence for spermidine producing increased MT/infectivity, the statement as written cannot be fully derived from the Passage Body.

158.Option: Spermidine and spermine exert their proautophagic effects in T. cruzi by directly inhibiting the activity of histone acetyl transferase, leading to transcriptional upregulation of ATG genes.
Evidence: The polyamine spermidine (Spd) has been recently described as a new modulator of autophagy since Spd inhibits the activity of histone acetyl transferase, leading to the upregulation of several ATG genes including ATG7, ATG11 and ATG15 [9].

When added to culture media, Spd is also able to directly induce autophagy in a transcription-independent manner. The mechanism has not been fully elucidated yet; however, this phenomenon could be due to the enhanced deacetylation of essential autophagy-related proteins such as ATG5 and ATG7 [10].
Our results showed that the presence of either Spd or Spm significantly increased the detection of Atg8.1 (Fig 5A).
158.Rationale: The first quoted sentence describes a mechanism (histone acetyl transferase inhibition leading to transcriptional upregulation of ATG genes) reported for spermidine in other systems, and the passage states spermidine can also induce autophagy and that the mechanism is not fully elucidated. For T. cruzi specifically, the passage only shows that spermidine and spermine increase Atg8.1 detection (i.e., induce autophagy). The passage does not provide evidence that in T. cruzi spermidine/spermine act by inhibiting histone acetyl transferase or by transcriptional upregulation of ATG genes. Indeed it explicitly notes a transcription-independent induction in other systems and that mechanisms are not fully determined. Therefore the detailed mechanistic claim for T. cruzi cannot be derived from the Passage Body.

158.Option: The increased infection rate of Vero cells by T. cruzi parasites subjected to autophagy induction is primarily due to enhanced parasite motility rather than increased metacyclic trypomastigote formation.
Evidence: The degree of cell infection was considered an indirect measure of the number of MT present in the samples at different conditions, as previously described [22].
Results showed that around 45% of cells were infected by TAU differentiated parasites, whereas 10% of the cells were infected by control parasites.

The percentage of MT differentiated up to 48 h was higher in both TAU medium at the first (TAU-F) and the total (TAU-T) period of metacyclogenesis than in controls (12.1 +/- 0.9% and 16.4 +/- 1.7% vs. 3.9 +/- 0,6%, Fig 6A).
158.Rationale: The Passage Body directly links higher Vero cell infection rates to increased numbers of metacyclic trypomastigotes (MT), explicitly stating infection rate was used as an indirect measure of MT abundance. There is no experimental data or mention concerning parasite motility as a cause of increased infection. Therefore the assertion that enhanced motility is the primary cause is not supported and cannot be inferred from the passage.

158.Option: The presence of wortmannin during the first or total period of differentiation in TAU medium significantly reduced the number of metacyclic trypomastigotes, indicating that autophagy inhibition leads to a complete block in parasite differentiation.
Evidence: Furthermore the addition of Wort to TAU during the first (Wort-F) or the total (Wort-T) period significantly reduced the number of MT in the parasite mixture, as compared to TAU-T condition.
In concordance to the above result, the addition of Rap at the first period of time (Rap-F) induced a significant increase of infectivity (Fig 6C).
158.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports that wortmannin (Wort) significantly reduced the number of metacyclic trypomastigotes when present during differentiation. However, it reports a significant reduction, not a complete block. There is no evidence in the Passage Body that differentiation was completely abolished by wortmannin. Thus the stronger causal claim that autophagy inhibition 'leads to a complete block in parasite differentiation' is not supported by the reported data.

158.Option: Bafilomycin abrogates autophagosome formation in T. cruzi due to its inhibition of the acidocalcisome acidification process, which is essential for PI3P synthesis and autophagosome biogenesis.
Evidence: Bafilomycin (Baf), which is another important autophagy inhibitor, is mainly used to study the autophagic flux [28]. The treatment of mammalian cells with Baf blocks the normal autolysosomal degradation, leading to accumulation of autophagic compartments at different maturation stages.
Unexpectedly, a significant reduction in the autophagosome number was observed when parasites were incubated in TAU in the presence of Baf, as compared to TAU medium alone (Fig 4A and 4B).

The number of autophagosomes was lower in parasites subjected to Baf in control medium than in non-treated parasites, indicating that Baf inhibits both basal and induced autophagy. Unlike the effect observed in mammalian cells, Baf abrogated autophagosome formation in T. cruzi, resulting in a complete inhibition of the autophagic response.

In mammalian cells Vps34 forms a complex with beclin-1 (the mammalian ortholog of yeast Atg6) and other proteins to promote the production of phosphatidylinositol 3-phosphate, thereby facilitating lipid membrane changes required for autophagosome formation and maturation [7].
158.Rationale: The Passage Body documents that bafilomycin (Baf) unexpectedly abrogates autophagosome formation in T. cruzi, in contrast to its effect in mammalian cells. However, it does not provide any experimental evidence that this effect is mediated via inhibition of acidocalcisome acidification or that acidocalcisome acidification is essential for PI3P synthesis and autophagosome biogenesis. The quoted sentence about Vps34 and PI3P production describes the role of Vps34 in autophagy generally but does not link bafilomycin to acidocalcisomes or PI3P synthesis in T. cruzi. Therefore the specific mechanistic claim in the option is not supported by the Passage Body.

159.Option: The induction of both IFN-γ and IL-10 production in vaccinated mice suggests that the immune response is balanced and may be sufficient for long-term protection against visceral leishmaniasis.
Evidence: As shown in Figure 3A, stimulation of splenocytes isolated from vaccinated groups G1 and G2 prior and after challenge with all three recall antigens (e.g. rA2-rCPA-rCPB, L. tar A2-CPA-CPB-CTE-EGFP lysate and L. infantum lysate) elicited a significantly higher IFN-\u03b3 production than control groups G3, G4 and G5.
The production of IL-10 upon antigen stimulation at 4-week and especially at 8 weeks after challenge was lower in G1 and G2 vaccinated groups in comparison to the control groups (G3 to G5) (Fig. 3B).

Indeed, the Leishmania-specific IFN-\u03b3/IL-10 ratios upon stimulation with rA2-rCPA-rCPB and L. tar A2-CPA-CPB-CTE-GFP recall antigens were higher in G1 and G2 compared to the control G3, G4 and G5 mice both at 4 and 8 weeks after challenge.
IFN-\u03b3 levels were maintained high even 8 weeks post-challenge in both G1 (404.5\u00b1101 pg/ml) and G2 (331\u00b127 pg/ml) (Fig. 3A).

only the vaccinated groups G1 and especially G2, which was immunized with Live L. tar A2-CPA-CPB-CTE/Live L. tar A2-CPA-CPB-CTE vaccine regimen, were able to control the infection as illustrated by the significant differences observed at 12 weeks after challenge in comparison to the controls (G1 with G4 and G2 with G3 and G5).
159.Rationale: The passage reports that vaccinated mice (G1 and G2) produced high IFN-\u03b3 and measurable IL-10 with elevated IFN-\u03b3/IL-10 ratios, that these cytokine profiles correlated inversely with parasite burden, and that protection (reduced parasite loads) persisted through the measured follow-up (up to 12 weeks). From these data it is epidemiologically reasonable to infer that a balanced response characterized by IFN-\u03b3 together with controlled IL-10 is associated with sustained protection in this model, i.e. may be sufficient for longer-term protection within the time frame studied.

159.Option: The Live/Live prime-boost regimen using recombinant L. tarentolae A2-CPA-CPB-CTE provides superior protection against visceral leishmaniasis compared to the DNA/Live regimen and to control groups, as evidenced by significantly lower parasite burden in the spleen and improved preservation of splenic architecture.
Evidence: As shown in Figure 2, immunization using a DNA A2-CPA-CPB-CTE/Live L. tar A2-CPA-CPB-CTE (G1) and L. tar A2-CPA-CPB-CTE/L. tar A2-CPA-CPB-CTE (G2) prime-boosting regiments drastically (p<0.01) reduced the infection levels in both the liver (Fig. 2A and 2B) and the spleen (Fig. 2D and 2E) at 4 weeks after L. infantum challenge in contrast to the control groups (G3 to G5, n=4 at each point/organ).

only the vaccinated groups G1 and especially G2, which was immunized with Live L. tar A2-CPA-CPB-CTE/Live L. tar A2-CPA-CPB-CTE vaccine regimen, were able to control the infection as illustrated by the significant differences observed at 12 weeks after challenge in comparison to the controls (G1 with G4 and G2 with G3 and G5). Thus, the G2 vaccination regimen protects against infection better than G5 (Live L. tar/Live L. tar).

The production of IFN-\u03b3 in response to rA2-rCPA-rCPB antigens reached the highest level (524\u00b116.4 pg/ml) at 4 weeks after challenge in G2 (Live/Live). This was significantly (p<0.05) different from G1 (DNA/Live) (404\u00b119.9 pg/ml).
Normal follicle formation of the spleen was noted in G1 and G2 in 4th and 8th week but the normal follicles were disappeared in the other groups (G3, G4 and G5) (Fig. 5A).
We could not find parasites in the spleen in vaccinated groups (G1 and G2) but they could be seen in nearly all non-vaccinated groups (G3, G4 and G5, Fig. 5B).
159.Rationale: The passage presents direct experimental comparisons showing that both vaccinated regimens reduced parasite burdens relative to controls, and notes that the Live/Live (G2) regimen performed particularly well ('especially G2'), achieving higher IFN-\u03b3, higher nitrite production and better control at later time points. Histopathology data show preserved splenic architecture in vaccinated mice (including G2). Taken together the data support the inference that the Live/Live recombinant L. tarentolae prime-boost provides superior protection relative to controls and is at least as good, and in some measures better, than the DNA/Live regimen.

159.Option: The use of cationic solid-lipid nanoparticles (cSLN) as a delivery system for the DNA vaccine enhances antigen expression and is critical for the observed protective immunity in the DNA/Live regimen.
Evidence: For DNA vaccination with rA2-CPA-CPB-CTE, the pcDNA-A2-CPA-CPB-CTE was formulated into cationic lipid particles with nanometer range (∼240–250 nm). It was shown previously that this formulation facilitates the uptake by dendritic cells and macrophages, thereby enhancing antigen expression, processing and presentation and resulting in stronger immune effects [59].

Group 1 (DNA cSLN/Live) immunized with pcDNA-A2-CPA-CPB-CTE-cSLN (50 µg of pcDNA-A2-CPA-CPB-CTE formulated by cSLN nanoparticles ... ) as a prime and with 2×107 recombinant L. tarentolae A2-CPA-CPB-CTE as a boost; ... group 4 [(empty vector pcDNA-cSLN (prime)/Live L. tarentolae wild type (boost) as a control)];
159.Rationale: While the passage states that cSLN formulation has been shown previously to enhance uptake and antigen expression, the present study does not provide direct experimental evidence that cSLN delivery was the critical determinant of protection in the DNA/Live regimen. There is no comparison of DNA vaccination with and without cSLN, and the control group used the empty vector formulated with cSLN.
