Evidence: Proliferation was higher in H69 cells treated with NDMA and ESP of C. sinensis than in control cells. Compared to control cells, the average increase for cells treated with NDMA was 112%, for cells treated with ESP was 120% (P < 0.05), and for cells treated with NDMA + ESP was 146% (P < 0.001). NDMA and ESP had synergistic effects on cell proliferation (Fig 1A).
The proportions of G2/M-phase cells in each condition were as follows: control, 17%; NDMA, 20%; ESP, 26%; NDMA + ESP, 37% (P = 0.007).
The expression levels of Cx26 and Cx43 were high in NDMA + ESP-treated cells, as confirmed by immunoblotting (Fig 3).
Real-time PCR showed that Cx43 silencing resulted in the downregulation of Cx26 and Cox-2 (Fig 5C and 5D, respectively).
We found that the silencing of Cx43 reduced ESP- and NDMA-induced cell proliferation and the expression of Cox-2.
180.Rationale: Both Cx26 and Cx43 are upregulated in NDMA + ESP-treated cells, but the passage provides functional evidence implicating Cx43 in driving the proliferative response: knockdown of Cx43 reduced ESP/NDMA-induced proliferation and reduced Cox-2 (and also lowered Cx26). There is no experimental evidence isolating Cx26 as the primary driver; instead, the data indicate Cx43 has a causal role. Therefore the claim that Cx26 is primarily responsible is not supported by the passage.

181.Option: The high compliance with repeated PZQ treatments and the low frequency of adverse events suggest that multiple doses of praziquantel are both safe and highly effective in eliminating Schistosoma mansoni infections in school-aged children.
Evidence: In the intense treatment group, compliance to each following treatment was unexpectedly high, from 100% in week 2 (second treatment) to 98% in week 6 (fourth and final treatment).
Most of the adverse events were mild and all of them resolved 24 hours after treatment. Adverse events decreased with subsequent treatments in the intense treatment group.
A significantly higher CR (86%, 95% CI 75–92%) was observed in the intense treatment group, (P<0.01; primary outcome, both CRs measured 10 weeks after first treatment).
POC-CCA-based CRs were much lower compared to CRs based on the KK technique; only 18% (95% CI 11–27%) in the standard treatment group and 36% (95% CI 26–46%) in the intense treatment group (P<0.01).
Based on POC-CCA, measured at the final follow-up time point, 82% (95% CI 72–89%) and 64% (95% CI 54–74%) POC-CCA positives were observed in the standard and intense treatment group, respectively.
181.Rationale: While the passage provides clear evidence of very high compliance with repeated PZQ dosing and that most adverse events were mild and transient, the claim that multiple doses are "highly effective in eliminating" infections is not supported when both diagnostic methods are considered. KK-based CRs and IRRs improve with repeated dosing, but POC-CCA (which detects active worm infection) shows much lower CRs and many children remain POC-CCA positive at the final follow-up. Therefore, although safety is supported, elimination (i.e., nearly complete cure) is not demonstrated by the combined diagnostic evidence.

181.Option: Since the POC-CCA test detects active infections and the KK technique detects eggs, the higher cure rate observed with KK must be due to the test's greater specificity, indicating that PZQ is highly effective in killing adult worms.
Evidence: The field-applicable and commercially available point-of-care circulating cathodic antigen (POC-CCA) urine test, which identifies active worm infections by detection of schistosome CCA in urine, has shown a higher sensitivity for detecting S. mansoni infections than the KK technique [20–22].

Most studies reporting on the efficacy of PZQ have used microscopy-based methods, such as urine filtration for S. haematobium and the stool-based Kato-Katz (KK) technique for S. japonicum and S. mansoni. However, these methods lack sensitivity, especially for detection of low-intensity infections [17, 18]. Hence, reported CRs based on these parasitological methods are likely an overestimation [19, 20].
A significantly higher CR (86%, 95% CI 75–92%) was observed in the intense treatment group, (P<0.01; primary outcome, both CRs measured 10 weeks after first treatment).
POC-CCA-based CRs were much lower compared to CRs based on the KK technique; only 18% (95% CI 11–27%) in the standard treatment group and 36% (95% CI 26–46%) in the intense treatment group (P<0.01).

It is known that PZQ targets adult worms, therefore a direct determination of PZQ efficacy would be to measure the number of worms instead of eggs (which are usually used as a proxy for worm burden) with a highly accurate diagnostic tool.
181.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that POC-CCA has higher sensitivity and that microscopy-based methods like KK "lack sensitivity" and may overestimate cure rates. The higher KK-based cure rates are therefore more plausibly explained by KK's lower sensitivity (missing low-intensity or active infections) rather than by greater specificity implying true worm killing. The passage does not support the assertion that KK's higher CR "must be due to" greater specificity or that it proves high PZQ efficacy against adult worms; in fact, it argues the opposite (that direct detection of worms or antigens is more informative).

181.Option: The use of the POC-CCA test revealed a significantly lower cure rate compared to the KK technique, indicating that PZQ may be less efficacious than previously reported due to persistent active infections despite treatment.
Evidence: The field-applicable and commercially available point-of-care circulating cathodic antigen (POC-CCA) urine test, which identifies active worm infections by detection of schistosome CCA in urine, has shown a higher sensitivity for detecting S. mansoni infections than the KK technique [20–22].
A significantly higher CR (86%, 95% CI 75–92%) was observed in the intense treatment group, (P<0.01; primary outcome, both CRs measured 10 weeks after first treatment).
POC-CCA-based CRs were much lower compared to CRs based on the KK technique; only 18% (95% CI 11–27%) in the standard treatment group and 36% (95% CI 26–46%) in the intense treatment group (P<0.01).

Most studies reporting on the efficacy of PZQ have used microscopy-based methods ... However, these methods lack sensitivity, especially for detection of low-intensity infections [17, 18]. Hence, reported CRs based on these parasitological methods are likely an overestimation [19, 20].

It is known that PZQ targets adult worms, therefore a direct determination of PZQ efficacy would be to measure the number of worms instead of eggs (which are usually used as a proxy for worm burden) with a highly accurate diagnostic tool.
181.Rationale: The passage provides direct data showing much lower CRs when using POC-CCA (which detects active worm antigens) versus KK (which detects eggs and is less sensitive). The authors note that microscopy-based CRs are likely overestimates. Therefore, it is reasonable to infer from the study's diagnostic comparison that PZQ's apparent efficacy may be lower than previously reported based on egg-detection methods, because POC-CCA reveals persistent active infections after treatment.

181.Option: The discrepancy between KK and POC-CCA results suggests that the POC-CCA test is unreliable for detecting Schistosoma mansoni infections in post-treatment settings, particularly in children with low-intensity infections.
Evidence: The field-applicable and commercially available point-of-care circulating cathodic antigen (POC-CCA) urine test, which identifies active worm infections by detection of schistosome CCA in urine, has shown a higher sensitivity for detecting S. mansoni infections than the KK technique [20–22].

Most studies reporting on the efficacy of PZQ have used microscopy-based methods, such as urine filtration for S. haematobium and the stool-based Kato-Katz (KK) technique for S. japonicum and S. mansoni. However, these methods lack sensitivity, especially for detection of low-intensity infections [17, 18]. Hence, reported CRs based on these parasitological methods are likely an overestimation [19, 20].
Based on POC-CCA, measured at the final follow-up time point, 82% (95% CI 72–89%) and 64% (95% CI 54–74%) POC-CCA positives were observed in the standard and intense treatment group, respectively.
181.Rationale: The passage describes POC-CCA as more sensitive and suitable for surveillance and notes that microscopy-based methods (KK) lack sensitivity and likely overestimate cure. The discrepancy (higher KK CRs, lower POC-CCA CRs) is therefore more plausibly explained by KK's insensitivity rather than POC-CCA being unreliable. The study provides no evidence that POC-CCA is unreliable in post-treatment settings; on the contrary, the passage emphasizes its higher sensitivity and role in detecting active infections.

181.Option: The increase in POC-CCA positivity after the fourth treatment in the intense group indicates that repeated praziquantel doses may stimulate worm fecundity or accelerate parasite maturation, leading to renewed egg production.
Evidence: Based on POC-CCA, measured at the final follow-up time point, 82% (95% CI 72–89%) and 64% (95% CI 54–74%) POC-CCA positives were observed in the standard and intense treatment group, respectively.

Based on POC-CCA (Fig 3C and 3D), the overall prevalence did not change dramatically and the proportion of 3+ scores (indicating high infection level) remained similar over time in the standard treatment group as well as in the intense treatment group.
The proportion of POC-CCA negatives (including traces) increased over time, particularly in the intense treatment group.
181.Rationale: The passage does not report an increase in POC-CCA positivity after the fourth treatment; rather, it reports that overall POC-CCA prevalence did not change dramatically and that negatives increased over time, especially in the intense group. There is no data or statement suggesting that repeated PZQ stimulates worm fecundity or accelerates maturation. This causal biological claim is speculative and not supported by the study results presented in the passage.

182.Option: The fact that H2B-derived peptides were the most immunogenic in inducing IFN-γ responses suggests that H2B is the dominant protective antigen in human leishmaniasis and should be prioritized in future vaccine development.
Evidence: However, among these peptides, H2BI and H2BII showed the highest IFN-γ levels, although not statistically significant when compared to unstimulated cultures. We further analyzed these 2 peptides in a larger group of healed CL and in healthy individuals. As shown in Fig 1, significant levels of IFN-γ were detected in healed CL, when compared to unstimulated cultures (Fig 1B). In addition, H2BI and H2BII were not able to induce a significant IFN-γ response in healthy individuals (Fig 1B).

IFN-γ levels induced by P21 pool (including all multi-epitope peptides) in cured CL individuals (median/IQR: 1207/928) were similar to those induced by SLA in the same group (1182/1837), (p>0.05) (Fig 3).
182.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that H2BI and H2BII induced relatively high IFN-γ levels and that these peptides produced significant IFN-γ in healed CL subjects (and not in healthy controls). This supports that H2B-derived peptides are immunogenic. However, the option goes further to claim H2B is the dominant protective antigen and should be prioritized for vaccine development. The Passage Body does not provide evidence of protective efficacy (e.g., reduced infection, clinical protection) attributable to H2B in humans—only immunogenicity data are presented. Therefore the stronger causal/protective conclusion is not directly supported by the passage.

182.Option: The ability of the multi-epitope peptide pools to induce high levels of IFN-γ and TNF-α, but not IL-10, indicates that they can effectively mimic the immune response seen in natural infection and may serve as a reliable surrogate for live antigen exposure in vaccine development.
Evidence: IFN-γ levels induced by P21 pool (including all multi-epitope peptides) in cured CL individuals (median/IQR: 1207/928) were similar to those induced by SLA in the same group (1182/1837), (p>0.05) (Fig 3).
Significant TNF-α levels were induced by P21 in cured (median/IQR in stimulated; unstimulated cultures: 10/13; 0/1.9) (p = 0.001), but not in healthy individuals (5.4/2; 1.9/10) (p>0.05) (Fig 6A).
No IL-10 was observed in response to peptides used either individually (except low but significant levels observed for H2BII) or as peptide pools (Figs 4 and 5).

Interestingly, IFN-γ levels induced by P21 pool (including all multi-epitope peptides) in cured CL individuals (median/IQR: 1207/928) were similar to those induced by SLA in the same group (1182/1837), (p>0.05) (Fig 3).
When compared to SLA induced response; no statistically significant difference was observed for P8, P9, P14, P16 and P21 pools.
182.Rationale: The Passage Body supports that some peptide pools (notably P21) induce IFN-γ and TNF-α in cured CL subjects and that several pools produce IFN-γ responses comparable to SLA (the soluble leishmania antigen), and that peptides generally do not induce IL-10. This provides partial support for the idea that peptide pools can replicate certain aspects of the immune signature seen with SLA stimulation. However, the option's assertion that they 'can effectively mimic the immune response seen in natural infection and may serve as a reliable surrogate for live antigen exposure' is stronger than the data justify. The passage shows similarity in some cytokine readouts but does not demonstrate comprehensive equivalence to natural infection or SLA across all immune parameters, nor does it provide evidence of in vivo surrogate performance or protective efficacy. Therefore the broad claim of being a reliable surrogate for live antigen exposure is not fully supported by the presented data.

182.Option: The selected multi-epitope peptide pools were able to induce both CD4+ and CD8+ IFN-γ-secreting T cell responses, as well as a significant increase in the frequency of CD4+ and CD8+ central memory T cells (TCM) in individuals with healed cutaneous leishmaniasis.
Evidence: We observed a significantly higher percentage of CD4+T cells producing IFN-γ in response to all selected peptide pools, in comparison to unstimulated cultures (median/IQR in stimulated; unstimulated cultures: P8: 0.7/1.9; 0.2/0.7, P9: 1.6/2.5; 0.3/0.6, P13: 4/6; 0.1/0.6, P14: 1.1/1.1; 0.2/0.7, P16: 3/4; 1/1.3 and P21: 1.2/3.1; 0.1/0.6) (p≤0.009) (Fig 8A and S1 Fig).

As shown in Fig 9, a significant increase in the percentage of IFN-γ+ CD8+ T cells was observed after stimulation with P8 (median/IQR in stimulated; unstimulated cultures: 2.5/2.6; 2/1.4), P9 (2.6/2.8; 2/1.4), P13 (6.1/3.8; 2.1/0.9), P16 (3.3/4.8; 2/1.4) and P21 (4.6/7; 2/1.4) (p≤0.03).

A significant increase in the percentage of CD4+ TCM as well as CD8+ TCM cells was observed in response to P8 (median/IQR in stimulated; unstimulated cultures for CD4+ and CD8+ TCM: 1.1/1.3; 0.3/0.1% and 0.3/0.3; 0.06/0.06), P13 (2/2; 0.4/0.8% and 0.5/1.3; 0.06/0.2), P14 (2/0.9; 0.3/0.1% and 0.3/0.4; 0.06/0.06) and P21 (2.3/1.4; 0.4/0.8% and 0.9/1.3; 0.06/0.2) (p≤0.04) (Fig 11A and 11C).
182.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports statistically significant increases in IFN-γ-producing CD4+ T cells and IFN-γ-producing CD8+ T cells after stimulation with multiple peptide pools, and also documents significant increases in both CD4+ and CD8+ central memory (TCM) frequencies in response to several peptide pools (P8, P13, P14, P21). Thus the option is directly supported by the presented data.

182.Option: The suppressive effect of some peptide pools on IL-10 production suggests that these peptides may help prevent excessive inflammatory responses, thereby contributing to a balanced immune response in vaccinated individuals.
Evidence: No IL-10 was observed in response to peptides used either individually (except low but significant levels observed for H2BII) or as peptide pools (Figs 4 and 5).
However, a suppressive effect on IL-10 production was observed after stimulation with PII, P6, P8, P10, P13, P14 and P18 (Figs 4C and 5).
182.Rationale: The Passage Body documents that certain peptide pools produced a suppressive effect on IL-10 production. However, the interpretation offered in the option—that suppression of IL-10 would 'help prevent excessive inflammatory responses' and contribute to a balanced immune response—is not supported by the data. IL-10 is an anti-inflammatory cytokine; suppressing IL-10 could plausibly reduce anti-inflammatory control and potentially permit increased inflammation rather than preventing it. The passage provides no evidence linking the observed IL-10 suppression to prevention of excessive inflammation or to an improved balance in vivo following vaccination. Thus the causal interpretation in the option is not derivable from the passage.

182.Option: The induction of multifunctional CD4+ T cells producing IFN-γ, TNF-α, and IL-2 by the peptide pools suggests that these pools can elicit a robust and sustained protective immune response in individuals with healed leishmaniasis.
Evidence: As shown in Fig 10, no significant increase was observed in the percentage of CD4+ T cells producing IFN-γ, IL-2 and TNF-α, in response to both peptide pools and SLA in cured CL individuals.

A significant increase in the frequencies of bifunctional IFN-γ+/TNF-α+, IL-2+/TNF-α+, and monofunctional IFN-γ+ and TNF-α+ CD4+ T cells, was shown in response to SLA and to peptide pools P13, P14 and P21 (p<0.04) (Fig 10).
P21 was the only pool to induce a significant increase in the IL-2+ CD4+ T cell population.
182.Rationale: The Passage Body explicitly states that there was no significant increase in the percentage of CD4+ T cells simultaneously producing IFN-γ, IL-2 and TNF-α (i.e., true triple-function multifunctional cells) in response to peptide pools or SLA. While the passage does report increases in bifunctional and monofunctional CD4+ subsets for some peptide pools, the specific claim that peptide pools induced multifunctional CD4+ T cells producing all three cytokines is contradicted by the reported data. Furthermore, extrapolating that such induction would confer robust and sustained protective immunity is not supported by presented evidence of in vivo protection. Therefore this option is not supported by the Passage Body.

183.Option: The 'Backpack PCR' platform's reliance on test strip-based detection reduces the risk of contamination compared to agarose gel electrophoresis, making it more suitable for use in field settings.
Evidence: HDA assays typically rely on agarose gel electrophoresis, a proven means of effective target detection, such techniques are prone to sample contamination [33], and are difficult to perform in a field setting.
the field-friendly, “Backpack PCR” platform we describe here utilizes test strip-based DNA detection methods.
Such technology partners the advantages of standardizing assay readouts with the capacity to minimize laboratory equipment needs.

a number of assays conducted in conjunction with the Global Programme to Eliminate Lymphatic Filariasis and other tropical disease control and elimination programs [35] make use of strip or card-based detection techniques [36–39].
183.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that agarose gel electrophoresis is "prone to sample contamination" and is "difficult to perform in a field setting." It also describes the Backpack PCR platform as using "test strip-based DNA detection methods" that "standardiz[e] assay readouts" and "minimize laboratory equipment needs," and notes that strip/card-based detection is being used in programmatic assays. From these statements it is reasonable to infer that replacing gel-based visualization with test-strip detection reduces reliance on the contamination-prone gel workflow and is therefore more suitable for field use. The combination of explicit concern about gels and the promoted advantages of test strips supports the option's conclusion.

183.Option: The development of the 'Backpack PCR' platform provides proof-of-concept for the creation of similar field-friendly molecular xenomonitoring assays for other mosquito-borne pathogens, including Wuchereria bancrofti.
Evidence: Here we describe the development of a novel diagnostic platform, which we have termed “Backpack PCR”, coupling a rapid mosquito DNA extraction procedure with the test strip-based detection of B. malayi DNA.

While acknowledging the potential utility of backpack PCR as a tool in Brugia-endemic locations, the primary contribution of this work is likely its service as a proof-of-concept study for the parallel development of a similar assay for the detection of W. bancrofti. Such development is currently underway.
183.Rationale: The authors explicitly state that the primary contribution of the study is as a proof-of-concept for developing a similar field-friendly assay for W. bancrofti, and they describe the Backpack PCR as a novel, portable platform coupling rapid extraction with strip-based detection. Thus the passage directly supports the broader implication that this development demonstrates feasibility of analogous MX assays for other mosquito-borne filarial pathogens, including W. bancrofti.

183.Option: The 'Backpack PCR' platform's ability to detect both B. malayi and B. pahangi indicates that it can be used to monitor zoonotic transmission cycles in co-endemic regions, providing insights into animal reservoirs of filarial infection.
Evidence: However, B. pahangi genomic DNA produced positive results, indicating cross-reactivity, when examined by both visualization techniques (S1 Fig).
This is not surprising since the HhaI repeat found in B. malayi has a sequence that is 89% identical to the HhaI repeat found in B. pahangi [40].
Thus, the assay we describe here is Brugia genus-specific, but not B. malayi species-specific.
183.Rationale: The passage documents cross-reactivity with B. pahangi and concludes the assay is Brugia genus-specific rather than B. malayi species-specific. While detection of B. pahangi indicates the assay will pick up that animal parasite, the authors do not claim it can distinguish human versus animal transmission cycles or that it provides actionable insights into animal reservoirs. In fact, cross-reactivity could confound efforts to specifically monitor zoonotic cycles. Therefore, the specific inference that the platform can be used to monitor zoonotic transmission cycles and animal reservoirs is not supported by the passage and oversteps the evidence.

183.Option: The 'Backpack PCR' platform's high sensitivity in detecting a single B. malayi L3 larva in mosquito pools demonstrates that molecular xenomonitoring can replace traditional human-based surveillance methods for lymphatic filariasis.
Evidence: PCR-based detection of parasite DNA in insect vectors, termed molecular xenomonitoring (MX), is an effective and non-invasive alternative, capable of indirectly measuring parasite burden within endemic locations [17–27].

The NaOH-based extraction method was sufficiently efficient to give consistent detection of a single B. malayi L3-staged worm in all pools of up to 20 uninfected mosquitoes and in 9/10 pools containing 25 mosquitoes (Table 1).
For all samples tested, real-time PCR results agreed with the miniPCR results using both test strip-based detection and electrophoresis-based detection (Table 2).
183.Rationale: The passage establishes that MX is an effective, non-invasive alternative and shows high analytical sensitivity for detecting a single L3 larva in mosquito pools, with strong agreement with real-time PCR. However, replacing traditional human-based surveillance requires evidence that MX measurements correlate reliably with human infection prevalence, can meet programmatic decision thresholds, and are validated for stopping MDA—none of which is provided in the passage. The authors present the platform as a field-friendly tool and proof-of-concept, but do not claim or provide data sufficient to conclude MX can fully replace human-based surveillance. Thus the stronger claim of replacement is not derivable from the presented evidence.

184.Option: The induction of a strong DTH response by AAL and AAS treatment is a result of non-specific inflammation rather than antigen-specific cell-mediated immunity.
Evidence: Chemotherapeutic intervention and cure is generally associated with the acquisition of a DTH response and consequently “classical” cell-mediated immunity [39].

The response was evaluated by measuring the difference in footpad swelling at 24 h, 48 h and 72 h following intradermal inoculation of the test footpad with 50 μl (800 μg ml−1) of FT compared to the PBS-injected contra-lateral footpad [14].

AAL and AAS treated mice showed the strongest DTH response at 200 mg/kg. b.w.; a significant increase in footpad thickness was observed at 24 h (0.45±0.07 and 0.48±0.14, respectively) as compared with the INF (013±0.004) control group, followed by 100 and 50 mg/kg b.w. (Fig. 3).
184.Rationale: The passage describes the DTH measurement as an antigen-specific assay (FT antigen intradermal vs PBS contralateral) and explicitly links acquisition of DTH with "classical" cell-mediated immunity. The observed increased footpad swelling was measured after inoculation with leishmanial antigen (FT) compared to PBS, demonstrating antigen-specific CMI rather than non-specific inflammation. Therefore the option claiming non-specific inflammation is not supported by the passage.

184.Option: The therapeutic efficacy of AAL and AAS in leishmaniasis is primarily due to their direct leishmanicidal activity mediated by programmed cell death, independent of immune modulation.
Evidence: AAL and AAS administered to 10 weeks infected BALB/c mice at 200 mg/kg b.w. for 10 consecutive days caused 95.45±2.05% and 95.84±1.95% (P<0.001) reduction of parasite burden in spleen and 86.67±2.53% and 89.12±1.92% (P<0.001) in liver, respectively (Fig. 1A & B) at 10 days post treatment.

Mice treated with AAL and AAS (200 mg/kg b.w.) induced significantly elevated levels of serum IFN-γ (2771±50.91 and 3033±396.69 pg ml−1) and reduced levels of IL-4 (4020±91.92 and 3961.5±208.24 pg ml−1) and IL-10 (4231.5±459.27 and 4077.5±35.0 pg ml−1) compared to untreated infected controls INF (low IFN-γ; 1236±12.37, high IL-4; 5696±79.9 and high IL-10; 5049±101.47) (Fig. 6A).
AAL and AAS treated mice showed the strongest DTH response ... as compared with the INF ...

AAL and AAS treatment (200 mg/kg.b.w) significantly up-regulated the expression of CD80 (20.3% and 21.13%) and CD86 (14.6% and 16.0%) as well as co-expression of CD80/86 (21.9% and 26.0%) on F4/80 cells.
184.Rationale: While the extracts reduced parasite burden, the passage also presents multiple lines of immune modulation associated with treatment: increased IFN-γ, reduced Th2 cytokines, enhanced DTH, upregulation of co-stimulatory molecules, increased NO production and expansion of CD4+/CD8+ and IFN-γ-producing T cells. There is no evidence in the passage that efficacy is "primarily" due to direct induction of programmed cell death or that efficacy is independent of immune modulation. Thus the claim of primary, immune-independent leishmanicidal activity mediated by programmed cell death is unsupported by the provided data.

184.Option: The upregulation of CD80 and CD86 on antigen-presenting cells by AAL and AAS is a consequence of reduced parasite load rather than a direct immunomodulatory effect.
Evidence: AAL and AAS administered to 10 weeks infected BALB/c mice at 200 mg/kg b.w. for 10 consecutive days caused 95.45±2.05% and 95.84±1.95% (P<0.001) reduction of parasite burden in spleen and 86.67±2.53% and 89.12±1.92% (P<0.001) in liver, respectively (Fig. 1A & B) at 10 days post treatment.

AAL and AAS treatment induced maximum up-regulation of CD80 (11.7% and 15.7%) and CD86 (12.6% and 15.4%) expression along with CD80/86 (13.1% and 17.0%) co-expression in comparison with infection control...

Therefore the causal claim that upregulation is a consequence rather than a direct immunomodulatory effect cannot be derived from the provided data.

184.Option: The elevated IgG2a/IgG1 ratio in AAL and AAS-treated mice indicates a general enhancement of humoral immunity rather than a Th1-polarized response.
Evidence: The highest IgG2a/IgG1 ratio was found in AAL (2.09) and AAS (1.92) -treated mice at 200 mg/kg b.w., followed by AMB treatment group (1.56).

Evidence: Mice treated with AAL and AAS (200 mg/kg b.w.) induced significantly elevated levels of serum IFN-γ (2771±50.91 and 3033±396.69 pg ml−1) and reduced levels of IL-4 (4020±91.92 and 3961.5±208.24 pg ml−1) and IL-10 (4231.5±459.27 and 4077.5±35.0 pg ml−1) compared to untreated infected controls INF (low IFN-γ; 1236±12.37, high IL-4; 5696±79.9 and high IL-10; 5049±101.47) (Fig. 6A).
