The text does not present data on pig movement as the cause of clustering or on increased infection risk in distant areas due to pig movement. Therefore the causal claim in the option is not supported by the presented data.

224.Option: The increased frequency of HLA-DRpos CD4 T cells in W. bancrofti-infected individuals is likely due to the presence of microfilariae in the bloodstream, which are known to trigger strong immune responses.
Evidence: W. bancrofti infection was diagnosed using an ELISA specific for circulating filarial antigen (TropBio Og4C3 serum ELISA, Townsville, Australia) as described previously [22] in bio banked cryopreserved plasma, information on the presence of microfilariae was not available.

Frequencies of HLA-DRpos CD4 T cells were significantly increased in subjects with W. bancrofti infection (n = 33 median: 10.71%) compared to subjects either without any helminth infection (n = 42, median 6.97%, p = 0.016 Mann-Whitney test, univariable analysis, Fig 3A) or the combined group of individuals harboring other helminths (S. haematobium, S. mansoni, T. trichiura, A. lumbricoides, hookworms) (n = 151 median 7.38%, p = 0.009).
Almost all W. bancrofti-infected subjects (32 of 33) were also infected with other helminths (Table 1) impeding an evaluation of only monoinfected individuals.
224.Rationale: The passage does report increased HLA-DRpos CD4 T cells in W. bancrofti-infected individuals, but it explicitly states that information on the presence of microfilariae was not available. Therefore one cannot attribute the observed CD4 activation to microfilariae in the bloodstream from the data provided. Furthermore, nearly all W. bancrofti-infected subjects had other helminth co-infections, so attributing activation specifically to microfilariae is not supported by the passage. Because the necessary data on microfilarial presence are absent, the causal claim in this option is not derivable from the passage.

224.Option: W. bancrofti infection is linked to systemic CD4 T cell activation, providing a possible mechanism for the increased HIV-susceptibility observed in W. bancrofti-infected individuals.
Evidence: Recently, we demonstrated for the first time an increased risk of HIV acquisition in individuals infected with the filarial nematode Wuchereria bancrofti, the agent that causes lymphatic filariasis [8]. Our data shows that W. bancrofti is an independent risk factor for HIV acquisition even when adjusting for sexual behavior, condom use, circumcision, marital status, age and gender, among other factors.
Activated CD4 T cells are a major cellular reservoir for continuous HIV replication in vivo, as shown in several studies [17–20].

Frequencies of HLA-DRpos CD4 T cells were significantly increased in subjects with W. bancrofti infection (n = 33 median: 10.71%) compared to subjects either without any helminth infection (n = 42, median 6.97%, p = 0.016 Mann-Whitney test, univariable analysis, Fig 3A) or the combined group of individuals harboring other helminths (S. haematobium, S. mansoni, T. trichiura, A. lumbricoides, hookworms) (n = 151 median 7.38%, p = 0.009).
The significant association of W. bancrofti infection with HLA-DRpos CD4 T cell counts was confirmed in the multivariable analysis (Table 2).
224.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence that W. bancrofti infection is associated with increased systemic CD4 T cell activation (higher frequencies of HLA-DRpos CD4 T cells), and it also states that activated CD4 T cells are a major cellular reservoir for HIV replication. The authors previously demonstrated that W. bancrofti is independently associated with increased HIV acquisition. Combining these documented findings, it is epidemiologically valid to infer that W. bancrofti–linked systemic CD4 activation provides a plausible biological mechanism contributing to the observed increased HIV susceptibility in infected individuals. The inference is supported by the data presented (significant associations confirmed in multivariable analysis) and by established immunologic relevance of activated CD4 cells to HIV.

224.Option: The lack of significant difference in CD8 T cell activation after multivariable adjustment implies that W. bancrofti infection does not influence systemic immune activation in the study population.
Evidence: The significant increase in activated T cells in W. bancrofti-infected individuals was confirmed for the HLA-DRpos CD38pos CD4 and CD8 T cells.

Frequencies of HLA-DRpos CD8 T cell were increased in subjects with W. bancrofti (mean 29.5%) infection compared to subjects without filarial infection (mean 20.8%). However, this difference was not significant after adjusting for study site and other confounding factors in the multivariable analysis (Tables 3 and S2).

Frequencies of HLA-DRpos CD4 T cells were significantly increased in subjects with W. bancrofti infection ... The significant association of W. bancrofti infection with HLA-DRpos CD4 T cell counts was confirmed in the multivariable analysis (Table 2).
224.Rationale: The option overgeneralizes from a non-significant adjusted CD8 result to claim that W. bancrofti does not influence systemic immune activation. The passage shows a clear and statistically confirmed increase in CD4 T cell activation associated with W. bancrofti infection (multivariable-confirmed). Although the CD8 activation difference lost significance after adjustment, the significant CD4 activation demonstrates that W. bancrofti does influence systemic immune activation in this population. Thus the conclusion in this option is not supported by the data.

224.Option: Treatment of W. bancrofti infection may reduce immune activation and thereby decrease HIV transmission in areas where lymphatic filariasis is prevalent, as suggested by the observed immunological changes.
Evidence: Infections with W. bancrofti are chronic and persist over many years despite antihelminthic treatment.
In the study area, governmental treatment of W. bancrofti started in 2009. However, none of the 235 participants reported participation in the mass drug administration program.

Frequencies of HLA-DRpos CD4 T cells were significantly increased in subjects with W. bancrofti infection ... The significant association of W. bancrofti infection with HLA-DRpos CD4 T cell counts was confirmed in the multivariable analysis (Table 2).

Recently, we demonstrated for the first time an increased risk of HIV acquisition in individuals infected with the filarial nematode Wuchereria bancrofti ... Our data shows that W. bancrofti is an independent risk factor for HIV acquisition ...
224.Rationale: While the passage documents that W. bancrofti infection is associated with increased CD4 T cell activation and with increased HIV acquisition risk, it provides no data showing that treatment reduces immune activation or HIV transmission. In fact, the text explicitly states that W. bancrofti infections are chronic and can persist despite antihelminthic treatment, and none of the study participants had participated in the mass drug administration program, so the study cannot assess treatment effects. Therefore the causal claim that treatment may reduce activation and HIV transmission is not derivable from the presented data; it remains a hypothesis requiring direct evidence.

225.Option: The high incidence of undifferentiated fever (UF) cases that did not meet the clinical criteria for dengue fever (DF) or dengue hemorrhagic fever (DHF) suggests that the clinical spectrum of symptomatic dengue infection is broader than previously recognized, and that current diagnostic criteria may miss a substantial proportion of cases.
Evidence: Undifferentiated fever (UF) was defined if the subject did not meet the clinical criteria for DF (i.e. acute febrile illness with at least two of the following symptoms: headache, retro-orbital pain, myalgia, arthralgia, rash, hemorrhagic manifestations, leucopenia) or DHF (i.e. fever with hemorrhagic tendency, thrombocytopenia and evidence of plasma leakage) but was serologically proved to have dengue infection.
There were 156 cases (49.8%) of UF, 123 cases (39.3%) of DF, and 34 cases (10.9%) of DHF including 22 cases (7.0%), 5 cases (1.6%), and 7 cases (2.2%) of DHF grade 1, 2 and 3, respectively.
This article aims to describe the incidence and clinical manifestations of symptomatic dengue infection occurring over the 3-year study of this cohort, from 2006 to 2008.
225.Rationale: The passage defines UF as serologically confirmed dengue that does not meet clinical criteria for DF or DHF, and reports that nearly half (49.8%) of symptomatic dengue cases were UF. These facts directly support the inference that the symptomatic clinical spectrum includes many presentations that do not meet standard clinical case definitions, implying that symptom-based or criteria-based diagnosis would miss a substantial proportion of infections. Thus the conclusion is derivable from the cohort data presented.

225.Option: The clinical manifestations of dengue infection are highly variable across different serotypes, with DEN4 being associated with more severe disease despite its lower prevalence.
Evidence: Dengue serotypes were identified by either mosquito inoculation or RT-PCR in 259 cases (82.7%) including 115 (36.7%), 80 (25.6%), 41 (13.1%), and 23 (7.3%) cases of DEN1, 2, 3, and 4, respectively.

Table 3 shows symptoms and disease severity from different dengue serotypes. DEN4 was found to cause only UF, DF and DHF grade 1 and seemed to have less headache, anorexia, nausea/vomiting and rash compared to other DEN serotypes. However, these differences are not statistically significant.
225.Rationale: While the passage reports some differences by serotype, it explicitly states DEN4 caused only UF, DF and DHF grade 1 (i.e., not more severe disease) and that observed differences were not statistically significant. The option's claim that DEN4 is associated with more severe disease contradicts the passage. Additionally, the passage does not support the strong statement that clinical manifestations are 'highly variable' across serotypes beyond nonsignificant trends. Therefore this option cannot be derived from the passage.

225.Option: The high proportion of undifferentiated fever (UF) cases in this cohort suggests that dengue infection is more commonly asymptomatic than previously believed.
Evidence: Undifferentiated fever (UF) was defined if the subject did not meet the clinical criteria for DF ... or DHF ... but was serologically proved to have dengue infection.
In two studies, infected Thai children, ranging from 49 [3] to 87% [4], were asymptomatic.
There were 156 cases (49.8%) of UF, 123 cases (39.3%) of DF, and 34 cases (10.9%) of DHF...
225.Rationale: UF cases are, by definition, symptomatic febrile illnesses that do not meet DF or DHF criteria; they are not asymptomatic. The passage separately reports prior studies of asymptomatic infection, but the cohort's high UF proportion reflects symptomatic but non-classical presentations, not asymptomatic infection. Therefore the option conflates UF with asymptomatic infections and is not supported by the passage.

225.Option: The presence of diarrhea in dengue infection may be related to plasma leakage that causes malabsorption, suggesting it could serve as an early predictor of severe dengue disease.
Evidence: It was found that nausea/vomiting, abdominal pain, rash, diarrhea and petechiae were statistically more common in DHF compared to DF and UF.

It was noted that the prevalence of most of the symptoms was highest during the first 2 days of illness and then slowly declined. The exceptions are for anorexia, nausea/vomiting, abdominal pain and diarrhea that tended to increase in prevalence in DHF during day 3–5 of illness.
DHF (i.e. fever with hemorrhagic tendency, thrombocytopenia and evidence of plasma leakage)
225.Rationale: The passage documents that diarrhea is more common in DHF and that diarrhea prevalence increases in later days (day 3–5 or 4–6) of illness in DHF cases. However, it does not state any mechanistic link between diarrhea and plasma leakage or malabsorption, nor does it indicate diarrhea functions as an early predictor—indeed, diarrhea appears later in the illness in DHF. Because the causal mechanism and early predictor claim are not provided in the passage, this inference is not supported by the study data.

225.Option: The WHO's new guidelines for dengue diagnosis may fail to identify UF cases because clinicians are unlikely to suspect dengue in patients presenting with mild, non-specific symptoms.
Evidence: At RH, the pediatrician made clinical diagnosis without awareness of confirmatory laboratory test for dengue. Clinical diagnosis of DF, DHF and grading of DHF were made using WHO's criteria [2] after the subject recovered.
Undifferentiated fever (UF) was defined if the subject did not meet the clinical criteria for DF ... or DHF ... but was serologically proved to have dengue infection.

The detailed data on clinical manifestations (i.e. daily symptoms until recovery) of 71 UF cases were not obtained. Therefore only 85 UF cases are used for analysis. The clinical diagnosis of these 71 and 85 UF cases are shown in table 1. Significantly more cases in the group whose data were obtained were diagnosed as acute febrile illness/suspected viral infection (p<0.001).
225.Rationale: The passage shows that UF cases are serologically confirmed dengue that do not meet WHO clinical criteria and that clinicians often diagnose mild cases as 'acute febrile illness/suspected viral infection.' However, the option specifically asserts that 'WHO's new guidelines' (not described in the passage) may fail to identify UF cases and that clinicians are 'unlikely to suspect dengue'—claims that go beyond the presented data. The passage does not discuss any 'new' WHO guidelines or provide evidence about clinicians' likelihood to suspect dengue outside the study context, so this broader claim cannot be derived solely from the passage.

226.Option: The high prevalence of tungiasis in dogs and their frequent movement between households and villages suggest that they are the most important reservoirs for the spread of T. penetrans in rural Uganda.
Evidence: Pigs were significantly more affected than other species (pigs vs. dogs, p<0.0001; pigs vs. cats, p = 0.02; pigs vs. goats, p<0.0001.
Pigs had the highest parasite load followed by goats and dogs: median = 8 lesions (inter-quartile range (IQR) = 3–30; range of 1–246 lesions per pig);
Dogs, cats and poultry roam freely on compounds throughout the year.
Pig tungiasis occurred in nine of the 10 villages, while in dogs, tungiasis occurred in only five villages.
226.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence that pigs—not dogs—had the highest prevalence and the highest parasite loads (pigs significantly more affected than dogs, p<0.0001; pigs had the highest parasite load). While dogs do roam freely (supporting potential movement), tungiasis in dogs was observed in fewer villages and with much lower intensity than in pigs. Therefore the data do not support the inference that dogs are the most important reservoirs for spread; instead the passage identifies pigs as the most affected and highest-load host species.

226.Option: The fact that pigs are not confined during the crop-growing season suggests that their role in transmission is limited to the dry season, when sand flea activity peaks.
Evidence: a cross sectional study was conducted between January 22 and March 28, 2014 which coincided with the middle and end of the dry season when the attack rate of T. penetrans usually peaks [20].
the majority (154 out of 155; 99.4%) confined pigs on earthen floors during the crop growing season and released them to scavenge after harvest.
Pig tungiasis occurred in nine of the 10 villages, while in dogs, tungiasis occurred in only five villages.
226.Rationale: The passage documents both the seasonality (attack rate peaks in the dry season) and pig management (pigs confined during crop-growing season and released after harvest). However, the statement that pigs' role in transmission is 'limited to the dry season' is stronger than what the data support. The study shows pigs are infected and implicated as major hosts, but does not provide direct evidence that their role is confined strictly to the dry season. The cross-sectional data were collected during the dry season (when transmission is high), so they cannot by themselves demonstrate absence of a role in other seasons. Thus this temporal-limitation inference is not supported by the passage.

226.Option: The absence of tungiasis in cattle and chickens suggests that these species are not susceptible to T. penetrans infection, and therefore cannot contribute to transmission in the study area.
Evidence: Rats (n = 65) and all poultry species examined were not infected even in households where other animal species and humans were heavily infected.
Pigs, dogs, goats and a single cat were the only infected domestic mammalian species out of the seven examined (Table 2).
T. penetrans infects a wide range of domestic, peri-domestic and wild mammals such as pigs, dogs, cats, goats, cattle, rodents, elephants, jaguars, monkeys and even armadillos.
226.Rationale: While the study observed no infections in rats and poultry (including chickens) and found only pigs, dogs, goats and one cat infected in this sample, the passage also explicitly states that T. penetrans can infect a wide range of mammals including cattle. The absence of detected infection in cattle/chickens in this cross-sectional sample does not prove species-level non-susceptibility or inability to contribute to transmission in other contexts. Therefore the strong categorical claim that they are not susceptible and cannot contribute to transmission is not supported by the data presented.

226.Option: Pigs were identified as the major hosts of T. penetrans in the study area, and the strong correlation between human and pig tungiasis prevalence and infection intensity supports their role as the primary reservoir for zoonotic transmission.
Evidence: Pigs were identified as the most important domestic animal hosts for T. penetrans.
Pigs had the highest parasite load followed by goats and dogs: median = 8 lesions (inter-quartile range (IQR) = 3–30; range of 1–246 lesions per pig);
Pigs were significantly more affected than other species (pigs vs. dogs, p<0.0001; pigs vs. cats, p = 0.02; pigs vs. goats, p<0.0001.
The prevalence of human tungiasis at household and village levels correlated strongly with the number of lesions in pigs at the respective levels (rho = 0.5, p<0.0001; rho = 0.8, p = 0.002).
In multivariate analysis, presence of animal tungiasis in households was strongly influenced by presence of human tungiasis (OR = 6.5, p < 0.0001) and presence of pigs (OR = 5.9, p = 0.0002).
226.Rationale: The passage explicitly states pigs were the most important domestic hosts and documents that pigs had the highest parasite burdens and were significantly more affected than other species. It also provides strong statistical correlations between pig infection intensity/prevalence and human tungiasis at household and village levels, and multivariate models show the presence of pigs strongly increases odds of animal tungiasis. Together these findings support the epidemiological inference that pigs act as a primary reservoir contributing to zoonotic transmission in this setting.

226.Option: The strong correlation between human and animal tungiasis is likely due to shared environmental exposure rather than direct zoonotic transmission, indicating that pigs play a minimal role in the transmission cycle.
Evidence: A strong correlation existed between the prevalence of households with human tungiasis and those with animal cases within the 10 villages as illustrated in Fig 3A. (rho = 0.85, p = 0.002).
Animal tungiasis increased the odds of the occurrence of human tungiasis in households by six times (OR = 6.1, 95% CI 3.3–11.4%; p<0.001) and vice versa.
Pigs were identified as the most important domestic animal hosts for T. penetrans.
226.Rationale: While shared environmental exposure could contribute to the observed correlation, the passage provides multiple lines of evidence implicating pigs specifically (highest prevalence and parasite load, strong correlations between pig lesion counts and human prevalence, and multivariate analyses showing presence of pigs increases odds of animal tungiasis). The statement that the correlation is 'likely due to' shared environment and that pigs play a minimal role is not supported by the data presented; the study's findings instead support a substantive role for pigs in the transmission cycle. The cross-sectional design cannot fully disentangle environmental versus direct zoonotic transmission, but the available evidence does not justify minimizing the role of pigs as the option asserts.

227.Option: After accounting for spatial autocorrelation, the key environmental determinants of tsetse presence in the study area were forest cover and riverine vegetation, while savannah vegetation and elevation were negatively associated with tsetse presence.
Evidence: In the autologistic model, forest (p<0.05, OR = 1.105) and riverine vegetation (p<0.05, OR = 1.008) were positively correlated with tsetse presence.
Savannah vegetation (p<0.05, OR = 0.993) and elevation (p<0.05, OR = 0.997) were negatively correlated.
These three land cover classes and elevation are, thus, considered to be important determinants of tsetse presence and absence in the study area.
227.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports the autologistic (spatial) model results showing forest and riverine vegetation as positive predictors and savannah vegetation and elevation as negative predictors of tsetse presence, and states these factors are important determinants after accounting for spatial autocorrelation. Therefore the option is directly supported by the text.

227.Option: Temperature was the most influential factor in determining tsetse presence in the multivariate logistic regression model, with a strong positive association (p<0.05, OR = 2.63).
Evidence: Seven covariates were included in the multivariate logistic regression model. The significant covariates were; rainfall, elevation, temperature, cropland, savannah vegetation, forest, and riverine vegetation.
Cropland, temperature and rainfall failed to retain their significant association with tsetse presence (p>0.05) after accounting for spatial autocorrelation.
227.Rationale: Although temperature was included among covariates in the multivariate model, the passage states that temperature did not retain a significant association after applying the autologistic model (p>0.05). The specific OR = 2.63 is not reported in the passage. Thus the claim that temperature was the most influential factor with OR = 2.63 is not supported by the provided text.

227.Option: Rainfall and cropland were found to be positively associated with tsetse presence in the final autologistic regression model, indicating their importance in supporting tsetse populations.
Evidence: The significant covariates were; rainfall, elevation, temperature, cropland, savannah vegetation, forest, and riverine vegetation.
However, the covariates cropland, riverine vegetation, elevation and rainfall presented only very small positive associations, with wide confidence intervals.
Cropland, temperature and rainfall failed to retain their significant association with tsetse presence (p>0.05) after accounting for spatial autocorrelation.
227.Rationale: While rainfall and cropland showed small positive associations in the non-spatial multivariate model, the passage clearly states that cropland and rainfall did not retain significance (p>0.05) after accounting for spatial autocorrelation in the autologistic model. Therefore the claim that they were positively associated in the final autologistic model is not supported by the text.

227.Option: Savannah vegetation and elevation were positively associated with tsetse presence, as they provide suitable microclimatic conditions for the survival of G. f. fuscipes.
Evidence: Covariates; cropland, forest, riverine vegetation, woody vegetation, NDVI, elevation, temperature and rainfall were all positively correlated (p<0.05, Odds ratio (OR)>1), while savannah vegetation, herbaceous vegetation and built-up area were negatively correlated (p<0.05, OR<1) with tsetse presence.

In the autologistic model, forest (p<0.05, OR = 1.105) and riverine vegetation (p<0.05, OR = 1.008) were positively correlated with tsetse presence. Savannah vegetation (p<0.05, OR = 0.993) and elevation (p<0.05, OR = 0.997) were negatively correlated.
227.Rationale: The passage reports that savannah vegetation was negatively correlated with tsetse presence in both univariate and autologistic analyses, and that elevation was negatively correlated in the autologistic model. Thus the statement that savannah vegetation and elevation were positively associated is contradicted by the reported results and cannot be inferred from the passage.

228.Option: Groundwater iron concentration is a predominant schistosomiasis risk factor, with high iron levels in improved water sources associated with increased surface water use and higher transmission risk.
Evidence: The addition of WASH variables only slightly increased the R2 value from 0.15 to 0.17 and decreased the RMSE from 9.47 to 9.03.
However, iron concentration became by far the most important variable.

The importance of iron concentration was also evident in the predicted prevalence surfaces, with high values on the western side of the Atiwa Mountain Range (Fig 6) coinciding with high groundwater iron content (S21 Fig, Supporting Information).

Groundwater quality variables were included because prior studies [29,32,33] suggested that elevated iron and TDS concentrations in BHs may increase reliance on contaminated surface water bodies, thereby potentially serving as indirect risk factors for schistosomiasis transmission.
228.Rationale: The passage reports that when WASH variables were added to the model, iron concentration emerged as "by far the most important variable" and predicted high-prevalence areas coincided with high groundwater iron content. The authors also state the rationale for including groundwater iron: prior studies suggested elevated iron in boreholes (improved water sources) may increase reliance on contaminated surface water, serving as an indirect risk factor. Taken together, the passage supports the inference that groundwater iron concentration is a predominant risk factor and that high iron in improved sources can be linked (via increased surface water use) to higher transmission risk.

228.Option: The high predictive power of the final model, as indicated by R2 and RMSE values, suggests that environmental and WASH variables together can reliably forecast schistosomiasis prevalence at the community level.
Evidence: The R2 values for all models were relatively low (<0.20), indicating that environmental variables alone were not able to describe more than 15–20% of the variability in S. haematobium prevalence, regardless of RS data source or extraction mask.
The addition of WASH variables only slightly increased the R2 value from 0.15 to 0.17 and decreased the RMSE from 9.47 to 9.03.
228.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that model R2 values were relatively low (<0.20) and that adding WASH variables produced only a small increase in R2 (from 0.15 to 0.17) and a modest decrease in RMSE. These metrics do not indicate high predictive power or reliable community-level forecasting. Therefore the claim of high predictive power is not supported by the reported model performance.

228.Option: Improved water access, as measured by functional improved water sources (FIWS), is a significant predictor of reduced schistosomiasis risk in the study communities.
Evidence: FIWS and SWAP access indicators were not important in the final model.
The addition of WASH variables only slightly increased the R2 value from 0.15 to 0.17 and decreased the RMSE from 9.47 to 9.03.
228.Rationale: The passage directly reports that indicators of functional improved water sources (FIWS) were not important in the final model. Thus there is no support in the presented results for FIWS being a significant predictor of reduced schistosomiasis risk.

228.Option: The use of surface water access points (SWAPs) as a mask for environmental variable extraction produced the highest correlation between predicted and observed prevalence values, indicating that SWAPs are the most accurate proxy for schistosomiasis transmission risk.
Evidence: Models derived using the SWAP mask produced the highest correlation values using both Landsat 8 (r = 0.76, p < 0.01) and Sentinel-2 data (r = 0.67, p < 0.01) (Table 4).
it appears that the SWAP mask resulted in more precise prediction, including correct delineation of water bodies as high-risk locations (Fig 5, panel A6).

the most ecologically relevant method is to match school-based schistosomiasis prevalence with environmental variables extracted from points within the “community” where known contact with water bodies occurs (m6).
228.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence that models using the SWAP mask (m6), which corresponds to known surface water contact sites, produced the highest correlations between predicted and observed prevalence and yielded more precise spatial predictions. The authors also describe SWAP-based extraction as the most ecologically relevant approach for linking environmental predictors to transmission locations. Therefore it is reasonable to infer from the passage that SWAPs served as the most accurate proxy among the tested masks for transmission risk.

228.Option: Elevated iron concentration in groundwater is a direct cause of schistosomiasis infection, as it increases the viability of Schistosoma cercariae in water bodies.
Evidence: Groundwater quality variables were included because prior studies [29,32,33] suggested that elevated iron and TDS concentrations in BHs may increase reliance on contaminated surface water bodies, thereby potentially serving as indirect risk factors for schistosomiasis transmission.
However, iron concentration became by far the most important variable.
The addition of WASH variables only slightly increased the R2 value from 0.15 to 0.17 and decreased the RMSE from 9.47 to 9.03.
228.Rationale: While the passage documents an association between groundwater iron concentration and predicted schistosomiasis prevalence (iron became the most important variable), it does not provide any mechanistic evidence that iron directly increases cercarial viability. The text explicitly frames elevated iron as potentially leading to increased use of contaminated surface water (an indirect pathway), not as directly enhancing parasite survival. Moreover, the modest model fit indicates association rather than proof of direct causation. Therefore the claim that elevated iron in groundwater is a direct cause via increased cercarial viability is not supported by the passage.

229.Option: Urban residents in USVI are at higher risk of leptospirosis due to greater exposure to floodwater and standing water after hurricanes, which are known to harbor Leptospira spp.
Evidence: Leptospira spp. can survive in fresh water and wet soil for weeks to months in appropriate environmental conditions [7,8].
As demonstrated in 2017, outbreaks can occur after extreme weather events like hurricanes and floods [1,9–14].

Characteristics significantly associated with seropositivity by bivariate analysis included residing in an urban neighborhood (pRR: 3.0; 95% CI: 1.0–9.0, p = 0.03), fishing as an occupation (pRR: 9.2; 95% CI: 2.3–36.6; p<0.001), seeing rodents or evidence of rodents in or around the house or land (pRR: 2.6; 95% CI: 1.2–6.0; p = 0.01), ...

Characteristics and exposure factors independently associated with seropositivity by multivariate logistic regression included contact with cows or their body fluids (OR = 39.5, 95% CI = 9.0–172.7, p = <0.001); seeing rodents/evidence of rodents or contact with rodents or their body fluids (OR = 2.6, 95% CI = 1.1–5.9, p = 0.02); and increasing age (OR = 1.02, 95% CI = 1.002–1.04, p = 0.03).
229.Rationale: The passage does state that Leptospira can survive in fresh water and that outbreaks can follow hurricanes/floods, and it reports a bivariate association between urban residence and seropositivity. However, it does not provide evidence that urban residents have greater exposure to floodwater/standing water after hurricanes than non-urban residents, nor that this is the mechanism for the association. Importantly, urban residence did not remain an independent predictor in the multivariate analysis, which identified specific exposure factors (contact with cows, rodent contact, increasing age) as independently associated. Therefore, the specific causal interpretation that urban residents are at higher risk because they have greater post-hurricane floodwater exposure is not supported by the Passage Body and cannot be derived solely from the presented data.

229.Option: The association between urban residence and leptospirosis seropositivity suggests that urban environments in USVI may have higher levels of environmental contamination with Leptospira spp. due to increased rodent populations and poor infrastructure.
Evidence: Characteristics significantly associated with seropositivity by bivariate analysis included residing in an urban neighborhood (pRR: 3.0; 95% CI: 1.0–9.0, p = 0.03), ... seeing rodents or evidence of rodents in or around the house or land (pRR: 2.6; 95% CI: 1.2–6.0; p = 0.01), ... contact with rodents or their body fluids (pRR: 8.0; 95% CI: 1.9–34.1, p = 0.002), ... contact with cows or their body fluids (pRR: 14.0; 95% CI: 5.6–35.2; p<0.001), ...

Neighborhood type was subjectively assigned by interviewers as rural, urban, or suburban at the time of survey–using the following guidelines: urban if within a major city, suburban if somewhat populated and primarily residential, and rural if sparsely populated with unpaved roads.

Characteristics and exposure factors independently associated with seropositivity by multivariate logistic regression included contact with cows or their body fluids (OR = 39.5, 95% CI = 9.0–172.7, p = <0.001); seeing rodents/evidence of rodents or contact with rodents or their body fluids (OR = 2.6, 95% CI = 1.1–5.9, p = 0.02); and increasing age (OR = 1.02, 95% CI = 1.002–1.04, p = 0.03).
229.Rationale: While the passage links rodent contact and rodent presence to seropositivity, and shows a bivariate association between urban residence and seropositivity, it does not provide data demonstrating that urban environments in USVI have higher environmental contamination, larger rodent populations, or poorer infrastructure compared to non-urban areas. Moreover, neighborhood type was assigned subjectively, and urban residence did not persist as an independent predictor in the multivariate model. The specific causal statement attributing the urban association to increased rodent populations and poor infrastructure is not directly supported by the Passage Body and requires assumptions beyond the presented evidence.

229.Option: Urban residents in USVI are more likely to have been exposed to Leptospira spp. through contact with domestic animals such as cows and rodents, which are more prevalent in urban settings.
Evidence: Characteristics significantly associated with seropositivity by bivariate analysis included residing in an urban neighborhood (pRR: 3.0; 95% CI: 1.0–9.0, p = 0.03), ... seeing rodents or evidence of rodents in or around the house or land (pRR: 2.6; 95% CI: 1.2–6.0; p = 0.01), ... contact with rodents or their body fluids (pRR: 8.0; 95% CI: 1.9–34.1, p = 0.002), contact with cows or their body fluids (pRR: 14.0; 95% CI: 5.6–35.2; p<0.001), ...

Characteristics and exposure factors independently associated with seropositivity by multivariate logistic regression included contact with cows or their body fluids (OR = 39.5, 95% CI = 9.0–172.7, p = <0.001); seeing rodents/evidence of rodents or contact with rodents or their body fluids (OR = 2.6, 95% CI = 1.1–5.9, p = 0.02); and increasing age (OR = 1.02, 95% CI = 1.002–1.04, p = 0.03).

Neighborhood type was subjectively assigned by interviewers as rural, urban, or suburban at the time of survey–using the following guidelines: urban if within a major city, suburban if somewhat populated and primarily residential, and rural if sparsely populated with unpaved roads.
229.Rationale: The passage shows that contact with cows and rodents is strongly associated with seropositivity, and that there was a bivariate association between urban residence and seropositivity. However, it does not state that cows and rodents are more prevalent in urban settings in USVI; in fact, cows are typically associated with rural/agricultural settings, and the survey did not provide data on the distribution of these animals by community type. Additionally, urban residence did not remain significant in the multivariate model. Thus, the specific inference that urban residents are more likely exposed because these domestic animals are more prevalent in urban areas is not supported by the Passage Body.

229.Option: The finding of significantly increased seropositivity in urban areas should be interpreted cautiously as the classification of community type (rural, suburban, or urban) was determined subjectively by the survey team, and this association did not remain significant in multivariate logistic regression analysis.
Evidence: Neighborhood type was subjectively assigned by interviewers as rural, urban, or suburban at the time of survey–using the following guidelines: urban if within a major city, suburban if somewhat populated and primarily residential, and rural if sparsely populated with unpaved roads.
Characteristics significantly associated with seropositivity by bivariate analysis included residing in an urban neighborhood (pRR: 3.0; 95% CI: 1.0–9.0, p = 0.03), ...

Characteristics and exposure factors independently associated with seropositivity by multivariate logistic regression included contact with cows or their body fluids (OR = 39.5, 95% CI = 9.0–172.7, p = <0.001); seeing rodents/evidence of rodents or contact with rodents or their body fluids (OR = 2.6, 95% CI = 1.1–5.9, p = 0.02); and increasing age (OR = 1.02, 95% CI = 1.002–1.04, p = 0.03). Reporting being full or part Caucasian/White race was protective against seropositivity (OR = 0.2, 95% CI = 0.04–0.7, p = 0.02) by multivariate logistic regression.
229.Rationale: The Passage Body reports a significant bivariate association between urban residence and leptospirosis seropositivity, but it also documents that neighborhood type was subjectively assigned by interviewers. Crucially, urban residence is not among the factors that remained independently associated with seropositivity in multivariate logistic regression; instead, specific exposures (cow contact, rodent contact) and age were independent predictors. Epidemiologically, this combination of subjective classification and loss of significance after adjustment supports a cautious interpretation: the bivariate urban association may reflect confounding by specific exposures or misclassification, and cannot be taken as conclusive evidence of an independent urban effect.

229.Option: The higher seropositivity among urban residents reflects a true geographic disparity in leptospirosis risk, indicating that urban areas in USVI should be prioritized for targeted public health interventions.
Evidence: Characteristics significantly associated with seropositivity by bivariate analysis included residing in an urban neighborhood (pRR: 3.0; 95% CI: 1.0–9.0, p = 0.03), ...

Neighborhood type was subjectively assigned by interviewers as rural, urban, or suburban at the time of survey–using the following guidelines: urban if within a major city, suburban if somewhat populated and primarily residential, and rural if sparsely populated with unpaved roads.

Characteristics and exposure factors independently associated with seropositivity by multivariate logistic regression included contact with cows or their body fluids (OR = 39.5, 95% CI = 9.0–172.7, p = <0.001); seeing rodents/evidence of rodents or contact with rodents or their body fluids (OR = 2.6, 95% CI = 1.1–5.9, p = 0.02); and increasing age (OR = 1.02, 95% CI = 1.002–1.04, p = 0.03).
229.Rationale: Although there was a bivariate association between urban residence and seropositivity, the Passage Body indicates that neighborhood classification was subjective and that urban residence did not remain an independent predictor in multivariate analysis. The multivariate model points to specific exposure risks (cow and rodent contact) and age as independent factors. Therefore, concluding a true geographic disparity and prioritizing urban areas for interventions is not directly supported by the presented data without further investigation to rule out confounding, misclassification, or identification of the underlying exposure pathways driving the observed bivariate association.

230.Option: The real-time surveillance system demonstrated in this study relies on a centralized, paper-based reporting framework that is highly efficient and error-prone, as evidenced by the high completion rates and remote monitoring of data.
Evidence: we customized a data collection platform named Magpi that uses mobile phones and real-time cloud-based storage with global positioning system (GPS) coordinates and time stamping [15].
Paper forms were distributed as a backup alternative to mHealth data collection (e.g. in case of cell phone loss or failure).
the mHealth team completed a mean of 97.3% of surveys administered per week (29.2 ± 0.63 of 30 surveys assigned per mHealth reporter).
230.Rationale: The passage describes a digital, smartphone-based Magpi platform with cloud storage and GPS/time-stamping as the primary system; paper forms are explicitly described only as a backup. Thus the claim that the system "relies on a centralized, paper-based reporting framework" is contradicted by the text. While the study reports high completion rates and remote monitoring, these are features of the digital Magpi system rather than evidence that the system is paper-based.

230.Option: The use of GPS stamps and timestamped data in the Magpi system ensures that all health events are accurately geolocated and temporally precise, enabling perfect spatial and temporal mapping of disease outbreaks in real time.
Evidence: we customized a data collection platform named Magpi that uses mobile phones and real-time cloud-based storage with global positioning system (GPS) coordinates and time stamping [15].

it became readily apparent that many geotracers malfunctioned or, most likely, many remote areas had weak or no coverage of cellular communication, causing limitations in acquiring GPS stamps and compromising the ability to produce maps using GPS coordinates.
some reporters used hard-copy forms and entered the data in the Magpi app later. This last practice resulted in no data loss but large single entries (e.g., > 10 households) with only one GPS stamp.
230.Rationale: Although the platform supports GPS and timestamps, the passage explicitly reports geotracer malfunctions, weak/no cellular coverage, and instances where data were batch-entered resulting in single GPS stamps. These statements contradict the claim of universally accurate geolocation and perfect spatial/temporal mapping in real time.

230.Option: The use of minimally trained lay mHealth reporters with basic smartphones enabled real-time monitoring of neurological health data in remote rural communities, allowing for near-real-time identification of disease hotspots, medication shortages, injuries, and deaths with a delay of no more than 1–7 days.
Evidence: Eight male and female Ugandan lay mHealth reporters, age 20–30 years, were recruited and trained.

the survey, which was applied once weekly, included information on coded household location, number of NS children/household, seizure frequency, availability of anti-seizure medication, and injuries and deaths.

the mHealth reporters were asked first to complete and store the surveys locally on their mobile devices in the field, and subsequently, depending on WiFi availability, upload them daily or weekly to a secure web-hosted database.

With the map interface, it is possible to zoom in and observe the geographic distribution of NS health-related data in a particular village, and even in a specific household. For instance, the close-up in Fig 2 highlights those households with at least one child with NS that did not have anti-seizure medication (Fig 2E1, green color), was injured (Fig 2E2, green color) or died (Fig 2E3) that week.
The most common technical issues were frequent power cuts, poor network coverage, slow upload speeds and unreliable GPS satellites.

On the first day of September, all mobile phones ran out of data because the network provider failed to renew the monthly phone bundle on time... some reporters stopped working when they realized their data bundles were exhausted. Other reporters used hard-copy forms and entered the data in the Magpi app later.
230.Rationale: The passage supports that minimally trained lay reporters using Magpi could collect weekly data and that the platform could visualize households lacking medication or reporting injuries/deaths, indicating near-real-time monitoring potential. However, the assertion of a guaranteed delay of no more than 1–7 days is not supported: uploads depended on WiFi/coverage and frequent technical issues (power cuts, poor network, exhausted data bundles, batch entry) introduced delays that could exceed that window. Therefore the precise claim about a maximum 1–7 day delay cannot be reliably derived from the passage.

230.Option: The high survey completion rate of 97.3% indicates that the mHealth system is fully independent of network connectivity and can operate without any delays in data transmission, even in areas with poor infrastructure.
Evidence: the mHealth team completed a mean of 97.3% of surveys administered per week (29.2 ± 0.63 of 30 surveys assigned per mHealth reporter).

the mHealth reporters were asked first to complete and store the surveys locally on their mobile devices in the field, and subsequently, depending on WiFi availability, upload them daily or weekly to a secure web-hosted database.
The most common technical issues were frequent power cuts, poor network coverage, slow upload speeds and unreliable GPS satellites.

On the first day of September, all mobile phones ran out of data because the network provider failed to renew the monthly phone bundle on time... some reporters stopped working when they realized their data bundles were exhausted.
230.Rationale: Although survey completion rates were high, the passage explicitly describes dependence on network/WiFi and frequent connectivity and power problems that caused delays, batch uploads, and interruptions. High completion reflects reporter compliance and local data storage capabilities, not independence from network connectivity or guaranteed absence of transmission delays.

230.Option: The mHealth system's success in data collection and monitoring is primarily due to the high level of formal medical training and clinical expertise of the lay reporters, who were able to accurately diagnose and report neurological conditions without supervision.
Evidence: Eight male and female Ugandan lay mHealth reporters, age 20–30 years, were recruited and trained. The training covered basic concepts of health and disease, the purpose and content of the program, clinical and social aspects of NS, use and maintenance of the equipment (cell phones, solar batteries, bicycles), questionnaire presentation, data collection and submission, home and community entry, informed consent administration and patient confidentiality.
The study was not designed or approved to conduct or implement a medical intervention.
A medical diagnostician (DLK) physically examined a sample of households reporting existing and newly reported NS cases...

Physical examination of the sample of newly reported and existing NS cases confirmed a diagnosis of NS. On examination, children newly reported by mHealth workers to have NS were found to have longstanding disease. Only one new case of Suspected NS was found in the sample...
230.Rationale: The passage indicates lay reporters received basic training rather than advanced formal medical education. Clinical confirmation was performed by a medical diagnostician. The system's success is therefore attributable to the digital platform, training in data collection, supervision, and monitoring processes rather than to high levels of clinical expertise among lay reporters or their ability to perform independent clinical diagnosis.

231.Option: The high proportion of multibacillary (MB) leprosy cases (up to 74.3% in 2019) suggests that the current leprosy elimination strategies in Yunnan are failing to prevent the progression of early-stage disease to advanced forms.
Evidence: Among the new cases of leprosy detected, the percentage of multibacillary (MB) cases was relatively high in most of the years investigated, ranging from 59.0% in 2008 to 74.3% in 2019 (Table 1).
Since 1990, MDT has been used to treat leprosy in every county in Yunnan.
early detection increased from 53.9% in time period 1 to 60.3% in time period 2 (P <0.01).
The percentage of detected cases involving G2D decreased slightly from 22.6% (n = 1550) in time period 1 to 19.2% (n = 812) in time period 2 (P <0.01) (Table 3).

After the special fund for leprosy was established by the central government in 2004, the number of newly detected leprosy patients decreased further to 136 cases in 2019, a decrease of 60% in time period 2 (Table 1).
231.Rationale: The passage does report a high and increasing proportion of MB cases (quote 1). However, concluding that elimination strategies are failing to prevent progression is not directly supported. The passage also documents widespread use of MDT since 1990, increases in early detection, decreases in grade 2 disability rates, and large declines in new case counts after interventions and the 2004 fund (quotes 2–5). These facts indicate improvement in control and earlier diagnosis rather than definitive failure to prevent progression; multiple explanations (e.g., changes in detection patterns, case-mix, or surveillance) could account for the high MB proportion. Therefore the causal claim that strategies are failing to prevent progression is not derivable solely from the passage.

231.Option: The observed reduction in the number of newly detected leprosy cases after 2004 can be explained by socioeconomic development, improved access to health services, an effective surveillance system, better travel opportunities, and improved education for the population.
Evidence: After the special fund for leprosy was established by the central government in 2004, the number of newly detected leprosy patients decreased further to 136 cases in 2019, a decrease of 60% in time period 2 (Table 1).

The strategies and interventions implemented to promote early detection of leprosy include a leprosy elimination program, symptom surveillance, contact examination, focused survey, training of medical staff, health education, and financial investments to improve transport and reconstruct the dermatology department and leprosy village.

After many years of intensive effort, leprosy has been effectively controlled in China through the implementation of effective strategies and measures, including sustainable support and investment from the government, the establishment of an effective surveillance system, management of leprosy patients, and treatment of leprosy, first with dapsone (DDS) monotherapy and then, after 1990, with multidrug treatment (MDT).

The general goal of the NLEP is to “control the leprosy epidemic effectively, eliminate the harm of leprosy, protect people’s health and promote the harmonious development of the economy and society”.
231.Rationale: The passage links the post-2004 decline in newly detected cases temporally to the establishment of a central special fund and to implementation of multiple interventions (quote 1). It explicitly lists an effective surveillance system, health education, training of staff, and financial investments to improve transport and dermatology services (quotes 2–3), all of which correspond to improved access to services, surveillance, travel/transport, and education. While the passage does not present a formal causal attribution analysis, these programmatic elements and government investment are presented as the principal measures associated with the observed reduction, so the inference that the decline can be explained by those factors is supported by the passage.

231.Option: The continued presence of leprosy in 17 highly endemic counties indicates that multidrug treatment (MDT) has been ineffective in preventing transmission in remote, mountainous regions of Yunnan.
Evidence: Since 1990, MDT has been used to treat leprosy in every county in Yunnan.
By the end of 2019, 85.5% (100/117) of the leprosy-endemic counties had achieved the leprosy elimination target (Fig 3). Currently, 17/117 (14.5%) of the counties remain leprosy-endemic regions.
231.Rationale: The passage documents both long-term, province-wide use of MDT and that 17 counties remain endemic (quotes 1–2). However, asserting that MDT has been ineffective at preventing transmission in remote regions is a causal judgement not directly supported. The persistence of endemicity in some counties could reflect access barriers, delayed diagnosis, social determinants, or programmatic challenges rather than MDT biological ineffectiveness. The passage does not provide evidence that MDT failed biologically to prevent transmission in those areas; therefore the strong causal statement is not derivable from the provided information.

231.Option: The increase in early detection rates from 53.9% to 60.3% between time periods 1 and 2 suggests that contact examination and health education have been the primary drivers of the reduction in leprosy burden in Yunnan.
Evidence: early detection increased from 53.9% in time period 1 to 60.3% in time period 2 (P <0.01).

The strategies and interventions implemented to promote early detection of leprosy include a leprosy elimination program, symptom surveillance, contact examination, focused survey, training of medical staff, health education, and financial investments to improve transport and reconstruct the dermatology department and leprosy village.

After the special fund for leprosy was established by the central government in 2004, the number of newly detected leprosy patients decreased further to 136 cases in 2019, a decrease of 60% in time period 2 (Table 1).
231.Rationale: The passage shows early detection improved and lists contact examination and health education among several interventions introduced (quotes 1–2), and documents declines in cases after program investments (quote 3). However, the passage does not provide evidence that contact examination and health education were the primary (i.e., main) drivers of the reduction versus other factors such as MDT, financial investment, surveillance strengthening, or broader programmatic actions. The data do not quantify the relative contribution of specific interventions, so attributing primary causality to contact examination and health education is not supported solely by the passage.

231.Option: The substantial decline in leprosy case detection and the reduction in leprosy-endemic counties in Yunnan Province over the past 30 years can be attributed to the implementation of multidrug treatment (MDT), the establishment of a special fund for leprosy, and the development of a comprehensive surveillance system.
Evidence: With the introduction of MDT, the number of newly detected leprosy patients declined significantly.
In 2004, a special fund was established by the central government to combat leprosy in the region. The amount given from 2004 to 2019 was 150 million CNY (US$25 million).

After the special fund for leprosy was established by the central government in 2004, the number of newly detected leprosy patients decreased further to 136 cases in 2019, a decrease of 60% in time period 2 (Table 1).

After many years of intensive effort, leprosy has been effectively controlled in China through the implementation of effective strategies and measures, including sustainable support and investment from the government, the establishment of an effective surveillance system, management of leprosy patients, and treatment of leprosy, first with dapsone (DDS) monotherapy and then, after 1990, with multidrug treatment (MDT).

By the end of 2003, 39% (46/117) of the leprosy-endemic counties had achieved the leprosy elimination target. By the end of 2019, 85.5% (100/117) of the leprosy-endemic counties had achieved the leprosy elimination target (Fig 3).
231.Rationale: The passage explicitly links the introduction of MDT to significant declines in newly detected cases and documents a further marked decline after establishment of the 2004 special fund (quotes 1–3). It also states that effective control has been achieved through government support, surveillance, management, and MDT (quote 4), and provides data showing a large increase in counties meeting the elimination target between 2003 and 2019 (quote 5). These statements together support the inference that MDT implementation, the special fund, and improved surveillance and programmatic support contributed to the long-term reduction in leprosy burden in Yunnan.

232.Option: The high proportion of rabies cases in children under 15 years of age suggests that the current surveillance system is more effective at detecting cases in younger populations.
Evidence: Table 1 shows that, of the 14 cases of human rabies, 12 were children below 15 years of age and 8 were male.
Remarkably, none of the 12 cases of rabies identified from April to July were notified through the routine surveillance system.
Rabies was not suspected and for this reason, they were not initially recorded as rabies.
232.Rationale: The passage does report a high proportion of cases in children (<15 years), but it also states that none of the identified cases were reported through routine surveillance and that rabies was not suspected. Those facts contradict the inference that the surveillance system is more effective at detecting younger cases. There is no evidence in the passage that surveillance preferentially detects children; instead the data indicate under-detection across the board.

232.Option: The absence of rabies cases in the routine surveillance system during the outbreak indicates that the surveillance system is functioning effectively by excluding false positives.
Evidence: Remarkably, none of the 12 cases of rabies identified from April to July were notified through the routine surveillance system.
Rabies was not suspected and for this reason, they were not initially recorded as rabies.
Only 72 (8.7%) of animal bite victims had an animal bite investigation form filled out and the majority of these forms were incomplete.
232.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that known rabies cases were not notified by routine surveillance. That absence reflects failure to capture true cases (under-reporting), not successful exclusion of false positives. Additional evidence of incomplete record-keeping at Prophylaxis Centers further undermines the claim that the system is functioning effectively; the passage gives no support for the idea that the surveillance absence indicates appropriate exclusion of false positives.

232.Option: The poor quality of records at Prophylaxis Centers is the primary reason for the under-reporting of rabies cases in Mozambique.
Evidence: Only 72 (8.7%) of animal bite victims had an animal bite investigation form filled out and the majority of these forms were incomplete.
Remarkably, none of the 12 cases of rabies identified from April to July were notified through the routine surveillance system.
Rabies was not suspected and for this reason, they were not initially recorded as rabies.
In Maputo City, of ten victims who attended health centers, nine were given only tetanus vaccine and parents were told that it was sufficient. They were not referred to the Prophylaxis Center.
232.Rationale: While the passage documents poor record quality at Prophylaxis Centers (low completion and incomplete forms), it also provides other clear contributors to under-reporting: clinical failure to suspect/diagnose rabies and failures in clinical management/referral. The passage does not provide evidence that poor records are the primary cause; rather, it describes multiple factors that together explain under-reporting. Therefore the claim that poor records are the primary reason is not supported by the text.

232.Option: The failure to diagnose rabies in clinical settings, combined with a lack of clinical awareness among health professionals, contributes to the under-reporting of rabies cases in Mozambique.
Evidence: Rabies was not suspected and for this reason, they were not initially recorded as rabies.
Diagnosis was based on a combination of clinical findings and epidemiological history, without laboratory confirmation.

In Matola, one victim attended a health center and received only tetanus vaccine and the parents were informed that it was sufficient. Three victims attended the Prophylaxis Center in Matola and the parents were told that there was no vaccine available. In Maputo City, of ten victims who attended health centers, nine were given only tetanus vaccine and parents were told that it was sufficient. They were not referred to the Prophylaxis Center. The one case who was referred was only given two doses of vaccine.
232.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that rabies was not suspected in some admitted patients and therefore not recorded as rabies. It also documents that health centers frequently provided only tetanus vaccine and failed to refer bite victims for appropriate post-exposure prophylaxis, indicating gaps in clinical recognition and management. Together these statements support the epidemiological inference that diagnostic failure and limited clinical awareness contribute to under-reporting.

232.Option: None of the cases of human rabies that occurred during the outbreak were notified through the routine surveillance system, showing that rabies is seriously under-reported.
Evidence: We detected a total of 12 cases of fatal rabies admitted to Maputo Central Hospital during the period between April and July 2014.
Remarkably, none of the 12 cases of rabies identified from April to July were notified through the routine surveillance system.
232.Rationale: The passage directly reports that 12 confirmed fatal rabies cases were identified during the investigation and that none of these were notified via routine surveillance. From an epidemiological perspective, failure to notify known cases to the surveillance system is clear evidence of significant under-reporting. The passage supports both the factual statement and the inference about serious under-reporting.

233.Option: The failure to sustain a Th17 response beyond 14 days in the murine model indicates that this subset is not essential for long-term fungal control, as its decline coincides with disease remission.
Evidence: The numbers of Th17 cells (CD4hi IL-17+) rose sharply during the early stages of the disease (until 14 days after infection) and fell gradually during later time points in both footpad (Fig 1D and S3B Fig) and dLN (Fig 1D and S4B Fig).
The numbers of Th1 (CD4hi IFN-γ+) cells in the footpad increased steadily over 21 days post-infection and then decreased (Fig 1C and S3C Fig)...

Taking into consideration that this is a self-healing animal model of CBM, these results indicate that the increase in the Th17 population in the infection site during the first 14 days, followed by Th1 population increase, closely assisted by a growing Treg population until disease resolution, may represent the ideal balance of these populations when aiming at CBM remission.
Neutralization of IL-17 highly increased CFUs at day 7 post-infection (Fig 3D).

These data suggest that Th17 cells might contribute to fungal elimination at the early stages of infection, whereas Th1 cells might be needed during the early and later stages of infection to eliminate the fungal pathogen.
233.Rationale: The passage documents a sharp early Th17 peak (until day 14) that then declines while Th1 increases and the disease resolves. It explicitly states Th17 contributes to early fungal elimination and that Th1 is required during later stages. Thus, from the provided experimental findings one can infer that sustaining a Th17 response beyond 14 days is not required for long-term control because clearance/remission is associated with the later Th1-dominated phase (i.e., long-term control aligns with Th1/Treg dynamics rather than prolonged Th17 presence). The evidence that IL-17 neutralization impairs early control supports that Th17 is important early but the overall temporal pattern shows remission occurs after Th17 declines and Th1/Treg rise.

233.Option: The initial Th17 response in the footpad is primarily responsible for the recruitment of neutrophils, which are essential for the early elimination of fungal conidia and hyphae, while Th1 cells become critical later for clearing muriform cells.
Evidence: The numbers of Th17 cells (CD4hi IL-17+) rose sharply during the early stages of the disease (until 14 days after infection) and fell gradually during later time points in both footpad (Fig 1D and S3B Fig) and dLN (Fig 1D and S4B Fig).
Neutralization of IL-17 highly increased CFUs at day 7 post-infection (Fig 3D).
B3Z / BWZ cells were stimulated in a 96-well plate with F. pedrosoi conidia, fragments of hyphae and muriform cells at multiplicity of infection (MOI) of 1.
All three forms of F. pedrosoi stimulated Dectin-2 and to a lesser extent Dectin-1 signaling.
233.Rationale: While the passage shows an early Th17 response and that IL-17 contributes to early fungal control, it does not state anything about neutrophil recruitment or directly link Th17 to neutrophil-mediated clearance. Although morphotypes (conidia, hyphae, muriform cells) and CLR recognition are described, there is no experimental evidence in the passage that Th17 cells recruit neutrophils or that neutrophils are essential for elimination of conidia/hyphae, nor is there evidence that Th1 specifically clears muriform cells. Because the option asserts specific mechanisms (neutrophil recruitment and morphotype-specific roles) that are not provided in the passage, this inference is not supported by the presented data.

233.Option: The coordinated action of CD4+ T lymphocyte subpopulations, with initial Th17 polarization followed by Th1 dominance and Treg involvement, is essential for effective fungal clearance and disease remission in experimental chromoblastomycosis.
Evidence: The numbers of Th17 cells (CD4hi IL-17+) rose sharply during the early stages of the disease (until 14 days after infection) and fell gradually during later time points in both footpad (Fig 1D and S3B Fig) and dLN (Fig 1D and S4B Fig).
The numbers of Th1 (CD4hi IFN-γ+) cells in the footpad increased steadily over 21 days post-infection and then decreased (Fig 1C and S3C Fig)...
Foxp3+ Treg cells also expand during the infection, being more numerous in dLN (Fig 1G).

Taking into consideration that this is a self-healing animal model of CBM, these results indicate that the increase in the Th17 population in the infection site during the first 14 days, followed by Th1 population increase, closely assisted by a growing Treg population until disease resolution, may represent the ideal balance of these populations when aiming at CBM remission.

In vivo depletion of CD25+ cells leads to Treg cells’ impairment in draining lymph nodes (dLN), reflected by a reduction in fungal burden after 14 days of infection. Furthermore, fungal cells were not cleared in later stages of the disease, prolonging CBM clinical features in those animals.
Neutralization of IL-17A and IFN-γ neutralization hindered fungal cell elimination in the course of the disease.
233.Rationale: The passage provides direct experimental evidence that Th17 cells expand early, Th1 cells expand later, and Treg cells increase during infection up to resolution. Functional perturbations support the necessity of these subsets: IL-17 and IFN-γ neutralization impaired fungal elimination, and Treg depletion altered fungal burden dynamics and delayed clearance. The authors explicitly interpret the temporal sequence (Th17 → Th1 with Treg assistance) as representing an 'ideal balance' for remission in this self-healing model. Therefore the coordinated action of these CD4+ subsets is supported as essential for effective clearance and remission in the experimental model.

233.Option: The chronic inflammatory process observed in human CBM patients may be due to an unbalanced immune response that fails to transition from Th17 to Th1 dominance, resulting in persistent fungal burden and inadequate regulation by Treg cells.
Evidence: in situ studies on CBM patients, as well as in experimental models, show a correlation between Th2 response and disease severity, together with a high fungal load. The milder form of the disease is related to a Th1 profile, with high IFN-γ production, lower levels of IL-10, and lower fungal burden.
An unbalanced response may favor the persistence of pathogens in infected tissues, worsening the patient’s clinical condition.

Taking into consideration that this is a self-healing animal model of CBM, these results indicate that the increase in the Th17 population in the infection site during the first 14 days, followed by Th1 population increase, closely assisted by a growing Treg population until disease resolution, may represent the ideal balance of these populations when aiming at CBM remission.
233.Rationale: The passage states that unbalanced immune responses can favor pathogen persistence and cites associations in patients between Th2 responses and worse disease, and that in the mouse model a Th17→Th1/Treg sequence aligns with remission. However, the specific claim about human chronic inflammation being due to failure to transition from Th17 to Th1 dominance (with inadequate Treg regulation) is an extrapolation beyond the direct experimental data. The experimental results demonstrating the transition and its importance derive from a murine self-healing model; while the authors note that unbalanced responses may favor persistence and reference patient observations related to Th2/Th1, they do not present direct evidence from human CBM that a failed Th17→Th1 transition underlies chronic inflammation. Therefore this specific inference about human chronic CBM is not directly supported by the Passage Body.

234.Option: The identification of genotypes previously described in animals suggests potential zoonotic and anthroponotic transmission of Enterocytozoon bieneusi in Mozambique.
Evidence: Enterocytozoon bieneusi has a high genetic diversity and is capable of colonizing/infecting a broad spectrum of mammal and avian species.
Groups 1 and 2 comprise genotypes able to infect a broad range of mammalian species including humans and are, therefore, considered potentially zoonotic.
Nucleotide sequences of the ITS revealed the presence of four distinct E. bieneusi genotypes, three previously reported in humans (Type IV, S2, and Peru11) and a novel genotype (named HhMzEb1).

Phylogenetic analysis revealed that the novel genotype HhMzEB1 clustered within Group 1 when its relationship with other genotypes of human and animal origin previously reported in Africa was evaluated (Fig 1).
234.Rationale: The passage states that E. bieneusi infects a broad spectrum of mammal and avian species and that Group 1 genotypes (and Group 2) are capable of infecting a broad range of mammalian species including humans and are considered potentially zoonotic. The study identified genotypes already reported in humans and a novel genotype that clusters within Group 1 (a group with zoonotic potential). Together, these facts support the epidemiological inference that transmission in Mozambique may include zoonotic (animal-to-human) and anthroponotic (human-to-human) pathways rather than being restricted to a single host-to-host route.

234.Option: The detection of E. bieneusi genotypes in both rural and urban settings suggests that transmission is primarily driven by environmental contamination rather than animal contact.
Evidence: In Zambézia, 807 asymptomatic schoolchildren and 290 children presenting with gastrointestinal complaints seeking for medical care were investigated in 10 and six districts of the province, respectively (Table 2 and S1 and S2 Tables).
Three out of four children investigated lived in rural areas.
In the rural Manhiça district, 109 asymptomatic children and 41 children presenting with gastrointestinal complaints ... were investigated (Table 2 and S3 and S4 Tables).
All these children lived in rural areas.

Infections are acquired through ingestion of food and water contaminated with E. bieneusi spores, or through direct contact with faeces of infected persons and animals or with contaminated soils [2,12,13].
234.Rationale: The passage documents that the infected children were from rural settings (explicitly noting that 'All these children lived in rural areas' and that three out of four children investigated lived in rural areas). It does not present data showing detection in urban settings. While the passage lists environmental transmission routes (contaminated food/water/soil), there is no evidence in the study that transmission is primarily driven by environmental contamination rather than animal contact. Therefore the specific inference that transmission is primarily environmentally driven is not supported by the data presented.

234.Option: The high genetic diversity of E. bieneusi in Mozambique, as evidenced by the identification of a novel genotype, suggests that the parasite is undergoing rapid evolution in this region.
Evidence: Enterocytozoon bieneusi has a high genetic diversity and is capable of colonizing/infecting a broad spectrum of mammal and avian species.
Based on ITS nucleotide sequences nearly 500 genotypes have been validated according to current nomenclature standards that are distributed into 11 distinct phylogenetic groups [10,14].
Nucleotide sequences of the ITS revealed the presence of four distinct E. bieneusi genotypes, three previously reported in humans (Type IV, S2, and Peru11) and a novel genotype (named HhMzEb1).
234.Rationale: While the passage documents that E. bieneusi overall has high genetic diversity globally and that this study discovered a novel genotype, detecting a novel genotype in a cross-sectional survey does not by itself demonstrate rapid evolution occurring specifically in Mozambique. The data show presence of diverse genotypes and one novel type, but there are no temporal data, mutation rates, or comparative analyses presented that would support an inference of rapid local evolution. Thus the claim of rapid evolution is not derivable from the presented evidence.

234.Option: The presence of multiple E. bieneusi genotypes in both symptomatic and asymptomatic children indicates that clinical symptoms are not reliably associated with specific genotypes.
Evidence: In the Zambézia province, E. bieneusi was found in 3 asymptomatic children.
In Maputo province, E. bieneusi was found in six male children, one presenting with moderate-to-severe diarrhoea and 5 asymptomatic.
Nucleotide sequences of the ITS revealed the presence of four distinct E. bieneusi genotypes, three previously reported in humans (Type IV, S2, and Peru11) and a novel genotype (named HhMzEb1).

Among the 4 boys infected with the novel genotype, HhMzEb1, one was HIV positive, presented with moderate-to-severe diarrhoea, and was co-infected with Cryptosporidium meleagridis. Another boy with this genotype was co-infected with C. parvum, and a third one was co-infected with Rotavirus.
234.Rationale: The study detected multiple genotypes across children with and without symptoms: in Zambézia all positive children were asymptomatic, whereas in Maputo there was one symptomatic and five asymptomatic positive children. The same genotypes (including the novel HhMzEb1) were found in asymptomatic and symptomatic individuals. This pattern supports the inference that clinical presentation (symptomatic vs asymptomatic) is not consistently linked to a particular E. bieneusi genotype in these data. Note that small numbers limit confidence, but the passage provides direct evidence that specific genotypes were present in both clinical states.

234.Option: The fact that all identified genotypes belong to Group 1 implies that E. bieneusi in Mozambique is primarily transmitted through direct human-to-human contact.
Evidence: Groups 1 and 2 comprise genotypes able to infect a broad range of mammalian species including humans and are, therefore, considered potentially zoonotic.
Nucleotide sequences of the ITS revealed the presence of four distinct E. bieneusi genotypes, three previously reported in humans (Type IV, S2, and Peru11) and a novel genotype (named HhMzEb1).

Phylogenetic analysis revealed that the novel genotype HhMzEB1 clustered within Group 1 when its relationship with other genotypes of human and animal origin previously reported in Africa was evaluated (Fig 1).
234.Rationale: The passage does not state that all identified genotypes belong to Group 1; it explicitly notes only that the novel genotype clustered within Group 1. Moreover, Group 1 genotypes are described as having broad mammalian host ranges and being potentially zoonotic—not as indicating primarily human-to-human transmission. Thus inferring that Group 1 membership implies transmission is primarily direct human-to-human contact is not supported by the text, which instead emphasizes zoonotic potential and multiple transmission routes (ingestion of contaminated food/water, contact with feces, contaminated soils).

235.Option: The successful completion of five rounds of MDA in Sri Lanka, combined with high MDA compliance rates, ensures that transmission assessment surveys are sufficient to confirm the interruption of lymphatic filariasis transmission.
Evidence: TAS results may be useful for deciding whether MDA can be stopped, TAS cannot guarantee that LF transmission has been interrupted in evaluation units (EUs), which are typically districts with populations that may exceed 1 million.
Thus all EUs "passed" TAS including the coastal Galle District EU, where high rates for filariasis markers were noted in two PHI study areas.
Filarial DNA rates exceeded the target of 0.25% in 10 of 19 PHIs.
235.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that TAS "cannot guarantee that LF transmission has been interrupted" and provides an empirical example where all EUs "passed" TAS while high filariasis marker rates were observed in smaller PHI areas (e.g., Galle).
