The detection of filarial DNA in mosquitoes in many PHIs further contradicts the claim that passing TAS is sufficient to confirm interruption. Therefore the option's claim that TAS are sufficient is not supported by the passage.

235.Option: The high rates of antifilarial antibodies in primary school children across most PHIs suggest that transmission assessment surveys are highly sensitive for detecting ongoing filariasis transmission in endemic areas.
Evidence: Antifilarial antibodies were detected in primary school children in 17 of 19 PHIs. Antibody rates exceeded the target rate of 2% in 10 of 19 PHIs;

More than 17,000 primary grade school children were tested in TAS in 337 schools located in 11 EUs in 8 districts and in Colombo town (Table 5). The numbers of positive CFA tests were well below the TAS threshold level of 18 (critical cut-off value) in all EUs. Thus all EUs “passed” TAS including the coastal Galle District EU, where high rates for filariasis markers were noted in two PHI study areas.
235.Rationale: The passage shows that antibody testing detected elevated rates in many PHIs, while TAS (which tests antigenemia) found very few positives and all EUs passed. This indicates that antibody testing identified evidence of exposure not captured by TAS, implying TAS are not highly sensitive for all signals of ongoing transmission. The evidence thus contradicts the option's assertion that TAS are highly sensitive based on the antibody findings.

235.Option: The fact that none of the children with positive CFA tests in TAS surveys had microfilaremia indicates that transmission assessment surveys are reliable indicators of the absence of active filarial transmission.
Evidence: None of the 16 children with positive CFA tests in TAS surveys had microfilaremia.
Filarial DNA rates exceeded the target of 0.25% in 10 of 19 PHIs.
Sixteen of 65 CFA-positive subjects (age range 23–70 yr) were positive for Mf (mean count 14 per 60 µl range 1–51), and 68% of Mf carriers were males.
235.Rationale: While TAS found that none of the CFA-positive children had microfilaremia, the passage also reports persistent evidence of infection in the wider community and vectors: filarial DNA was detected in mosquitoes in many PHIs and some adults were microfilaremic. Thus the absence of microfilaremia among the small number of CFA-positive children in TAS does not demonstrate that active transmission is absent; TAS missed other indicators of ongoing transmission. Therefore the inference that TAS reliably indicate absence of active transmission is not supported by the passage.

235.Option: The detection of filarial DNA in mosquitoes in some PHIs suggests that transmission assessment surveys are overly conservative and should be abandoned in favor of molecular xenodiagnosis for all post-MDA evaluations.
Evidence: Filarial DNA rates exceeded the target of 0.25% in 10 of 19 PHIs.
The present study provided an opportunity to gain further experience with these parameters in the post-MDA setting.

TAS results may be useful for deciding whether MDA can be stopped, TAS cannot guarantee that LF transmission has been interrupted in evaluation units (EUs), which are typically districts with populations that may exceed 1 million.
235.Rationale: The passage documents that molecular xenodiagnosis (MX) detected filarial DNA in mosquitoes and that the study aimed to assess the relative value of different methods. However, it does not state that TAS are "overly conservative" nor does it recommend abandoning TAS in favor of MX for all evaluations. Instead, the narrative describes MX as a complementary, more intensive surveillance method for detecting low-level persistence. The prescriptive claim to abandon TAS is not supported by the passage's evidence or stated aims.

235.Option: While transmission assessment surveys may be useful for decisions regarding stopping mass drug administration, they are not sufficient to show that lymphatic filariasis transmission has been interrupted, as evidenced by the detection of filarial DNA in mosquitoes and persistent antigenemia in adults in areas that passed TAS.
Evidence: TAS results may be useful for deciding whether MDA can be stopped, TAS cannot guarantee that LF transmission has been interrupted in evaluation units (EUs), which are typically districts with populations that may exceed 1 million.
Thus all EUs "passed" TAS including the coastal Galle District EU, where high rates for filariasis markers were noted in two PHI study areas.
Filarial DNA rates exceeded the target of 0.25% in 10 of 19 PHIs.
Sixteen of 65 CFA-positive subjects (age range 23–70 yr) were positive for Mf (mean count 14 per 60 µl range 1–51), and 68% of Mf carriers were males.
235.Rationale: This option reflects the passage's explicit statements and empirical findings. The authors note that TAS can inform decisions about stopping MDA but "cannot guarantee" interruption of transmission. Empirically, all EUs passed TAS while subsequent, more intensive PHI-level surveillance and molecular xenomonitoring found filarial DNA in mosquitoes and persistent antigenemia/microfilaremia among adults in some areas (including Galle). These findings support the inference that TAS alone are not sufficient to demonstrate interruption of transmission.

236.Option: The integration of mHealth tools with community health volunteers enables the collection of high-quality photographic data for therapeutic response assessment, which is as accurate as in-person evaluation and supports the scalability of digital health interventions in rural settings.
Evidence: This evaluation strategy has been found to be sensitive and specific, relative to in-person assessment [12].
The Guaral +ST app for Android is designed to monitor cutaneous leishmaniasis treatment and assess therapeutic response [11].
The app allowed CHV to monitor the clinical characteristics and evolution of the lesions, and to take and transmit photographs.

mHealth strategies have shown potential to improve clinical management and epidemiological surveillance of skin neglected tropical diseases [5–10] and other infectious diseases in resource limited settings.
The use of mHealth tools by health volunteers offers the opportunity to overcome these barriers.
236.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the photographic evaluation strategy implemented in the app "has been found to be sensitive and specific, relative to in-person assessment," and that the app enabled CHV to take and transmit lesion photographs and to monitor lesion evolution. The authors also describe mHealth strategies as having potential to improve clinical management in resource-limited settings and note that use of mHealth by health volunteers "offers the opportunity to overcome" access barriers in the remote study area. These statements together support the inference that integrating mHealth with CHV enables collection of photographic data adequate for therapeutic response assessment and that this approach is feasible and supportive of scaling digital health interventions in rural settings.

236.Option: The use of a mobile health app by community health volunteers significantly improved the proportion of patients monitored at target study week 26 compared to standard institution-based follow-up, despite challenges related to connectivity and security.
Evidence: Among these, 26/49 (53.1%) were evaluated by active follow-up at this time in the intervention arm, and none (0/25, 0%) of those in the randomized control arm who, according to the standard-of-care were instructed to return for evaluation at week 13 and 26 (difference = 53.1%, 95% confidence interval 39.1–67.0%, p<0.001).
travel to the town of Tumaco, where the health centers are located, may take up to 8 hours, which restricts access to health services.
Armed conflict has contributed to internal population displacement.
Due to a technical error, the exact dates of visits were not retained in the app dataset, but rather only as the target study week.
236.Rationale: The passage clearly supports the first part of the statement: use of the app by CHV materially increased the proportion of patients followed at target study week 26 (53.1% vs 0%, p<0.001). However, the latter clause – that this improvement occurred "despite challenges related to connectivity and security" – is not fully supported by the text. The passage documents remoteness (travel up to 8 hours) and mentions that armed conflict has contributed to displacement (implying security concerns), and it notes a "technical error" in the app dataset, but it does not explicitly describe problems with network connectivity or directly state that security/connectivity challenges were present and overcome. Because the specific claim about connectivity and security challenges is not documented in the Passage Body, the option overstates what can be derived.

236.Option: The mHealth intervention significantly reduced the burden on health facilities by shifting the responsibility for treatment monitoring to community health volunteers, thereby improving access to care in remote areas.
Evidence: In the case of patients who received any part of their treatment at the CIDEIM health facility in the town of Tumaco, monitoring was carried out by CHV, liaising with the health facility.
The app allowed CHV to monitor the clinical characteristics and evolution of the lesions, and to take and transmit photographs.
The use of mHealth tools by health volunteers offers the opportunity to overcome these barriers.

The virtual absence of information on response to treatment for the two control groups after completion of treatment, precluded evaluation of the influence of active follow-up monitoring on management of adverse events or the outcome of treatment.
236.Rationale: The Passage Body documents that CHV using the app carried out monitoring and that mHealth can overcome access barriers, which supports that monitoring activity can be shifted into the community. However, the assertion that the intervention "significantly reduced the burden on health facilities" is not directly measured or reported in the passage. The authors explicitly state that the lack of data from control groups "precluded evaluation of the influence of active follow-up monitoring on management of adverse events or the outcome of treatment," and no metrics of facility burden (e.g., visit counts, staffing, resource use) are provided. Therefore the causal claim that facility burden was reduced cannot be derived from the Passage Body.

236.Option: Despite the challenges of poor connectivity and mobility, the mHealth intervention led to a complete resolution of treatment non-adherence in the intervention arm, enabling full therapeutic monitoring for all patients.
Evidence: the average number of ampoules administered, as a percentage of the number prescribed, was 88% or higher in each of the three groups. There was no statistically significant difference in the compliance with the prescribed treatment between the randomized arms (Table 2).

Among these, 26/49 (53.1%) were evaluated by active follow-up at this time in the intervention arm, and none (0/25, 0%) of those in the randomized control arm ... (difference = 53.1%, 95% confidence interval 39.1–67.0%, p<0.001).
Due to a technical error, the exact dates of visits were not retained in the app dataset, but rather only as the target study week.

The virtual absence of information on response to treatment for the two control groups after completion of treatment, precluded evaluation of the influence of active follow-up monitoring on management of adverse events or the outcome of treatment.
236.Rationale: The passage does not support the claim of a "complete resolution of treatment non-adherence" or "full therapeutic monitoring for all patients." Adherence (measured as percent of prescribed ampoules administered) averaged about 88% or higher across groups, with no statistically significant difference between arms, so there was not a demonstrated elimination of non-adherence. Moreover, only approximately half (53.1%) of participants in the intervention arm were evaluated at week 26, so monitoring was not complete for all patients. The passage also does not document poor connectivity explicitly; it notes a technical error in the app dataset but provides no evidence that the intervention overcame connectivity or mobility problems to achieve complete adherence or universal monitoring. Consequently this option overstates what can be inferred.

237.Option: The spatial clustering of high household density and lower SES in coastal Kenya is primarily driven by proximity to main roads and aquatic habitats favorable for Anopheles mosquitoes and snails.
Evidence: Results based on the Gi*(d) test showed that high density households and lower income were clustered in all villages (Figure 5).
Clusters of high household density were often located near the main road (Figure 5, Figure S1 in Text S1).
In contrast, people with lower SES were usually clustered away from the main roads (Figure 5, Figure S1 in Text S1).
Low-income houses were clustered closer to sites that were suitable for snail hosts of S. haematobium (Figure 5 and Figure S1 in Text S1).
An. funestus was significantly more abundant in Jego and Magodzoni, and An. gambiae was more abundant only in Jego (Table 2, Wilcoxon test, p<0.05; Fisher's LSD, p<0.05).
237.Rationale: The passage describes spatial clustering of high household density and of low SES and notes that high household density clusters were often near main roads while low-SES houses were usually clustered away from main roads and closer to sites suitable for snail hosts. These mixed spatial relationships show that proximity to main roads and aquatic habitats are associated with different cluster types, not that clustering is 'primarily driven' by those factors. The statement that clustering is 'primarily driven' by proximity to main roads and aquatic habitats therefore overstates what the data demonstrate and is not directly supported by the quoted text.

237.Option: The challenge of polyparasitism is increasingly recognized through i) our increasing appreciation of the health and social burden of chronic/recurrent infections, ii) more sensitive diagnostics, which indicate that concurrent polyparasitism is much more prevalent than previously thought, and iii) new inexpensive approaches to treatment and transmission control that are increasingly more accessible.
Evidence: Because of their combined long-term effects, these infections appear to play a significant but, as yet, incompletely defined synergistic role in the causation of chronic clinical and sub-clinical human disease and poverty [6], [7], [8].

Although 'wormy villages' have been described empirically in the past [23], new advances in diagnostic technology have increased test sensitivity and specificity for these parasites, revealing that in endemic areas, chronic parasitic infection with Schistosoma spp. [24], Plasmodia spp. [25], and/or filaria [26] are much more common than previously thought.

In holoendemic areas such as coastal Kenya or Papua New Guinea, malaria prevalence, as detected by PCR is 60–75%, more than double the previous estimates of 20–33% by blood smear microscopy, with ≥10% carrying two or more malaria species [25], [27].
These findings indicate the need to carefully re-evaluate the risk of infection and parasite-related morbidity in exposed populations.
In the era of integrated parasite control programs, it is important to define those factors that enhance risk of co-infection.

By combining remotely sensed and directly measured environmental factors with new aspects of social geography, along with implementation of new diagnostics methods and the use of advanced statistical tools, our analysis provides new insights into polyparasitism that can inform the design and application of more effective, population-based control strategies.
237.Rationale: The passage explicitly states an increased appreciation of the chronic health and social burden of these infections and documents that more sensitive diagnostics have revealed substantially higher prevalence and frequent co-infections than previously recognized. The authors also situate their work in the context of integrated parasite control programs and state that their findings can inform more effective population-based control strategies. While the passage does not use the single word 'inexpensive,' it does support the three-part trend described: growing recognition of polyparasitism's burden, improved detection revealing greater prevalence, and movement toward integrated, population-level control measures—i.e., evolving approaches to management and treatment accessibility.

237.Option: The high prevalence of co-infections in children and young adults is primarily due to the prior mass drug administration (MDA) campaign in 2003, which reduced hookworm and Trichuris infections in the 10–19 age group.
Evidence: Overall, children and young adults were more likely to be infected with malaria parasites, S. haematobium, and Trichuris, while adults were more likely to be infected with filariasis and hookworms.
Polyparasitism (two or more infections) was highest in the 10–19 age group.
In Milalani and Nganja, hookworm infections declined in the 10–19 age group before rising again in the older age groups.
237.Rationale: The passage documents age patterns, including high polyparasitism in the 10-19 age group and an observed dip in hookworm in that age range in specific villages, but it contains no information about any prior mass drug administration (MDA) campaign in 2003 or attribution of age-specific prevalence patterns to such a campaign. Therefore the specific causal claim that the 2003 MDA caused the observed patterns is not supported by the study text.

237.Option: The integration of socio-ecological data and spatial analysis techniques in this study demonstrates that environmental factors are the dominant drivers of polyparasitism in coastal Kenya.
Evidence: We hypothesized that environmental factors are the key determinants of transmission potential for these parasites, and that these interact with demographic and socio-economic factors to determine the observed spatial/demographic patterns of parasitic disease.
Village and environmental factors were major independent correlates of infection.

demographic factors (age, gender, and education), SES, and household characteristics (construction, use of bednets, water source, sanitation, and number of inhabitants) were significantly associated with parasite infections and co-infections.
After controlling for demographic factors and SES, living in Kinango was protective with regard to several single infections and for co-infections.
237.Rationale: The passage supports that environmental and village factors are important correlates and that the study integrated socio-ecological and spatial methods. However, it also reports that demographic, SES, and household characteristics were significantly associated with infections and co-infections. The evidence therefore supports a multi-factorial causation where environmental factors are important but not unambiguously the sole or dominant drivers. Claiming they are 'dominant drivers' overstates what the presented results demonstrate.

237.Option: The lack of significant correlation between mosquito abundance and malaria or filariasis cases suggests that vector control measures are ineffective in reducing transmission in coastal Kenya.
Evidence: Results from the GAM applied to the entomological data (Table 4) showed no significant correlation between presence or number of collected mosquitoes and malaria or filariasis cases.
However, not surprisingly, bednet use was protective against malaria.
237.Rationale: The passage reports a lack of significant correlation between the entomological measures collected and malaria or filariasis cases, but it does not conclude—and does not provide evidence—that vector control measures are ineffective. Indeed, it separately reports that bednet use was protective against malaria, which is a form of vector control. The absence of correlation in the entomological sampling can have many explanations (sampling timing, methods, spatial/temporal mismatch between mosquito trapping and human infection, etc.) and does not support the broad causal inference that vector control measures are ineffective.

238.Option: Despite significant creatinine elevation in one patient, significant differences were not recorded in any of mean laboratory values at the end of the therapy, suggesting that routine laboratory assessment is not mandatory during oral miltefosine treatment.
Evidence: Creatinine elevation was the only laboratory data complication which detected in one patient (3.70%) which raised from 0.80 mg/dl in the beginning to 2.0 mg/dl after 14 days.
Patient with disturbed laboratory data were excluded from the study and referred to the nephrologist.
There were no significant differences in mean values of hematological, hepatic, and renal parameters in treatment groups.
238.Rationale: The passage documents a clinically important creatinine rise in one patient (0.80 to 2.0 mg/dl) that led to exclusion and nephrology referral, and it separately reports that mean laboratory values did not change significantly. From these facts one cannot infer that routine laboratory monitoring is unnecessary: a single substantial adverse laboratory event demonstrates that relying on group mean stability can mask individual serious toxicity. The passage gives no evidence or analysis to support the policy claim that routine laboratory assessment is not mandatory, so that recommendation cannot be derived from the presented data.

238.Option: The high rate of clinical improvement and low recurrence over 12 months of follow-up in this cohort suggests that oral miltefosine treatment leads to permanent sterilization of L. tropica infection in most patients.
Evidence: Of 27 enrolled patients for systemic miltefosine treatment, 25 patients completed treatment. In the first follow up visit (three months after treatment) 23 patients (85.18%) gained complete or partial cure, including 14 definitely cured patients and 9 partially cured patients.

In both second and third follow up sessions (sixth and twelfth month post treatment) 22 patients had complete or partial cure (81.48%) including 17 definitely cured patients, corresponding to a cure rate of 68% on a per protocol analysis, and 62.96% according to intention to treat analysis and 5 partially cured patients.
Recurrence of lesions was only occurred in one patient (3.70%) during 6 month follow up.
All CR lesions were confirmed according to negative skin smear test.
238.Rationale: The passage shows substantial clinical improvement (62.96% ITT; 68% per-protocol) and low observed clinical recurrence (3.7%) with negative skin smears for clinically resolved lesions. However, the statement that treatment leads to 'permanent sterilization' of L. tropica is a biological claim (complete elimination of the parasite) that is stronger than the clinical and limited parasitological data provided. Negative skin smears confirm absence of detectable parasites at lesion sites at the time of CR, but the study does not provide molecular or long-term parasitological evidence to demonstrate permanent sterilization in most patients. Therefore the permanent sterilization conclusion is not supported by the passage.

238.Option: The program to investigate miltefosine for new world cutaneous leishmaniasis (NWCL) was initiated in 2001 with an uncontrolled, open-label, dose-ranging study in Colombia, in which the per-protocol cure rate of 94% was reported by 2.5 mg/kg oral miltefosine per day after four weeks.
Evidence: Oral miltefosine has previously demonstrated a 95% cure rate in Indian visceral leishmaniasis [6–7].

In Iran, as endemic place for OWCL, therapeutic effects of oral miltefosine on ZCL have been studied previously. Mohebali and colleagues (2007) declared miltefosine at least as good as MA for the treatment of ZCL [8].
To date, no experimental study reported effects of oral miltefosine in treatment of ACL.
238.Rationale: The passage mentions prior studies of miltefosine in Indian visceral leishmaniasis and in zoonotic CL (ZCL) in Iran, and it emphasizes that no experimental study had reported effects in ACL prior to this work. It does not mention any 2001 Colombia study, NWCL program initiation, or a 94% per-protocol cure rate in an uncontrolled dose-ranging Colombian trial. Thus the specific historical and effectiveness details in this option are not present in the passage and cannot be derived from it.

238.Option: The therapeutic effect of oral miltefosine on MA-resistant anthropontic cutaneous leishmaniasis caused by L. tropica in Iran is effective and nearly safe, as evidenced by a high rate of clinical improvement and low recurrence over 12 months of follow-up.
Evidence: It was an uncontrolled before-after study ... on 27 patients with MA resistant ACL.
Volunteers received miltefosine orally at a dosage of ∼2.5 mg/kg daily for 28 days ...

Of 27 enrolled patients for systemic miltefosine treatment, 25 patients completed treatment. In the first follow up visit (three months after treatment) 23 patients (85.18%) gained complete or partial cure, including 14 definitely cured patients and 9 partially cured patients.

In both second and third follow up sessions (sixth and twelfth month post treatment) 22 patients had complete or partial cure (81.48%) including 17 definitely cured patients, corresponding to a cure rate of 68% on a per protocol analysis, and 62.96% according to intention to treat analysis and 5 partially cured patients.
Recurrence of lesions was only occurred in one patient (3.70%) during 6 month follow up.
Nausea, but not vomiting, was the most subjective complication during the study which detected in 9 (33.33%) patients.
Creatinine elevation was the only laboratory data complication which detected in one patient (3.70%) which raised from 0.80 mg/dl in the beginning to 2.0 mg/dl after 14 days.
There were no significant differences in mean values of hematological, hepatic, and renal parameters in treatment groups.
238.Rationale: The passage provides direct clinical and follow-up data from MA-resistant L. tropica ACL patients treated with oral miltefosine: a majority experienced complete or partial clinical improvement at 3 months (85.18%) and this proportion remained similar at 6 and 12 months (81.48%), with only one documented recurrence (3.7%). Adverse events included nausea in one-third and one notable creatinine elevation requiring exclusion; overall, mean laboratory parameters did not change significantly. These data support an epidemiological inference that oral miltefosine demonstrated measurable effectiveness and an acceptable (but not risk-free) safety profile in this cohort, i.e., it was effective and nearly safe in this specific, treatment-refractory population.

238.Option: The higher cure rates observed in previous studies of miltefosine for zoonotic CL in Iran may be attributed to the self-limiting nature of L. major infections, which could explain the relatively lower cure rate in this cohort of patients with chronic L. tropica infections.
Evidence: anthroponotic cutaneous leishmaniasis (ACL) and zoonotic cutaneous leishmaniasis (ZCL) caused by L. tropica and L. major respectively, are the most common types of CL in Iran [3].

In Iran, as endemic place for OWCL, therapeutic effects of oral miltefosine on ZCL have been studied previously. Mohebali and colleagues (2007) declared miltefosine at least as good as MA for the treatment of ZCL [8].
Mean duration of lesions was 3.45±0.76 years (ranges between 8 months and 20 years).
To date, no experimental study reported effects of oral miltefosine in treatment of ACL.
238.Rationale: The passage documents that different Leishmania species cause ACL (L. tropica) and ZCL (L. major) and notes prior miltefosine study results in ZCL. It also shows that patients in this ACL cohort had chronic lesions (mean duration ~3.45 years). However, the claim that higher cure rates in previous ZCL studies are attributable to the self-limiting nature of L. major infections is a causal explanation that is not provided or supported by the passage. The study does not present comparative data on the natural history of L. major versus L. tropica, nor does it analyze whether spontaneous resolution of L. major accounts for reported cure rates. Therefore attributing differences in cure rates to self-limitation of L. major cannot be derived from the provided text.

239.Option: The majority of V. cholerae isolates from household contacts and water sources within the same household were closely related by MLVA, indicating that transmission occurred either through a shared environmental source or person-to-person contact.
Evidence: We identified 31 MLVA genotypes: 7 MLVA genotypes from both clinical and water isolates, 9 genotypes from only water isolates, and 15 genotypes from only clinical isolates.

When the relatedness of water and clinical isolates from the same household was compared, all households had at least one clinical and water isolate with an identical MLVA genotype or a single locus variant of the same MLVA genotype.

Among the ten households with multiple infected household members, nine out of ten had isolates from different household members with identical MLVA genotypes or single locus variants of the same MLVA genotype.

Isolates collected from the same household had significantly fewer pairwise differences in MLVA loci than those from different households (mean: 0.98 pairwise differences in MLVA loci (same household) vs. 1.79 (different household), p<0.0001).
239.Rationale: The passage reports that multiple MLVA genotypes were observed overall but explicitly states that for households where both water and clinical isolates were available, every such household had at least one clinical–water isolate pair that was identical or a single locus variant. Additionally, most households with multiple infected members had identical or single-locus-variant clinical isolates, and isolates from the same household were significantly more similar than isolates from different households. These findings support the inference that isolates within households are closely related and that transmission within households likely occurred either via a shared environmental source (water) or via transmission between people.

239.Option: The presence of V. cholerae in both source and stored water within households indicates that household water contamination is primarily due to poor sanitation infrastructure rather than person-to-person transmission.
Evidence: Thirteen percent of households (4) had V. cholerae in their source water during the surveillance period (all groundwater sources), and 27% (8) had V. cholerae in stored household drinking water.
All households with detectable V. cholerae in their water source had unimproved latrines.
All households with V. cholerae in their source water also had V. cholerae in stored drinking water.

When the relatedness of water and clinical isolates from the same household was compared, all households had at least one clinical and water isolate with an identical MLVA genotype or a single locus variant of the same MLVA genotype.

Among the ten households with multiple infected household members, nine out of ten had isolates from different household members with identical MLVA genotypes or single locus variants of the same MLVA genotype.
239.Rationale: While the passage documents that all households with detectable source water V. cholerae had unimproved latrines (supporting an association between poor sanitation and contaminated source water), it also presents MLVA evidence showing close genetic matches between clinical and water isolates and identical genotypes among multiple infected household members. Those genetic findings are consistent with person-to-person transmission or contamination of household water by an infected person. The passage does not provide direct evidence that poor sanitation is the primary cause to the exclusion of person-to-person spread, so the stronger causal attribution ('primarily due to poor sanitation rather than person-to-person transmission') is not justified by the data presented.

239.Option: The fact that stored water samples had a higher proportion of V. cholerae than source water samples indicates that household-level contamination is the primary driver of V. cholerae transmission, independent of the index case.
Evidence: Thirteen percent of households (4) had V. cholerae in their source water during the surveillance period (all groundwater sources), and 27% (8) had V. cholerae in stored household drinking water.
All households with V. cholerae in their source water also had V. cholerae in stored drinking water.

When the relatedness of water and clinical isolates from the same household was compared, all households had at least one clinical and water isolate with an identical MLVA genotype or a single locus variant of the same MLVA genotype.

Among the ten households with multiple infected household members, nine out of ten had isolates from different household members with identical MLVA genotypes or single locus variants of the same MLVA genotype.
239.Rationale: Although stored water was positive in a larger proportion of households than source water, the data do not demonstrate that household-level contamination is the primary driver independent of the index case. The passage shows that source contamination and stored water contamination co-occur (all households with contaminated source water also had contaminated stored water), and genetic matches between clinical and water isolates suggest linkage between cases and water. Additionally, identical genotypes among household members point to within-household transmission dynamics (which could include contamination by the index case). The data do not establish that stored-water contamination is the main driver independent of the index case, so the strong, exclusive claim is not supported.

239.Option: The high diversity of MLVA genotypes observed in clinical and water samples suggests that V. cholerae infections in household contacts are primarily due to multiple independent environmental exposures rather than person-to-person transmission.
Evidence: We identified 31 MLVA genotypes: 7 MLVA genotypes from both clinical and water isolates, 9 genotypes from only water isolates, and 15 genotypes from only clinical isolates.

There were multiple MLVA genotypes among isolates collected from a single sample. This was similar for clinical samples (mean: 1.9 MLVA genotypes, range: 1–3) and water samples (mean: 2.1 genotypes, range: 1–4), p = 0.53.

Among the ten households with multiple infected household members, nine out of ten had isolates from different household members with identical MLVA genotypes or single locus variants of the same MLVA genotype.

Isolates collected from the same household had significantly fewer pairwise differences in MLVA loci than those from different households (mean: 0.98 pairwise differences in MLVA loci (same household) vs. 1.79 (different household), p<0.0001).
239.Rationale: Although the study observed substantial genotype diversity overall and multiple genotypes within samples, the passage also provides strong evidence of within-household genetic clustering: most households with multiple infected members had identical or single-locus-variant genotypes, and isolates from the same household were significantly more similar than those from different households. These findings contradict the claim that infections are primarily due to multiple independent environmental exposures; instead, they indicate a mixture of sources, including within-household transmission or shared household exposures. Therefore, the assertion that infections are primarily due to independent environmental exposures is not supported by the data presented.

239.Option: The significantly higher odds of symptomatic V. cholerae infection among household contacts with V. cholerae in their water source suggests that waterborne transmission is the dominant mode of infection, with person-to-person transmission playing a minor role.
Evidence: Contacts with V. cholerae in their source water had a significantly higher odds of a symptomatic V. cholerae infection (OR: 5.49, 95% CI: 1.07, 28.08) (25% (V. cholerae in water source) vs. 6% (no V. cholerae in water source)).

When the relatedness of water and clinical isolates from the same household was compared, all households had at least one clinical and water isolate with an identical MLVA genotype or a single locus variant of the same MLVA genotype.

Among the ten households with multiple infected household members, nine out of ten had isolates from different household members with identical MLVA genotypes or single locus variants of the same MLVA genotype.

Isolates collected from the same household had significantly fewer pairwise differences in MLVA loci than those from different households (mean: 0.98 pairwise differences in MLVA loci (same household) vs. 1.79 (different household), p<0.0001).
239.Rationale: The passage does show a statistically significant association between contaminated source water and symptomatic infection, supporting an important role for waterborne transmission. However, genetic data showing identical or single-locus-variant genotypes among household members and close genetic relatedness within households indicate that person-to-person transmission (or contamination of household items/water by cases) also plays a significant role. The data do not quantify the relative contribution of waterborne versus person-to-person transmission to justify declaring waterborne transmission as the dominant mode with person-to-person transmission being minor. Thus the strong, exclusive inference in this option is not supported by the evidence presented.

240.Option: The immunosuppressive effect of M. corti tetrathyridia excretory-secretory products is due to the presence of multiple low-molecular-weight proteins that are selectively secreted during active infection.
Evidence: Taken together, our bio-activity based fractionation analyses suggest that (a) protein(s) of MW higher than 20 kDa might mediate the immunosuppressive activity of McES.

A clear clustering of the protein banding pattern along three definable MW groups was observed for the major protein-containing fractions (Fig 9D). These groups of proteins were arbitrarily defined as group i (>130kDa to 25kDa), group ii (60kDa to 20kDa) and group iii (< 20kDa). Active fractions were principally found within the MW group (i) suggesting that the active principle within McES might be of a MW >20kDa since fractions from group ii and iii showed minimal to no activity (Fig 9C and 9D).
240.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports that active (immunosuppressive) fractions were principally in the high molecular weight group (>25 kDa / >20 kDa) and that fractions corresponding to lower molecular weights (group ii and iii, including <20 kDa) showed minimal to no activity. Therefore the claim that multiple low-molecular-weight proteins are responsible contradicts the experimental fractionation results and cannot be derived from the presented data.

240.Option: The immunosuppressive effect of M. corti tetrathyridia excretory-secretory products on dendritic cells is mediated by glycoproteins with a molecular weight greater than 20 kDa.
Evidence: These results indicated that one or several intact parasite glycoproteins, but not separate protein and carbohydrate entities, mediate(s) DC suppression by McES.
Taken together, our bio-activity based fractionation analyses suggest that (a) protein(s) of MW higher than 20 kDa might mediate the immunosuppressive activity of McES.
240.Rationale: Biochemical depletion experiments showed that removing either proteins (heat-inactivation) or carbohydrates (metaperiodate) abolished activity and that mixing separate protein-depleted and carbohydrate-depleted preparations did not restore activity, leading to the conclusion that intact glycoprotein(s) mediate suppression. Independent fractionation (IEX followed by gel filtration) placed the active fractions in the high-MW group and the authors explicitly state that proteins of MW >20 kDa likely mediate the immunosuppressive activity. Together, these data support the inference that the active component(s) are glycoprotein(s) >20 kDa.

240.Option: The ability of M. corti tetrathyridia to suppress dendritic cell activation is dependent on the metabolic activity of the parasite, as heat-killed larvae failed to recruit CD11c+ cells in vivo.
Evidence: We found that viable tetrathyridia significantly recruited cells within the peritoneum at day 7 p.i. when compared to dead tetrathyridia or to the mock control (3-fold more than the dead larvae and 15-fold more than the mock injections) (Fig 1C).

These results indicate that only live M. corti tetrathyridia massively attracted host CD11c+ cells up to day 7 post-infection whereas dead (ametabolic) larvae recruited CD11c+ cells just for the 3 days that followed injection.

Given the ability of live helminths to excrete-secrete molecules which directly interact with host immune cells [11,23] and considering the reported predominance of dendritic cells within murine peritoneal CD11c+ cells, our data suggest a central role for ES products of M. corti (McES) in the modulation of host DC responses in vivo.

As expected, as little as 5 μg/ml of McES reduced LPS-driven IL-12p70 production by 50% in BMDC cultures whereas a similar amount of McH failed to show any suppressive effect on LPS-driven IL-12p70 release by BMDCs (Fig 5).
240.Rationale: The authors show that only live (metabolically active) larvae robustly recruit CD11c+ cells and argue that ES products from live parasites are central to DC modulation in vivo. Complementary in vitro data show that secreted ES products (McES) suppress IL-12p70 production while larval homogenates do not. These results support the inference that suppression depends on parasite metabolic activity (i.e., active secretion of immunomodulatory products), consistent with heat-killed larvae failing to recruit CD11c+ cells and therefore not providing active ES products.

240.Option: The immunosuppressive effect of M. corti tetrathyridia excretory-secretory products on dendritic cells is primarily due to the presence of cysteine-rich secretory proteins and annexins, which are known to inhibit proteases and modulate membrane dynamics.

Together these statements support the epidemiological/material-science inference that the observed biological activity plausibly arises from a synergistic interaction (silver acting as a co-catalyst) that modifies redox/catalytic activity at surface defects.

245.Option: The larvicidal activity of AgCNPs is primarily due to the presence of oxygen vacancy-related (point defect) highly redox-active Ce3+ species, which are responsible for the observed biological effects.
Evidence: The general bioactivity observed for CNPs is often ascribed to their chemical interaction with the local, biological environment and their catalytic response to (radical) oxygen species [28–30]. In particular, catalysis occurs at surface defects, such as oxygen vacancies, and is highly dependent on physicochemical conditions [31–35].
The ratio of Ce3+ to Ce4+ redox states within the material was calculated as 34.7% Ce3+ for AgCNPs.

Incorporation of the known larvicidal agent, silver, into the CNP structure may allow for an increased total larvicidal response. Specifically, it may be that ceria and silver could function as co-catalysts which impact mosquito health conditions via unique reaction pathways affecting endogenous biochemical substrates.
Data shows that negligible free silver remains in the solutions, indicating observed effects on mosquitoes are due to incorporated silver and not free silver ion or metal.
245.Rationale: While the passage links CNP bioactivity to catalysis at surface defects/oxygen vacancies and reports a measurable Ce3+ fraction, it does not provide direct evidence that the larvicidal activity of AgCNPs is primarily or solely due to Ce3+-related oxygen vacancies. The text also emphasizes silver incorporation and the potential for silver and ceria to act as co-catalysts, indicating that silver likely contributes mechanistically. Therefore the specific causal claim that Ce3+ species are the primary cause is not directly supported by the passage.

245.Option: The observed biological activity of AgCNPs against Ae. aegypti does not result from free Ag+.
Evidence: XPS analysis showed 0.72at% silver incorporation for AgCNPs.
This data suggests the presence of negligible amounts of silver contaminants/dissociation products (i.e., free silver ion or silver nanoclusters) in AgCNP solution.
Data shows that negligible free silver remains in the solutions, indicating observed effects on mosquitoes are due to incorporated silver and not free silver ion or metal.
The amount of silver utilized to induce larval death is reduced in AgCNP versus free silver ion (0.22ppb vs. 4.2ppm, respectively).
245.Rationale: The passage reports ICP-MS and XPS data showing negligible free silver in AgCNP solutions and explicitly states that observed effects are due to incorporated silver rather than free silver ion or metal. It also highlights that effective silver amounts in AgCNP are far lower than free Ag+ controls, consistent with activity not being driven by free Ag+.

245.Option: The larvicidal effect of AgCNPs is enhanced in the presence of larval food due to the formation of organic coronae that stabilize the nanoparticles and increase their bioavailability.
Evidence: Believing the presence of abundant organic matter (i.e., larval food) may blunt the nanoceria larvicidal activity, experiments were performed where food was withheld for 24h and 48h side-by-side with the appropriate controls for each condition.

When larval food was present in rearing water during the nanoparticle exposure (Fig 6A), AgCNP and AgNO3 had significantly greater larvicidal impact on 3rd instar Ae. aegypti larvae in comparison to the control group (25.2±2.39% and 81.5±2.14% larvae dead, respectively, p-values <0.001).

When larval food was withheld for either (b) 24h or (c) 48h following treatment with AgCNPs, the observed larvicidal activity was even greater compared to control (p-value <0.001). Now in the AgCNP groups compared to their respective 24h and 48h food-withheld controls, the mean proportions of larvae killed were 65.7±3.28% and 57.8±3.68% (p-values <0.001).
245.Rationale: The passage reports that the presence of larval food was believed to blunt nanoceria activity and shows experimentally that withholding food increased AgCNP larvicidal activity (higher mortality when food withheld). There is no evidence presented about formation of organic coronae that stabilize particles or increase bioavailability; the empirical results are the opposite of the option's claim, so this option is not supported.

245.Option: The reduction in egg clutch observed in adult mosquitoes fed AgCNPs via blood meal is due to the physical obstruction of oocytes by undissolved nanoparticles in the reproductive tract.
Evidence: A significant reduction in egg clutch was observed when AgCNPs were administered to mosquitoes via a blood meal.
The median egg clutch of 74.5 eggs for the AgCNP group was significantly less than the median egg clutches of control and AgNO3 groups 84 and 85 eggs, respectively (p-values <0.05).

The maximum intensity z-projections of ovarioles/ovaries from mosquitoes which failed to oviposit showed a very interesting phenomenon occurring specifically in the ovarioles of AgCNP-exposed mosquitoes (Fig 11C). When the z-projections from those mosquitoes were compared to the z-projections of ovarioles at different time points in the egg-growth timeline of control mosquitoes, it was revealed that oocytes in the AgCNP-exposed mosquitoes appeared to have suffered a growth arrest by or before 16h PBM.
Nanoparticles or AgNO3 were added to blood immediately prior to feeding and sonicated for 20min to ensure even suspension (Table 1).
245.Rationale: While the passage documents reduced egg clutch and shows oocytes with growth arrest in AgCNP-exposed mosquitoes, it does not provide evidence that the cause is physical obstruction by undissolved particles. The authors note arrested oocyte growth (a biological effect) and also state nanoparticles were sonicated to produce even suspension prior to feeding. No direct observations or statements indicate undissolved particles mechanically obstructing oocytes; thus the specific causal claim of physical obstruction is not supported by the passage.

247.Option: The identification of SNPs at TNFSF pathways associated with T1R outcomes in populations from Vietnam and Brazil suggests that a universal genetic test for leprosy reactions can be developed with minimal validation across different populations.
Evidence: SNPs at NINJ1, TLR1, IL6, and TNFSF8 are clearly associated with reactional phenotypes (TR1 or TR2) [28–32].
However, so far few of these genes have been replicated or functionally characterized to support the epidemiological findings.

Here, we confirmed the association between IL6 and NOD2 SNPs and leprosy reactions among Brazilians, and demonstrated that the NOD2 genotype associated with a decreased risk for developing a reaction is also related to lower levels of IL-6 in the sera of non-reactional patients.
247.Rationale: The passage does report SNP associations in different genes and notes findings in multiple populations, but it explicitly states that few genes have been replicated or functionally characterized. That undermines the claim that a universal genetic test could be developed with minimal cross-population validation. The study itself presents replication of some markers (IL6, NOD2) in Brazilians, which implies the need for replication/validation rather than minimal validation. Therefore the conclusion that a universal test can be developed with minimal validation is not supported by the passage.

247.Option: The failure to detect associations with TLR1 SNPs in this study, despite prior reports, indicates that genetic markers for leprosy reactions are unreliable and should not be pursued further in future research.
Evidence: TLR1 SNPs rs5743592 and rs4833095 were included in this study in order to replicate previous findings regarding their association to leprosy per se and reactional episodes [29,30,42–44].
From the 15 candidate SNPs (total counts and frequencies detailed at S3 Table), 2 showed different Kaplan–Meier curves between genotype groups (log-rank p-value < 0.05).
Here, we confirmed the association between IL6 and NOD2 SNPs and leprosy reactions among Brazilians...
247.Rationale: While the passage indicates that only two of the 15 tested SNPs showed significant Kaplan–Meier differences (IL6 and NOD2), it does not support the sweeping conclusion that genetic markers are unreliable or that research should cease. On the contrary, the passage confirms reproducible associations for IL6 and NOD2, demonstrating that genetic markers can be informative. The absence of replicated TLR1 findings in this dataset does not justify abandoning genetic research; rather it illustrates the need for further replication and functional characterization, as the authors themselves note.

247.Option: The presence of risk alleles for leprosy reactions does not have additive or synergistic effects, suggesting that combining multiple genetic markers may not be feasible for developing a predictive genetic test.
Evidence: To investigate the association between genetic markers and reaction outcomes we constructed survival curves for each SNP (stratified by genotype groups) as a descriptive approach to evaluate the occurrence of reaction in leprosy patients over time.
From the 15 candidate SNPs (total counts and frequencies detailed at S3 Table), 2 showed different Kaplan–Meier curves between genotype groups (log-rank p-value < 0.05).
To estimate the SNP effects on reaction occurrence, the Cox proportional-hazards regression model was applied including adjusted analyses for gender and clinical forms (strata).
247.Rationale: The study evaluated individual SNP associations (Kaplan–Meier and Cox models) but does not report analyses of additive or synergistic effects between multiple risk alleles. Because the passage contains no data or analysis about combined genetic effects, one cannot infer that alleles do not have additive or synergistic effects. The absence of reported multi-locus interaction results does not constitute evidence that combining markers is infeasible.

247.Option: The lack of association between IL6 rs2069845 and serum IL-6 levels in this study suggests that genetic markers are not useful for predicting leprosy reactions, as they do not correlate with measurable biological outcomes.
Evidence: AG genotype and AG/GG carriers of IL6 rs2069845 were associated with having an increased risk of developing a reaction (aHR = 1.88, p = 0.0008 and aHR = 1.73, p = 0.002, respectively).
Neither was IL-6 production associated with IL6 rs2069845 SNP (Fig 3A).
individuals carrying NOD2 rs751271-GT showed a borderline difference (p = 0.05), suggesting lower IL-6 production (GT median: 0.008) as compared to GG individuals (GG median: 0.53) (Fig 3B).
247.Rationale: Although the IL6 rs2069845 genotype was not associated with measured serum IL-6 levels in this dataset, the same IL6 SNP was associated with increased risk of reactions, and a NOD2 genotype linked to reaction risk showed a borderline association with IL-6 levels. Thus, the passage provides mixed results: some genetic markers associate with clinical outcomes and at least one genetic marker relates to a measurable cytokine change. The single negative genotype–cytokine correlation (IL6 rs2069845 vs IL-6 levels) does not justify the blanket conclusion that genetic markers are not useful for prediction.

247.Option: Since the study confirmed gender and clinical form as risk factors for leprosy reactions, a genetic test should be developed to complement these non-genetic variables in predicting reaction outcomes.
Evidence: As observed, the length of time before a leprosy reaction, as described by survival curves, shows a significant difference when considering the variables of gender (p = 0.002) and clinical form (p < 0.001).
To estimate the SNP effects on reaction occurrence, the Cox proportional-hazards regression model was applied including adjusted analyses for gender and clinical forms (strata).

The NOD2 rs751271-GT genotype and G-allele carriers (GT/GG) were associated with protection against a reaction’s occurrence, even after adjustment (aHR = 0.45, p = 0.002 and aHR = 0.56, p = 0.01, respectively).
AG genotype and AG/GG carriers of IL6 rs2069845 were associated with having an increased risk of developing a reaction (aHR = 1.88, p = 0.0008 and aHR = 1.73, p = 0.002, respectively).
247.Rationale: The passage documents that non-genetic variables (gender, clinical form) are significant risk factors and that genetic markers (NOD2 and IL6 SNPs) are independently associated with reaction risk after adjustment for those variables. From an epidemiological perspective, this pattern supports the inference that genetic testing could complement established non-genetic predictors to improve prediction of reaction risk. While the passage does not present a validated predictive test or combined model, the evidence supports the logical proposition that developing a genetic test to complement non-genetic factors is warranted.

248.Option: The high antibody titers observed in geese following DENV2 vaccination, reaching up to 1:6,400,000, suggest that goose-derived IgY could be used as a prophylactic vaccine alternative in humans due to its strong immunogenicity and long-lasting response.
Evidence: Of the 104 eggs that were measured, the average serum endpoint titer across all weeks post vaccination was 1:924,807 with the highest titer being 1:6,400,000 at 7 weeks post vaccination (Fig 1B).
We suggest that IgY, the avian homolog to mammalian IgG, which naturally fails to bind FcγR, could be used as alternative therapeutic antibodies to treat DENV-induced disease.

There remains an unmet need for an effective dengue therapeutic that is able to shorten the duration of the illness, prevent the development of severe disease, and reduce the severity of common symptoms [11].
248.Rationale: The passage documents very high antibody titers in immunized geese, but it frames IgY as a source of therapeutic antibodies rather than as a human prophylactic vaccine. The evidence supports immunogenicity in geese and utility as a harvested antibody product, not that goose-derived IgY functions as or substitutes for a human vaccine. The passage does not present data on immunizing humans with goose-derived IgY, on durability of protection in humans, or on safety/efficacy of IgY as a prophylactic vaccine in humans. Therefore the specific inference that goose-derived IgY could be used as a prophylactic vaccine alternative in humans is not supported by the passage.

248.Option: The inability of anti-DENV IgY to bind mammalian Fcγ receptors means that it does not induce antibody-dependent enhancement (ADE) of dengue virus infection, making it a promising therapeutic candidate for treating dengue without increasing the risk of severe disease.
Evidence: Another key advantage of IgY is its inability to bind mammalian FcγR; therefore, it is possible that anti-DENV IgY will be able to neutralize a viral infection without inducing ADE [18].
Anti-DENV2 IgY was not able to enhance DENV2 infection in vitro, thereby providing protection in the absence of ADE.

In the enhancement experiment, whereas the positive control anti-DENV Mab E60 generated ~15% infection at its peak enhancement titer, neither the anti-DENV IgY nor control IgY sera were enhancing at any dilution tested (Fig 3).

Similar to the positive control E60 N297Q mAb, 100% therapeutic efficacy was also observed with 1 mg anti-DENV2 IgY (n = 4) administered 24 hours post-infection (p = 0.0019), while 1 mg of control IgY yielded 25% protection (n = 4, p = 0.04 comparing equal amounts of anti-DENV2 IgY and control IgY) (Fig 4A).
248.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that IgY does not bind mammalian Fcγ receptors and links FcγR binding to ADE. Empirical data in the study show that anti-DENV2 IgY did not enhance infection in vitro, unlike a positive control known to mediate enhancement, and that therapeutic administration of anti-DENV2 IgY protected mice in vivo. Together these statements and data support the inference that because IgY lacks FcγR binding it does not induce ADE and is therefore a promising therapeutic candidate that neutralizes virus without increasing enhancement risk.

248.Option: The recognition of novel epitopes on the DENV2 E protein by anti-DENV2 IgY suggests that IgY-based therapies may be effective across all dengue serotypes due to conserved structural motifs.
Evidence: There are four distinct serotypes (DENV1, DENV2, DENV3, DENV4) that differ at the amino acid level by 25–40% [5].
We confirmed that anti-DENV2 IgY binds to previously uncharacterized epitopes, and furthermore full-length IgY and alternatively spliced IgY also bind epitopes that are unique from one another.
Specifically, we demonstrate that anti-DENV2 IgY is able to neutralize DENV2 in vitro and provide protection in the AG129 mouse model when administered therapeutically following lethal challenge.

Some of the peptides recognized by the full-length and alternatively spliced anti-DENV2 IgY were similar, while others appeared unique to each of the different antibody populations. Interestingly, some of the peptides recognized by the anti-DENV2 IgY appeared to be distinct from previously described epitopes targeted by either human or mouse anti-DENV2 sera [33–51].
248.Rationale: The passage shows that anti-DENV2 IgY recognizes novel and sometimes unique epitopes on the DENV2 E protein, and it notes there are four serotypes with substantial sequence divergence (25–40%). However, the study presents only DENV2 data; it does not provide any evidence that those epitopes are conserved across serotypes or that the IgY cross-neutralizes other serotypes. Therefore the claim that IgY therapies may be effective across all dengue serotypes cannot be derived from the presented evidence.

248.Option: The moderate protection observed with control IgY in vivo suggests that IgY may exert non-specific immune activation in mice, potentially enhancing innate immune responses and reducing viral load independently of neutralizing antibodies.
Evidence: The control IgY serum did not yield a measurable NT50 titer in either experiment.

Similar to the positive control E60 N297Q mAb, 100% therapeutic efficacy was also observed with 1 mg anti-DENV2 IgY (n = 4) administered 24 hours post-infection (p = 0.0019), while 1 mg of control IgY yielded 25% protection (n = 4, p = 0.04 comparing equal amounts of anti-DENV2 IgY and control IgY) (Fig 4A).
Treatment with 500-μg anti-DENV2 IgY (n = 6) provided 66% protection (p = 0.0035), and treatment with 50 μg anti-DENV2 IgY (n = 10) provided 33% protection.
248.Rationale: The passage documents that control (naïve) IgY lacked measurable neutralizing activity in vitro yet provided partial (25%) protection at 1 mg in the mouse model. This observation shows an effect of control IgY on survival but the mechanism is not described in the text. Inferring that the mechanism is non-specific immune activation (enhancement of innate responses) is speculative and not supported by any mechanistic data in the passage. Therefore this causal inference about mechanism cannot be derived solely from the presented evidence.

248.Option: Because IgY does not bind human complement, it is unlikely to contribute to immune enhancement during secondary dengue infections, thereby reducing the risk of vascular leakage and severe disease.
Evidence: Furthermore, IgY does not interact with other Fc-binding receptors, which limits its ability to elicit an inflammatory reaction in humans [19–24]. Although IgY is able to activate complement in the avian system, its unique structure prevents it from being able to activate human complement [23].
it has also been proposed that complement activation during secondary heterotypic DENV infection by non-neutralizing antibodies contributes to immune enhancement.
Therefore, the inability of anti-DENV IgY to induce complement activation could prove to be detrimental or beneficial depending on the individual disease state.
248.Rationale: The passage states that IgY cannot activate human complement and that complement activation by non-neutralizing antibodies has been proposed to contribute to immune enhancement in dengue. However, the authors explicitly caution that the inability to activate complement "could prove to be detrimental or beneficial depending on the individual disease state." The passage does not provide direct evidence that lack of complement activation by IgY will reduce the risk of vascular leakage or severe disease in secondary infections. Consequently, the definitive causal claim that IgY will reduce risk of severe disease via this mechanism is not supported by the provided text.

249.Option: Despite repeated rounds of school-based deworming, hookworm remains prevalent in adult men in Kwale County, suggesting that school-based deworming alone is insufficient to control community-wide hookworm transmission.
Evidence: Annual school-based deworming with albendazole has been implemented successfully since 2012 (with a prior round in 2009) with programmatic coverage of 82% of pre-school and school-age children (2–14 years) in 2014 [19, 20].
Prevalence of hookworm infection was 19.1% (95% confidence interval [CI]: 16.4–21.7%); 12.5% in under 5s, 17.4% in 5–14 years and 20.7% in adults;
Hookworm prevalence increased with age across both sexes, and was higher in males (Fig 3a).
A higher proportion of the MHI infections were found in adults (ρ = 0.77, p<0.001) (Fig 4b), consistent with patterns shown in Fig 3a.
249.Rationale: The passage documents sustained, high-coverage school-based deworming targeting 2–14 year olds but then reports that adults—particularly men—have equal or higher hookworm prevalence (20.7% in adults) and a higher proportion of moderate-to-heavy infections. This pattern (ongoing adult infection despite intensive child-focused treatment) supports the epidemiologic inference that school-based deworming alone is unlikely to be sufficient to interrupt community-wide hookworm transmission because adults constitute an untreated reservoir contributing to persistence.

249.Option: The observed age-related increase in hookworm infection intensity among adults suggests that the current deworming strategy is ineffective because it does not account for the biological differences in susceptibility between age groups.
Evidence: Infection intensity remained fairly constant through childhood, before increasing at age twenty and above in males, and thirty and above in females.

Annual school-based deworming with albendazole has been implemented successfully since 2012 (with a prior round in 2009) with programmatic coverage of 82% of pre-school and school-age children (2–14 years) in 2014 [19, 20].
being dewormed in the last 12 months were all found to be protective behaviours for hookworm infection.
249.Rationale: While the passage reports an age-related rise in hookworm intensity among adults, it does not provide evidence that this is due to inherent biological differences in susceptibility by age. The passage instead reports protective effects of recent deworming and behaviours (e.g., 'being dewormed in the last 12 months were all found to be protective behaviours'), which points to programmatic coverage, exposure, or reinfection dynamics rather than an unaddressed biological susceptibility. Therefore the causal claim that the strategy is ineffective specifically because it 'does not account for biological differences' is not supported by the presented data.

249.Option: The high prevalence of hookworm in adults, particularly men, suggests that the current deworming strategy is ineffective in preventing reinfection due to poor compliance among adult populations.
Evidence: Annual school-based deworming with albendazole has been implemented successfully since 2012 (with a prior round in 2009) with programmatic coverage of 82% of pre-school and school-age children (2–14 years) in 2014 [19, 20].
For the two most recent MDA rounds prior to this survey, 2008 and 2011, programmatic coverage levels of 62.7% and 58.3% were reported [11, 20, 21].
Prevalence of hookworm infection was 19.1% (95% confidence interval [CI]: 16.4–21.7%); 12.5% in under 5s, 17.4% in 5–14 years and 20.7% in adults;
The male:female ratio was 0.67, due to under-sampling of adult males, particularly in the 20–40 year age group (Fig 3a).
249.Rationale: The passage shows high adult hookworm prevalence but provides no direct evidence on adult compliance with deworming (no adult-specific coverage or adherence data are presented). Reported coverage figures are for school-based programmes and older LF MDA rounds (general population coverage years earlier) but do not establish that poor adult compliance is the cause of persistent adult infection. Moreover, sampling under-represents adult males, complicating inference about adult compliance. Thus the specific attribution of persistent adult infection to 'poor compliance among adult populations' is not supported by the passage alone.

249.Option: The high prevalence of hookworm in adults, despite school-based deworming, indicates that the treatment is ineffective against adult worms, and therefore, alternative drugs should be used in future interventions.
Evidence: Annual school-based deworming with albendazole has been implemented successfully since 2012 (with a prior round in 2009) with programmatic coverage of 82% of pre-school and school-age children (2–14 years) in 2014 [19, 20].
Prevalence of hookworm infection was 19.1% (95% confidence interval [CI]: 16.4–21.7%); 12.5% in under 5s, 17.4% in 5–14 years and 20.7% in adults;
being dewormed in the last year were protective against heavier infections.
249.Rationale: The passage documents that recent deworming is associated with protection against infection and lower intensity, which argues against a blanket conclusion that albendazole/mebendazole are ineffective against adult worms. The observed adult prevalence could reflect untreated adult reservoirs, reinfection dynamics, or gaps in population coverage rather than intrinsic drug failure against adult parasites. The passage does not provide any data demonstrating drug inefficacy against adult-stage worms or treatment failures that would justify recommending alternative drugs; therefore this inference is not supported.

249.Option: The persistence of moderate-to-heavy hookworm infections in adults indicates that the current deworming strategy has failed to reduce transmission, and therefore, the treatment regimen should be changed to target only the most vulnerable age groups.
Evidence: A higher proportion of the MHI infections were found in adults (ρ = 0.77, p<0.001) (Fig 4b), consistent with patterns shown in Fig 3a.
Prevalence of hookworm infection was 19.1% (95% confidence interval [CI]: 16.4–21.7%); 12.5% in under 5s, 17.4% in 5–14 years and 20.7% in adults;

Annual school-based deworming with albendazole has been implemented successfully since 2012 (with a prior round in 2009) with programmatic coverage of 82% of pre-school and school-age children (2–14 years) in 2014 [19, 20].

The global community is increasingly looking beyond these targets to ask whether transmission interruption is possible [4–7], with several large-scale impact evaluations currently examining the impact of mass deworming targeting all ages [8–10].
249.Rationale: While the passage shows persistence of moderate-to-heavy infections in adults (adults harbour a higher proportion of MHI infections), recommending that the regimen be changed to 'target only the most vulnerable age groups' is not supported by the evidence. Targeting only vulnerable (child) groups is precisely the existing strategy and would be unlikely to address the documented adult reservoir. Moreover, the passage notes interest in evaluating mass deworming that targets all ages, implying that addressing adults (not restricting to vulnerable groups) is the epidemiologically plausible alternative. The claim that the current strategy 'has failed to reduce transmission' may be partially supported (adult reservoir persists), but the policy recommendation to restrict targeting to only the most vulnerable contradicts the observed age-distribution of infection and is not derivable from the data provided.

250.Option: The combination of CPA, CPB and CPC resulted in enhanced protection in liver (>93%) and spleen (>98%) which was even higher than all other CPs based vaccines tested against Leishmania so far.
Evidence: the mean hepatic and splenic parasite burden (LDU) were lowered by ∼93% and ∼98% respectively, in animals immunized with cocktail antigens as compared to the adjuvant controls at 3 months post infection.

Highest level of protection against L. donovani was however achieved in hamsters immunized with the liposomal triple antigen cocktail which showed 13 log-fold and 16 log-fold reductions in the parasite burden in liver and spleen, respectively, compared to adjuvant controls (p<0.0001) after 3 months of infectious challenge.

Evidence is presented here for the first time on the efficacy of highly potent liposomal formulations of recombinant CPA, CPB and CPC along with MPL-TDM adjuvant, in a hamster model against L. donovani, with an aim towards its further development for human vaccination.
250.Rationale: The passage explicitly documents the reported reductions (~93% liver, ~98% spleen) and that the triple antigen cocktail achieved the highest protection in this study. However, the option's second clause—claiming this was "even higher than all other CPs based vaccines tested against Leishmania so far"—is not supported by the Passage Body. The passage does not present comparative data against all prior CP-based vaccines or make a claim that these reductions exceed every other CP vaccine tested previously. The quoted text that this is the first presentation of these liposomal formulations further indicates absence of comprehensive historical comparisons in the passage. Therefore the numeric claim is supported but the global comparative claim is not derivable from the passage.

250.Option: Although direct comparison of our work with previous reports is difficult, it is noteworthy that rCPC individually induced better protection than promising vaccine candidates like gp63, ORFF, LelF-2 tested in murine VL against L. donovani.
Evidence: rCPC induced greater protection than both rCPA and rCPB, when injected alone.

hamsters vaccinated with liposomal rCPC showed a parasitic burden of 5.963 log10±0.269 in the liver and 6.77 log10±0.463 in the spleen, with an impressive 4 (log10)-fold decrease in the parasite burden compared to hamsters receiving liposomal rCPA (9.55 log10±1.037 in the liver and 11.5 log10±0.467 in the spleen, p<0.0001) and 2 (log10)-fold reduction compared to liposomal rCPB (7.413 log10±0.016 in the liver and 10.02 log10±0.451 in the spleen, p<0.0001).
Compared to an array of antigens that have been tested, very few are sufficiently promising to be carried out to Phase I clinical trials or advanced preclinical work against VL [12].
250.Rationale: The passage supports that rCPC alone induced better protection than rCPA and rCPB in this study. However, it does not provide any data or statements comparing rCPC to the other named vaccine candidates (gp63, ORFF, LelF-2) tested elsewhere. The passage explicitly notes many antigens have been tested but does not make direct comparisons to those specific candidates. Therefore the specific comparative claim to gp63/ORFF/LelF-2 cannot be derived from the Passage Body.

250.Option: The cocktail vaccine induced a synergistic enhancement of protection due to the cumulative increase in T-cell epitopes in a non-antagonistic manner.
Evidence: Taken together, these finding suggest that all three CPs present in the cocktail vaccine act synergistically to counteract the impaired T cell response after challenge for improved protection.

Our aim in this study was therefore towards a comparative evaluation of prophylactic potential of CPA, CPB and CPC, individually, as well as a multi-antigen cocktail vaccine, maximizing the exposed antigenic epitopes to protect against VL.

Highest level of protection against L. donovani was however achieved in hamsters immunized with the liposomal triple antigen cocktail which showed 13 log-fold and 16 log-fold reductions in the parasite burden in liver and spleen, respectively, compared to adjuvant controls (p<0.0001) after 3 months of infectious challenge.

Significant enhancement of antigen-specific splenocyte proliferation was observed for liposomal rCPC (p<0.001) and liposomal cocktail (p<0.0001) vaccinated animals compared to adjuvant controls. Highest percent lymphoproliferation was observed in hamsters vaccinated with triple antigen cocktail which was significantly higher than rCPA (p<0.0001), rCPB (p<0.0001) and rCPC (p<0.0001) as single antigen.
250.Rationale: The Passage Body explicitly states that the three CPs in the cocktail "act synergistically" to improve T cell responses and that the study's aim included "maximizing the exposed antigenic epitopes." Empirical results show the cocktail produced the highest lymphoproliferation and the greatest reductions in parasite burden versus single-antigen vaccines. Those statements support the inference that the cocktail induced a synergistic enhancement of protection, plausibly attributable to cumulative epitopes without antagonism, as reflected by improved immune readouts and superior protection compared to singles. The exact molecular mechanistic wording "cumulative increase in T-cell epitopes in a non-antagonistic manner" is an interpretation consistent with the authors' stated aim and observed synergistic outcomes; therefore it is derivable from the passage.

250.Option: The use of TLR agonists with cationic liposomes allows concurrent antigen presentation along with targeting pattern recognition receptor (PRR) pathways, for effective expansion of effector T cells.
Evidence: Monophosphoryl lipid A (MPLA) is a Toll-like receptor 4 (TLR4) agonist with more than 100,000 human doses safely administered as a part of licensed hepatitis B and Human papillomavirus vaccines [6].
When used together, both the adjuvants i.e. MPL and TDM non-specifically activate the immune system, allowing a better response to the associated immunogen [10].

CLSM of MΦ cells incubated for 2 hrs with both Rh123-labeled liposomes (green), LysoTracker Red (red) and DAPI(blue) simultaneously showed successful co-localization (yellow) of labeled liposomes in acidic compartments such as lysosomes (Fig. S3, B) which confirms antigen presentation by MΦ.
combining MPL-TDM with cationic liposomes result in increased the adjuvant activity of the formulation on the APCs in vitro, serving our rationale for its utilization.

Splenocytes from all the CP vaccinated hamsters proliferated in response to corresponding antigen... Significant enhancement of antigen-specific splenocyte proliferation was observed for liposomal rCPC (p<0.001) and liposomal cocktail (p<0.0001) vaccinated animals compared to adjuvant controls.

all immunized groups receiving either of liposomal rCPA, rCPB and rCPC singly or as cocktail produced significant upregulation of IL-2 and IFN-γ mRNAs in comparison to controls, which progressively increased at 2 and 3 months post infection (Fig. 7A).
250.Rationale: The passage identifies MPL (in MPL-TDM) as a TLR4 agonist and documents that MPL-TDM plus cationic liposomes increased adjuvant activity on APCs. Confocal data show liposomes co-localize to lysosomes in macrophages, indicating antigen presentation by APCs. The immunization results show enhanced splenocyte proliferation and upregulation of key effector cytokines (IL-2, IFN-γ), consistent with expansion of effector T cell responses. From these statements it is epidemiologically and immunologically reasonable to infer that combining a TLR agonist with cationic liposomes enables concurrent antigen presentation and PRR targeting that promotes effector T cell expansion; this inference is supported by the passage.

250.Option: The liposomal formulation of rCPC alone provided the highest level of protection among all tested antigen formulations, surpassing even the cocktail vaccine.
Evidence: Highest level of protection against L. donovani was however achieved in hamsters immunized with the liposomal triple antigen cocktail which showed 13 log-fold and 16 log-fold reductions in the parasite burden in liver and spleen, respectively, compared to adjuvant controls (p<0.0001) after 3 months of infectious challenge.
rCPC induced greater protection than both rCPA and rCPB, when injected alone.
