hamsters vaccinated with liposomal rCPC showed a parasitic burden of 5.963 log10±0.269 in the liver and 6.77 log10±0.463 in the spleen... Highest level of protection against L. donovani was however achieved in hamsters immunized with the liposomal triple antigen cocktail...
250.Rationale: The passage clearly reports that although rCPC alone induced greater protection than rCPA and rCPB, the triple antigen cocktail produced the highest protection overall (largest log-fold reductions in liver and spleen). Therefore the claim that rCPC alone provided the highest level of protection, surpassing the cocktail, contradicts the passage data and is not derivable.

251.Option: The absence of differences in life cycle duration between infected and non-infected ticks indicates that R. conorii conorii infection does not affect the overall fitness of Rh. sanguineus in laboratory conditions.
Evidence: No difference was observed between the duration of life cycle of R. conorii conorii-infected and non-infected ticks held at 25°C, but the average weight of engorged females and eggs was found to be significantly lower in infected ticks.
The average weight of 31 infected engorged females was 0.38111 g (range, 0.2518–0.5389 g) compared to 0.4749 g (range, 0.2333–0.6203 g) for six non-infected engorged females (p = 0.0136).
The average weight of eggs from one female infected tick was 0.2099 g (range, 0.0998–0.2976 g, 31 samples) compared to 0.2919 g (range, 0.1321–0.4271 g, 6 samples) for non-infected eggs (p = 0.0021).
251.Rationale: The passage does report no difference in life cycle duration at 25°C, but it also documents biologically meaningful differences in other fitness-related parameters (significantly lower weights of engorged females and their eggs). ‘‘Overall fitness’’ encompasses more than development time (e.g., fecundity), so the absence of a difference in life cycle duration alone does not justify the conclusion that infection does not affect overall fitness in laboratory conditions.

251.Option: The lower fecundity of R. conorii conorii-infected female ticks, indicated by reduced engorged female and egg weights, suggests that the prevalence of infection in a tick population would gradually decline and disappear without periodic re-infection.
Evidence: the average weight of engorged females and eggs was found to be significantly lower in infected ticks.
The average weight of 31 infected engorged females was 0.38111 g ... compared to 0.4749 g ... for six non-infected engorged females (p = 0.0136).
The average weight of eggs from one female infected tick was 0.2099 g ... compared to 0.2919 g ... for non-infected eggs (p = 0.0021).

Twelve successive generations were obtained, and the infected colony is still growing in our laboratory as of this writing (Figure 2). The PCR assay was positive for specimens of all stages of these generations for rickettsial detection.
These data suggest 100% transstadial and transovarial transmission of R. conorii conorii in Rh. sanguineus ticks.

The filial transmission rate (FIR ... ) of R. conorii conorii was 99.07% (107/108), 94.3% (66/70) and 95.5% (21/22) in larvae from several infected females of the 3rd, 4th, and 11th generations, respectively.
251.Rationale: While infected females and their eggs weigh less (a proxy for reduced fecundity), the passage also presents strong evidence of highly efficient vertical transmission and successful maintenance of infection over at least twelve laboratory generations with very high filial infection rates. Those results contradict the claim that infection prevalence would necessarily decline and disappear without periodic re-infection; in the laboratory the infection persisted despite reduced weights.

251.Option: The 100% transovarial transmission of R. conorii conorii in Rh. sanguineus over twelve generations implies that the infection can be maintained indefinitely in tick populations without the need for vertebrate reservoirs.
Evidence: Twelve successive generations were obtained, and the infected colony is still growing in our laboratory as of this writing (Figure 2). The PCR assay was positive for specimens of all stages of these generations for rickettsial detection.

For the 10th generation, all engorged nymphs (20/20), all adults randomly chosen (16/16), 4/4 females after laying eggs and the pools of eggs from these females tested positive by RT-PCR for rickettsial DNA. These data suggest 100% transstadial and transovarial transmission of R. conorii conorii in Rh. sanguineus ticks.

These data suggested that transstadial transmission (transfer of bacteria from stage to stage) but also transovarial transmission (TOT, the transfer of bacteria from adult female ticks to the subsequent generation of ticks via the eggs) of R. conorii conorii occurs in ticks and consequently that Rh. sanguineus ... could act not only as a vector but also as a reservoir of R. conorii conorii [1].
251.Rationale: The passage directly documents essentially complete transstadial and transovarial transmission across many laboratory generations and explicitly states that these data suggest Rh. sanguineus could act as a reservoir. From an epidemiological perspective, efficient vertical transmission that sustains infection across multiple consecutive generations supports the inference that the bacterium can be maintained within tick populations without requiring vertebrate reservoirs, at least under the laboratory conditions observed.

251.Option: The high mortality rate of R. conorii conorii-infected engorged nymphs and adults at 37°C compared to non-infected ticks suggests that temperature extremes may prevent the establishment of new MSF foci in warmer regions.
Evidence: Significantly more engorged, infected nymphs maintained at 37°C died than non-infected nymphs maintained at the same temperature (17–67%, 101/300 and 4–7%, 15/300, respectively, p = 0.0001).
In the third batch, which was maintained at 37°C for one month, 37.5–85% of infected ticks and 15–40% of non-infected ticks died (75/120 vs. 29/120, p = 0.0001).

In conclusion, infected ticks (nymphs and adults) had a comparatively higher rate of mortality at 37°C than at 25°C ... Moreover, only engorged nymphs and adults survived at high and low temperatures.

Rh. sanguineus has become one of the most globally widespread ticks because of its specialized feeding and association with domestic dogs ... This tick is highly adapted to warm climates ... Thus, Rh. sanguineus has spread globally between 50°N and 35°S [3]. However, MSF due to R. conorii conorii is known to be endemic in North Africa and southern Europe.
251.Rationale: The passage shows infected ticks experience higher mortality at 37°C in the laboratory, but it also states that Rh. sanguineus is highly adapted to warm climates and is widespread in warm regions. The leap from higher laboratory mortality at a specific elevated temperature to the conclusion that temperature extremes will prevent establishment of MSF foci in warmer regions involves ecological and epidemiological assumptions not demonstrated in the passage (e.g., field microclimates, host availability, behavioral adaptations). Thus the preventive-establishment inference is not directly supported by the presented laboratory data alone.

251.Option: The reactivation phenomenon observed in R. conorii conorii-infected ticks at elevated temperatures explains why infected ticks are more likely to survive winter conditions in temperate climates.
Evidence: In the 3 experiments with engorged nymphs, 88–100% of the R. conorii conorii-infected nymphs that had been previously maintained at 4°C died after being transferred to 25°C (53–80% died without molting) compared to 41% (62/150) of non-infected nymphs after one month (288/300 vs. 62/150, p = 0.0001).
In conclusion, infected ticks (nymphs and adults) had a comparatively higher rate of mortality at 37°C than at 25°C, and after a 4°C cooling when compared to non-infected ticks.
None of these stages, either infected or non-infected, could tolerate low (4°C) or high (37°C) temperatures for one month (data not shown).
251.Rationale: The passage contains no description of a ‘‘reactivation’’ phenomenon at elevated temperatures, nor does it present evidence that infected ticks are more likely to survive winter conditions; in fact, the data show higher mortality among infected ticks after exposure to cold (4°C) and upon transfer back to 25°C. Therefore the statement invoking reactivation as an explanation for greater winter survival is not supported by the passage and is contradicted by the reported mortality data.

252.Option: The addition of Quil A significantly (P<0.05) increased IgG2c but not significantly IgG1 titers by ∼5–10 fold (Fig. 5B–C). In summary, a single 1 µg dose of insect-cell derived CHIKV VLPs induced potent CHIKV-specific neutralizing antibody and IgG titers.
Evidence: The addition of Quil A significantly (P<0.05) increased IgG2c but not significantly IgG1 titers by ∼5–10 fold (Fig. 5B–C).
In summary, a single 1 µg dose of insect-cell derived CHIKV VLPs induced potent CHIKV-specific neutralizing antibody and IgG titers.
252.Rationale: Both sentences are verbatim statements from the Passage Body. The passage explicitly reports the Quil A effect on IgG2c (and not IgG1) and summarizes that a single 1 µg VLP dose induced potent neutralizing antibody and IgG titers, so the option is directly supported by the provided evidence.

252.Option: The complete CHIKV structural cassette was cloned downstream the strong polyhedrin promoter of the AcMNPV baculovirus (Ac-S27). Western analysis on cell and medium fractions of Sf21-cells that were infected with Ac-S27 indicated that glycoprotein processing (glycosylation, furin cleavage and surface localization) is efficient and that the functionality of E1 as fusion protein is retained.
Evidence: The coding sequence of the structural polyprotein was cloned downstream the polyhedrin promoter in an AcMNPV backbone, after which Sf21 cells were infected with a MOI = 10 TCID50/ml.
Expression analysis has made clear that E1 and E2 are both glycosylated and that a fraction of PE2 is processed by furin.

PNGase F treatment resulted in an expected reduction in molecular mass of CHIKV-E1 in both the cell fraction and purified VLPs (Fig. 1C, lane 2–5), indicating that E1 was efficiently N-glycosylated by the insect cells.
Treated cells displayed ring-like structures (Fig. 2, middle and bottom), indicating that the glycoproteins are indeed exposed at the surface of the infected cells.
Therefore, it can be concluded that E1 retains its fusogenic properties when expressed in Sf-cells.
252.Rationale: The Passage states the structural cassette was cloned downstream of the polyhedrin promoter in AcMNPV (Ac-S27). It documents glycoprotein glycosylation (PNGase F data), furin processing of PE2, surface exposure by immunofluorescence, and retention of E1 fusogenic function via syncytia assays. Those statements together support the option's conclusion about efficient processing and retained E1 function.

252.Option: The 1 µg non-adjuvanted VLP vaccine elicited a similar neutralizing antibody response as compared to 10 µg of inactivated virus (Fig. 5A), yet induced lower IgG2c titers (Fig. 5C). This might suggest that chemical inactivation results in generation of antibodies with reduced neutralizing activity, although further experimentation would be required to confirm this.
Evidence: The 1 µg VLP dose induced neutralizing antibody titers comparable with those observed after vaccination with 10 µg of inactivated CHIKV (Fig. 5A).
At the 1 µg dose, VLPs produced ∼10–20 fold, significantly lower antibody titers (IgG1 P<0.05, IgG2c P<0.01) than 10 µg inactivated CHIKV.
252.Rationale: The factual components of the option (similar neutralizing titers; lower IgG2c titers for 1 µg VLP versus 10 µg inactivated virus) are supported by the Passage. However, the subsequent inferential claim—that chemical inactivation might produce antibodies with reduced neutralizing activity—is not supported by the Passage. In fact, the Passage shows comparable neutralizing activity between 1 µg VLP and 10 µg inactivated CHIKV, so suggesting inactivation reduces neutralizing activity contradicts the data and represents an unsupported causal inference. Therefore the overall option is not derivable from the Passage.

252.Option: A single low-dose immunization with 1 µg of non-adjuvanted CHIKV VLPs provided complete protection against viraemia and foot swelling caused by CHIKV infection.
Evidence: All animals vaccinated with 1 µg VLPs were completely protected against viraemia (Fig. 6A).
The 1 µg dose of VLPs (with or without Quil A) provided complete protection against foot swelling, while the 0.1 µg dose reduced the peak foot swelling from 60–70% to 20–30% (Fig. 6B).
252.Rationale: The Passage explicitly reports that all mice receiving 1 µg VLPs were completely protected against viraemia and that the 1 µg dose (with or without adjuvant) provided complete protection against foot swelling. The option restates these results and is directly supported by the quoted data.

252.Option: The VLPs induced a balanced IgG1/IgG2c response, in contrast with CHIKV infection where IgG2c responses dominate [12]. Whether this would have an important effect on protection is unclear.
Evidence: CHIKV-specific IgG1 and IgG2c titers were determined by ELISA, and broadly similar titers were seen for IgG1 and IgG2c (Fig. 5B, C), which contrasts with natural CHIKV infection where IgG2c dominates [12].
These results illustrate that non-adjuvanted VLPs can provide complete protection against CHIKV-induced arthritis.
252.Rationale: The Passage explicitly states that IgG1 and IgG2c titers were broadly similar after VLP vaccination, and contrasts this pattern with natural infection where IgG2c dominates. The Passage does not provide data directly linking isotype balance to differences in protective efficacy beyond showing that the VLPs (which induce a balanced response) can protect in the mouse model; thus the statement that the importance of this balance for protection is unclear is a reasonable characterization consistent with the Passage and not contradicted by it.

253.Option: The persistence of H. ducreyi after MDA is due to the high rate of reinfection following clinical cure, which is more common in children than in adults.
Evidence: The infectivity of H. ducreyi is high [7], and reinfections following clinical and microbiological cure of H. ducreyi genital ulcers are common [8].
However, in rural villages of Lihir Island, MDA drastically decreased the proportion of ulcers containing T. p. pertenue DNA, but had limited impact on H. ducreyi [12].
Overall, 16 (21%, 95%CI 12–31) of 78 asymptomatic participants had H. ducreyi bacterial DNA on the skin, and 1 (1%) had T. p. pertenue DNA.
253.Rationale: While the passage states that reinfections following cure of H. ducreyi genital ulcers are common, that statement refers to genital ulcer disease and does not provide evidence that reinfection after clinical cure is the primary reason for community persistence after MDA, nor does it provide any evidence that reinfection is "more common in children than in adults." The passage provides direct evidence of asymptomatic skin carriage and environmental detection of H. ducreyi, which are alternative explanations for persistence after MDA. Because the age-specific claim (more common in children) is not supported by the text, the option overreaches and cannot be derived from the passage.

253.Option: The presence of H. ducreyi DNA in flies and bed linens indicates that these vectors are the primary source of new infections in the community.
Evidence: Flies (n = 10) were caught outside 10 houses of patients with ulcers; 9 (90%) of 10 flies had H. ducreyi DNA and 5 (50%) had amplifiable T. p. pertenue DNA (Table 3).

The buffer used to transport the flies yielded amplifiable H. ducreyi DNA in 6 (60%) of 10 specimens, but none yielded amplifiable T. p. pertenue DNA, perhaps suggesting different location of the two bacteria in flies (i.e. T. p. pertenue as a regurgitating pathogen in the gut), or dilution effect related to differential bacterial load of H. ducreyi and T. p. pertenue carried by affected flies.
Environmental contamination was observed in 3 (50%) of 6 bed sheets sampled from 6 households with index cases; 2 sheets had detectable H. ducreyi DNA and 1 had detectable T. p. pertenue DNA.
Nevertheless, there is no clear evidence of H. ducreyi or yaws transmission by insects or fomites.
253.Rationale: The passage documents detection of H. ducreyi DNA on flies and bed linens, but explicitly states that there is "no clear evidence of H. ducreyi or yaws transmission by insects or fomites." Detection of DNA on environmental samples supports the hypothesis that flies and linens could be reservoirs or vectors, but does not prove they are the primary source of new infections. The option asserts a causal primary-source role that is not established by the evidence in the passage, so it cannot be derived solely from the provided text.

253.Option: The persistence of H. ducreyi after mass azithromycin treatment may be explained by asymptomatic skin colonization, environmental reservoirs such as flies and bed linens, and incomplete penetration of azithromycin to the skin surface.
Evidence: However, in rural villages of Lihir Island, MDA drastically decreased the proportion of ulcers containing T. p. pertenue DNA, but had limited impact on H. ducreyi [12].

If H. ducreyi adheres and survives on the healthy skin of asymptomatic carriers, this could enhance its persistence in the community and its transmission, because skin-colonizing bacteria might escape systemic azithromycin treatment and could infect the skin after a minor abrasion.
Asymptomatic colonization of exposed skin may serve as a reservoir for transmission to family members, classmates, or playmates.
Overall, 16 (21%, 95%CI 12–31) of 78 asymptomatic participants had H. ducreyi bacterial DNA on the skin, and 1 (1%) had T. p. pertenue DNA.

Of 17 cultures obtained from asymptomatic contacts, 8 (47%) yielded no growth and 9 (53%) yielded small gram negative rods or coccobacilli. Six of the gram negative organisms were tested by PCR and 2 were confirmed to be H. ducreyi.
Flies (n = 10) were caught outside 10 houses of patients with ulcers; 9 (90%) of 10 flies had H. ducreyi DNA...
Environmental contamination was observed in 3 (50%) of 6 bed sheets sampled from 6 households with index cases; 2 sheets had detectable H. ducreyi DNA...
253.Rationale: This option synthesizes multiple findings and hypotheses explicitly presented in the passage: (1) mass azithromycin had limited impact on H. ducreyi despite reducing T. p. pertenue, (2) the authors hypothesize that adherence and survival on healthy skin of asymptomatic carriers could permit persistence because such skin-colonizing bacteria "might escape systemic azithromycin treatment," (3) the study detected H. ducreyi DNA and culture-confirmed organisms on intact skin of asymptomatic participants, and (4) H. ducreyi DNA was frequently detected on flies and in bed linens. These observations directly support the multi-factor explanation offered in the option, making it derivable from the passage.

253.Option: The high prevalence of H. ducreyi in asymptomatic children suggests that the organism is more transmissible through sexual contact than through non-intact skin contact.
Evidence: Both infections are thought to be exclusively transmitted through non-intact skin contact with infectious lesions.
Overall, 16 (21%, 95%CI 12–31) of 78 asymptomatic participants had H. ducreyi bacterial DNA on the skin...

Asymptomatic carriage of H. ducreyi was demonstrated by PCR in the genital mucosa of about 2% of sex workers in The Gambia [14], but no studies address whether either H. ducreyi or T. p. pertenue potentially colonize non-genital skin.
253.Rationale: The passage states that both infections are thought to be transmitted through non-intact skin contact, and documents substantial asymptomatic carriage on non-genital skin in children. Although H. ducreyi can be found in genital mucosa in sex workers, the data presented show non-genital asymptomatic skin carriage in children, which supports non-sexual transmission via skin contact rather than implying greater transmissibility through sexual contact. The option's assertion contradicts the transmission assumptions and available evidence in the passage and is therefore not supported.

253.Option: The limited impact of azithromycin on H. ducreyi is likely due to widespread resistance to macrolides in the H. ducreyi population.
Evidence: Cutaneous strains of H. ducreyi are also susceptible to macrolides in vitro [11].
However, in rural villages of Lihir Island, MDA drastically decreased the proportion of ulcers containing T. p. pertenue DNA, but had limited impact on H. ducreyi [12].

If H. ducreyi adheres and survives on the healthy skin of asymptomatic carriers, this could enhance its persistence in the community and its transmission, because skin-colonizing bacteria might escape systemic azithromycin treatment and could infect the skin after a minor abrasion.
253.Rationale: The passage explicitly notes that cutaneous strains are susceptible to macrolides in vitro, which argues against the conclusion that widespread macrolide resistance explains the limited impact of azithromycin. Instead, the authors propose alternative explanations (skin colonization that may escape systemic treatment, environmental reservoirs) to account for persistence after MDA. There is no evidence in the passage documenting macrolide resistance in the studied H. ducreyi population, so this option is not supported by the provided text.

254.Option: The frequent and prolonged interactions between humans and bats in buildings, combined with common attempts to remove bats, create repeated opportunities for pathogen exposure through both direct and indirect contact, as well as through domestic animals that may act as intermediate hosts.
Evidence: Over 70% of people reported bat use of their buildings for >5 years (n = 72), with bat presence for 5–10 years most commonly reported (χ2 = 36.52, P < 0.01, Fig 1).
Most properties (88%) had bat presence year-round (n = 90).
Close to half of participants (39%) reported direct contact with bats, including people touching bats (n = 40) and one report of being bitten.

Over 90% of respondents reported indirect contact with bats, mostly through interactions with their feces and urine (n = 98). Daily occurrences of indirect contact were reported by most participants (78%, χ2 = 285.06, P < 0.01, Fig 2B)...
Attempts to remove bats from buildings were reported by almost 80% of participants (n = 81). Of those reporting removal efforts, almost half reported direct contact with bats (n = 34).
Bats returned to the building after removal efforts in over 90% of cases (n = 76).
Domestic animals can also be exposed to these pathogens after contact with bats and their excreta or fluids [25].

Some also reported burning (n = 6), feeding domestic cats (n = 2), and burying bat carcasses (n = 1). Interestingly, 13 respondents reported seeing domestic animals (dogs, cats, and chickens) consume dead bats on their property, most often their own animals.
254.Rationale: The passage documents prolonged and frequent bat presence in buildings (year-round, >5 years), high prevalence of indirect contact (>90%, often daily) and substantial direct contact (~39%), and widespread human attempts to remove bats that often involve direct contact. It also reports domestic animals consuming dead bats and notes that domestic animals can be exposed to bat-borne pathogens. These facts together provide direct support for the inference that these spatially overlapping contacts create repeated opportunities for pathogen exposure via direct and indirect routes and through domestic animals that could act as intermediate hosts.

254.Option: The high frequency of indirect contact with bat excreta and the common practice of disposing of dead bats by throwing them over property lines suggest that bat-borne pathogens are likely to spread widely within communities through environmental contamination.
Evidence: Over 90% of respondents reported indirect contact with bats, mostly through interactions with their feces and urine (n = 98). Daily occurrences of indirect contact were reported by most participants (78%, χ2 = 285.06, P < 0.01, Fig 2B)...

We asked a subset of participants about the presence of dead bats on their properties (n = 79). Nearly 65% reported dead bats on properties (n = 51). Most removed dead bats, usually by throwing them over property lines (n = 30) or swept them outside (n = 7).
Interestingly, 13 respondents reported seeing domestic animals (dogs, cats, and chickens) consume dead bats on their property, most often their own animals.
254.Rationale: The passage clearly shows frequent indirect contact with bat excreta and that many people dispose of dead bats by throwing them over property lines, which could create environmental contamination risk. However, the statement that bat-borne pathogens are 'likely to spread widely within communities' goes beyond the data presented. The study documents contact patterns and disposal practices that increase exposure risk but provides no direct evidence of pathogen presence, environmental contamination measurements, or transmission events at community scale. Therefore the claim of likely widespread community spread via environmental contamination is not directly supported by the passage and is an overreach of the provided evidence.

255.Option: The use of household water treatment methods such as boiling significantly reduces the risk of infection from Cryptosporidium and Giardia in drinking water, as demonstrated by the 6 log10 reduction in viable parasites.
Evidence: To account for the effects of boiling water (the only point of use treatment method used by the study population) on parasite viability, we used WHO reported [39] 6 log10 reduction values for Cryptosporidium and Giardia from boiling (i.e. 6 log10 = 99.9999% reduction) and used household survey data collected from study households (n = 355) to estimate the fraction of households that boiled their drinking water (9% and 16% of deep and shallow tubewell users, respectively, reported boiling; see Table 1, B, boiling rates) (Appendix B.5 in Supporting Information).

For both parasites, diarrhea estimates were least sensitive to the volume of water ingested, the fraction of the population that treated water by boiling, the morbidity ratio, and recall bias as these parameters had sensitivity indices generally below 0.2.
255.Rationale: Although the model incorporates a WHO 6 log10 reduction for boiling and uses measured household boiling rates, the sensitivity analysis showed that the fraction of the population that boiled was among the parameters with low influence on diarrhea estimates (sensitivity indices generally below 0.2). Thus, the passage does not support the claim that household boiling is a key driver of variation in estimated disease burden; it reduces viability per-event but was not a major determinant of modeled burden variation in this study (likely because few households boiled).

255.Option: The spatial proximity of tubewells to household latrines is the main determinant of whether detected parasites are from human or non-human sources, directly influencing disease burden estimates.
Evidence: Scenario two (ENV) assumed the probability of a parasite shed by a human (H) was directly proportional to the ratio of the estimated environmental load of parasites shed by the population of humans to the total estimated environmental load shed by the population of humans, livestock, and domestic animals in Puri District [15].

To calculate the parasite environmental loading rates for humans and each animal species, we used four primary pieces of information as described previously [15]: parasite prevalence, parasite shedding rate, host population demographics, and host fecal production rates (see Appendix B.6.2 in S1 Supporting Information for further details).

Scenario three (MST) used microbial fecal source tracking (MST) results by year and tubewell type for the same protozoa-tested tubewell water samples [55]. MST uses molecular methods to detect enteric bacteria specific to a host to identify the likely source of fecal contamination. We assumed the probability of a parasite being shed by a human (H) was directly proportional to the ratio of the prevalence of human host-specific MST markers to the prevalence of non-human animal MST markers in tested tubewells (see H in Table 1 for MST scenario) (see Appendix B.6.3 in S1 Supporting Information for further details).
255.Rationale: The passage describes specific modeled approaches (ENV and MST) that infer human vs. non-human contribution from environmental loading calculations and molecular markers, and lists the data sources used (prevalence, shedding rates, demographics, fecal production). It does not state or present evidence that spatial proximity to latrines is the main determinant; that factor is not described as the primary basis for assigning human vs. non-human source attribution in the modeling. Therefore the option is not supported by the passage.

255.Option: The concentration of Cryptosporidium and Giardia in tubewell water varies significantly between years, which is the primary driver of differences in estimated disease burden between 2012 and 2013.
Evidence: A two-sample Kolmogorov-Smirnov test indicated concentrations were significantly different between years (P-value = < 0.001).
Comparing years, 2012 had a much higher estimated prevalence of diarrhea compared to 2013 under all scenarios.

In 2012, deep tubewell users were estimated to have higher diarrhea prevalence than shallow tubewell users, with Cryptosporidium often causing more symptomatic infections than Giardia across human pathogenicity scenarios. In 2013, deep and shallow tubewell users were estimated to have similar diarrhea prevalence, with considerably more symptomatic Cryptosporidium than Giardia infections.
255.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports that measured protozoal concentrations in tubewells differed significantly between 2012 and 2013 (KS test P < 0.001) and that the modeled diarrhea prevalence was much higher in 2012 than 2013 under all scenarios. Because the QMRA-SIR modeling uses year-specific concentration distributions as a primary input, the documented year-to-year concentration differences provide a direct, plausible explanation for the differing estimated disease burdens between the two years. The passage links higher 2012 concentrations to higher modeled prevalence, supporting this inference.

255.Option: The morbidity ratio of symptomatic infections for Cryptosporidium and Giardia is the most sensitive parameter in the model, leading to the greatest variation in estimated disease burden.
Evidence: Of the parameters examined, Cryptosporidium 7-day recall diarrhea period prevalence estimates were most sensitive to the rate parameter used in the dose-response model, the fraction of detected parasites assumed to be viable, and the method recovery of parasites from tubewell samples.
For Giardia, diarrhea estimates were most sensitive to model parameters representing viability, method recovery, and the concentration of Giardia observed in tubewell samples.

For both parasites, diarrhea estimates were least sensitive to the volume of water ingested, the fraction of the population that treated water by boiling, the morbidity ratio, and recall bias as these parameters had sensitivity indices generally below 0.2.
255.Rationale: The sensitivity analysis results reported in the passage identify different parameters (dose-response rate r, viability, method recovery, and for Giardia concentration) as the most influential on modeled diarrhea prevalence. The morbidity ratio is explicitly listed among the parameters with low sensitivity (indices generally below 0.2). Therefore the claim that the morbidity ratio is the most sensitive parameter is contradicted by the passage.

255.Option: The fraction of parasites in drinking water that are from humans versus non-human animal hosts and that are species infectious-to-humans has important implications for infection risk and diarrhea disease burden estimates.
Evidence: We simulated three different scenarios to estimate the fraction of parasites detected in a tubewell sample that was a species infectious-to-humans (Sp), with Sp determined as follows: ... Scenario one (HUM) assumed humans shed all of the detected parasites and 100% of both parasites were species infectious-to-humans. ... Scenario two (ENV) assumed the probability of a parasite shed by a human (H) was directly proportional to the ratio of the estimated environmental load of parasites shed by the population of humans to the total estimated environmental load shed by the population of humans, livestock, and domestic animals in Puri District [15]. ... Scenario three (MST) used microbial fecal source tracking (MST) results by year and tubewell type for the same protozoa-tested tubewell water samples [55].

Across all tubewell types and years, assuming all parasites were shed from humans (HUM) increased diarrhea prevalence estimates by two-fold or more, compared to using fractions based on environmental parasite loading estimates (ENV) for Puri District (see Table H in S1 Supporting Information).

The results indicate that as much as 65.8% or as little as 2.9% of the all-cause diarrhea burden in < 5 children can be attributed to waterborne infections from Cryptosporidium and Giardia in tubewell water. Depending on the scenario examined (HUM, MST, ENV), we see a wide range of estimated attributable fractions...
255.Rationale: The study explicitly modeled alternate assumptions about the fraction of detected parasites that are human-origin and species infectious-to-humans (HUM, ENV, MST), and the simulated disease burden varied markedly across these scenarios (e.g., two-fold differences and attributable fractions ranging from ~2.9% to 65.8%). These statements demonstrate that the human vs. non-human source fraction and species infectious-to-humans status are key factors affecting infection risk and diarrhea burden estimates in the modeling framework.

256.Option: The fact that 74.9% of patients reported trismus indicates that surgical release of ankylosis is ineffective and should be avoided in Noma survivors.
Evidence: except 23.3% (n = 38) and 4.9% (n = 8) medical records, which have reported intact mouth opening (Grade 0) and with no mouth opening information, respectively, the remaining 71.8% (n = 117) have reported grade-2 to grade-4 mouth opening limitations that related with the disease. Of these, 32.5% (n = 53), 25.2% (n = 41), and 13.5% (n = 22) have reported grade-1, grade-2, and grade-3 mouth opening limitations, respectively. Furthermore, a single locked jaw (grade-4) case has been reported.

Most survivors present with prominent facial deformities, trismus or ankylosis of the temporomandibular joint, and extensive muscle and skin contracture, which leads to the difficulty of opening and closing of the mouth, thereby, trouble in mastication and swallowing, oral incontinence, and speech difficulties [12].

Treatment of acute Noma includes transfusion of blood and intravenous fluids, administration of antibiotics, a high protein diet supplement, and debridement of necrotic areas [19]. The surgical phase is usually initiated 6 to 18 months after a period of quiescence [20].
256.Rationale: The option asserts (a) a specific percentage (74.9%) and (b) that surgical release is ineffective and should be avoided. The passage does not state 74.9%—it reports 71.8% (n = 117) with mouth-opening limitations and gives a detailed breakdown of grades; therefore the numeric claim is unsupported. More importantly, the passage describes trismus/ankylosis as a common sequela and notes that surgery is part of a later 'surgical phase' (initiated 6–18 months after quiescence), but it provides no data on the effectiveness or harm of surgical release of ankylosis. There is therefore no basis in the passage to conclude that surgical release is ineffective or should be avoided. Because the causal/effectiveness claim is not supported by the provided clinical data, the option is incorrect.

256.Option: The study's findings indicate that Noma is primarily a disease of malnutrition and poor sanitation, and therefore, improving public health infrastructure alone would eliminate the disease in Ethiopia.
Evidence: The major risk factors of ANUG/Noma in children are malnutrition, diarrheal diseases, measles infection, lack of proper sanitation, and poor living conditions.

Alternatively, typical predisposing risk factors in young adults, including those who serve in the military, include: poor oral hygiene, smoking, viral respiratory infections, and immune defects, such as HIV/AIDS [5–7].

Ethiopia is one of the countries in the Noma-belt region where people, particularly children, are profoundly affected by the disease. However, attention and resources dedicated to this devastating condition remains inadequate [24].
256.Rationale: While the passage clearly lists malnutrition and lack of sanitation among major risk factors, it also lists other predisposing factors (poor oral hygiene, smoking, viral infections, immune defects) and describes complex clinical and social sequelae. The statement that improving public health infrastructure alone would eliminate Noma is a strong causal claim that is not supported by the data presented. The passage does not provide evidence that addressing only infrastructure (or sanitation) would be sufficient to eliminate disease; it only identifies multiple risk factors and highlights inadequate resources. Therefore the inference that infrastructure improvements alone would eliminate Noma cannot be derived from the passage and is incorrect.

256.Option: The presence of dental anarchy in 14 patients suggests that Noma survivors are at increased risk of developing oral cancer due to chronic inflammation and tissue damage.
Evidence: Furthermore, out of the 109 medical records, 14 have reported different levels of disoriented teeth.
The disease is associated with very high morbidity and mortality [9]. Associated septicemia, intracranial infection, and pneumonia are the leading causes of death.

Most survivors present with prominent facial deformities, trismus or ankylosis of the temporomandibular joint, and extensive muscle and skin contracture, which leads to the difficulty of opening and closing of the mouth, thereby, trouble in mastication and swallowing, oral incontinence, and speech difficulties [12].
256.Rationale: The passage documents dental involvement including 14 records with disoriented teeth, and describes severe chronic tissue damage and functional impairment among survivors. However, it does not mention oral cancer, carcinogenesis, or provide data linking chronic Noma-related inflammation to subsequent development of oral malignancy. Inferring an increased oral cancer risk from the presence of disoriented teeth and tissue damage would require evidence not provided in this study. Therefore this specific inference is not supported by the passage and is incorrect.

256.Option: The high prevalence of combined facial defects and severe functional limitations in Noma survivors, particularly trismus and dental anarchy, indicates that the disease leads to substantial long-term disability and social marginalization.
Evidence: Nearly 56% (n = 91) of the total facial defects reported in the overall medical records are combined in form.

Most survivors present with prominent facial deformities, trismus or ankylosis of the temporomandibular joint, and extensive muscle and skin contracture, which leads to the difficulty of opening and closing of the mouth, thereby, trouble in mastication and swallowing, oral incontinence, and speech difficulties [12].

except 23.3% (n = 38) and 4.9% (n = 8) medical records, which have reported intact mouth opening (Grade 0) and with no mouth opening information, respectively, the remaining 71.8% (n = 117) have reported grade-2 to grade-4 mouth opening limitations that related with the disease. Of these, 32.5% (n = 53), 25.2% (n = 41), and 13.5% (n = 22) have reported grade-1, grade-2, and grade-3 mouth opening limitations, respectively. Furthermore, a single locked jaw (grade-4) case has been reported.
On the other hand, 25.8% (n = 42) are reported to have healthy teeth, while the remaining 66.9% (n = 109) have reported a varying number of missing teeth.
Often, those patients that survive are not only severely disfigured, but also rejected from family and society [22].
256.Rationale: The passage provides multiple lines of clinical and epidemiological evidence that support this inference: more than half (56%) of cases had combined multi-region facial defects; a large majority (71.8%) had significant mouth-opening limitations (trismus), with graded functional impairment; dental loss was common (66.9% with missing teeth); and survivors suffer prominent disfigurement and social rejection. These documented anatomic, functional, and psychosocial sequelae together logically support the conclusion that Noma results in substantial long-term disability and social marginalization among survivors. This inference is directly supported by the passage.

256.Option: The high prevalence of ANUG in children under 10 years in Ethiopia suggests that early intervention for ANUG could prevent the progression to Noma and reduce the overall disease burden.
Evidence: In many cases, the disease begins to develop from acute necrotizing ulcerative gingivitis (ANUG) [2].

ANUG is a non-contagious anaerobic infection associated with the proliferation of borrelia vincentii and fusiform bacteria [3,4]. The condition primarily affects children under the age of 10 years [3].
The major risk factors of ANUG/Noma in children are malnutrition, diarrheal diseases, measles infection, lack of proper sanitation, and poor living conditions.
256.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that Noma often begins as ANUG and that ANUG/Noma primarily affects children under 10 years, with risk factors such as malnutrition and poor living conditions. From an epidemiologic standpoint, if Noma commonly develops from ANUG, then timely recognition and treatment of ANUG in the high-risk pediatric population would plausibly interrupt that causal progression and reduce Noma incidence. While the passage does not provide intervention trial data, the inference that early intervention for ANUG could prevent progression and reduce disease burden is a logical and evidence-consistent public-health inference derived from the described natural history.

257.Option: The high level of non-synonymous polymorphisms in gp43 is due to sequencing errors and methodological artifacts, which artificially inflated the dN/dS ratio and led to false detection of positive selection.
Evidence: No false polymorphisms due to sequencing errors were found in the sequences.
Because no such pattern is observed in Figure 2, we consider this explanation unlikely.

Selection models provided a significantly better fit to the data than the neutral models (Table 5); comparisons of M2 versus single-ratio and neutral models yielded LRT values of 18.106 (df = 2, P<0.0001) and 9.14 (df = 2, P = 0.0103), respectively.
Using the Bayesian Empirical Bayes (BEB) analysis, 19 sites with a posterior probability greater than 95% of having a greater than 1 value were identified.
257.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that sequencing errors were not found in the sequences and rejects sequencing-error artifact as an explanation for elevated dN ("Because no such pattern is observed in Figure 2, we consider this explanation unlikely"). Moreover, independent codon-based likelihood analyses of gp43 produced highly significant LRTs and identified multiple sites with posterior probability >95% of ω>1, which supports genuine positive selection rather than an artifact. Therefore the claim that the high non-synonymous polymorphism is due to sequencing errors/methodological artifacts is not supported by the Passage Body.

257.Option: The gp43 gene is under strong purifying selection, as indicated by the low levels of genetic variation and the absence of significant positive selection in the M-K test.
Evidence: For the gp43 case, the M-K test yielded no significant results between H. capsulatum and P. brasiliensis (Fischer's exact test, P = 0.40, Table 3).

Selection models provided a significantly better fit to the data than the neutral models (Table 5); comparisons of M2 versus single-ratio and neutral models yielded LRT values of 18.106 (df = 2, P<0.0001) and 9.14 (df = 2, P = 0.0103), respectively.
Using the one-ratio model (M0), the average value of ω for the gp43 gene was 1.168 - significantly higher than for any of the housekeeping genes [48].
Using the Bayesian Empirical Bayes (BEB) analysis, 19 sites with a posterior probability greater than 95% of having a greater than 1 value were identified.
257.Rationale: Although the M-K test for gp43 was not significant (P = 0.40), multiple other analyses contradict the assertion of strong purifying selection. Codon-based likelihood models fit the gp43 data significantly better under models allowing sites with ω>1, the average ω under M0 is >1, and BEB identified many sites under positive selection. These results indicate evidence of positive selection at specific sites rather than uniform strong purifying selection. Thus the option's inference is not supported by the passage.

257.Option: The long time to the most recent common ancestor (TMRCA) of gp43 alleles indicates that the gene has undergone recent positive selection, leading to rapid fixation of beneficial mutations in the P. brasiliensis lineage.
Evidence: The TMRCAs for S1 and PS2 were estimated based on genetic variation at the gp43 locus and seven other nuclear loci. The results showed that the TRMCA for the gp43 alleles is longer than for any other gene in P. brasiliensis (Table 6), indicating that the polymorphism in gp43 is significantly older than the polymorphism in the other genes (Signed rank test; P<0.01).
This constitutes evidence for balancing selection [49],[50].
Conversely, the TRMCAs for cdc42, p27 and fks were significantly lower than the other genes as is expected if a gene is under positive directional selection.
257.Rationale: The passage states that gp43 has a longer TMRCA than other genes, indicating older polymorphism, and explicitly interprets this as evidence for balancing selection. Recent positive directional selection that causes rapid fixation would produce a reduced (not elongated) TMRCA and low polymorphism. The passage even contrasts genes with low TMRCA as expected under positive directional selection. Therefore the option's causal claim (long TMRCA indicates recent positive fixation) is contrary to the data and interpretations presented.

257.Option: The gp43 gene has evolved under positive directional selection, as evidenced by the significant results of the M-K test and the fixation of advantageous amino acid replacements in P. brasiliensis.
Evidence: For the gp43 case, the M-K test yielded no significant results between H. capsulatum and P. brasiliensis (Fischer's exact test, P = 0.40, Table 3).

Selection models provided a significantly better fit to the data than the neutral models (Table 5); comparisons of M2 versus single-ratio and neutral models yielded LRT values of 18.106 (df = 2, P<0.0001) and 9.14 (df = 2, P = 0.0103), respectively.
Using the Bayesian Empirical Bayes (BEB) analysis, 19 sites with a posterior probability greater than 95% of having a greater than 1 value were identified.

The TRMCA for the gp43 alleles is longer than for any other gene in P. brasiliensis..., indicating that the polymorphism in gp43 is significantly older than the polymorphism in the other genes (Signed rank test; P<0.01). This constitutes evidence for balancing selection [49],[50].
257.Rationale: This option incorrectly asserts that the M-K test provided significant evidence (the passage reports a non-significant M-K result for gp43, P = 0.40). While codon-based models do show evidence of sites with ω>1, the overall pattern for gp43 — including an unusually long TMRCA and the authors' interpretation that this is evidence for balancing selection — is inconsistent with a simple interpretation of positive directional selection leading to fixation. The passage supports maintenance of old polymorphisms (balancing selection) and site-specific positive selection, not a genome-wide recent directional sweep with fixation as claimed here.

257.Option: The persistence of non-synonymous intra- and trans-specific gp43 polymorphisms within and between lineages of the P. brasiliensis complex suggests they have been maintained by historical or contemporary selection.
Evidence: Selection models provided a significantly better fit to the data than the neutral models (Table 5); comparisons of M2 versus single-ratio and neutral models yielded LRT values of 18.106 (df = 2, P<0.0001) and 9.14 (df = 2, P = 0.0103), respectively.
Using the Bayesian Empirical Bayes (BEB) analysis, 19 sites with a posterior probability greater than 95% of having a greater than 1 value were identified.

The TMRCAs for S1 and PS2 were estimated based on genetic variation at the gp43 locus and seven other nuclear loci. The results showed that the TRMCA for the gp43 alleles is longer than for any other gene in P. brasiliensis (Table 6), indicating that the polymorphism in gp43 is significantly older than the polymorphism in the other genes (Signed rank test; P<0.01).
This constitutes evidence for balancing selection [49],[50].

Additional evidence for the balancing selection hypothesis in gp43 comes from the haplotype network previously described for this gene, in which several high frequency haplotypes are separated by long branches [7].
257.Rationale: The Passage Body reports multiple lines of evidence indicating selection acting on gp43: codon-based likelihood models identify numerous sites with ω>1 and significant LRTs, consistent with non-neutral evolution at specific amino acid positions. In addition, gp43 shows an unusually long TMRCA relative to other loci and a haplotype structure of high-frequency haplotypes separated by long branches; the authors explicitly interpret these observations as evidence for balancing selection. Together, these findings support the inference that persistent non-synonymous polymorphisms in gp43 have been maintained by historical or contemporary selection rather than neutral drift alone.

258.Option: Heterotypic neutralizing antibodies, particularly against DENV-4, were more strongly associated with protection against DENV-2 infection than homotypic antibodies, suggesting that cross-protection may be more effective than serotype-specific immunity in some cases.
Evidence: For DENV-2 clusters, age-adjusted models were better using both homotypic DENV-2 reference NTs (AUC = 0.740 [95% CI 0.460, 0.960]) and heterotypic DENV-4 reference NTs (AUC = 0.880 [95% CI 0.680, 1.000]) than other heterotypic NTs (Table 3).
Heterologous protection is thought to be at least partly mediated by temporary cross-protective neutralizing antibodies from earlier infections [12].
We showed an association between homotypic NTs and the likelihood of subsequent infection with DENV-1, -2 and -4.
258.Rationale: The passage reports that for DENV-2 clusters the age-adjusted model including heterotypic DENV-4 NTs had a higher AUC (0.880) than the model including homotypic DENV-2 NTs (0.740), indicating a stronger association of DENV-4 heterotypic NTs with PCR status for DENV-2 in this dataset. The authors also discuss the biological plausibility of heterologous (cross-) protection mediated by cross-reactive neutralizing antibodies. Taken directly from the reported model performance, it is therefore supportable from the passage to infer that heterotypic antibodies—particularly DENV-4 NTs—showed a stronger association with protection against DENV-2 in this study, consistent with the statement that cross-protection may in some cases outperform serotype-specific immunity. (Note: the passage data are limited in size and the wording here reflects an association reported in the study rather than proof of mechanism.)

258.Option: Pre-existing homotypic neutralizing antibody titers measured by PRNT were positively associated with protection against infection by DENV-1, -2, and -4, with the level of protection varying by serotype.
Evidence: We showed an association between homotypic NTs and the likelihood of subsequent infection with DENV-1, -2 and -4.
When all serotypes were combined, PCR status was significantly associated with the log of homotypic NT both alone and adjusted for age (Table 2).
For DENV-1 clusters, age-adjusted models to predict PCR status were better (based on AIC and AUC) when using homotypic DENV-1 NTs (AUC = 0.833 [95% CI 0.590, 1.000]) than heterotypic NTs (Table 3).
For DENV-4 clusters, age-adjusted models were better using homotypic DENV-4 NTs (AUC = 0.889 [95% CI 0.667, 1.000]) than heterotypic NTs (Table 3).
Homotypic reference strain NT cutoff values were 11, 11, 16 and 323 for all serotypes combined, DENV-1, DENV-4, and DENV-2, respectively.
For all serotypes combined, the observed odds ratio of becoming infected (i.e., being PCR positive) if day 0 homotypic NT was ≥11 was 0.153 (95% CI 0.023, 0.700).
258.Rationale: The passage explicitly states an association between pre-existing homotypic PRNT titers and subsequent infection status for DENV-1, -2 and -4. Serotype-specific analyses showed better model performance using homotypic NTs for DENV-1 and DENV-4, and homotypic DENV-2 NTs also contributed to prediction for DENV-2 clusters. The reported serotype-specific NT cutoff values and differing odds ratios demonstrate that the level of protection varies by serotype. Therefore this option is directly supported by the reported findings.

258.Option: The protective effect of neutralizing antibodies is consistent across all dengue serotypes, with similar titers required for protection regardless of the infecting serotype.
Evidence: Homotypic NT cutoff values were 11, 11, 16 and 323 for all serotypes combined, DENV-1, DENV-4, and DENV-2, respectively.

For DENV-1 clusters, age-adjusted models to predict PCR status were better ... when using homotypic DENV-1 NTs (AUC = 0.833 [95% CI 0.590, 1.000]) ... For DENV-4 clusters, age-adjusted models were better using homotypic DENV-4 NTs (AUC = 0.889 [95% CI 0.667, 1.000]) ... For DENV-2 clusters, age-adjusted models were better using both homotypic DENV-2 reference NTs (AUC = 0.740 [95% CI 0.460, 0.960]) and heterotypic DENV-4 reference NTs (AUC = 0.880 [95% CI 0.680, 1.000]) than other heterotypic NTs (Table 3).

Neutralizing antibodies (and especially cross-reactive antibodies) decrease substantially over time, however, and their kinetics can be quite variable depending on factors such as DENV serotype from previous and current infection, disease severity, host genetics and immunological status [17].
258.Rationale: The passage shows that the PRNT cutoff titers associated with apparent protection differ markedly by serotype (e.g., 11 for DENV-1 and DENV-4 versus 323 for DENV-2) and that model performance (AUC) differs by serotype and by whether homotypic or heterotypic NTs are used. The authors also note variability in antibody kinetics. Therefore it is not supported by the passage to claim consistent protective effect or similar titers across serotypes; the data indicate heterogeneity by serotype.

258.Option: The lack of clinical efficacy against DENV-2 in the recent dengue vaccine trial in Thailand may have partly been due to insufficiently high DENV-2 NTs, suggesting that vaccine-induced antibody levels should be optimized to match natural infection thresholds.
Evidence: Recently, the first human efficacy trial of a dengue vaccine candidate was completed in Thailand showing good neutralizing antibody response to all four DENV serotypes after vaccination, but no clinical efficacy against DENV-2 infection [4].
The relevance of DENV neutralizing antibodies for protection or modulation of DENV infection, therefore, remains unclear.

When different DENV-2 strains were used for PRNT, the AUC using Asian I 1974 strain appeared to have the best fit. The AUC using DENV-2 homologous virus was comparable to that using DENV-2 reference strain.
258.Rationale: Although the passage notes a vaccine trial with no clinical efficacy against DENV-2 and discusses neutralizing antibodies and strain-specific PRNT differences, it does not present direct evidence that vaccine failure was caused by insufficient DENV-2 NTs or that vaccine-induced antibody levels were below protective natural thresholds. The authors explicitly state that the relevance of neutralizing antibodies for protection 'remains unclear.' Inferring that insufficient vaccine-induced DENV-2 NTs caused the lack of efficacy goes beyond the data presented and therefore is not derivable solely from the passage.

258.Option: The study design using geographic clusters with matched exposed but uninfected individuals provides definitive evidence that neutralizing antibodies are the primary mechanism of protection against dengue virus infection.
Evidence: Day 14/15 PCR positive subjects were classified as "susceptible" to DENV infection since they had confirmed infection.

In order to create a "non-susceptible" comparison group, we identified contact subjects from the two studies who lived in the same house as another subject who was DENV positive on day 0, but were themselves DENV negative by nested PCR and IgM ELISA on both days 0 and 15 (KPSII study), or by NS1 ELISA and IgM ELISA on both days 0 and 14 (DEVOL study). These DENV negative "non-susceptible" subjects were presumed to have a high likelihood of exposure to DENV during the cluster investigation, yet still did not become infected.
We showed an association between homotypic NTs and the likelihood of subsequent infection with DENV-1, -2 and -4.

Altogether, 49 subjects were available for analysis: 17 PCR positive "susceptible" subjects (six DENV-1, five DENV-2 and six DENV-4), and 32 PCR negative "non-susceptible" subjects (13 from DENV-1 clusters, 10 DENV-2 and 9 DENV-4).
These CIs should not be used to assess statistical significance because they are post-hoc comparisons based on the cutoffs indicated by the ROC plot analysis.
258.Rationale: The geographic cluster design with exposed but uninfected comparators allowed measurement of pre-exposure neutralizing antibody titers and demonstrated associations between homotypic NTs and subsequent infection status. However, the study is observational with a small sample (49 subjects), reports associations (not causal proof), and the authors caution about post-hoc comparisons and uncertainty in estimates. The passage does not claim, and the data do not constitute, 'definitive evidence' that neutralizing antibodies are the primary mechanism of protection. Other mechanisms and confounding cannot be excluded based on the presented design and results.

259.Option: Sub-microscopic P. vivax infections are primarily detected in symptomatic patients due to the higher parasite density associated with clinical illness, as shown by the higher PCR prevalence in fever patient surveys.
Evidence: In 13 surveys of clinical malaria suspects conducted in different transmission settings (1997–2010), the prevalence of P. vivax infections determined by LM varied widely from 0.7% to 86.0% of the patients. Although PCR detected more P. vivax infections in some studies, overall it did not have a significantly higher prevalence than LM in fever patients (Wilcoxon matched-pairs signed rank test, P = 0.278, Fig. 8).
This suggests that amongst symptomatic patient populations SM P. vivax and P. falciparum are not prevalent.
The proportion of individuals with symptoms ranged from 0.0% to 11.4%, averaging 2.8%. This means that between 88.6 and 100.0% (average 97.1%) of individuals with SM P. vivax were asymptomatic.
259.Rationale: The passage directly reports that among fever/clinically suspected patients PCR did not detect significantly more P. vivax than LM (P=0.278) and explicitly states that SM P. vivax is not prevalent among symptomatic patients. Additionally, SM infections are described as overwhelmingly asymptomatic (average 97.1% asymptomatic). Therefore the claim that SM P. vivax infections are primarily detected in symptomatic patients and that fever-patient PCR prevalence is higher is contradicted by the passage.

259.Option: The high proportion of asymptomatic sub-microscopic P. vivax infections in the community is primarily due to recent antimalarial drug use, which rapidly reduces parasite density and prevents symptom onset.
Evidence: The proportion of individuals with symptoms ranged from 0.0% to 11.4%, averaging 2.8%. This means that between 88.6 and 100.0% (average 97.1%) of individuals with SM P. vivax were asymptomatic.
Only one survey reported that recent antimalarial treatment (<4 weeks prior to survey) was associated with a significant increase in risk of having SM P. vivax infection [33].
259.Rationale: While the passage documents a very high proportion of SM P. vivax infections being asymptomatic, it provides only a single survey linking recent antimalarial use to increased risk of SM infection. That single observation does not support the stronger causal statement that recent drug use is the primary reason for the high proportion of asymptomatic SM infections. The passage does not present sufficient evidence to attribute primary causation to recent antimalarial use.

259.Option: The use of whole blood for DNA extraction in PCR assays significantly increases the risk of false-positive results in detecting sub-microscopic P. vivax infections, which undermines the reliability of PCR-based surveillance.
Evidence: All surveys described the type of blood and DNA extraction method used for PCR analysis. Fourteen surveys used blood from filter paper while 17 surveys used whole blood (primarily>150 µL) for DNA extraction.

However, the average prevalence of SM P. vivax in community surveys determined by PCR using whole blood was significantly higher than that using blood from filter papers (Mann Whitney test, P = 0.004).
The prevalence and relative proportion of SM P. vivax could vary because of differences in quality of microscopy and PCR.
259.Rationale: The passage reports that PCR using whole blood yields a significantly higher detected prevalence of SM P. vivax compared to filter-paper samples, which is consistent with greater sensitivity with larger blood volumes. There is no statement in the passage indicating these higher detections are false positives or that whole-blood methods reduce reliability. The evidence supports a sensitivity difference rather than increased false positivity, so the specific claim about increased false-positive risk and undermined reliability is not supported by the passage.

259.Option: The prevalence of sub-microscopic P. vivax infections is significantly higher in areas with high transmission intensity compared to low transmission intensity, as indicated by the higher number of PCR-positive cases in these regions.
Evidence: A negative relationship between LM P. vivax prevalence and proportion of SM P. vivax infections was identified (Fig. 7), with LM P. vivax prevalence identified as a significant factor for predicting the proportion of samples that are PCR positive/LM negative (SM) in cross-sectional surveys where the LM P. vivax prevalence is less than 45% (P < 0.001, Deviance = 7.32, df = 29).
The regression equation describing this relationship is: proportion SM P. vivax  =  exp (−0.162–4.163× LM P. vivax prevalence).
259.Rationale: The passage describes a negative relationship between LM-determined prevalence (a proxy for transmission intensity) and the proportion of infections that are sub-microscopic: as LM prevalence increases (higher transmission), the proportion SM decreases. Thus the statement that SM prevalence is higher in high-transmission areas is contrary to the findings reported in the passage and is not supported.

259.Option: The relative proportion of sub-microscopic P. vivax infections is significantly higher than that of the sympatric P. falciparum in the same study settings, and this proportion increases in areas with lower transmission intensity.
Evidence: The relative proportion of P. vivax not detected by LM (i.e. SM infections) ranged from 1.5% to 100.0%, with a mean of 69.5% (Fig. 3).
The prevalence of SM P. falciparum ranged from 0.0% to 61.3%, constituting, on average, 55.7% of P. falciparum infections (range 0.0% to 100.0%, Fig. 4).

The prevalence of SM P. vivax in these communities was not significantly different to that of SM P. falciparum (Wilcoxon matched-pairs signed rank test, P = 0.78, Fig. 5A). However, the average relative proportion of SM was significantly higher in P. vivax infections compared to P. falciparum in the same study (Wilcoxon matched-pairs signed rank test, Median difference  = 5.2%, P = 0.045, Fig. 5B).

A negative relationship between LM P. vivax prevalence and proportion of SM P. vivax infections was identified (Fig. 7), with LM P. vivax prevalence identified as a significant factor for predicting the proportion of samples that are PCR positive/LM negative (SM) ... The regression equation describing this relationship is: proportion SM P. vivax  =  exp (−0.162–4.163× LM P. vivax prevalence).
259.Rationale: The passage presents direct comparisons showing the mean relative proportion of SM infections is higher for P. vivax (mean 69.5%) than for sympatric P. falciparum (mean 55.7%), and reports a statistically significant higher relative proportion in P. vivax within the same studies (P=0.045). It also reports a negative relationship between LM prevalence (proxy for transmission intensity) and the proportion of SM P. vivax infections, with the regression equation indicating that the proportion SM increases as LM prevalence decreases. Together, these findings support the inference that P. vivax has a higher relative SM proportion than sympatric P. falciparum and that the SM proportion increases in lower-transmission settings.

260.Option: The high level of awareness about NTDs among medical and nursing students in Egypt is capable of achieving significant and long-lasting results in controlling these diseases.
Evidence: Only one fourth (26%) of the students knew the meaning of NTDs; the main source of their knowledge is social media, followed by epidemiology course and mass media (TV/Radio).
Low percentage of the studied medical and nursing students (ranged from 4.3 to 23.9%) correctly identified the names of individual NTDs (Table 2).

Very low percentage of the students (5%) knew specific activities for NTDs prevention and control in Egypt, while more than one third of them (34%) expressed their willingness to participate in these control activities.
Nearly half of the students agreed on the poor level of awareness regarding NTDs in Egypt (Table 3).
260.Rationale: The passage reports low levels of knowledge (only 26% knew the meaning of NTDs; very low percentages correctly identified NTDs or control activities) and that nearly half perceived poor public awareness. Therefore the premise of this option (a high level of awareness among students) is contradicted by the study data; any claim that such high awareness exists and would achieve lasting control cannot be derived from the passage.

260.Option: The lack of explicit mention of the term 'neglected tropical diseases' (NTDs) in the epidemiology course materials at Cairo University is associated with a significant knowledge gap among medical and nursing students regarding NTDs and their control activities.
Evidence: Neither the term “neglected tropical disease” nor the abbreviation “NTDs” was mentioned explicitly in the course materials or specifications.
Only one fourth (26%) of the students knew the meaning of NTDs; the main source of their knowledge is social media, followed by epidemiology course and mass media (TV/Radio).

The epidemiology course was mentioned as a single source of knowledge by 8 students (4.3%), while it was mentioned with other sources by 6 students (3.3%); 4 of them mentioned it with mass media (TV/Radio) and social media.
Low percentage of the studied medical and nursing students (ranged from 4.3 to 23.9%) correctly identified the names of individual NTDs (Table 2).
Very low percentage of the students (5%) knew specific activities for NTDs prevention and control in Egypt...
260.Rationale: The passage documents two co-occurring facts: the epidemiology course materials do not explicitly mention the term 'NTDs', and students display substantial knowledge gaps (low awareness, poor ability to identify NTDs, and little knowledge of control activities). From an epidemiological perspective, this supports an association between omission in curriculum content and poor student knowledge. The study design (cross-sectional) cannot prove causation, but the observed co-occurrence justifies stating an association, which is what this option asserts.

260.Option: The lack of NTDs in the curriculum is not a significant factor in the low knowledge levels among students, as most students learned about NTDs through social media and mass media.
Evidence: Neither the term “neglected tropical disease” nor the abbreviation “NTDs” was mentioned explicitly in the course materials or specifications.
Only one fourth (26%) of the students knew the meaning of NTDs; the main source of their knowledge is social media, followed by epidemiology course and mass media (TV/Radio).

The epidemiology course was mentioned as a single source of knowledge by 8 students (4.3%), while it was mentioned with other sources by 6 students (3.3%); 4 of them mentioned it with mass media (TV/Radio) and social media.
Low percentage of the studied medical and nursing students (ranged from 4.3 to 23.9%) correctly identified the names of individual NTDs (Table 2).
260.Rationale: The passage shows that social media is the most commonly reported source among the subset who knew the meaning of NTDs, but it does not indicate that 'most students' learned about NTDs through media—only 26% knew the meaning at all. Moreover, the passage documents curriculum omission and low knowledge but does not provide evidence to rule out the curriculum as a contributing factor. Therefore the strong claim that curriculum omission is not a significant factor is not supported by the data in the passage.

260.Option: The inclusion of NTDs in the epidemiology course curriculum would likely lead to a significant increase in student willingness to participate in NTDs control activities.
Evidence: Neither the term “neglected tropical disease” nor the abbreviation “NTDs” was mentioned explicitly in the course materials or specifications.
Only one fourth (26%) of the students knew the meaning of NTDs; the main source of their knowledge is social media, followed by epidemiology course and mass media (TV/Radio).

The epidemiology course was mentioned as a single source of knowledge by 8 students (4.3%), while it was mentioned with other sources by 6 students (3.3%); 4 of them mentioned it with mass media (TV/Radio) and social media.

Very low percentage of the students (5%) knew specific activities for NTDs prevention and control in Egypt, while more than one third of them (34%) expressed their willingness to participate in these control activities.
260.Rationale: While the passage indicates low curriculum coverage and reports a 34% willingness to participate, it contains no evidence testing whether including explicit NTD content in the course would increase willingness. Predicting a causal effect of curriculum change on willingness is speculative and not derivable solely from the cross-sectional data presented. The passage does not provide pre/post or experimental data to support this causal inference.

260.Option: The poor public awareness of NTDs in Egypt is primarily due to insufficient media coverage of control programs, despite adequate knowledge among healthcare students.
Evidence: Only one fourth (26%) of the students knew the meaning of NTDs; the main source of their knowledge is social media, followed by epidemiology course and mass media (TV/Radio).
Low percentage of the studied medical and nursing students (ranged from 4.3 to 23.9%) correctly identified the names of individual NTDs (Table 2).
Nearly half of the students agreed on the poor level of awareness regarding NTDs in Egypt (Table 3).
Very low percentage of the students (5%) knew specific activities for NTDs prevention and control in Egypt...
260.Rationale: The passage documents poor public awareness and low student knowledge—contradicting the claim of 'adequate knowledge among healthcare students.' It also does not provide evidence about media coverage of control programs or that insufficient media coverage is the primary cause of poor public awareness. Therefore the asserted primary attribution to media coverage and the premise of adequate student knowledge are not supported by the study findings.

261.Option: The method is capable of detecting the effect of benznidazole on T. cruzi epimastigotes at concentrations as low as 0.0195 μg/mL, indicating high sensitivity to drug action.
Evidence: each well of the VDRL plate was filled with 100μL of LIT medium in the presence or absence of 4x104 epimatigotes per well, containing 7 different concentrations of benznidazole: 0, 1.95x10-2, 7.81 x10-2, 31.25 x10-2, 1.25, 5 and 20 μg/mL (ranging from 0.075 to 76.8μM)

The activity of the Biospeckle pattern as a function of benznidazole concentration shows curves that fit similar Polynomial regressions at all the tested times (Fig 13) with a decrease of the activity as a function of concentration and a clustering of the values (mean of the clustered low concentration values of 48.39±2.91, see S1 Data for details) of the four time curves up to a concentration of benznidazole of 1.25μg/mL.

A closer look at Figs 12, 13, 14, 15 and 16 reveals that all the time curves have the smallest value of the activity of the Biospeckle pattern in a range that goes from 5 to 20μg/mL (19.2 to 76.8μM, respectively). This range of values represents the concentration of benznidazole that gives maximal inhibition under the assayed conditions. However, 1.25μg/mL (4.8μM) could be considered as the inflexion point that suggests the existence of a minimal concentration that affects the activity of the Biospeckle pattern, separating the effect of benznidazole at low concentrations from the effect at higher concentrations.

As these results show, the present method reveals in a relatively short time, that the effect of benznidazole is dependent on the elapsed time and concentration of the drug, as has been demonstrated by other authors in assays that require a much longer time [3].
261.Rationale: While the experiment included a very low concentration (1.95x10^-2 μg/mL = 0.0195 μg/mL), the Passage indicates that the Biospeckle responses for the lower concentration range cluster together and that the inflection/minimal concentration that affects activity appears near 1.25 μg/mL. The strongest inhibition (minimal activity) was observed in the 5–20 μg/mL range. Thus the Passage does not support the specific claim that the method reliably detects benznidazole effects at 0.0195 μg/mL; instead it indicates detectable and meaningful changes emerge at higher concentrations (around ≥1.25 μg/mL and maximal at 5–20 μg/mL). Therefore the statement claiming high sensitivity down to 0.0195 μg/mL is not supported by the provided text.

261.Option: The Biospeckle pattern is sensitive to changes in parasite motility and aggregation, which may be influenced by drug-induced taxis, suggesting that the method detects early behavioral responses to benznidazole.
Evidence: the activity of the Biospeckle pattern refers to the values of A, B or C, depending on the equation that is selected for calculation.
As shown in Fig 15, there is an instantaneous effect at 1min that remains with similar activity values at least for one hour for all the benznidazole concentrations.

As these results show, the present method reveals in a relatively short time, that the effect of benznidazole is dependent on the elapsed time and concentration of the drug, as has been demonstrated by other authors in assays that require a much longer time [3].

Traditional IC50 determination requires a measure of the viability of the organism as a function of concentration of the tested drug. In this work the Biospeckle pattern is taken as a measure of the activity of the parasites and the relation of this pattern to viability and drug action has to be determined.
261.Rationale: The Passage demonstrates that the Biospeckle method detects rapid changes in a metric labeled 'activity' (including an instantaneous effect at 1 min), which implies sensitivity to early changes in parasite behavior or activity. However, the text does not describe or provide evidence that the Biospeckle signal specifically reflects motility versus aggregation, nor does it mention drug-induced taxis. Moreover, the authors explicitly state that the relationship between the Biospeckle pattern and parasite viability/drug action still needs determination. Therefore inferring detection of motility/aggregation changes or taxis is speculative and not derivable solely from the Passage.

261.Option: The activity of the Biospeckle pattern is proportional to the quantity of parasites present in the assay well and remains stable despite desiccation up to around 200 minutes.
Evidence: This indicates that if the density is kept constant, the Biospeckle pattern is proportional to the quantity of parasites in the test wells within the evaluated range.
Therefore, the system is detecting the parasites in the whole well.

the results indicate that although there is a marked desiccation effect, the Biospeckle pattern remains stable up to around 200 minutes, indicating that as was seen in Figs 2, 3 and 4 (Effect of Parasite Quantity), the Biospeckle pattern under the conditions of this work, reveals the total number of parasites within the well of the VDRL plate.
261.Rationale: The Passage explicitly states that, with density held constant, the Biospeckle signal is proportional to parasite quantity and that the system detects parasites in the whole well. It also directly reports that despite measurable desiccation, the Biospeckle pattern remained stable up to about 200 minutes under the experimental conditions. Both elements of the option are therefore supported by verbatim statements in the Passage.

261.Option: The Biospeckle pattern remains stable over time because the VDRL plate's flat-bottomed design minimizes optical distortion and prevents evaporation-induced signal drift.
Evidence: The VDRL plate was recommended as a specific and reproducible system by the World Health Organization in 1980 [11].
The design and dimensions of the VDRL flat test wells are adapted to optical microscopes and magnifiers and they are available in most health centers worldwide.
