Although the hotline existed and was intended to respond to reports, the study respondents reported low personal use: "very few participants said they would call 117 right away" and "Only three participants had ever called 117." Additionally, one respondent reported calling six times and "not receiving a response," which undermines the claim of rapid response times. Therefore the assertion of wide use and trust is not supported by the passage.

292.Option: The use of chlorine spray by ambulance workers was widely perceived as a necessary and effective public health measure, with most participants expressing confidence in its safety and efficacy.
Evidence: About one-third of participants expressed the belief that chlorine was harmful to people.

Chlorine suffocates patients. If a patient has been vomiting and toileting then in the end he or she is suffocated with chlorine, because when they spray the patient in the vehicle they usually close the door where the patient will be placed and no air enters the cabin.
Two respondents even believed it might be the chlorine spray that was killing people and not Ebola:

One day the team came to pick up a female patient … She was strong enough to walk from the house to the ambulance but when they arrived at the treatment center the lady became so weak and couldn’t walk because of the chlorine sprayed in the ambulance. It was the chlorine. She didn’t have Ebola. The chlorine weakened her. The effect of chlorine is just like when one is locked up in a newly painted room. It suffocates people just like Shelltox (insecticide spray).
292.Rationale: The passage reports substantial concerns about chlorine: "About one-third of participants expressed the belief that chlorine was harmful to people," and multiple respondents provided vivid accounts alleging harm from chlorine spray. While a few participants used chlorine for hand-washing, the text does not indicate that most participants expressed confidence in its safety or efficacy. Thus the claim that chlorine spray was widely perceived as necessary and trusted is not supported.

292.Option: The high Ebola-related fatality rate in 2014 in Sierra Leone, combined with the delayed reporting of test results, likely led to widespread public skepticism about the accuracy and reliability of the Ebola laboratory test.
Evidence: As of January 2016, Ebola resulted in 3,956 cumulative confirmed deaths in Sierra Leone [3].
returning results on an Ebola test took a minimum of 48 hours [22].
When asked what they knew about the laboratory Ebola test, nearly half of participants said they did not know anything.
Four respondents mentioned the test occurring only after a person had died.
Many respondents expressed concern or even disbelief that the testing process was valid:

I hear they take the blood specimen of people but even there it is not clear to me. They can take the blood of somebody under the pretext that he/she has Ebola but at the end of the day you will hear that that person has been discharged.
292.Rationale: The passage documents a large number of deaths ("3,956 cumulative confirmed deaths") and a delay in laboratory reporting ("returning results on an Ebola test took a minimum of 48 hours"). It also documents low public knowledge about the laboratory test ("nearly half of participants said they did not know anything"), reports that some thought testing happened only after death, and explicitly notes that "many respondents expressed concern or even disbelief that the testing process was valid." Combining these facts, it is epidemiologically reasonable to infer that the high fatality context plus delayed results contributed to widespread skepticism about test accuracy and reliability among the study participants.

292.Option: Participants’ reluctance to call 117 was primarily due to financial barriers and lack of access to transportation, rather than fear or mistrust of the response system.
Evidence: In response to the question “what would you do if you had a fever?” very few participants said they would call 117 right away.
Three-quarters of participants said that if they got a fever they would go to a local health center before calling 117.
One participant said that he could not afford to go to a health center and so he would pray.

Participants were more willing to call 117 if they observed symptoms in another person. However, about one-third of participants stated they were afraid of community members’ anger if they were to call.
With 117, the ambulance takes people away and sometimes they never come back … I think this is what frightens people.
292.Rationale: The passage identifies fear and mistrust as prominent reasons for not calling 117: respondents feared ambulances taking people away and not returning, and "about one-third" feared community anger. While one participant mentioned inability to afford a health center, financial barriers are presented as one factor for a single respondent, not the primary or dominant reason. The text more directly supports fear/mistrust as key drivers of reluctance to use the hotline, so the option's attribution of primary cause to financial/transport barriers is not supported by the passage.

292.Option: Despite high reported intentions to use the Ebola response system, participants in this study expressed significant apprehension about calling the national hotline and using the response system, which may have contributed to low actual usage rates during the outbreak.
Evidence: In September 2014, over 90% of participants in a national household survey said they would go to a hospital or health facility if they thought they had Ebola [10].
In December 2014, 95% agreed that a person had a higher chance of survival if s/he immediately went to a health facility [11].
In response to the question “what would you do if you had a fever?” very few participants said they would call 117 right away.
Only three participants had ever called 117.

Participants were more willing to call 117 if they observed symptoms in another person. However, about one-third of participants stated they were afraid of community members’ anger if they were to call.
With 117, the ambulance takes people away and sometimes they never come back … I think this is what frightens people.
292.Rationale: The passage contrasts high reported intentions from national surveys ("over 90%... would go to a hospital"; "95% agreed...higher chance of survival") with the qualitative findings from this study, where "very few participants said they would call 117 right away" and "Only three participants had ever called 117." The study participants expressed fear and apprehension (fearing ambulances and community backlash), which plausibly helps explain the low observed usage among these respondents. Thus the inference that apprehension despite high stated intentions may have contributed to low actual usage rates is supported by the evidence presented.

293.Option: Because ZIKV immunology is still in its infancy, we hypothesize that, similar to other flaviviruses (e.g., Yellow fever virus (YFV), Japanese encephalitis virus (JEV), and Tick-borne encephalitis virus (TBEV)), nAbs are a critical component of the protective immune response.
Evidence: Because human infections with ZIKV have historically been sporadic, and, until recently, limited to small-scale epidemics, neither the disease caused by ZIKV nor the molecular determinants of immunity have been well characterized.
The envelope (E) glycoprotein is a target for broadly protective neutralizing antibodies in ZIKV and other flaviviruses and is an attractive candidate immunogen for inclusion in ZIKV vaccines [20].

Accordingly, we recently developed an animal model for Asian-lineage ZIKV infection in Indian-origin rhesus macaques (Macaca mulatta), and demonstrated that immune responses elicited by infection with Asian ZIKV protected against detectable viremia following rechallenge with the same homologous ZIKV strain [22].

Serum neutralizing antibody responses also were measured by plaque reduction neutralization tests (PRNT90), and all animals exhibited neutralizing antibody (nAb) titers ≥20 as early as 21 dpi (Fig 4D).

However, we did observe an increase in nAb titers after re-challenge despite the lack of detectable viremia in the animals (Fig 4D) suggesting that low level virus replication did occur, indicating protection from disease rather than sterilizing immunity.
293.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that ZIKV immunology is not well characterized and identifies the E glycoprotein as a target for broadly protective neutralizing antibodies in ZIKV and other flaviviruses. The experimental data show that animals developed measurable neutralizing antibody titers after infection and that nAb titers rose after rechallenge even when viremia was undetectable, consistent with nAbs playing a key role in protection. Thus the inference that neutralizing antibodies are a critical component of protective immunity is directly supported by the passage.

293.Option: The continuing efficacy of YFV vaccines in reducing yellow fever disease in both Africa and South America suggests that the antigenic differences between individual YFV strains do not facilitate resistance to vaccine-induced immunity, i.e., diversity of YFV sequences appears to have little consequence for the use of currently formulated vaccines and/or the development of new YFV vaccines.
Evidence: ZIKV was first isolated from the blood of a sentinel rhesus monkey during yellow fever virus surveillance studies in the Zika forest of Uganda [6].

Because human infections with ZIKV have historically been sporadic, and, until recently, limited to small-scale epidemics, neither the disease caused by ZIKV nor the molecular determinants of immunity have been well characterized.
293.Rationale: The passage only mentions yellow fever virus (YFV) in a historical context and does not present any data or discussion about YFV vaccine efficacy, YFV antigenic diversity, or vaccine escape. The provided quotes show only that YFV was part of historical surveillance and that ZIKV immunity is poorly characterized; they do not support any inference about the impact of YFV sequence diversity on vaccine performance. Therefore the specific claim about continuing YFV vaccine efficacy and antigenic consequences cannot be derived from the passage.

293.Option: The protection against detectable viremia from homologous and heterologous rechallenge suggests that the immunologic barrier for complete protection may be comparatively low, such that vaccines with acceptable safety may have desirable efficacy even if they are not highly immunogenic.
Evidence: Immune responses against MR766 provided protection against viremia in all three macaques when re-challenged with an Asian ZIKV strain and dose that productively infected 8/8 naïve macaques.
Viral RNA was undetectable in plasma (Fig 2B), saliva, and urine at all timepoints through 21 days post re-challenge.

However, we did observe an increase in nAb titers after re-challenge despite the lack of detectable viremia in the animals (Fig 4D) suggesting that low level virus replication did occur, indicating protection from disease rather than sterilizing immunity.

Because human infections with ZIKV have historically been sporadic, and, until recently, limited to small-scale epidemics, neither the disease caused by ZIKV nor the molecular determinants of immunity have been well characterized.
293.Rationale: While the passage demonstrates that prior infection conferred protection against detectable viremia on homologous and heterologous rechallenge, it does not quantify the immunologic threshold required for protection nor does it provide evidence that vaccine-level immune responses (of differing magnitudes) would be sufficient. The observed protection could reflect robust natural immune responses (including nAbs and cellular immunity), but extrapolating this to claim that the immunologic barrier is 'comparatively low' and therefore that modestly immunogenic vaccines will be efficacious is not supported by the data presented. The passage even notes that precise molecular determinants of immunity are not well characterized, limiting such conclusions.

293.Option: The protection from detectable viremia observed in animals previously infected with African ZIKV suggests that immunity elicited by any Asian ZIKV infection should be sufficient to confer complete protection against all Asian ZIKV strains, similar to what has been described for YFV, TBEV, and JEV.
Evidence: The E protein amino acid identity among all Asian lineage ZIKVs is >99%, and as a group, these are only ~96% and 97% amino acid identical to representative East African and West African viruses, respectively [21].

Therefore, we hypothesized that if immunity elicited by infection of macaques with MR766 protects against reinfection with Asian ZIKV infection, immunity elicited by any Asian ZIKV infection should be sufficient to confer complete protection against subsequent Asian ZIKV reinfection.
Immune responses against MR766 provided protection against viremia in all three macaques when re-challenged with an Asian ZIKV strain and dose that productively infected 8/8 naïve macaques.

There was a significant increase in protection against infection with Asian ZIKV (Exact unconditional test) in animals previously infected with African ZIKV (n = 3) as compared to animals only challenged with Asian ZIKV (n = 8, p = 0.0016).
293.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence that infection with African-lineage MR766 protected macaques against an Asian-lineage challenge and notes that Asian ZIKV strains are highly (>99%) similar in E protein sequence; the authors explicitly hypothesize reciprocity of protection among Asian strains. This supports the inference that immunity elicited by one Asian ZIKV strain might protect against other Asian strains. However, the option extends this claim by adding 'similar to what has been described for YFV, TBEV, and JEV.' The passage contains no data or discussion about YFV, TBEV, or JEV cross-protection or vaccines, so the comparative clause is unsupported. Because the option makes an unsupported comparative statement beyond the passage's data, the option as written is not fully derivable from the passage.

294.Option: Mebendazole treatment administered during pregnancy may have masked the true effect of prenatal helminth infection on infant cognitive development due to high cure rates and reduced reinfection.
Evidence: At ANC visits, women were given IPTp, iron and folic acid as part of the ANC package in Benin. Women were also given a total of 600 mg of mebendazole (100 mg twice daily for 3 days) at the first ANC visit and again if they were tested positive for helminths at second ANC visit. Intake of mebendazole was not directly monitored.
294.Rationale: The passage documents that mebendazole was administered and that intake was not directly monitored, which establishes that treatment occurred but does not provide any evidence about cure rates, reinfection rates, or their effects on measured associations. The option asserts a specific mechanism (high cure rates and reduced reinfection) that is not supported or quantified in the Passage Body. Because the required claim about high cure rates and masking the true effect relies on information not provided in the passage, it cannot be derived solely from the passage and is therefore incorrect.

294.Option: The lack of association between maternal helminth infection and infant helminth infection suggests that prenatal helminth exposure does not influence the child's susceptibility to infection.
Evidence: As part of the APEC study, stool samples were collected from a subsample of children (N = 186) at 6, 9 or 12 months for examination for helminths.
The prevalence (95% CI) of helminth infection among children by age one was 32.8% (26.0%-39.6%).
294.Rationale: The passage reports that infant stool samples were collected in a subsample and provides the prevalence of helminth infection among children, but it does not present any analysis or result comparing maternal helminth status to infant helminth status. There is no statement of a lack of association between maternal and infant infections. Therefore the conclusion that prenatal exposure does not influence child's susceptibility cannot be derived from the passage.

294.Option: The association between maternal helminth infection and infant cognitive development is likely mediated by maternal anemia and low birth weight, as these are known risk factors for poor neurodevelopment.
Evidence: Over the past decades, many studies have confirmed helminth infection during pregnancy as a risk factor for maternal iron deficiency (ID) and anemia[3,13,14].
However, evidence remains limited on the effects on adverse birth outcomes such as low birth weight (LBW) [15] which is known to be associated with poorer cognitive function in children.[16]
Infant characteristics at birth or age one-year including birth weight, preterm birth and infant helminth infection were hypothesized to be within the causal pathway (as mediators).

With the exception of the association between gross motor scores and the occurrence of helminth infection over the course of pregnancy, sensitivity analyses performed by further adjusting for infant preterm status and weight-for-age, yielded similar results in the association between infants gross motor function and prenatal helminth infection.
294.Rationale: While the passage acknowledges that helminth infection is a risk factor for maternal iron deficiency/anemia and that low birth weight is associated with poorer cognitive outcomes, the study treated birth outcomes and related infant characteristics as potential mediators and performed sensitivity analyses adjusting for preterm status and weight-for-age. Those sensitivity analyses yielded largely similar results (at least for gross motor outcomes), which does not support a clear conclusion that the observed association is 'likely mediated' by anemia and low birth weight. The study did not present direct mediation analyses demonstrating that maternal anemia or LBW explain the association; therefore the mediation claim cannot be derived from the Passage Body.

294.Option: Prenatal helminth infection, particularly with hookworms, is associated with poorer cognitive and gross motor functions in one-year-old children, independent of infant helminth infection and other known risk factors.
Evidence: The difference in mean ELC scores between children whose mothers were infected with helminths at first ANC visit and those whose mothers were not infected with any helminth remained significant after adjusting for maternal education, child sex and HOME score (p = 0.013).

Pregnant women who were infected with helminths at least, once during pregnancy had children with poorer ELC scores, thus-4.4 (95% CI: -7.2 to-1.5) compared to those of mothers who were never infected during pregnancy after adjustment (see Table 5).

After adjusting for gravidity, maternal education, family possession, child sex and HOME score, helminth infection at first ANC visit was negatively associated with infant gross motor function (p = 0.028).

We observed that mothers who were infected with hookworms during the first ANC visit had children who scored less in the gross motor scale, -4.9 (95% CI: -8.6 to-1.1), compared to those whose mothers were never infected with hookworms at first ANC visit.
Infant characteristics at birth or age one-year including birth weight, preterm birth and infant helminth infection were hypothesized to be within the causal pathway (as mediators).
294.Rationale: The Passage Body provides clear evidence that prenatal helminth infection is associated with lower Early Learning Composite (ELC) scores and that hookworm infection at first ANC is associated with lower gross motor scores after adjustment for multiple confounders (maternal education, HOME score, child sex, etc.). Thus the association with poorer cognitive and gross motor outcomes is supported. However, the option additionally asserts that these associations are 'independent of infant helminth infection and other known risk factors.' While the analyses adjusted for many covariates (the 'other known risk factors'), the passage explicitly treats infant helminth infection as a possible mediator and does not present adjustment results demonstrating independence from infant helminth infection. Because independence from infant helminth infection is not demonstrated in the Passage Body, the full statement in the option cannot be derived and is therefore incorrect.

294.Option: The low sensitivity of the Kato-Katz technique for helminths may have resulted in measurement error, which could have biased the observed association between maternal helminth infection and infant cognitive development toward the null.
Evidence: Thick smears of retrieved stool samples were immediately prepared and assessed using the Kato-Katz technique as described by the WHO[21]. One of the two slides prepared from each sample was systematically and independently examined by two laboratory technicians under a microscope. The mean of the two results was calculated and reported.

T. trichiura, A. lumbricoides and S. mansoni were not independently considered as specific exposures in the univariate and multivariate analyses owing to their low prevalence throughout pregnancy. Also, due to the low intensity of hookworms in our study population, the WHO cut-off for classification[22] was not applicable.
294.Rationale: The passage describes use of the Kato-Katz technique and notes low prevalence and low intensity of some species in this population, but it does not state that the Kato-Katz technique has low sensitivity or quantify any resulting measurement error. The option asserts a methodological limitation (low sensitivity causing bias toward the null) that is not discussed or evidenced in the Passage Body. As the claim depends on external knowledge about diagnostic sensitivity and its likely impact on bias—not provided in the passage—it cannot be derived solely from the Passage Body and is therefore incorrect.

295.Option: The detection of Bunyamwera virus in Anopheles gambiae and Culex univittatus in the Lake Victoria region indicates that these species are the primary vectors responsible for human outbreaks of Bunyamwera fever in East Africa.
Evidence: Among mosquito pools from 32 species sampled in Homa Bay County, Bunyamwera virus (Orthobunyavirus) was the only vertically transmitted arbovirus (pathogenic to vertebrates) detected.

It was identified by HRM analysis (Fig 2A), culture, and DNA sequencing (143 nt; 100% identity to GenBank accession KM507344, S1 Fig) from female Anopheles gambiae from Luanda Nyamasare (1/77 pools) and Cx. univittatus from Rusinga (2/31 pools) (Table 2) that were reared from larvae sampled in November 2012.

Although widespread arboviral activity in human populations has been documented in the Lake Victoria and Lake Baringo basins, the role of vertical transmission among mosquito vectors in the maintenance of arboviruses within ecologies remains poorly understood [17].
295.Rationale: The passage documents detection of Bunyamwera virus in An. gambiae and Cx. univittatus reared from larvae, but it does not provide any data on human outbreaks, attack rates, vector competence in nature, or comparative importance of these species in human transmission. Detection alone does not establish that these species are the primary vectors responsible for human outbreaks. The authors also note that the role of vertical transmission and maintenance of arboviruses in ecologies remains poorly understood, underscoring the lack of evidence to make the broader claim about primary responsibility for human outbreaks.

295.Option: The vertical transmission of Bunyamwera virus in Anopheles gambiae and Culex univittatus indicates that these mosquito species are more likely to transmit the virus to humans than other known vectors in the region.
Evidence: Among mosquito pools from 32 species sampled in Homa Bay County, Bunyamwera virus (Orthobunyavirus) was the only vertically transmitted arbovirus (pathogenic to vertebrates) detected.

It was identified by HRM analysis (Fig 2A), culture, and DNA sequencing (143 nt; 100% identity to GenBank accession KM507344, S1 Fig) from female Anopheles gambiae from Luanda Nyamasare (1/77 pools) and Cx. univittatus from Rusinga (2/31 pools) (Table 2) that were reared from larvae sampled in November 2012.
vertical transmission of arboviruses from adult female mosquitoes to their offspring can also maintain viruses in circulation for generations within mosquito populations [6–10].
295.Rationale: The study demonstrates vertical transmission (detection in adults reared from field-collected larvae) in these species, but it does not compare transmission efficiency to humans across species or provide epidemiologic data on human infection risk. The passage does not present evidence that these species are more likely than other vectors to transmit Bunyamwera virus to humans; therefore the comparative statement is not supported by the provided data.

295.Option: The presence of Bunyamwera virus in adult mosquitoes reared from field-collected larvae suggests that the virus is primarily maintained in the environment through contamination of aquatic breeding sites rather than through vertical transmission.
Evidence: vertical transmission of arboviruses from adult female mosquitoes to their offspring can also maintain viruses in circulation for generations within mosquito populations [6–10].

To investigate how vertical transmission in different mosquito species in Homa Bay and Baringo counties of Kenya may be maintaining endemic arboviruses in circulation, we set out to identify arboviral infections in laboratory-reared adults of field-caught larvae and pupae.
Among mosquito pools from 32 species sampled in Homa Bay County, Bunyamwera virus (Orthobunyavirus) was the only vertically transmitted arbovirus (pathogenic to vertebrates) detected.

It was identified by HRM analysis (Fig 2A), culture, and DNA sequencing (143 nt; 100% identity to GenBank accession KM507344, S1 Fig) from female Anopheles gambiae from Luanda Nyamasare (1/77 pools) and Cx. univittatus from Rusinga (2/31 pools) (Table 2) that were reared from larvae sampled in November 2012.
295.Rationale: The authors framed the study to detect vertical transmission by testing adults reared from field-collected immatures, and they explicitly describe Bunyamwera virus as a 'vertically transmitted arbovirus' detected in those adults. The findings are consistent with vertical transmission (infection present in immature-derived adults), not with primary maintenance by contamination of aquatic breeding sites. The passage provides no evidence of environmental contamination of breeding water as the primary maintenance mechanism; thus the option contradicts the study's interpretation.

295.Option: The absence of Bunyamwera virus in Baringo County samples suggests that the virus is not capable of persisting in mosquito populations outside the Lake Victoria region due to climatic or ecological differences.
Evidence: It was identified by HRM analysis (Fig 2A), culture, and DNA sequencing (143 nt; 100% identity to GenBank accession KM507344, S1 Fig) from female Anopheles gambiae from Luanda Nyamasare (1/77 pools) and Cx. univittatus from Rusinga (2/31 pools) (Table 2) that were reared from larvae sampled in November 2012.
However, no vertically transmitted pathogenic arbovirus was detected in Baringo County samples.
295.Rationale: While the study reports no vertically transmitted pathogenic arbovirus detected in the Baringo County samples, this negative finding in the sampled locations and times does not justify the broad conclusion that the virus cannot persist outside Lake Victoria or that climatic/ecological differences are the cause. The passage contains no ecological or longitudinal data testing persistence across regions or explaining absence; absence in these samples could reflect limited sampling, temporal variation, or low prevalence rather than incapacity to persist elsewhere.

295.Option: The detection of Bunyamwera virus in adult mosquitoes reared from field-collected larvae indicates that this pathogenic arbovirus can persist through transstadial development and may be maintained in mosquito populations via vertical transmission, suggesting a potential reservoir role for Anopheles gambiae and Culex univittatus in the Lake Victoria region.
Evidence: vertical transmission of arboviruses from adult female mosquitoes to their offspring can also maintain viruses in circulation for generations within mosquito populations [6–10].

To investigate how vertical transmission in different mosquito species in Homa Bay and Baringo counties of Kenya may be maintaining endemic arboviruses in circulation, we set out to identify arboviral infections in laboratory-reared adults of field-caught larvae and pupae.
Among mosquito pools from 32 species sampled in Homa Bay County, Bunyamwera virus (Orthobunyavirus) was the only vertically transmitted arbovirus (pathogenic to vertebrates) detected.

It was identified by HRM analysis (Fig 2A), culture, and DNA sequencing (143 nt; 100% identity to GenBank accession KM507344, S1 Fig) from female Anopheles gambiae from Luanda Nyamasare (1/77 pools) and Cx. univittatus from Rusinga (2/31 pools) (Table 2) that were reared from larvae sampled in November 2012.
295.Rationale: The study detected Bunyamwera virus sequences and cultured virus from adult mosquitoes that were reared from field-collected larvae, and the authors explicitly describe it as a 'vertically transmitted arbovirus' in their samples. Detection in laboratory-reared adults originating from immature stages supports persistence through development (transstadial/vertical transmission) and is consistent with maintenance within mosquito populations. Given the species in which the virus was found (An. gambiae and Cx. univittatus) in the Lake Victoria region, it is epidemiologically reasonable—based on the presented data—to infer these species may act as reservoirs maintaining the virus locally. The passage does not claim definitive proof of reservoir status, but the evidence supports the inferred potential reservoir role.

296.Option: The inter-population redundancy of amino acid sequences in V1 and V4 indicates that these regions are functionally constrained and therefore less likely to contribute to immune evasion in syphilis patients.
Evidence: V1 and V4 presented a strong shared sequence capacity. The sequence IASDGGAIKH in V1 was observed in five strains (5/14) and DVGHKKENAANVNGTVGA in V4 was shared across seven strains (7/14).

Theoretically, through gene conversion, variations in the V regions would generate millions of chimeric tprK variants, resulting in a constant alteration in the T. pallidum antigenic profile [21]. Therefore, the tprK gene is acknowledged to have a pivotal role in immune evasion and pathogen persistence [22, 23].
296.Rationale: The passage documents inter-population redundancy in V1 and V4 (shared amino acid sequences across multiple strains). However, concluding that this redundancy proves functional constraint and that these regions are therefore less likely to contribute to immune evasion is not supported by the passage. The authors also emphasize that gene conversion can generate vast antigenic diversity in the V regions and that tprK plays a pivotal role in immune evasion. The presence of shared sequences could reflect functional constraints, recent common ancestry, or selective processes, but the passage does not provide direct evidence distinguishing these causes or stating that V1/V4 do not contribute to immune evasion. Therefore the specific inference in the option is not derivable solely from the passage.

296.Option: The low frequency of minor variants in tprK (mostly 1–5%) indicates that these variants are non-functional and do not contribute to the antigenic diversity of T. pallidum during primary infection.
Evidence: Apart from the detected predominant sequence within seven V regions, there was still a mixture of minor variants in each V region. Altogether, the frequency of all detected minor variants was almost below 20% (231/237) (Fig 2A).
The major proportion of the variants in primary syphilis samples was in the 1–5% (181/237) range, and the lowest was in the 10–60% (22/237) range.

Theoretically, through gene conversion, variations in the V regions would generate millions of chimeric tprK variants, resulting in a constant alteration in the T. pallidum antigenic profile [21]. Therefore, the tprK gene is acknowledged to have a pivotal role in immune evasion and pathogen persistence [22, 23].
296.Rationale: While the passage shows that most minor variants occur at low frequencies (1–5%), it does not state that these variants are non-functional or irrelevant to antigenic diversity. On the contrary, the authors describe a mechanism (gene conversion) that can generate extensive antigenic diversity and assert that tprK has a pivotal role in immune evasion. Low-frequency variants can represent a reservoir for selection and may become relevant under changing immune pressures. The option's categorical claim that low-frequency variants do not contribute to antigenic diversity is not supported by the presented data.

296.Option: The high frequency of predominant sequences in V1 and V6 regions suggests that these regions are under stronger purifying selection compared to other variable regions.
Evidence: The number of distinct sequences in the seven regions of the tprK gene ranged from 21–76, with the highest number in V6 and the lowest in V1 across all samples.
there was a predominant sequence in each V region of ten samples directly from primary syphilis patients, and the proportion of this sequence was almost above 80%.
The frequency was found to be decreased in the V2, V5, V6 and V7 regions, and the frequency in V6 of X-6 was even lower at 20.8%.
296.Rationale: The passage documents that V6 has the highest number of distinct sequences (high diversity) and that V1 has the lowest number. It also reports predominant sequences in many V regions, but it specifically notes decreased predominant-sequence frequency in V6 (and others) for some samples. The claim that both V1 and V6 are under stronger purifying selection is inconsistent with the documented high diversity in V6. The passage does not provide direct measures of selection pressure (e.g., dN/dS), so inferring stronger purifying selection for these regions—especially V6—is not supported by the presented evidence.

296.Option: The length variation in tprK sequences, particularly in V3, V6, and V7, is likely due to random insertions and deletions during PCR amplification, which introduces artifacts into the sequencing data.
Evidence: The length of the captured sequences within each V region was also found to be variable, particularly in V3, V6 and V7, with 11 or 12 forms.

When the length of all sequences within each sample was calculated, the length of all differed by 3 bp or multiples of 3 bp. Interestingly, although the lengths of V3, V6 and V7 were particularly variable across all populations, these lengths continued to change by 3 bp. In this regard, the lengths of V1, V4 and V5 appeared to vary in intervals of 6 bp.
No sequence yielded a tprK frame shift or premature termination.
296.Rationale: The passage reports systematic length variation that changes by 3 bp (codon-preserving increments) and notes no frameshifts or premature terminations, which argues against random PCR-induced indel artifacts (which would be expected to produce arbitrary-length changes including frameshifts). The described patterns (multiples of 3 bp and consistent intervals) are more consistent with biologically constrained insertion/deletion processes that preserve reading frame rather than random PCR errors. The methods also describe careful amplification and validation; the option's claim that length variation is likely PCR artifact is not supported by the passage.

296.Option: The presence of a high proportion of a predominant sequence and numerous low-frequency minor variants in the tprK gene of primary syphilis patients suggests a fitness-based evolutionary strategy where dominant sequences are optimized to evade immune recognition, while minor variants may represent transient diversity that could be selected for under immune pressure.
Evidence: Within the tpr family, the antigen-coding tprK has been found to be the direct target of the human immune response [14],

Theoretically, through gene conversion, variations in the V regions would generate millions of chimeric tprK variants, resulting in a constant alteration in the T. pallidum antigenic profile [21]. Therefore, the tprK gene is acknowledged to have a pivotal role in immune evasion and pathogen persistence [22, 23].
there was a predominant sequence in each V region of ten samples directly from primary syphilis patients, and the proportion of this sequence was almost above 80%.

Apart from the detected predominant sequence within seven V regions, there was still a mixture of minor variants in each V region. Altogether, the frequency of all detected minor variants was almost below 20% (231/237) (Fig 2A).
The major proportion of the variants in primary syphilis samples was in the 1–5% (181/237) range, and the lowest was in the 10–60% (22/237) range.
296.Rationale: The passage establishes that tprK is a target of the human immune response and that gene conversion can generate extensive antigenic diversity for immune evasion. Empirically, the authors observed a dominant (predominant) sequence in most V regions at high proportions (often >80%) alongside numerous low-frequency minor variants (most 1–5%). This distribution is consistent with an evolutionary scenario in which a currently fit (dominant) variant predominates in the face of host immunity while a pool of low-frequency variants provides standing variation that could be favored if immune pressures change. While the passage does not perform formal fitness assays, the combination of mechanistic background (gene conversion and immune evasion) and the observed frequency spectrum supports the proposed fitness-based strategy as a valid epidemiological and evolutionary inference.

297.Option: The fact that leprosy prevalence remains high in this region is likely due to the underreporting of PKDL cases, as both diseases share similar clinical presentations and diagnostic challenges.
Evidence: Considerable underreporting of PKDL is suspected.
An important condition in the differential diagnosis of PKDL is leprosy.
bringing total number of prevalent active leprosy cases to 44 ... on a population of 25,686 screened, i.e. a prevalence rate of 17.1 per 10,000.
297.Rationale: The passage states that PKDL may be underreported and that leprosy is an important differential diagnosis for PKDL, which supports the possibility of diagnostic confusion. However, the passage does not provide evidence that the high measured leprosy prevalence is caused by misclassified PKDL cases. The data show an observed leprosy prevalence of 17.1 per 10,000 based on clinical confirmation, and there is no statement or analysis in the passage attributing this high leprosy prevalence to underreported PKDL. Therefore the causal claim that leprosy prevalence remains high because of PKDL underreporting is not supported by the passage.

297.Option: The observed prevalence of leprosy in the study population (17.1 per 10,000) is consistent with national elimination targets, suggesting that the disease has been effectively controlled at the local level.
Evidence: bringing total number of prevalent active leprosy cases to 44 ... on a population of 25,686 screened, i.e. a prevalence rate of 17.1 per 10,000.
The global target of a prevalence of less than 1 case per 10,000 population was achieved by 2000.
297.Rationale: The passage defines the elimination target as a prevalence of less than 1 case per 10,000. The observed local prevalence of 17.1 per 10,000 is substantially higher than that target. Thus the observed prevalence is not consistent with the elimination target and does not indicate effective local control.

297.Option: The low prevalence of PKDL (1.1 per 10,000) in the studied region indicates that the elimination of visceral leishmaniasis has been successfully achieved and sustained in this area.
Evidence: three were confirmed, equivalent to a prevalence rate of 1.2 per 10,000.
Considerable underreporting of PKDL is suspected.
The importance of PKDL lies in the fact that PKDL lesions are infectious to sand flies and may thus sustain transmission of L.donovani, acting as inter-epidemic reservoirs.
The Muzaffarpur Health and Demographic Surveillance System (HDSS) ... 339 new VL cases were reported from 2007 to 2018
297.Rationale: The passage reports a low measured PKDL prevalence (1.2 per 10,000) but also explicitly states that considerable underreporting of PKDL is suspected. Moreover, PKDL prevalence alone is not presented as a direct indicator of successful elimination of visceral leishmaniasis (VL); the passage discusses VL case counts over time and the role of PKDL as a potential reservoir but does not claim that a low measured PKDL prevalence proves VL elimination. Therefore the inference that PKDL prevalence demonstrates successful and sustained VL elimination is not derivable from the passage.

297.Option: The high spatial clustering of leprosy cases within 25 meters of an affected household suggests that leprosy transmission is primarily driven by close household contact, making household-based screening sufficient for effective case detection.
Evidence: Four out of 44 cases (9.1%) were found among 402 persons living in the same household as a leprosy patient, who make up 1.2% of the population.
Among the neighbors at less than 25 meters of an active leprosy case, who make up 3.5% of the total population, 15.9% of the leprosy patients were found.

With those living at more than 100 meters as reference category we found rate ratios of 6.3 (95% CI 1.9–21.0) and 3.6 (95% CI 1.3–10.2) respectively for those living in the same household and neighbors at less than 25 meters of a leprosy patient. Beyond 25 meters differences observed were no longer statistically significant
297.Rationale: The passage provides strong evidence of spatial clustering at very close distances (household and <25 meters), indicating increased risk in immediate neighbors as well as household members. However, the data show elevated risk both within households and among immediate neighbors (<25 m), so transmission cannot be ascribed solely to within-household contact. The claim that household-based screening alone would be sufficient is not supported because a substantial fraction of cases occur among very close neighbors outside the household. The passage does not evaluate the sufficiency of household-only screening or provide evidence that it would detect most cases.

297.Option: Leprosy is far from eliminated and requires intensified case finding approaches, as evidenced by a prevalence rate of 17.1 per 10,000 in a rural area of Bihar, India, which is 17 times the elimination target.
Evidence: bringing total number of prevalent active leprosy cases to 44 ... on a population of 25,686 screened, i.e. a prevalence rate of 17.1 per 10,000.
The global target of a prevalence of less than 1 case per 10,000 population was achieved by 2000.

The fact that the leprosy is now considered eliminated from India as the target has been met, in all probability means that less resources are made available, less case finding activities are conducted, and therefore the incidence does not change. To examine this hypothesis, more data on leprosy at population level are needed.
Blok et al. predict that leprosy is likely to remain a problem in highly endemic regions.
297.Rationale: The passage explicitly states the elimination target (<1 per 10,000) and reports a local measured prevalence of 17.1 per 10,000, which is well above the target (approximately 17-fold higher). The authors also discuss how reduced resources and reduced case-finding following declared 'elimination' can mask ongoing transmission and call for more population-level data; models predict persistence of leprosy in highly endemic areas. These statements support the inference that leprosy is not eliminated in this rural area and that intensified case-finding and control efforts are warranted.

298.Option: Monoclonal antibodies that bind to the N- and C-terminus of GP1 and GP2 are sufficient to provide complete protection in guinea pigs when administered at any time after infection.
Evidence: An initial characterization demonstrated they bound to a variety of GP segments, and that all 8 MAbs were able to pull down ZEBOV GP1,2 in an immunoprecipitation assay.
The combination of neutralizing MAbs (1.5 mg 1H3+1.5 mg 2G4+2 mg 4G7) was given to guinea pigs either 1 day before, or 1, 2 or 3 days after a 1000 LD50 GA-ZEBOV infection, and survival followed.
In contrast all animals receiving the neutralizing MAb combination at 2 dpi. survived.
When the treatment was given on 3 dpi the percent survival dropped to 66.7% with a mean time to death of 11.17±3.09 days.
298.Rationale: The passage states MAbs bind to a variety of GP segments and pull down GP1,2, but it does not state specifically that MAbs binding the N- and C-terminus of GP1 and GP2 are sufficient. Crucially, timing matters: the neutralizing combination gave complete protection when given at 2 dpi but survival dropped when given at 3 dpi. Therefore the claim that such MAbs provide complete protection 'when administered at any time after infection' is not supported by the data.

298.Option: The failure of KZ52 to protect non-human primates despite success in guinea pigs suggests that monoclonal antibodies are unlikely to be effective in higher animal models.
Evidence: One neutralizing MAb KZ52 was 100% efficacious in guinea pigs, but did not protect NHPs [26], [35].
The MAbs tested so far are not 100% efficacious in all animal models therefore further research is needed for more effective antibodies.
298.Rationale: The passage documents a single example (KZ52) where an antibody protected guinea pigs but failed in NHPs, and more generally notes variable efficacy across models. However, this does not justify the broad inference that monoclonal antibodies are unlikely to be effective in higher animal models overall. The data show variability and the need for further research, but do not support a blanket conclusion that mAbs will be ineffective in higher models.

298.Option: The ability of monoclonal antibodies to improve survival in guinea pigs is primarily due to their neutralizing capacity, as demonstrated by the complete protection seen with the combination of neutralizing MAbs 1H3, 2G4, and 4G7.
Evidence: The PRNT50 demonstrated that MAbs 1H3, 2G4, and 4G7 were neutralizing with a PRNT50 at a 1/200, 1/800, and 1/6,400 dilution, respectively (Figure 1).
In contrast all animals receiving the neutralizing MAb combination at 2 dpi. survived.
Overall, there are a variety of mechanisms employed by MAbs to improve survival, and the ability of the MAb to neutralize the virus is not essential.
298.Rationale: While the neutralizing combination produced complete protection at 2 dpi, the passage explicitly states that multiple mechanisms can underlie MAb-mediated protection and that neutralization is not essential. Therefore it is not supported to conclude that improved survival is 'primarily due to' neutralizing capacity; the data show neutralizing antibodies can be effective but do not exclude other mechanisms as the primary reason in general.

298.Option: A combination of neutralizing monoclonal antibodies targeting different regions of the Ebola virus glycoprotein provides complete protection in guinea pigs when administered up to 2 days after infection, representing a clinically feasible treatment window.
Evidence: The PRNT50 demonstrated that MAbs 1H3, 2G4, and 4G7 were neutralizing with a PRNT50 at a 1/200, 1/800, and 1/6,400 dilution, respectively (Figure 1).
An initial characterization demonstrated they bound to a variety of GP segments, and that all 8 MAbs were able to pull down ZEBOV GP1,2 in an immunoprecipitation assay.
The combination of neutralizing MAbs (1.5 mg 1H3+1.5 mg 2G4+2 mg 4G7) was given to guinea pigs either 1 day before, or 1, 2 or 3 days after a 1000 LD50 GA-ZEBOV infection, and survival followed.
In contrast all animals receiving the neutralizing MAb combination at 2 dpi. survived.
298.Rationale: The factual elements are supported: the three named MAbs are neutralizing, they bind GP segments, and the neutralizing combination administered at 2 dpi produced complete protection in guinea pigs. However, the statement adds that they target 'different regions' (not explicitly demonstrated for that specific trio) and that a 2-day window is 'clinically feasible.' The passage provides no data on clinical feasibility in humans, and does not explicitly demonstrate that the three neutralizing MAbs target non-overlapping/different regions. Because those additional claims are not supported solely by the passage, the option as written cannot be fully derived from the provided evidence.

298.Option: The most effective monoclonal antibody treatment protocol involves administering a single dose of neutralizing MAbs 1 day before infection, as this timing maximizes survival in both mice and guinea pigs.
Evidence: In general, the MAbs are more effective when given 1 day after a lethal MA-ZEBOV infection.

Using the most effective MAb dose of 100 µg, the treatment time was extended... A single dose of 100 µg for each MAb was injected either 1 or 4 days before a lethal MA-ZEBOV infection, or at 1, 2, or 3 dpi, and survival followed.
Receiving the combination either one day before or after resulted in a survival rate of 50%, with the meantime to death of 11.17±3.09 and 7.92±0.42, respectively.
In contrast all animals receiving the neutralizing MAb combination at 2 dpi. survived.
298.Rationale: The passage indicates that post-exposure treatment (especially 1–2 days after infection) was generally more effective than pre-exposure treatment in mice, and that in guinea pigs the neutralizing combination yielded 100% survival when given at 2 dpi but only 50% survival when given 1 day before infection. Thus the claim that a single dose 1 day before infection maximizes survival in both models is not supported by the experimental data.

299.Option: The urine-based ELISA and dipstick tests show significant clearance of anti-Leishmania antibodies in 90% of treated VL patients within six months, suggesting their potential as a test of cure.
Evidence: Out of 18 urine samples, 16 showed a significant decrease (p<0.0001) in the urine IgG titre only after six months (day > 180) since the treatment started (Fig 5).

Out of 18 follow-up samples, 16 were entirely negative on and after six months of the treatment initiation (day >180). Two samples remained positive after 6 months of treatment till the data reported (S1 Table).
Our results confirmed the potential of urine samples in ELISA and dipstick test for the noninvasive diagnosis of VL and PKDL and as a test of clinical cure for human VL.
299.Rationale: The passage provides longitudinal data on 18 treated VL patients showing that 16/18 (~89%, i.e. approximately 90%) had a significant decline in urine IgG titres by >180 days and that 16/18 were entirely negative by dipstick at six months. The authors explicitly state these results confirm the potential of urine-based assays as a test of clinical cure. Therefore the inference that urine-based ELISA and dipstick show significant clearance in about 90% of treated patients within six months and have potential as a test of cure is directly supported by the presented data.

299.Option: Urine-based ELISA and dipstick tests are superior to serum-based rK39-RDTs for diagnosing VL in all endemic regions, including East Africa, due to their higher sensitivity and specificity.
Evidence: their diagnostic performance varies from moderate in Latin America to even low in East African region
The test showed positivity in 95 of 97 VL cases, yielding a sensitivity of 97.94% for detection of Leishmania exposure.
Overall specificity was calculated using 75 non-VL urine samples of controls and found 100% specificity without any false positives.

Results of the dipstick test showed positivity in all VL urine samples, acquiring a sensitivity of 100% (97/97). None of the urine samples taken from negative controls showed reactivity with the dipstick assay, resulting in 100% specificity (75/75) of the test.
In comparison to urine dipstick test, serum-based rK39-RDT showed 95.87% sensitivity (93/97) and 97.33% specificity (73/75).
299.Rationale: While the study data from the Indian sites show higher sensitivity and specificity for urine ELISA/dipstick than the serum rK39-RDT in this population, the passage explicitly notes geographic variability in rK39 performance ('...low in East African region'). The study provides no data from other endemic regions such as East Africa or Latin America. Therefore the blanket claim that urine tests are superior 'in all endemic regions, including East Africa' is not supported by the presented evidence and cannot be derived from this passage.

299.Option: The dipstick test's high specificity and sensitivity in detecting VL and PKDL make it a reliable tool for diagnosing relapse in patients who remain positive after six months of treatment.
Evidence: Results of the dipstick test showed positivity in all VL urine samples, acquiring a sensitivity of 100% (97/97). None of the urine samples taken from negative controls showed reactivity with the dipstick assay, resulting in 100% specificity (75/75) of the test.
All 14 PKDL urine samples tested had positive results in dipstick assay thus showing 100% sensitivity of the test.

Out of 18 follow-up samples, 16 were entirely negative on and after six months of the treatment initiation (day >180). Two samples remained positive after 6 months of treatment till the data reported (S1 Table).
299.Rationale: The passage demonstrates excellent diagnostic performance of the dipstick for initial diagnosis and that two patients remained dipstick-positive at six months. However, diagnosing relapse requires evidence that persistent positive dipstick results at six months correspond to clinical relapse or parasitological failure; the passage does not provide such outcome or parasitological follow-up for those persistently positive patients. Therefore one cannot conclude from the provided data that the dipstick is a reliable tool to diagnose relapse in patients who remain positive after six months.

299.Option: The delayed decline in urine antibody titres after treatment suggests that the immune response to Leishmania is slower to resolve in urine than in serum, indicating a longer duration of immune activity in the urinary system.
Evidence: Leishmania-specific antibodies remain within the infected blood for years after treatment. Thus serum-based antibody detection tests cannot serve as a test of cure [12].

There is no significant decline in the titre just after completing the treatment (day 30). Out of 18 urine samples, 16 showed a significant decrease (p<0.0001) in the urine IgG titre only after six months (day > 180) since the treatment started (Fig 5).
299.Rationale: The passage states that serum antibodies can remain for years after treatment, making serum tests unsuitable as tests of cure, while urine antibody titres declined significantly in most patients by six months. The option asserts the opposite (that urine antibody decline is slower than serum), which contradicts the presented evidence. The data do not support the claim that immune resolution is slower in urine than in serum.

299.Option: The persistence of anti-Leishmania antibodies in serum for several years after treatment makes serum-based tests unsuitable for monitoring treatment response, but urine-based tests overcome this limitation by detecting antibodies only during active infection.
Evidence: Leishmania-specific antibodies remain within the infected blood for years after treatment. Thus serum-based antibody detection tests cannot serve as a test of cure [12].
Out of 18 urine samples, 16 showed a significant decrease (p<0.0001) in the urine IgG titre only after six months (day > 180) since the treatment started (Fig 5).

Out of 18 follow-up samples, 16 were entirely negative on and after six months of the treatment initiation (day >180). Two samples remained positive after 6 months of treatment till the data reported (S1 Table).
Our results confirmed the potential of urine samples in ELISA and dipstick test for the noninvasive diagnosis of VL and PKDL and as a test of clinical cure for human VL.
299.Rationale: The first clause—that serum antibodies persist for years and thus serum tests are unsuitable as tests of cure—is directly stated in the passage. The passage also provides evidence that urine antibody titres decline in most patients by six months and that dipstick/ELISA may serve as tests of clinical cure. However, the option's absolute claim that urine-based tests 'detect antibodies only during active infection' is not supported: two patients remained urine-positive at six months, and the study does not claim that urine antibodies are present exclusively during active infection in all cases. Therefore the categorical second half of the option ('only during active infection') overstates the evidence and cannot be derived from the passage.

300.Option: The fact that 72% of dog owners adopted offspring of free-roaming dogs indicates that the rural population is generally accepting of stray dogs, suggesting that community-based dog adoption programs could be implemented without significant resistance.
Evidence: Dog owners who adopted their pets “off the street” were less likely to get their dogs vaccinated than those who either purchased them or were given them (OR 0.08, 95%CI 0.01–0.4, p = 0.001).

Participants from the high/middle socio-economic level considered FRD were useful (OR 3.09, 95%CI 1.06–8.97, p = 0.03), were likely to feed them (OR2.81, 95%CI 1.34–5.88, p = 0.005), and would take an injured stray dog to a veterinarian (OR 2.33 95%CI 1.0–5.48, p = 0.04).

The younger respondents (≤34 years) did not consider FRD a threat to human health (OR 0.2, 95% CI 0.04–0.97, p = 0.05), and were more likely to feed them (OR 2.2, 95%CI 1.1–4.5, p = 0.04) than older participants.
300.Rationale: The passage does not state "72%" or any explicit overall adoption proportion; that specific figure is not present and therefore cannot be assumed. Moreover, the data show heterogeneity in attitudes and behaviors (e.g., adopters off the street were less likely to vaccinate their dogs, and positive attitudes are concentrated in certain socio-economic and age groups). These findings do not support the broad conclusion that the rural population is generally accepting of stray dogs to the extent that community-based adoption programs would encounter no significant resistance. Thus the option overstates and extrapolates beyond the provided data.

300.Option: Respondents from the high/middle socio-economic level had more positive attitudes towards free-roaming dogs, including being more likely to feed them and take injured dogs to a veterinarian, which could be leveraged to promote responsible dog ownership and population management.
Evidence: Participants from the high/middle socio-economic level considered FRD were useful (OR 3.09, 95%CI 1.06–8.97, p = 0.03), were likely to feed them (OR2.81, 95%CI 1.34–5.88, p = 0.005), and would take an injured stray dog to a veterinarian (OR 2.33 95%CI 1.0–5.48, p = 0.04).
300.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports that respondents in the high/middle socio-economic group were more likely to view FRD positively, feed them, and take injured strays to a veterinarian. From an epidemiological and programmatic perspective, these observed positive attitudes constitute a strategic opportunity that could reasonably be leveraged in interventions promoting responsible ownership and FRD population management (e.g., engaging this demographic as community advocates or partners). This inference follows directly from the supplied data.

300.Option: Because younger respondents were less likely to view free-roaming dogs as a health threat, public health campaigns should prioritize youth education to reduce fear and promote coexistence with stray dogs.
Evidence: The younger respondents (≤34 years) did not consider FRD a threat to human health (OR 0.2, 95% CI 0.04–0.97, p = 0.05), and were more likely to feed them (OR 2.2, 95%CI 1.1–4.5, p = 0.04) than older participants.
300.Rationale: The passage shows younger respondents are already less likely to view FRD as a threat and are more likely to feed them. The option recommends prioritizing youth education to reduce fear and promote coexistence, but that contradicts the observed pattern (youth already less fearful). The passage does not provide evidence that youth require campaigns to reduce fear; if anything, they may already be more accepting. Therefore the specific recommended priority (youth education to reduce fear) is not supported by the presented data.

300.Option: The lack of significant factors influencing favorable practices for rabies elimination suggests that community-wide interventions should focus on improving access to post-exposure prophylaxis rather than changing attitudes toward free-roaming dogs.
Evidence: Pet ownership (p = 0.11), dog ownership (p = 0.1), and poultry ownership (p = 0.19) were offered to the multivariable logistic regression model. A stable multivariate logistic regression model with significant p-values could not be generated.
insufficient vaccination coverage of dogs, poor knowledge of post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP) amongst medical professionals and unreliable supply of ARV and RIG [12].
300.Rationale: While the passage reports that a stable multivariable model with significant predictors for attitudes/practices could not be generated, and it also documents supply and knowledge problems related to PEP, the leap to conclude interventions should prioritize improving PEP access rather than changing attitudes toward FRD is not directly derivable. The data do not demonstrate that attitude-change interventions would be ineffective, nor do they compare the relative impact of supply-side versus attitude-focused interventions. Thus the prescriptive prioritization in this option is not justified solely by the passage evidence.

300.Option: Since poultry owners were more likely to perceive free-roaming dogs as a threat due to attacks on backyard poultry, targeted rabies awareness campaigns should focus on livestock-owning households to improve preventive practices.
Evidence: A significant number of poultry owners reported that FRD attacked their backyard poultry for food (OR 3.2, 95%CI 1.07–12.1, p = 0.02).
households that owned poultry were found less likely to report the presence of a rabid dog to the authorities (OR 0.3, 95%CI 0.13–0.7, p = 0.01).
300.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that poultry owners experience direct negative impacts from FRD (attacks on poultry) and have distinct reporting behavior (less likely to report a rabid dog). These observed differences identify livestock-owning households as a specific subgroup with both heightened exposure to FRD-related problems and gaps in preventive/reporting behaviors. It is therefore a reasonable epidemiological inference that targeted awareness campaigns directed at livestock-owning households could be a strategic way to improve preventive practices and reporting in this at-risk subgroup.

301.Option: The detection of Rickettsia felis DNA in the cerebrospinal fluid of two fatal cases of acute neurological disease, combined with the absence of other identifiable pathogens and the presence of rickettsial-like CSF findings, suggests that R. felis may be a causative agent of severe neurological disease in humans.
Evidence: In this report we describe the identification of R. felis from the cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) of two fatal cases of acute neurological disease in North Sulawesi, Indonesia.
Two of 38 CSF samples (patients E-059 and E-063) were, however, positive for Rickettsia DNA as determined by genus-specific 23S rRNA qPCR.
Results were consistent with only Rickettsia felis (Table 1).
Amplicons were confirmed to be R. felis by Sanger sequencing (Figs 1–3).
All CSF and serum samples were negative for Leptospira and O. tsutsugamushi by qPCR.
Blood cultures were negative for both cases.
Retroactive testing for virus was negative for both [12].
Case 1 ... he was found to have CSF mononuclear pleocytosis.
301.Rationale: The passage directly reports recovery of R. felis DNA from the CSF of two fatal acute neurological cases, with species confirmation by sequencing. Multiple other pathogen tests on these cases were negative (Leptospira, O. tsutsugamushi, blood cultures, retroactive viral testing), and there was CSF evidence of CNS inflammation (mononuclear pleocytosis). Taken together, these findings support the inference that R. felis may have been the causative agent of the severe neurological disease in these two patients. The wording in the option is appropriately cautious ('may be'), matching the level of evidence presented (detection in CSF and exclusion of other tested pathogens).

301.Option: The seasonal co-circulation of Rickettsia felis with other rickettsial pathogens in Southeast Asia may compound healthcare system burden, particularly in pediatric intensive care units.
Evidence: It is naturally passaged generationally in fleas and has been found in a variety of other arthropods, including mosquitoes, but the exact mechanisms of transmission, including the possible role of vertebrate reservoirs, are still unclear [4].
Since the description of the first human case in 1994 [5] R. felis has been increasingly identified worldwide as a cause of disease [6–8].
Typically, it presents as a mild, febrile illness that can be syndromically confused with many other undifferentiated fevers, especially in Africa and Asia, where diagnostic resources are limited.
301.Rationale: The passage documents that R. felis is arthropod-associated and increasingly identified worldwide and that it can be confused with other febrile illnesses, but it does not provide any data on seasonality, co-circulation with other rickettsial pathogens, healthcare system burden, or impacts on pediatric intensive care units. The option extends beyond the reported findings and introduces claims (seasonal co-circulation and healthcare burden, specifically in pediatric ICUs) that are not supported by the passage.

301.Option: The 100% sequence identity of the 17-kDa and ompB genes between the Indonesian cases and the R. felis reference strain URRWXCal2 confirms that R. felis is a zoonotic pathogen with a stable transmission cycle in human populations.
Evidence: The 17-kDa and ompB sequences were 100% identical to the R. felis reference strain URRWXCal2 [3], and gltA was 99.91% homologous.

It is naturally passaged generationally in fleas and has been found in a variety of other arthropods, including mosquitoes, but the exact mechanisms of transmission, including the possible role of vertebrate reservoirs, are still unclear [4].
301.Rationale: While the genetic identity data strongly support that the organisms detected are R. felis (species identification), sequence identity alone does not 'confirm' zoonotic status or demonstrate a stable transmission cycle in human populations. The passage explicitly states that transmission mechanisms and the possible role of vertebrate reservoirs are still unclear. Thus, concluding a stable zoonotic transmission cycle in humans is not supported by the presented data.

301.Option: The absence of skin rash, eschar, or regional lymphadenopathy in both cases suggests that Rickettsia felis infection typically presents without classical rickettsial signs.
Evidence: Neither patient was noted on the charts to have skin rash, eschar, or regional lymphadenopathy.
Typically, it presents as a mild, febrile illness that can be syndromically confused with many other undifferentiated fevers, especially in Africa and Asia, where diagnostic resources are limited.
301.Rationale: The passage notes the absence of classical rickettsial signs in these two cases, but these are only two fatal adult cases and do not provide sufficient evidence to conclude what R. felis 'typically' presents like in general. Generalizing typical clinical presentation from two cases is not epidemiologically justified. The passage itself indicates variable presentation and diagnostic confusion in other settings, so the claim of typical absence of classical signs is not supported.

301.Option: The failure of standard TB therapy in both cases, combined with negative acid-fast staining, provides definitive evidence that Rickettsia felis is the primary cause of death.
Evidence: TB meningitis was initially suspected for both cases but neither CSF was positive by acid-fast bacilli stain and neither responded to presumptive TB treatment.
Two of 38 CSF samples (patients E-059 and E-063) were, however, positive for Rickettsia DNA as determined by genus-specific 23S rRNA qPCR.
Results were consistent with only Rickettsia felis (Table 1).
301.Rationale: Although the failure to respond to TB therapy and negative AFB stains reduce the likelihood that TB meningitis was the cause, these observations do not constitute 'definitive evidence' that R. felis was the primary cause of death. Definitive causation would require more comprehensive evidence (e.g., demonstration of viable organism, fulfillment of case definitions, exclusion of all other causes, temporal and pathological correlation). The study shows R. felis DNA in CSF and exclusion of several tested pathogens, but that does not prove causality definitively.

302.Option: The high in-hospital case fatality rate among elderly patients with severe dengue in this study, despite high-quality ICU care, suggests that age-related physiological decline and comorbidities are major contributors to poor outcomes.
Evidence: The in-hospital CFR was 41.3% (31 patients), and these patients had a median ICU stay of 4 (IQR: 1–8) days.
The mean age of the 75 patients was 72.3±9.3 years (ranging from 50.7 to 88.5 years, Table 1).
The most common comorbidities included hypertension (72.0%), diabetes (43.7%), and chronic kidney disease (22.7%).
The rates of underlying diseases and conditions necessitating blood component transfusions were similar between the survivors and non-survivors.
In particular, diabetes mellitus, hypertension, and aging are associated with a higher risk of DHF/DSS and a worse outcome [6, 15–18].
302.Rationale: The passage documents a high CFR and that patients were elderly with many comorbidities, and earlier literature is cited linking aging and comorbidities to worse dengue outcomes. However, the study data do not support the specific claim that comorbidities were major contributors to mortality in this cohort: the authors state that "The rates of underlying diseases ... were similar between the survivors and non-survivors." The passage also does not state anything about the quality of ICU care (the phrase "despite high-quality ICU care" is not supported). Therefore the conclusion that age-related decline and comorbidities are the major contributors cannot be directly derived from the presented data.

302.Option: The high in-hospital case fatality rate in elderly patients with severe dengue is due to the high prevalence of bloodstream infections, particularly late-onset candidemia, which contributed significantly to mortality.
Evidence: Nineteen bacterial and three fungal pathogens were identified in the bloodstream of 18 (23.7%) patients.
Notably, two patients experienced recurrent fever at the eighth and ninth day after the onset of DF, respectively, which was caused by candidemia.

The leading cause of fatality was multiple organ failure (14/31, 45.2%), ... Seven (22.6%) patients died of refractory shock and severe metabolic acidosis, six (19.4%) intracranial hemorrhage, three (9.6%) bloodstream infections, and one (3.2%) pneumonia with respiratory failure.
302.Rationale: Although bloodstream infections (including two late candidemia cases) occurred in 23.7% of patients and were responsible for three deaths, the passage shows that most deaths were due to multiple organ failure, shock, intracranial hemorrhage, etc. Only a minority of deaths (three of 31) were attributed to bloodstream infections. The multivariate predictors of death did not identify bloodstream infection as an independent predictor. Thus the statement that bloodstream infections (particularly candidemia) are the primary cause of the high CFR is not supported by the presented data.

302.Option: The high in-hospital case fatality rate among elderly patients with severe dengue in this study is primarily due to delayed access to medical care and inadequate fluid resuscitation, as evidenced by the high rate of cardiac arrest before ICU admission.
Evidence: Seven (9.3%) patients experienced cardiac arrest before ICU admission.

Five of them were admitted to the ICU immediately after the resuscitation. One experienced cardiac arrest during the endotracheal intubation at the second hour of emergency department (ED) arrival and was admitted to the ICU on the next day. The another one experienced cardiac arrest due to massive gastrointestinal bleeding and severe metabolic acidosis at the 23rd hour of ED arrival and was admitted to the ICU at the 45th hour of ED arrival.
In the multivariate analysis, cardiac arrest before ICU admission (hazard ratio [HR] 6.26 [95% confidence interval: 1.91–20.54]) ... were independent predictive factors for in-hospital fatality
302.Rationale: The passage does show that cardiac arrest before ICU admission occurred in some patients and was a strong independent predictor of death. However, there is no direct evidence presented that links cardiac arrest to delayed access to care or inadequate fluid resuscitation as the primary causal mechanism across the cohort. A few described timelines show individual variability, but the authors do not attribute the high CFR primarily to delayed access or inadequate fluid management. Therefore this causal inference cannot be reliably derived from the passage.

302.Option: The high in-hospital case fatality rate in this cohort is attributable to the limited availability of invasive life-supporting interventions such as hemodialysis and endotracheal intubation for elderly patients in Taiwan, which restricts their recovery potential.
Evidence: Approximately three-quarters (57, 76.0%) of all patients required ventilator support during the ICU stay.
At the ICU admission, 41 (54.7%) patients had AKI, according to the KDIGO criteria and 12 (29.3%) of whom needed renal replacement therapy during the ICU stay.
Four (5.3%) patients had end-stage renal disease and received maintenance dialysis.
302.Rationale: The passage documents that invasive life-support interventions (ventilator support, renal replacement therapy) were in fact provided to many patients, so there is no evidence of limited availability of such interventions. The authors do not state that restricted access to intensive interventions caused the high CFR. Thus the claim that limited availability of invasive life support is responsible for the high mortality is not supported by the data presented.

302.Option: The high in-hospital case fatality rate in this elderly cohort is primarily driven by the high APACHE II and SOFA scores, which reflect the severity of organ failure and are the most significant predictors of mortality.
Evidence: The non-survivors had higher APACHE II and SOFA scores and a higher rate of organ failure, including acute respiratory failure and AKI.
In the univariate Cox model, ... a high APACHE II or SOFA score ... were significantly associated with in-hospital fatality.

In the multivariate analysis, cardiac arrest before ICU admission (hazard ratio [HR] 6.26 [95% confidence interval: 1.91–20.54]), AKI at ICU admission (HR 2.48 [1.07–5.74]), and initial APTT longer than 48 s (HR 3.91 [1.69–9.07]) were independent predictive factors for in-hospital fatality after adjusting for gender, cardiac arrest before ICU admission, respiratory failure at ICU admission, presence of AKI at ICU admission and initial APTT longer than 48 s (Table 3).

APACHE II and SOFA scores include many clinical parameters and were not included in the final model of multivariate Cox analysis as they demonstrated collinearity with other variables. They were positively associated with acute respiratory failure, AKI, and cardiac arrest.
The area under the ROC curve (AUC) of APTT was 0.76 (95% confidence interval [CI]: 0.64–0.88), APACHE II score 0.84 (0.75–0.93), and SOFA score 0.85 (0.77–0.94).
302.Rationale: APACHE II and SOFA scores were associated with mortality (higher in non-survivors), and their ROC AUCs were high, reflecting good predictive ability for fatality. However, the multivariate analysis identified cardiac arrest before ICU admission, AKI at ICU admission, and initial APTT >48 s as independent predictors; APACHE II and SOFA were excluded from the final model due to collinearity. Thus, while APACHE II and SOFA reflect severity and are associated with mortality, the passage does not support the stronger claim that they are the primary independent drivers or the "most significant predictors" once other variables are accounted for.

303.Option: The availability of miltefosine in East Africa for visceral leishmaniasis treatment creates a strong foundation for immediate implementation of miltefosine as a first-line therapy for L aethiopica without further clinical evaluation.
Evidence: Miltefosine has been successfully used in more than 50 CL cases due to L aethiopica in Addis Ababa, but the findings have not been published (personal communication, Asrat Hailu).
The available in vitro data relating to these drugs suggest a good susceptibility of L aethiopica to miltefosine, paromomycin, pentamidine and amphotericin B (Table 5).
In terms of treatment of L aethiopica, the evidence base remains extremely limited.
randomized clinical trials are highly needed and should be undertaken.
303.Rationale: Although the passage notes some unpublished clinical use of miltefosine and favorable in vitro susceptibility, it also clearly states that the evidence base is extremely limited and randomized clinical trials are needed. There is no support for immediate implementation as first-line therapy without further clinical evaluation; the passage explicitly indicates that stronger clinical evidence is required.

303.Option: The national and WHO guidelines recommend intramuscular paromomycin as the preferential first-line treatment for LCL caused by L aethiopica, supported by strong clinical evidence from well-designed trials.
Evidence: Paromomycin ointment was highly effective in three Ethiopians treated in Israel.
