315.Rationale: The Passage Body shows that neutrophils from S100A8/A9-/- mice are more activated and that this state is associated with increased apoptosis; it also shows these neutrophils inhibit L3 motility in a DNA-dependent manner and that neutrophil depletion reverses the protective effect in knockouts. However, the specific causal ordering claimed in the option — that increased neutrophil activation is a consequence of enhanced apoptosis — is not demonstrated. The text describes association ('associated with an increased apoptosis') but does not show apoptosis causes activation. The remainder of the option (that neutrophil activity contributes to clearance) is supported, but the incorrect causal direction in the first clause makes the option not derivable directly from the Passage Body.

315.Option: S100A8/A9 inhibits L3-induced inflammation by decreasing chemokine production and neutrophil activation, thereby facilitating larval migration during L. sigmodontis infection.
Evidence: Our data indicates an anti-inflammatory role of S100A8/S100A9 on neutrophil activation, which reduced inflammatory responses and facilitated the migration of infective L3 larvae into the thoracic cavity.
Subcutaneous L. sigmodontis infection in S100A8/A9-/- mice resulted in a significantly reduced worm burden compared to WT infected animals (Fig 1B).
Concentrations of CXCL-1 (Fig 1E), CXCL-2 (Fig 1F), CXCL-5 (Fig 1G), and elastase (Fig 1H) were significantly increased in the thoracic cavity lavage of S100A8/A9-/- mice compared to WT controls.

Myeloperoxidase (MPO) as well as elastase, enzymes which are abundantly expressed in neutrophils, were increasingly released (MPO: p<0.05; elastase: p = 0.11) from bone marrow-derived neutrophils of S100A8/A9-/- mice compared to WT neutrophils at baseline conditions in vitro (Fig 4A and 4B).
315.Rationale: The Passage Body explicitly states that S100A8/A9 has an anti-inflammatory role on neutrophil activation, reducing inflammatory responses and facilitating L3 migration. Empirical findings support this: S100A8/A9-/- mice show reduced worm burden (less migration), together with increased neutrophil-associated chemokines (CXCL1/2/5) and neutrophil enzyme release and activation markers in knockouts relative to WT. Those observations are consistent with S100A8/A9 inhibiting L3-induced inflammation (decreasing chemokine production and neutrophil activation) in WT mice, thereby allowing larvae to migrate more successfully into the thoracic cavity.

316.Option: Despite the delay in disease progression and the reduction in virus load in blood and organs as evidenced by virus titration and IHC, ribavirin was not able to prevent the lethal pathophysiological cascade.
Evidence: Although one animal survived after treatment, ribavirin did not significantly increase the survival rate (p = 0.4).

However, the drug prolonged the time to death (median 3 vs. 6 days for placebo vs. ribavirin, p = 0.0007), reduced the levels of AST (p = 0.001) and ALT (p = 0.006) at day 2, reduced the virus titer in blood at day 2 (p = 0.0007), increased the weight at day 2 and 3 (p = 0.03 and p = 0.002, respectively), and reduced the terminal virus concentration in all organs when compared to placebo at day 3 (p<0.001 separately for each organ).
Histopathological analysis of organs collected at day 3 from ribavirin-treated mice revealed only small disseminated foci of necrosis; most of the liver parenchyma resembled naïve mice.
Virus antigen-positive cells (NP) were significantly reduced in liver and spleen compared to untreated or placebo-treated mice (data not shown).
However, ribavirin-treated mice that succumbed to infection on days 4–9 showed extensive bridging hepatocellular necrosis at the time of death (Fig. 2).

Both alterations are suggestive for strong monocyte/macrophage activation. However, in contrast to untreated mice, virus antigen was hardly detectable in liver tissue of the treated mice (Fig. 2), consistent with the low virus titer in all organs (Fig. 5, bottom).
Thus, ribavirin reduces CCHFV load and delays disease progression, but it does not prevent terminal liver necrosis, monocyte/macrophage activation, and lethal outcome in the IFNAR−/− mouse model.
316.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports that ribavirin reduced viremia and organ virus concentrations (virus titers and reduced NP antigen by IHC), and it delayed time to death and ameliorated some clinical chemistry measures. Nonetheless, treated mice still experienced extensive terminal hepatocellular necrosis, evidence of monocyte/macrophage activation, and ultimately death, and overall survival was not significantly improved. The provided sentences directly support the inference that, despite antiviral effects, ribavirin failed to avert the fatal pathophysiological cascade in this model.

318.Option: The 24-bp insertion in the chitotriosidase gene, which results in impaired enzyme activity, is associated with an increased risk for the development of Madurella mycetomatis mycetoma.
Evidence: In the gene encoding for chitotriosidase, a 24-bp insertion in exon 10 was described previously [12, 14, 19, 20].
It was described elsewhere that chitotriosidase activity is reduced when patients are heterozygous for this insertion and completely absent when they were homozygous for this allele [12, 14].
The insertion containing allele was found significantly more frequently in the patients than in the control group (p = 0.004).
Based on these data, the 24-bp insertion in the chitotriosidase gene does increase the risk for invasive mycetoma disease caused by M. mycetomatis (odds ratio 2.9; 95% CI 1.4–6.1).
318.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the 24-bp insertion reduces or abolishes chitotriosidase activity and that the insertion allele is significantly more frequent among patients than controls with an OR of 2.9 (p = 0.004). Therefore the insertion (which impairs enzyme activity) is associated with increased risk of M. mycetomatis mycetoma; this conclusion follows directly from the reported data.

318.Option: The high prevalence of M. mycetomatis in Sudanese soil and the presence of antibodies in the population indicate that the 24-bp insertion in chitotriosidase is a primary determinant of disease susceptibility in this population.
Evidence: In endemic areas in Sudan, M. mycetomatis DNA was found in the soil [3] and antibodies against mycetoma causative agents have been detected in the majority of the inhabitants in these areas [4].
However, it is not clear why only a minority of the exposed humans develop overt clinical infection.
Several environmental and patient-related factors have been described to influence the risk for the development of mycetoma, including concurrent schistosomiasis [5].
Based on these data, the 24-bp insertion in the chitotriosidase gene does increase the risk for invasive mycetoma disease caused by M. mycetomatis (odds ratio 2.9; 95% CI 1.4–6.1).
318.Rationale: While the passage documents high environmental exposure (soil DNA) and widespread antibody prevalence, and it shows the 24-bp insertion increases risk, it does not support the stronger claim that this insertion is the primary determinant of susceptibility. The authors explicitly note that only a minority develop clinical disease and that multiple environmental and patient-related factors influence risk. Thus the passage does not justify labeling the insertion as the primary determinant.

318.Option: The lack of association between AMCase polymorphisms and mycetoma suggests that AMCase is not involved in the host immune response to M. mycetomatis in endemic populations.
Evidence: We also found that both chitotriosidase and AMCase were found in the tissue surrounding the fungal grain, however both chitinases concentrated in the grain.

Allele frequencies of A50G and A290G in the patients were similar to those in the controls (p = 0.65; odds ratio 1.2; 95% CI 0.6–2.1; and p = 0.72; odds ratio 1.1; 95% CI 0.7–1.8, respectively).
318.Rationale: The passage reports no association between the studied AMCase polymorphisms and disease, but it also provides direct immunohistochemical evidence that AMCase is present and concentrated in and around the fungal grain and that AMCase antibodies bind to hyphae. Therefore absence of association for these polymorphisms does not support the categorical conclusion that AMCase is not involved in the host immune response; the passage contains evidence consistent with involvement.

318.Option: The 24-bp insertion in the chitotriosidase gene is associated with increased chitinase activity, which may contribute to a stronger immune response against M. mycetomatis in exposed individuals.
Evidence: It was described elsewhere that chitotriosidase activity is reduced when patients are heterozygous for this insertion and completely absent when they were homozygous for this allele [12, 14].
The insertion containing allele was found significantly more frequently in the patients than in the control group (p = 0.004).
Based on these data, the 24-bp insertion in the chitotriosidase gene does increase the risk for invasive mycetoma disease caused by M. mycetomatis (odds ratio 2.9; 95% CI 1.4–6.1).

319.Option: The high rate of positive environmental swabs on household furniture and children’s hands indicates that flies are the dominant vector for trachoma transmission in hyperendemic communities.
Evidence: Samples of the household environment showed that C. trachomatis DNA was present on household objects, children, and adults (Fig 1).

319.Rationale: The passage documents environmental contamination (furniture, hands) and notes that fly reduction is one promoted intervention, but it does not present any data demonstrating that flies are the dominant vector. Therefore the specific causal attribution that flies are the dominant vector is not supported by the study data and cannot be derived from the passage.

319.Option: In high prevalence villages, C. trachomatis DNA was detected on household objects, children's hands, and clothing, suggesting that environmental contamination may contribute to transmission, particularly in settings where hygiene practices such as frequent clothing washing are less common.

other possible routes of transmission of infected secretions, such as on hands of caretakers or clothing and bedding, may be present and could serve as targets for interventions.
washing clothes <3 days prior to the study visit was protective against obtaining a positive environmental swab (OR 0.17; 95% CI 0.03–0.88).

319.Option: The lack of positive environmental swabs in two villages with low trachoma prevalence indicates that environmental contamination is not a significant factor in disease transmission.
Evidence: The 23 positive environmental swabs were obtained from 13 households (Table 3). Twenty-one of the positive swabs, collected from 11 households, were from the same village, Njoge. Obtaining at least one positive environmental swab from the household was associated with village of residence (ranging from 0% in Mkutani and Banyi Banyi to 34% in Njoge, p = 0.011) and presence of ocular infection in the index child (percent positive swabs 34% vs. 3%, p = 0.001)

The absence of positives in some villages does not prove that environmental contamination is not an important transmission factor overall; variation could reflect local prevalence, sampling, or other factors. The passage does not provide evidence to support the absolute conclusion that environmental contamination is not significant.

Evidence: Maintaining facial hygiene and environmental improvements, largely focused on reduction of flies through latrine construction, have been promoted to reduce access to infected secretions [9–15].
Secretions were rarely wiped using a separate cloth (5%) or washed with water (2.5%).
washing clothes <3 days prior to the study visit was protective against obtaining a positive environmental swab (OR 0.17; 95% CI 0.03–0.88).

The study measured clothing/bedding washing and found an association for clothes washing, but it did not present evidence that face washing alone (which was rare in the sample) is sufficient. Therefore this strong causal claim is not supported by the presented data.

319.Option: The presence of C. trachomatis DNA in the household environment of children with active trachoma suggests that environmental sources are the primary route of transmission, especially in hyperendemic areas.
Evidence: Samples of the household environment showed that C. trachomatis DNA was present on household objects, children, and adults (Fig 1).

When we included presence of ocular infection in the index child in the model (Table 5, model A), only that variable was statistically significantly associated with obtaining at least one positive environmental swab from a household (OR 22.0; 95% CI 2.31–209).
319.Rationale: The study demonstrates environmental contamination and its association with an infected index child, and it discusses multiple possible transmission routes (household contacts, hands, clothing, flies). However, the data do not establish that environmental sources are the primary route of transmission. The strong association with the index child’s infection implies that infected persons contaminate the environment, but causation and primacy of environmental transmission over direct person-to-person spread or vectors are not proven by the presented data.

320.Option: The model output suggests that upstream sampling locations are ideal for environmental surveillance because they are less affected by environmental flow and pathogen decay.
Evidence: dilution and pathogen decay over time appear to play an important role in the downstream concentrations and probability of detection at 09:00 (Fig 4).

The highest probabilities of detection are concentrated in upstream branches that do not have ‘parent’ branches, and the lowest probabilities of detection are concentrated in downstream branches with large catchment populations, where larger volumes of wastewater flow and environmental flow converge (Fig 4).
Three of these branches (850, 912, and 937) capture smaller cumulative populations, but have much higher estimated detection probabilities.

In these low prevalence scenarios, large environmental surveillance wastewater channels may be unlikely to yield positive detections and sampling further upstream, at shared toilets or the building level, may be warranted, as suggested in Wang et al., 2020 [26].

Together, the five sampling branches in Table 3 would capture the entire catchment population included in the theoretical study area, but the detection probability is reduced at downstream branches. In this scenario, a more informative ES plan may include more upstream sites that capture as much of the upstream population as possible, while also maintaining a high probability of detection, rather than relying on a downstream convergence branch like 573 to provide a representative sample for the entire catchment population.
320.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports that dilution and decay reduce downstream concentrations and detection probabilities and that highest detection probabilities are concentrated in upstream branches. It also recommends prioritizing upstream sites rather than relying on downstream convergence branches. These statements support the inference that upstream sampling locations are preferable because they are less impacted by downstream dilution and decay.

320.Option: In communities with high typhoid prevalence, downstream sampling locations are more reliable for detecting transmission because they integrate signals from a larger catchment area.
Evidence: In high prevalence scenarios, the selection of sampling location is less important, as the detection probabilities are high throughout the system, particularly in the low flow and medium flow scenarios.

The highest probabilities of detection are concentrated in upstream branches that do not have ‘parent’ branches, and the lowest probabilities of detection are concentrated in downstream branches with large catchment populations, where larger volumes of wastewater flow and environmental flow converge (Fig 4).

Both branches have relatively low maximum probability of detection, likely due to dilution factors and decay rates as bacteria travel downstream and are continually diluted as wastewater flows converge.

However, it does not claim downstream locations are 'more reliable' than upstream; elsewhere the passage highlights that downstream convergence branches often have lower detection due to dilution and decay. Therefore the specific claim that downstream locations are more reliable (because they integrate a larger catchment) is not directly supported as a general inference and overstates what the passage provides.

both branches have relatively low maximum probability of detection, likely due to dilution factors and decay rates as bacteria travel downstream and are continually diluted as wastewater flows converge.

321.Option: In VL/HIV patients, neutrophil counts, as well as the ability of neutrophils to phagocytose bioparticles and express CD10, are not restored following treatment, suggesting that neutrophils play a key role in the poor prognosis of these patients.

These results demonstrate 4 different conjunctival cytokine response patterns to Ct as follows: no significant change, increase or decrease in correlation to bacterial load, or increase following treatment once the bacterial load was cleared.

The authors explicitly state that reductions in metabolic activity and Wolbachia content “reflect cessation of mf production.” These linked observations support the inference that the decline in Wolbachia in cultured females is attributable to loss of embryogenesis/reproduction—associated with absence of males—and indicate the culture system does not sustain reproductive biology (mf production).

Therefore the causal claim that the abundance increase is likely due to higher mean temperatures is not supported by the provided data.

none of the parameters estimated using the temperature-dependent responses from Mordecai et al. [22] differed significantly between the land-use types for either sampling year (Wilcox, Z = 0.43, p = 0.663; Fig 1).

biting rate was taken as the inverse of the median gonotrophic cycle length, and set as 0.12 for all sites.

326.Option: Pyriproxyfen exposure through autodissemination does not affect the survivorship of adult A. mellifera workers, indicating that the method poses no significant risk to pollinator populations.
Evidence: No difference in A. mellifera survivorship was observed in cage types 1, 3, and 4 (Log-rank, Chi-square = 0.59, DF = 2, P = 0.75).

Images of A. mellifera collected from cage type 3 show the indirect transfer of PPF from treated Ae. albopictus males and artificial nectar sources (Fig 4A, 4B, 4E and 4F).
The most abundant amounts of PPF were observed on PPF treated Ae. albopictus males (0.12 ± 0.05 ng/mL) and A. mellifera workers (0.49 ± 0.26 ng/mL) (Fig 6).

A significant lethal effect was observed in bioassays containing materials from an artificial nectar source, A. mellifera, and PPF treated Ae. albopictus males (ANOVA, F = 34.4, DF = 6, P < 0.0001) (Fig 5A).
326.Rationale: The passage does report no statistically significant difference in survivorship across certain cage types, but it also provides direct evidence that A. mellifera became contaminated with PPF (imaging and LC-MS) and that materials and insects from treatment cages produced significant lethal effects in bioassays. Thus the absence of a survivorship difference in these cage comparisons does not logically support the strong inference that autodissemination poses no significant risk to pollinator populations. On the contrary, contamination of bees and lethal bioassay results indicate potential risk. Therefore the option's conclusion is not supported by the totality of the presented evidence.

326.Option: The indirect transfer of pyriproxyfen to honey bees via artificial nectar sources is unlikely in natural settings because A. mellifera foraging behavior in laboratory cages does not reflect real-world foraging patterns.

Therefore one cannot infer from the provided experimental findings that indirect transfer is unlikely in natural settings; that claim requires external information or field data not present in the passage.

No experimental time-course demonstrating diminishing contamination or diminishing risk is provided. Thus the specific causal inference that persistence decreases over time because of those behaviors and that risk correspondingly diminishes cannot be derived from the study data presented.

Therefore the epidemiological/ecological inference that autodissemination with a persistent juvenile hormone analog like pyriproxyfen may pose significant risk to non-target pollinators via indirect contamination is directly supported by the study evidence.

326.Option: The primary route of pyriproxyfen transfer to non-target insects is direct contact during copulation between treated male and female mosquitoes, not through shared nectar sources.
Evidence: PPF transfer to Ae. albopictus females was also observed in cage type 2 and 3 as suggested by the presence of fluorescent powder on untreated females placed in the cages (Fig 4I and 4J).

Images of A. mellifera collected from cage type 3 show the indirect transfer of PPF from treated Ae. albopictus males and artificial nectar sources (Fig 4A, 4B, 4E and 4F).

A significant lethal effect was observed in bioassays containing materials from an artificial nectar source, A. mellifera, and PPF treated Ae. albopictus males (ANOVA, F = 34.4, DF = 6, P < 0.0001) (Fig 5A).

326.Rationale: The passage documents multiple transfer routes: male-to-female transfer (evidence of fluorescent powder on untreated females) and contamination of nectar sources with resulting indirect transfer to bees (bioassay lethality, imaging, and LC-MS detection). 327.Option: The presence of two melting peaks in the cow sample indicates that the HRM-Cyt b technique is prone to false positives due to nuclear mitochondrial DNA (Numts) contamination, limiting its reliability in field studies.
Evidence: Although some species exhibited the same Tm value, HRM profiles in these species were clearly discriminated and recognized as different genotypes, such as dog and sheep or pig and horse (Table 2, Figure S1).

Here we present a fast, inexpensive, accurate, and sensitive technique to identify blood-meal sources of triatomines based on Cyt b gene HRM analysis of host-specific genotypes and its application in field studies.
327.Rationale: The passage describes the cow two-peak result but explicitly states the genotype was still recognized with high confidence and that the two peaks were associated with different sample types (muscle vs blood). There is no mention of nuclear mitochondrial DNA (Numts) contamination causing false positives. Instead, the authors present HRM-Cyt b as accurate and sensitive. Therefore the specific claim that two peaks indicate Numts contamination and limit reliability is not supported by the Passage Body.

327.Option: The HRM-Cyt b technique is the only method capable of detecting mixed blood-meal sources in triatomine insects, as confirmed by the successful identification of both mouse and chicken DNA in a single sample.
Evidence: In this case, the melt curve showed two peaks belonging to mouse and chicken amplicons, with their respective Tm. In addition, the dissociation curve had a shape with two falls (Figure S2).
Therefore, we evaluated the possibility of detecting at least two different blood meal sources.

In triatomines, so far, the methods used for this purpose include serologic and PCR-based molecular techniques. ELISA-based serological techniques are the most frequently used in blood-meal determination of Chagas disease vectors [20]–[24].

Unlike serological tools, PCR-based molecular methods are more specific and usually easier to perform, although the design of specific primers for each species involved in transmission scenarios makes it difficult to identify the vector and mammals from the wild cycle where a wide variety of species exist.
327.Rationale: While the Passage Body documents that HRM-Cyt b successfully detected mixed mouse and chicken blood in a single sample, it also describes other methods (serologic and PCR-based) previously used for blood-meal determination. The passage does not claim HRM is the only method capable of detecting mixed feeds. Thus the absolute assertion of uniqueness is not supported.

Evidence: Here we present a fast, inexpensive, accurate, and sensitive technique to identify blood-meal sources of triatomines based on Cyt b gene HRM analysis of host-specific genotypes and its application in field studies.

In this case, the melt curve showed two peaks belonging to mouse and chicken amplicons, with their respective Tm. In addition, the dissociation curve had a shape with two falls (Figure S2). However, it also reports samples with Tm inconsistent with any standards, indicating unknown sources could be detected but not identified without reference standards. Therefore the claim that HRM can identify mixed sources even when standards are not available is not supported by the passage.

Evidence: Genotypes were defined by selecting a sample from each standard species as a reference control to identify unknown samples.

As mentioned above, four mixed samples, including one intradomiciliary insect, showed a peak with a Tm = 84.83±0.05, which was inconsistent with any of our standards (Table 4), indicating another additional blood-meal source that was not considered in this study.

The passage does not support the universal application without prior knowledge of local fauna.

Unlike serological tools, PCR-based molecular methods are more specific and usually easier to perform, although the design of specific primers for each species involved in transmission scenarios makes it difficult to identify the vector and mammals from the wild cycle where a wide variety of species exist.

A more powerful approach [29]–[31] is the heteroduplex assay of the mitochondrial cytochrome B (Cyt b) gene (reviewed in [19]). This technique can detect differences in the DNA sequences of this gene among species by differences on electrophoretic profiles [29].
327.Rationale: The Passage Body does support that Cyt b-HRM is highly sensitive and can detect blood-meal sources up to 30 days post-feeding. However, it does not provide evidence that HRM is superior to PCR-based methods for long-term surveillance. The passage discusses strengths and limitations of PCR-based approaches and other methods, but does not present a direct comparative evaluation proving HRM's superiority for long-term surveillance. 328.Option: The significant increase in eosinophil counts following ivermectin treatment is primarily due to the direct stimulatory effect of ivermectin on eosinophil production in the bone marrow.
Evidence: Median eosinophil counts rose from 171/μL to 1,095/μL (p<0.001).

Therefore the claim of primary causation by direct ivermectin stimulation of bone marrow cannot be derived from the passage.

While ivermectin induced inflammatory changes post-treatment, the passage does not provide evidence that the baseline polyclonal hypergammaglobulinemia is a direct consequence of ivermectin-induced immune activation; thus the stated causal inference cannot be drawn from the presented data.

328.Option: The increase in D-dimers observed after ivermectin treatment suggests both enhanced fibrinolysis and coagulation activation, which may be driven by intravascular dying microfilariae and inflammation-induced hypercoagulability, potentially contributing to the pathogenesis of post-ivermectin serious adverse events.
Evidence: Among hemostatic parameters, median D-dimers significantly increased, from 582 ng/mL to 745 ng/mL after IVM (p = 0.024) with a median relative variation of +34.7% (interquartile range: 1.44–72.5%).

328.Rationale: The passage shows a statistically significant rise in D-dimers after ivermectin alongside marked increases in inflammation markers (CRP, ESR, fibrinogen) and eosinophils. Primate data in the passage link degenerating intravascular microfilariae to fibrin deposition, endothelial damage and hemorrhage—pathways consistent with activation of coagulation and secondary fibrinolysis. The passage also notes that eosinophils can trigger coagulation via tissue factor and that thrombotic events have been reported in parasite-related hypereosinophilia and post-ivermectin SAEs. Together, these data support an inference that the post-treatment D-dimer rise reflects both increased coagulation (fibrin formation) and subsequent fibrinolysis, plausibly driven by intravascular dying microfilariae and inflammation, and that this process could contribute to post-ivermectin severe adverse events.

328.Option: The increase in D-dimers after ivermectin treatment is primarily due to the breakdown of fibrin clots formed during the immune response to dying microfilariae, with no contribution from coagulation activation.
Evidence: Among hemostatic parameters, median D-dimers significantly increased, from 582 ng/mL to 745 ng/mL after IVM (p = 0.024) with a median relative variation of +34.7% (interquartile range: 1.44–72.5%).

Eosinophils, known to express tissue factor, may trigger blood coagulation [5], leading to thrombosis.

Various inflammation markers changed significantly: CRP, ESR, fibrinogen and alpha-1-globulin levels increased (p = 0.038, p = 0.010, p = 0.002 and p = 0.044, respectively), while albumin level decreased p = 0.044).
Prothrombin fragment 1+2 did not significantly vary (252 versus 469 pmol/L, p = 0.313).

Although prothrombin fragment F1+2 did not significantly vary in the data presented, that single finding does not justify the strong claim that there is no contribution from coagulation activation. The passage therefore does not support the assertion that D-dimer rise is solely due to fibrinolysis without any coagulation activation.

Evidence: Indeed, IVM mass drug administration in onchocerciasis endemic regions may cause SAEs such as life-threatening encephalopathies in patients with high L. loa MFD above 30,000 mf/mL, due to its potent microfilaricidal effect [9].

Evidence from primate models suggests an association between post-IVM encephalopathy and hemostatic dysregulation, characterized by degenerating mfs in small vessels associated with fibrin deposition, endothelial damages, and hemorrhages [10].
Median eosinophil counts rose from 171/μL to 1,095/μL (p<0.001).

Various inflammation markers changed significantly: CRP, ESR, fibrinogen and alpha-1-globulin levels increased (p = 0.038, p = 0.010, p = 0.002 and p = 0.044, respectively), while albumin level decreased p = 0.044).

The passage contains no data or statements indicating that ivermectin has a direct pro-coagulant effect on endothelial cells independent of inflammation or microfilarial death. Therefore the claim that D-dimer rise is mainly due to a direct drug effect on endothelium, independent of other processes, cannot be derived from the passage.

329.Option: The Loopamp Leishmania Detection Kit has a shelf life of one year if stored between 1 and 30°C, and remains stable for 12 months at 30°C, making it suitable for use in high-temperature environments without cold chain requirements.

329.Rationale: The passage indicates the kit uses dried reagents that 'can be stored at room temperature,' but it provides no specific data on shelf life, exact temperature ranges (e.g. 1–30°C), or validated stability durations (e.g. 12 months at 30°C). The detailed storage claims in the option are not present in the passage and therefore cannot be derived from it.

329.Option: Given the high performance of the rK28 RDT and the excellent diagnostic accuracy of LAMP using peripheral blood, the Loopamp Leishmania Detection Kit can be used to replace invasive lymph node aspiration in most cases requiring parasite confirmation during VL diagnosis.
Evidence: Parasite confirmation by tissue aspirate microscopy is also used for test-of-cure, since serology is useless for this purpose as anti-Leishmania antibodies remain detectable up to several years after cure. Thus, this is another scenario where approaches to confirm Leishmania infection that are less invasive are required.

One method that could meet such criteria is loop-mediated isothermal amplification (LAMP) of DNA: it is performed at constant temperature (60–65°C) for target DNA amplification rather than thermocycling; is highly specific, based on a set of four primers recognizing six distinct regions on the target; it has high amplification efficiency and enables amplification within a short time; results can also be visualized using simple detection methods. Therefore, LAMP has emerged as a powerful tool for point-of-care diagnosis [12,13].

The Loopamp Leishmania Detection Kit had high specificity (99.01%), regardless of the type of sample tested (whole blood or buffy coat) or the sample processing method. The sensitivity was also high, with the best results obtained with whole blood processed either by the Boil & Spin method (97.62%) or by the QIAgen columns (100%).
Any of the LAMP methods as well as the RDT had a very good agreement with LNA-M (k = 0.96–0.99)...
329.Rationale: The passage identifies a clinical need for less invasive methods to confirm infection (e.g., for test-of-cure) because serology cannot be used for that purpose, and it explicitly states that LAMP could meet such criteria as a field-amenable, point-of-care molecular test. The empirical results reported show very high sensitivity and specificity for LAMP on peripheral blood and excellent agreement with the reference (LNA-M). Taken together, these statements support the epidemiological inference that LAMP performed on peripheral blood could replace invasive lymph node aspiration for parasite confirmation in most cases.

329.Option: The Loopamp Leishmania Detection Kit is the most sensitive and specific diagnostic tool available for VL, outperforming both serology and microscopy in all clinical contexts.
Evidence: Of the two serological test used, the rK28 RDT presented higher sensitivity (98.81%) and specificity (100%) than DAT, considering borderline results as either negative (88.10% and 78.22%) or positive (91.67% and 70.30%).

The Loopamp Leishmania Detection Kit had high specificity (99.01%), regardless of the type of sample tested (whole blood or buffy coat) or the sample processing method. The sensitivity was also high, with the best results obtained with whole blood processed either by the Boil & Spin method (97.62%) or by the QIAgen columns (100%).
Any of the LAMP methods as well as the RDT had a very good agreement with LNA-M (k = 0.96–0.99), while the agreement between DAT and LNA-M was inferior (k = 0.60–0.65, good agreement).
329.Rationale: The passage reports excellent performance for LAMP but also reports that the rK28 RDT had very high sensitivity and a specificity of 100%. LAMP's specificity (99.01%) is slightly lower in the reported results and its sensitivity varied by sample/preparation (though could reach 100% in one method). The statement that LAMP is the single most sensitive and specific test 'in all clinical contexts' is not supported by the comparative data presented, which show high but not uniformly superior performance across all contexts and tests. Therefore the absolute claim in the option is not derivable from the passage.

329.Option: Since the rK28 RDT has 98.81% sensitivity and 100% specificity, it can fully replace the need for any confirmatory testing in suspected VL cases, including during test-of-cure and relapse evaluation.
Evidence: Of the two serological test used, the rK28 RDT presented higher sensitivity (98.81%) and specificity (100%) than DAT...

Parasite confirmation by tissue aspirate microscopy is also used for test-of-cure, since serology is useless for this purpose as anti-Leishmania antibodies remain detectable up to several years after cure.
329.Rationale: Although the rK28 RDT shows excellent sensitivity and specificity for diagnosis in the study, the passage explicitly states that serology is useless for test-of-cure because antibodies remain detectable for years after cure. Therefore, serological tests (including the rK28 RDT) cannot replace confirmatory parasite-based testing for test-of-cure and relapse evaluation. The option's broad claim that RDT can fully replace any confirmatory testing (including test-of-cure and relapse) is contradicted by the passage.

330.Option: The standard Rose Bengal Test is unreliable in endemic settings due to cross-reactivity with other gram-negative bacteria, particularly Yersinia enterocolitica serotype O:9.
Evidence: Antibodies induced by some gram-negative bacteria (mainly Yersinia enterocolitica serotype O:9) react in all brucellosis tests but, while they may pose a problem in animals, this cross-reactivity is not relevant in human brucellosis because the syndromes caused by such bacteria are clinically very different [34].
Normally used with plain serum, RBT can be positive in persons from endemic areas and risk groups that do not have clinical symptoms, a problem largely solved by testing serum dilutions [21,30–32].

we report an estimation of the seroprevalence, and risk factors associated with occurrence of anti-Brucella antibodies among slaughterhouse workers in Uganda using serial testing with a combination of RBT and BrucellaCapt test, both of which have been validated and are valuable in the detection of contacts and short and long evolution cases of human brucellosis.
330.Rationale: The passage explicitly notes that although cross-reactive antibodies from bacteria such as Yersinia O:9 can react in brucellosis tests, this cross-reactivity "is not relevant in human brucellosis" because the clinical syndromes differ. The text also describes how RBT plain-serum positives in endemic areas are a known issue that can be addressed by using serum dilutions. The authors use serial testing (RBT plus BrucellaCapt) rather than asserting that RBT is broadly unreliable due to Yersinia cross-reaction. Thus the categorical assertion that the standard RBT is unreliable in endemic settings specifically because of Yersinia cross-reactivity is not supported by the passage.

330.Option: Despite the high likelihood of exposure to Brucella in slaughterhouse workers, the use of standard RBT with a titer ≥1:4 is sufficient for diagnosis under local conditions, reducing the need for additional confirmatory testing in most cases.
Evidence: Normally used with plain serum, RBT can be positive in persons from endemic areas and risk groups that do not have clinical symptoms, a problem largely solved by testing serum dilutions [21,30–32].

Out of the 31 sera positive in the standard RBT protocol, eight had a titre ≥ 1:8 in the modified RBT protocol, a very high titre in BrucellaCapt and seven self-reported symptoms (one case did not return this information).

Of the three sera that had an RBT titre of 1:4, two also had a high BrucellaCapt titre and one had a 1:640 titre, just above the proposed titre yielding optimal sensitivity (i.e., 95% for titres ≥ 1:320).

The remaining 21 RBT-positive sera had a titre 1:2 (i.e., only agglutinated with the plain serum) and of these only two had a 1:640 titre and 12 had a 1:320 titre with BrucellaCapt. When recorded, symptoms in these 21 slaughterhouse workers were variable.

we report an estimation ... using serial testing with a combination of RBT and BrucellaCapt test, both of which have been validated and are valuable in the detection of contacts and short and long evolution cases of human brucellosis.
330.Rationale: The passage demonstrates that plain RBT results (including low titres) can occur in asymptomatic persons in endemic settings and that assessing serum dilutions and complementary testing (BrucellaCapt) were used in this study. The breakdown of RBT-positive sera shows variability in concordance with BrucellaCapt and symptoms; only some RBT titres (e.g., higher modified RBT titres) corresponded consistently to high BrucellaCapt titres. The authors therefore employed serial testing rather than relying on a single RBT titre cutoff. The passage does not support the claim that a standard RBT titer ≥1:4 alone is sufficient to obviate confirmatory testing.

330.Option: The BrucellaCapt test is superior to the standard RBT for diagnosing brucellosis in Uganda because it detects both acute and long evolution cases and has higher sensitivity.
Evidence: The later tests include the technically demanding Coombs test, and the so called acid pH agglutination tests, namely the RBT and BrucellaCapt, which detect both acute and long evolution cases [21,25–29].
While the BrucellaCapt uses a microplate format and serum dilutions to obtain a diagnostic titre, the RBT is a rapid agglutination assay well suited for resource limited scenarios.

Of the three sera that had an RBT titre of 1:4, two also had a high BrucellaCapt titre and one had a 1:640 titre, just above the proposed titre yielding optimal sensitivity (i.e., 95% for titres ≥ 1:320).

we report an estimation of the seroprevalence ... using serial testing with a combination of RBT and BrucellaCapt test, both of which have been validated and are valuable in the detection of contacts and short and long evolution cases of human brucellosis.
Complementary testing was done using the BrucellaCapt test, performed according to the manufacturer’s instructions ... on all RBT positive sera samples.
330.Rationale: The passage describes BrucellaCapt as an acid-pH agglutination test that (like RBT) detects both acute and long-evolution cases, but explicitly notes that BrucellaCapt uses a microplate format and serial dilutions to obtain a diagnostic titre and that titres ≥ 1:320 yield optimal sensitivity (~95%). The study used BrucellaCapt as complementary/confirmatory testing for all RBT positives and employed serial RBT+BrucellaCapt testing to estimate seroprevalence. The text also highlights limitations of plain RBT (positivity in asymptomatic endemic persons unless dilutions are used). Taken together, the evidence in the passage supports the conclusion that BrucellaCapt offers superior diagnostic utility (particularly for capturing chronic/long-evolution infections and providing higher sensitivity at validated titre cutoffs) compared with relying only on the rapid slide agglutination RBT in this Ugandan context.

331.Option: The identification of compounds that inhibit both TGR and Prx2 in the dual-enzyme assay suggests that the most effective therapeutic strategy for schistosomiasis may involve dual-target inhibition of the parasite's redox cascade.
Evidence: Since inhibiting either TGR or Prx can potentially lead to schistosome death [22], we chose to screen both enzymes in one assay as a reconstituted redox cascade.

When tested against Prx2 in a GR-coupled hydrogen peroxide reduction assay none of the selected compounds showed activity up to the 50 µM top concentration tested (and by extension, none were active against GR, an enzyme related to TGR).
Prx2 was therefore ruled out as the target of any of the actives identified in the screen.
331.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that although the screen was designed to address both TGR and Prx2, target-deconvolution experiments showed that none of the selected compounds inhibited Prx2 and Prx2 was ruled out as a target. Therefore the evidence does not support the inference that the identified compounds inhibit both enzymes or that dual-target inhibition is what was found; rather the hits primarily target TGR. The claim that the most effective strategy is dual-target inhibition is not derivable from the Passage Body.

331.Option: The successful identification of potent inhibitors of TGR, combined with the lack of inhibition of human GR, suggests that these compounds may be used as broad-spectrum antiparasitic agents effective against multiple neglected tropical diseases.
Evidence: In contrast, humans possess two distinct enzymes, glutathione reductase (GR) and thioredoxin reductase (TrxR), which specifically recognize GSH and Trx as substrates, respectively [16].

When tested against Prx2 in a GR-coupled hydrogen peroxide reduction assay none of the selected compounds showed activity up to the 50 µM top concentration tested (and by extension, none were active against GR, an enzyme related to TGR).

The majority of active compounds demonstrated approximately the same, and in some instances improved (most notably with 7 and 8), potency against the isolated TGR as they did against the dual-enzyme system.
Such improved understanding of the organisms responsible for neglected tropical diseases (NTDs) presents opportunities for new drug development.
331.Rationale: The Passage Body supports that the identified hits act against parasite TGR and that Prx2 was not inhibited; it also notes the human redox enzymes differ from the parasite enzyme. However, there is no experimental evidence in the passage that these compounds inhibit other parasites or that they are broad-spectrum antiparasitic agents. The phrase "by extension, none were active against GR, an enzyme related to TGR" does not demonstrate lack of activity against human GR specifically, nor does the passage provide data on efficacy across multiple parasites or NTDs. Concluding broad-spectrum antiparasitic utility therefore requires extrapolation beyond the presented data and is not supported by the Passage Body alone.

331.Option: The identification of several novel structural series of TGR inhibitors, several of which are highly potent, provides both mechanistic tools for probing the redox balance in S. mansoni and starting points for developing medicinal leads for much-needed new treatments for schistosomiasis.
Evidence: Such improved understanding of the organisms responsible for neglected tropical diseases (NTDs) presents opportunities for new drug development.

In this report, we describe the miniaturization to 1536-well density of a cuvette-based assay for the TGR/Prx2 cascade which utilizes as a quantitative measure the decrease in fluorescence of the consumed NADPH substrate, the performance of a quantitative high-throughput screen (qHTS) [25] against 71,028 discrete compounds, and the initial characterization of several novel series of inhibitors.

Analysis of the qHTS results revealed 39 actives characterized by full concentration-response curves and IC50 values of better than 10 µM. After exclusion of antimony-containing compounds ... the following series and singletons were selected for further studies after SAR analysis (Figure 4): oxadiazole 2-oxides ..., phosphinic amides ..., phosphoramidite (singleton active, IC50 of 560 nM), and isoxazolone (singleton active, IC50 of 530 nM).

The majority of active compounds demonstrated approximately the same, and in some instances improved (most notably with 7 and 8), potency against the isolated TGR as they did against the dual-enzyme system. These results not only confirm the initial findings from the screen, but also further support the hypothesis of TGR being the sole target of these actives.
Since inhibiting either TGR or Prx can potentially lead to schistosome death [22], we chose to screen both enzymes in one assay as a reconstituted redox cascade.
331.Rationale: The Passage Body documents identification and confirmation of multiple novel structural series of inhibitors with sub-micromolar to low-micromolar potency, and shows these act primarily against TGR. It also frames TGR/Prx as critical to the parasite's antioxidant defense and notes that inhibiting these enzymes can lead to schistosome death. Within standard epidemiologic and drug-development reasoning, such validated, potent inhibitors provide mechanistic probes (to study parasite redox balance) and represent credible starting points for medicinal chemistry optimization toward new therapeutics. These inferences are directly supported by the presented data and statements about opportunities for drug development.

332.Option: The expansion of CD4+CD25+FOXP3+ T cells in hookworm-infected individuals is primarily driven by the direct induction of FOXP3 expression in naïve T cells by excretory-secretory products of nematodes.
Evidence: The parasite may promote its survival by excreting/secreting a panel of molecular immunosuppressive agents and, possibly, by stimulating the appearance of regulatory T-cell populations.
In the present study we described an augmentation of circulating CD4+CD25+FOXP3+ regulatory T cells in hookworm-infected individuals compared with healthy non-infected donors.

No differences in the proportion of cells expressing CTLA-4, GITR, TGF-β, IL-10, (Supplementary Figure S3) and median intensity of fluorescence were seen after hookworm antigen stimulation (p>0.05 for all).
When crude antigen was added to the in vitro cultures, the percentage and the absolute counts of CD4+CD25+FOXP3+ regulatory T cells co-expressing IL-17 was significantly reduced (p<0.0030).
332.Rationale: The passage reports an increase in circulating CD4+CD25+FOXP3+ T cells in infected individuals and notes that parasites secrete immunomodulatory products that may stimulate regulatory populations. However, the data presented do not show direct induction of FOXP3 in naive T cells by excretory-secretory (ES) products. Antigen stimulation experiments did not demonstrate increases in FOXP3 expression or other marker proportions (p>0.05 for many markers), and the only reported antigen effect was a reduction in IL-17 co-expressing Tregs. Thus the specific causal claim that expansion is primarily driven by direct induction of FOXP3 in naive T cells by ES products is not supported by the presented evidence.

332.Option: The absence of changes in T cell numbers or Treg counts in human primo-infection with N. americanus suggests that Treg-mediated immunosuppression is not a primary mechanism of hookworm survival in early infection.
Evidence: To investigate Treg activity in human hookworm infection, we have evaluated Treg frequencies, function and immune responses to hookworm antigens in N. americanus-infected individuals from a rural area of Minas Gerais state (Brazil).
In the present study we described an augmentation of circulating CD4+CD25+FOXP3+ regulatory T cells in hookworm-infected individuals compared with healthy non-infected donors.
Chronic human N. americanus infection is classically associated with a profound ablation of cell proliferation.
332.Rationale: The study population and results concern chronically infected individuals and document increased Treg frequency and functional effects in that chronic setting. The passage provides no data on primo-infection (early/primary infection) or on T cell/Treg dynamics during initial infection. Therefore one cannot infer from these chronic-infection data that Treg-mediated immunosuppression is absent or unimportant in early (primo-) infection. The option draws a conclusion about a time period (primo-infection) that the passage does not address.

332.Option: The reduction in CD4+CD25+FOXP3+IL-17+ cells after hookworm antigen stimulation indicates that IL-17 production is directly suppressed by parasite antigens, leading to a permanent shift toward a regulatory phenotype.
Evidence: When crude antigen was added to the in vitro cultures, the percentage and the absolute counts of CD4+CD25+FOXP3+ regulatory T cells co-expressing IL-17 was significantly reduced (p<0.0030).
Although there was a tendency in increase on the expression of IL-17 in ES stimulated blood it was not statistically significant (p = 0.3562) (Figure 3).

No differences in the proportion of cells expressing CTLA-4, GITR, TGF-β, IL-10, (Supplementary Figure S3) and median intensity of fluorescence were seen after hookworm antigen stimulation (p>0.05 for all).
332.Rationale: The passage shows that addition of crude hookworm antigen to whole blood cultures caused a significant reduction in the percentage and absolute counts of CD4+CD25+FOXP3+IL-17+ cells in vitro, which supports that parasite antigen can alter IL-17+ Treg frequencies in culture. However, the claim that IL-17 production is 'directly suppressed' by parasite antigens is stronger than the presented data can establish (the mechanism is not proven), and the further claim that this leads to a 'permanent shift toward a regulatory phenotype' is not supported at all — the experiments are short-term in vitro stimulations and no evidence of permanence or long-term phenotypic shift is provided. Thus the option overstates what can be inferred from the data.

332.Option: Hookworm-induced Treg activity may be able to control and divert selective proliferative and cytokine responses to numerous disorders, such as intestinal inflammation, airways inflammation/hyper-reactivity, diabetes, and multiple sclerosis.
Evidence: The discovery of regulatory T lymphocytes (Treg) that are actively involved in maintaining immune tolerance has recently led to new insights into mechanisms of tolerance breakdown and/or immunoregulation in human diseases, including those resulting from allergic, autoimmune, or infectious causes [14].
These results suggest that T regulatory cells do have the capacity to modulate the in vitro proliferative response in hookworm-infected patients.

Infection of N. americanus significantly increased the proportion of cells expressing CTLA-4 (p = 0.0002) and GITR (p<0.0001). Flow cytometric analysis also showed a significant augmentation of CD4+CD25+FOXP3+ cells producing IL-10 (p<0.0001), TGF-β (p<0.0001) and IL-17 (p = 0.0003) in hookworm infected individuals (Figure 2).
332.Rationale: The passage demonstrates that hookworm-associated Tregs can modulate antigen-specific proliferative responses in vitro and reminds that Tregs are relevant to allergic and autoimmune contexts more broadly. However, extrapolating from the observed in vitro modulation in infected donors to a conclusion that hookworm-induced Tregs 'may be able to control and divert' immune responses in a range of specific disorders (intestinal inflammation, airway hyper-reactivity, diabetes, multiple sclerosis) is speculative and not supported by the data presented. There are no experiments or clinical data in the passage testing the effect of hookworm infection or its Tregs on those specific diseases, so this broader therapeutic/clinical inference cannot be derived from the passage alone.

332.Option: The increased expression of GITR and CTLA-4 on Treg cells in hookworm-infected individuals is sufficient to fully account for the observed suppression of antigen-specific proliferation, independent of cytokine production.
Evidence: Infection of N. americanus significantly increased the proportion of cells expressing CTLA-4 (p = 0.0002) and GITR (p<0.0001). Flow cytometric analysis also showed a significant augmentation of CD4+CD25+FOXP3+ cells producing IL-10 (p<0.0001), TGF-β (p<0.0001) and IL-17 (p = 0.0003) in hookworm infected individuals (Figure 2).

in vitro cultures, where Tregs cells were depleted (dPBMC), showed a significant increase on the CD4+ cell proliferative response induced by crude (p = 0.0039) and ES (p = 0.0012) hookworm antigenic stimulation (Figure 4A).

The depletion of Tregs elicited the increase of IL-2 and lower levels of IL-10 in supernatants of dPBMCs with or without antigenic stimulation although statistical significance was not achieved (Supplementary Figure S4). No differences were also observed for IFN-γ and IL-5 (Supplementary Figure S4) after CD4+CD25+ depletion.
332.Rationale: While the passage documents increased proportions of Treg cells expressing the surface/inhibitory molecules CTLA-4 and GITR in infected individuals, it also documents increased production of regulatory cytokines (IL-10, TGF-β) by Treg populations and reports cytokine changes (IL-2 increase, IL-10 decrease trend) upon Treg depletion. The data therefore point to multiple potential mechanisms of suppression (surface molecules and cytokines). There is no experimental evidence in the passage that CTLA-4 and GITR expression alone are sufficient to fully explain the observed suppression independent of cytokine-mediated effects. Therefore the sufficiency and independence claims are not supported by the presented evidence.

333.Option: In endemic areas of intestinal schistosomiasis with low-intensity infections, the actual prevalence can be underestimated by up to 4.7 times when measured by the recommended standard procedure.
Evidence: most of the infected individuals in endemic areas harbor low parasite loads and are very unlikely to be detected with the commonly used parasitological methods [11,12].
It is very efficient in individuals with high to medium parasite loads, e.g. more than 100 eggs per gram of feces, but shows reduced sensitivity in individuals with low parasite loads.
Therefore, most of the infected individuals within the studied population had a light parasitic infection.

Importantly, the prevalence found in each age group, considering the combination of all parasitological exams (reference standard), was 1.7 to 4.7 times higher than the prevalence obtained with the recommended two KK slides from one fecal sample (Fig 4).

Overall, and taking into consideration the results of all the parasitological methods (reference standard), the prevalence of intestinal schistosomiasis reached 45.9%, which represents a 2.3 times increase in relation to the WHO’s recommended standard KK procedure.
333.Rationale: The passage states that most infections in the studied, long-term control setting are low-intensity and that the Kato-Katz (KK) method "shows reduced sensitivity in individuals with low parasite loads." The study found that when using a combined reference standard (multiple KK slides plus more sensitive methods), prevalence estimates were substantially higher than with the WHO-recommended two-slide KK procedure: across age groups the reference-standard prevalence was "1.7 to 4.7 times higher" than with the recommended two KK slides. These statements directly support the inference that standard recommended KK testing can underestimate true prevalence by up to 4.7-fold in low-intensity endemic settings.

334.Option: The hyaluronidase from Loxosceles venom is essential for the activation of phospholipase-D, thereby enabling the toxin to exert its dermonecrotic effects.
Evidence: The delivery of venom toxins to neighboring bite sites and into systemic circulation is assisted by molecules that degrade extracellular matrix constituents such as proteases and hyaluronidases [9], [10], [11], [12].

The idea that brown spider venom hyaluronidases play a role in the gravitational spreading of dermonecrosis and/or systemic diffusion of venom toxins, and then act as “spreading factors” comes from its degradative activity on hyaluronic acid and other glycosaminoglycans that mediate tissue integrity and stability.

On the other hand, the injection of recombinant hyaluronidase alone, PBS/BSA and a recombinant protein not related to hyaluronic acid hydrolysis (negative controls) did not show macroscopic erythema, ecchymosis or dermonecrosis (Fig. 6A and Fig.6B).

The length of dermonecrosis lesions in the rabbit skin of Dietrich's Hyaluronidase concomitantly injected with dermonecrotic toxin was substantially larger than for dermonecrotic toxin alone after 24 h (Fig. 6B).
334.Rationale: The passage describes hyaluronidase as an enzyme that degrades extracellular matrix components and acts as a 'spreading factor' that facilitates diffusion of venom constituents. It does not state or provide evidence that hyaluronidase activates phospholipase-D. Experimental data show hyaluronidase alone does not produce dermonecrosis but increases lesion size when combined with a dermonecrotic toxin, consistent with a facilitative/spreading role rather than an essential activator of phospholipase-D. Therefore the specific claim that hyaluronidase is essential for activation of phospholipase-D is not supported by the passage.

334.Option: The hyaluronidase from Loxosceles venom contributes to dermonecrotic injury by directly degrading collagen fibers and disrupting blood vessel walls in the dermis.
Evidence: recombinant hyaluronidase was able to disorganize the extracellular matrix from rabbit skin dermis (Fig. 7B-4).
disorganization of collagen fibers (Fig. 7B-3, 7B-4, 7B-5 and 7B-6) (white arrows);

By degrading glycosaminoglycans, the hyaluronidase reduces the viscosity of hyaluronic acid and renders the extracellular matrix less rigid. This change makes the matrix more permeable to other toxins and facilitates the spread of other venom constituents and inflammatory cell mediators [21], [22].
HA was completely degraded within 3 h (Fig. 5A, lane 3)
we noted that recombinant hyaluronidase degraded CS (Fig. 5B, lane 3) and did not degrade DS and HS (data not shown), similar to the native hyaluronidases from venom.

On the other hand, the injection of recombinant hyaluronidase alone, PBS/BSA and a recombinant protein not related to hyaluronic acid hydrolysis (negative controls) did not show macroscopic erythema, ecchymosis or dermonecrosis (Fig. 6A and Fig.6B).
334.Rationale: The passage shows that hyaluronidase disrupts the extracellular matrix and is associated with disorganization of collagen fibers in histology, but it also specifies that the enzyme degrades glycosaminoglycans (hyaluronic acid and chondroitin sulfate) rather than collagen. The experimental results show hyaluronidase alone did not cause dermonecrosis, indicating it is not directly the primary tissue-destructive agent. Thus while hyaluronidase contributes to extracellular matrix disorganization, there is no direct evidence in the passage that it enzymatically degrades collagen fibers or directly disrupts blood vessel walls; its role is described as facilitating spread of other toxins that produce those pathological changes. The statement that it 'directly' degrades collagen and disrupts vessels is not supported.

334.Option: The hyaluronidase from Loxosceles venom acts as a spreading factor that enhances the diffusion of other venom toxins, such as phospholipase-D, through the extracellular matrix, thereby increasing the area of dermonecrotic lesions and promoting gravitational spreading of skin injury.
Evidence: The idea that brown spider venom hyaluronidases play a role in the gravitational spreading of dermonecrosis and/or systemic diffusion of venom toxins, and then act as “spreading factors” comes from its degradative activity on hyaluronic acid and other glycosaminoglycans that mediate tissue integrity and stability.

By degrading glycosaminoglycans, the hyaluronidase reduces the viscosity of hyaluronic acid and renders the extracellular matrix less rigid. This change makes the matrix more permeable to other toxins and facilitates the spread of other venom constituents and inflammatory cell mediators [21], [22].

The length of dermonecrosis lesions in the rabbit skin of Dietrich's Hyaluronidase concomitantly injected with dermonecrotic toxin was substantially larger than for dermonecrotic toxin alone after 24 h (Fig. 6B).
The macroscopic gravitational spreading and edema induced by the two recombinant enzymes injected together were very similar to whole venom (Fig. 6C).

On the other hand, the injection of recombinant hyaluronidase alone, PBS/BSA and a recombinant protein not related to hyaluronic acid hydrolysis (negative controls) did not show macroscopic erythema, ecchymosis or dermonecrosis (Fig. 6A and Fig.6B).
334.Rationale: The passage explicitly frames hyaluronidase as a 'spreading factor' whose degradation of hyaluronic acid and other glycosaminoglycans increases matrix permeability and facilitates diffusion of other venom constituents. Experimentally, hyaluronidase alone produced no lesions but significantly increased lesion size and gravitational spreading when co-administered with a dermonecrotic toxin, and the combined effect resembled whole venom. These statements directly support the inference that hyaluronidase enhances diffusion of dermonecrotic toxins (e.g., phospholipase-D) and thereby increases lesion area and promotes gravitational spread.

334.Option: The hyaluronidase from Loxosceles venom functions as a major allergen that triggers anaphylactic reactions in susceptible individuals, contributing to the systemic symptoms observed in loxoscelism.
Evidence: Systemic involvement has also been reported including fever, malaise, weakness, nausea, vomiting and in severe cases, intravascular coagulation, hemolysis and acute renal disturbance [1], [2], [3], [4], [5].

The idea that brown spider venom hyaluronidases play a role in the gravitational spreading of dermonecrosis and/or systemic diffusion of venom toxins, and then act as “spreading factors” comes from its degradative activity on hyaluronic acid and other glycosaminoglycans that mediate tissue integrity and stability.

On the other hand, the injection of recombinant hyaluronidase alone, PBS/BSA and a recombinant protein not related to hyaluronic acid hydrolysis (negative controls) did not show macroscopic erythema, ecchymosis or dermonecrosis (Fig. 6A and Fig.6B).
334.Rationale: The passage describes systemic manifestations of loxoscelism (fever, malaise, coagulation disturbances, hemolysis, renal disturbance) but does not mention allergic mechanisms or anaphylaxis or present any data indicating hyaluronidase is an allergen. Instead, hyaluronidase is presented as an enzyme that degrades extracellular matrix glycosaminoglycans and facilitates spread of venom toxins. There is no experimental or textual evidence in the passage supporting the claim that it triggers anaphylactic reactions or acts as a major allergen; thus this option is not supported.

334.Option: The hyaluronidase from Loxosceles venom is primarily responsible for the direct induction of dermonecrotic lesions and systemic toxicity in brown spider bites.
Evidence: On the other hand, the injection of recombinant hyaluronidase alone, PBS/BSA and a recombinant protein not related to hyaluronic acid hydrolysis (negative controls) did not show macroscopic erythema, ecchymosis or dermonecrosis (Fig. 6A and Fig.6B).

The length of dermonecrosis lesions in the rabbit skin of Dietrich's Hyaluronidase concomitantly injected with dermonecrotic toxin was substantially larger than for dermonecrotic toxin alone after 24 h (Fig. 6B).

The idea that brown spider venom hyaluronidases play a role in the gravitational spreading of dermonecrosis and/or systemic diffusion of venom toxins, and then act as “spreading factors” comes from its degradative activity on hyaluronic acid and other glycosaminoglycans that mediate tissue integrity and stability.
334.Rationale: Experimental evidence in the passage shows that hyaluronidase alone did not produce dermonecrosis or macroscopic lesions, whereas co-administration with a dermonecrotic toxin increased lesion size and spreading. The authors explicitly describe hyaluronidase as a 'spreading factor' that facilitates diffusion of other toxins, rather than as the primary direct cause of dermonecrosis or systemic toxicity. Therefore the claim that hyaluronidase is primarily responsible for direct induction of lesions and systemic effects is not supported by the passage.

335.Option: The higher odds of cysticercosis in breeding female pigs compared to males indicates that reproductive status directly increases susceptibility to Taenia spp. infection.
Evidence: This research focused on an area of western Kenya ... Data from a cross-sectional survey ... were used.

The final model for presence of Taenia spp. antigen in pigs (Table 5) indicated that breeding sows had significantly higher odds of antigen presence compared to male pigs (OR = 10.35, p = 0.01), and flooding agricultural land and grassland demonstrated a positive association with the outcome (OR = 1.04, p = 0.004).
335.Rationale: The passage reports an association (higher odds in breeding sows) from a cross-sectional survey, but does not provide evidence of causality or mechanisms by which reproductive status would directly increase susceptibility. Observational cross-sectional data can show associations that may be confounded by age, management, or exposure differences (not described as causal in the text). Therefore a direct causal inference that reproductive status increases susceptibility cannot be derived from the passage.

335.Option: Environmental factors, including land cover and precipitation, influence the spatial distribution of Taenia spp. infections in humans and pigs, with flooding agricultural and grassland areas showing a non-linear association with cysticercosis risk.
Evidence: This research aimed to assess the hypothesis that spatial clustering of cysticercosis is the result of a combination of (a) localised transmission cycles giving rise to spatial aggregations and (b) the impact of environmental conditions on egg survival and, thus, onward transmission.
The percentage of land that was flooding agricultural or grassland had a quadratic relationship with presence of Taenia spp. antigen in humans (p = 0.04).
Precipitation was negatively associated with the outcome (OR = 0.998, p = 0.02).

The final model for presence of Taenia spp. antigen in pigs (Table 5) indicated that ... flooding agricultural land and grassland demonstrated a positive association with the outcome (OR = 1.04, p = 0.004).
335.Rationale: The passage explicitly set out to test environmental influences and reports statistical associations between land cover (flooding agricultural/grassland) and presence of Taenia spp. antigen in both humans and pigs, including a quadratic (non-linear) relationship in humans and a positive association in pigs. It also reports a statistical association with precipitation. These results support the inference that environmental factors influence spatial distribution and that flooding agricultural/grassland show a non-linear relationship with human cysticercosis risk.

335.Option: The spatial clustering of cysticercosis cases in households near tapeworm carriers is the primary driver of infection risk, overshadowing the influence of environmental factors.
Evidence: Spatial analyses have demonstrated significant clustering of taeniasis, porcine cysticercosis and human cysticercosis, with evidence of aggregation of human and porcine cysticercosis cases within close proximity to human tapeworm carriers [15–18].

Significant between-household variability was observed in the prevalence of Taenia spp. antigen (suggestive of cysticercosis) in humans, (likelihood ratio test p<0.005), but not in pigs (likelihood ratio test p>0.05).
The percentage of land that was flooding agricultural or grassland had a quadratic relationship with presence of Taenia spp. antigen in humans (p = 0.04).
The final model for presence of Taenia spp. antigen in pigs ... flooding agricultural land and grassland demonstrated a positive association with the outcome (OR = 1.04, p = 0.004).
335.Rationale: While the passage notes prior evidence of clustering near tapeworm carriers and shows between-household variability, the study also demonstrates statistically significant environmental associations. The passage does not provide evidence that household proximity to carriers is the 'primary driver' that overshadows environmental factors. Therefore the stronger claim of primary dominance is not supported by the presented results.

335.Option: The lack of correlation between microscopy and copro-antigen ELISA results in this study suggests that the copro-antigen ELISA is unreliable for detecting taeniasis, and thus, the observed spatial patterns in cysticercosis may be influenced by diagnostic inaccuracies.
Evidence: The correlation between the two diagnostic methods for taeniasis (microscopy and copro-antigen ELISA) was zero: four participants were positive for taeniasis via microscopy only, 397 were positive for taeniasis via copro-antigen detection only and none were positive using both methods.

Based on the lack of correlation between the two diagnostic methods, and unexpectedly high number of positive results, the taeniasis results were not used in further statistical analyses as a precautionary measure.

Taeniasis detection was carried out for human participants using microscopy (sensitivity 28.6% to 52.5%; specificity 85.7% to 99.9% [20,21]) and copro-antigen ELISA (sensitivity and specificity of 98% and 99.1%, respectively [20]), which identify current Taenia spp. infections.
335.Rationale: The passage documents a lack of concordance between microscopy and copro-antigen ELISA in this dataset and states the taeniasis results were excluded from further analysis. However, it also provides published sensitivity/specificity estimates for the copro-antigen ELISA, and the discordance in this study is described without concluding that the copro-antigen ELISA is inherently unreliable. Moreover, cysticercosis spatial analyses in the study are based on HP10 antigen detection (different assays). The passage does not support the broad claim that observed cysticercosis spatial patterns are driven by diagnostic inaccuracies of the copro-antigen ELISA.

335.Option: The negative association between precipitation and cysticercosis risk suggests that rainfall reduces environmental contamination, thereby decreasing transmission of Taenia spp. eggs.
Evidence: Precipitation was negatively associated with the outcome (OR = 0.998, p = 0.02).

Surface moisture and humidity are thought to be the main constraining factors for Taenia spp. eggs in the environment: the eggs are vulnerable to desiccation and survival is greatly reduced under dry conditions, regardless of temperature [11].
Egg survival is influenced by temperature and humidity, with tropical regions being particularly suitable for transmission [10].
335.Rationale: The passage reports a statistical negative association between precipitation and antigen presence, but does not provide mechanistic evidence that rainfall reduces environmental contamination; in fact, earlier text indicates that moisture and humidity favor egg survival and desiccation reduces survival. Thus the mechanistic claim that rainfall reduces contamination and transmission contradicts other statements in the passage and is not directly supported. The observed negative association could reflect complex, context-specific relationships (e.g., excess rainfall effects, landscape factors, or statistical confounding), but the specific causal mechanism stated in the option cannot be derived from the passage.

336.Option: The high confidence in domestically-made vaccines and the preference for them over foreign-made vaccines indicate that public trust in China’s vaccine regulatory system has been fully restored after past scandals.
Evidence: Fig 4 shows the findings on the confidence of domestic and foreign-made COVID-19 vaccines. The vast majority of the study participants reported that they were confident (48.7%, 95%CI 47.0 to 50.3) or completely confident (46.1%, 95%CI 44.5 to 47.8) in domestically-made COVID-19 vaccines.

Findings on the preference of domestically-made over foreign-made COVID-19 vaccines revealed that 64.2% (95%CI 62.6 to 65.8) reported a preference for domestically-made and 11.9% (95%CI 10.8 to 13.0) preferred foreign-made COVID-19 vaccines.

In China, vaccine hesitancy is a complex public health issue. In the last decade, vaccine scandals and a series of reports about the serious side-effects of vaccination have increased vaccination hesitancy and distrust in the country’s immunization program [4].
336.Rationale: The passage documents high current confidence and preference for domestically-made vaccines, but it also explicitly describes prior vaccine scandals that increased hesitancy and distrust. The data show current attitudes but do not provide longitudinal evidence comparing trust before and after the scandals, nor do they directly assess the regulatory system. Therefore the stronger claim that public trust has been "fully restored" is not supported by the presented cross-sectional survey data and cannot be derived from the passage.

336.Option: The perception of susceptibility to COVID-19 was not a significant predictor of vaccination intention, indicating that public health campaigns should focus on increasing fear of infection to drive vaccine uptake.
Evidence: The participants had low perceptions of susceptibility. The majority disagreed that there was a great chance of getting COVID-19 in the next few months (68.7%), were also not worried about the likelihood of getting COVID-19 (56.8%), and disagreed that it was currently possible that they would get COVID-19 (72.0%).
Most of the constructs in the HBM model were significantly associated with having a definite intention for COVID-19 vaccination in the univariate analysis.
Perception that vaccination decreases the chances of getting COVID-19 under the perceived benefit construct (OR = 3.14, 95% CI 2.05–4.83) was the strongest predictor for a definite intention.
336.Rationale: The passage does not support the claim that perceived susceptibility was not a significant predictor; it reports that most HBM constructs were significantly associated with definite intention in univariate analyses and does not single out susceptibility as non-significant. More importantly, the passage identifies perceived benefits (belief that vaccination reduces risk) as the strongest predictor of definite intention, not fear-based susceptibility. Thus recommending campaigns to increase fear of infection is not a conclusion derivable from the provided findings.

336.Option: The significant positive moderating effect of income level on the relationship between perceived susceptibility, severity, and cues to action with WTP suggests that public health interventions should prioritize higher-income groups to maximize vaccine uptake.
Evidence: The results of the moderating effect of income level on the relationship between the constructs of HBM and WTP are shown in Table 4. A significant positive moderating effect of income level on the relationship perceived susceptibility and WTP was found (β = 3.007, p = 0.045). Income level was also found significantly moderated the relationship between perceived severity and WTP (β = 3.980, p = 0.026).

This moderation effect of income level was not significant for the relationship between perceived benefit (β = 5.597, p = 0.064) and WTP. The level of income also had no moderating effect on the relationship between barriers (β = 0.743, p = 0.551) and WTP. Income level showed a strong statistically significant moderating effect on the relationship between cues to action (β = 0.313, p = 0.551) and WTP.
A multivariable multinomial logistic regression was employed to model factors associated with marginal WTP for the COVID-19 vaccine for three price ranges...
336.Rationale: The passage documents that income moderates the relationship between certain HBM constructs and willingness-to-pay (WTP), not directly vaccine uptake. The moderation refers to WTP (amount willing to pay), not necessarily the probability of accepting vaccination. Moreover, the quoted p-values show that cues to action did not show a significant moderating effect (p values quoted are not significant), and the passage does not provide evidence that prioritizing higher-income groups would "maximize vaccine uptake."
