Therefore prioritization of higher-income groups for uptake is not a conclusion that can be derived from the presented evidence.

336.Option: The fact that only 28.7% of respondents reported a definite intention to vaccinate suggests that the majority of the population in China is unwilling to accept a COVID-19 vaccine, necessitating mandatory vaccination policies.
Evidence: On the whole, a total of 2,950 (83.5%; 95%CI 82.3–84.8) participants responded yes to COVID-19 vaccine intent, while only 582 (16.4%; 95%CI 15.2–17.7) responded no.

By a more specific breakdown, the majority responded probably yes (54.8%; 95% CI 53.2–56.5) followed by definitely yes (28.7%; 95% CI 27.3–30.3). Only 4.5% (95% CI 3.9–5.3) responded definitely no and 11.9% (95% CI 10.8–13.9) reported probably no.
336.Rationale: The passage shows that 83.5% expressed some intention to vaccinate (probably or definitely yes), and only a small fraction were definitely unwilling. Thus it is incorrect to infer that the majority are unwilling. Further, recommending mandatory vaccination is a policy prescription not supported by the descriptive survey data alone. The data do not imply necessity for coercive policies and do not provide evidence about legal, ethical, or practical implications of mandates.

336.Option: Despite concerns about the safety and efficacy of the vaccine, the perception of benefits and the provision of adequate information were strong predictors of a definite intention to receive the COVID-19 vaccine.
Evidence: High perceptions of the benefits of COVID-19 vaccination were reported. The majority (87.2%) perceived the benefit of feeling less worry of contracting the coronavirus after getting the vaccine. A higher proportion (92.5%) perceived benefit of the COVID-19 vaccine in reducing the risk of infection and resultant complications.
Concerns about safety (72.6%) and efficacy (71.2%) were also notable.

Many of the participants (92.1%) reported that they would only receive the COVID-19 vaccine if given adequate information, and 82.3% reported that they will only take the vaccine if taken by many in the public.
Perception that vaccination decreases the chances of getting COVID-19 under the perceived benefit construct (OR = 3.14, 95% CI 2.05–4.83) was the strongest predictor for a definite intention.
336.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports strong perceived benefits and a very large proportion stating they would only vaccinate if given adequate information. It also shows safety and efficacy concerns are common. The strongest statistical predictor of definite intention was the belief that vaccination reduces the chance of infection (perceived benefit). These facts together support the inference that emphasizing benefits and providing adequate information can drive definite vaccination decisions despite existing safety/efficacy concerns.

337.Option: The multivariate regression model incorporating HI and socioeconomic/climatic variables is superior to simulation models in predicting DHF incidence, suggesting that real-world data can fully capture the dynamics of antibody-dependent enhancement.
Evidence: In multivariate analysis, the following six variables remained in the final regression model (Table 1; Figure 4): HI, winter temperature, summer temperature, APET, public large wells, and birth rate.
this multivariate predictive model still failed to reproduce the very wide variation in the observed incidence (compare Figure 2B vs Figure 5).

The predictability attained solely by HI was much inferior to that attained in any simulations (Figure 8). However, the predictability using the multivariate regression model was as good as that in simulations with cross-immunity, up to W = 8.
337.Rationale: The passage does not support the claim that the multivariate regression model is superior to simulation models or that real-world data fully capture antibody-dependent enhancement (ADE) dynamics. Although the multivariate model included HI and multiple covariates and its predictability was comparable to cross-immunity simulations up to a limited averaging window (W = 8), the authors explicitly state that the multivariate model "still failed to reproduce the very wide variation in the observed incidence." Thus it cannot be concluded that the regression model is superior to simulations or that it fully captures ADE dynamics.

337.Option: The observed non-monotonic relationship between DHF incidence and HI is primarily driven by age-dependent differences in the probability of manifesting severe illness, independent of cross-serotype immunity.
Evidence: GAM detected a non-monotonic relationship in the simulations with cross-immunity (Figure 7A), a negative relationship in those with age-dependency (Figure 7E), and a slightly positive relationship in the control simulations (Figure 7I).
Age-stratification of the simulation results generated a similar trend in the empirical data, regardless of the presence of cross-immunity or age-dependency (Figure 7B–D, F–H, J–L).
337.Rationale: The passage shows that non-monotonic patterns were produced specifically by simulations that incorporated cross-serotype cross-immunity, whereas simulations that included only age-dependency produced a negative relationship. Therefore, the non-monotonic relationship is not shown to be primarily driven by age-dependency independent of cross-serotype immunity. Instead, the evidence implicates cross-immunity as a key mechanism producing the non-monotonic pattern.

337.Option: The negative response of DHF incidence to transmission intensity at areas of intense transmission has important public health implications, regardless of its underlying mechanism.
Evidence: A mathematical model recently predicted that, due to the presence of transient cross-serotype immunity, the incidence of DHF and transmission intensity will be correlated negatively at high transmission intensities.
Univariate analysis of GAM revealed that the correlation between HI and incidence was positive below about HI = 30, while the correlation was negative above this HI value (Figure 2B; Figure 3).
Our findings have major implications for future epidemiological surveys and dengue control programs.
337.Rationale: The passage documents that at high HI (a surrogate for transmission intensity) the correlation with DHF incidence becomes negative, and the authors explicitly state that the findings have major implications for surveillance and control programs. Thus it is epidemiologically reasonable to infer that the negative response of incidence at high transmission levels carries important public health implications, even if the precise mechanism (e.g., cross-immunity, age-structure) is not definitively established within the passage.

337.Option: Since HI is strongly correlated with vector abundance, reducing HI to below 30 will inevitably lead to a significant reduction in DHF incidence across all transmission settings.
Evidence: HI is defined as the percentage of all surveyed houses in which Aedes larvae or pupae are present...
Univariate analysis of GAM revealed that the correlation between HI and incidence was positive below about HI = 30, while the correlation was negative above this HI value (Figure 2B; Figure 3).
Multivariate analysis estimated that, as HI decreases from 70 to 30, log incidence would increase by 0.6 (Figure 4A), which corresponds to an increase of 80%.
337.Rationale: The passage demonstrates that the relationship between HI and DHF incidence is non-monotonic: at HI values below ~30 the correlation is positive (higher HI associated with higher incidence), but at higher HI the correlation becomes negative. Moreover, the multivariate analysis indicates that decreasing HI from 70 to 30 could be associated with an increase in incidence. Therefore the claim that reducing HI below 30 will inevitably lead to reduced incidence in all settings is not supported and is contradicted by the presented results.

337.Option: The high variability in DHF incidence across districts is primarily due to differences in the number of public large water wells, which serve as the main breeding sites for Aedes mosquitoes.
Evidence: Aedes mosquitoes [2]–[4], which breed predominantly in water-holding containers within human habitats.
In multivariate analysis, the following six variables remained in the final regression model (Table 1; Figure 4): HI, winter temperature, summer temperature, APET, public large wells, and birth rate.
this multivariate predictive model still failed to reproduce the very wide variation in the observed incidence (compare Figure 2B vs Figure 5).
337.Rationale: While public large wells were one of several covariates retained in the final regression model, the passage does not state that differences in public large wells are the primary cause of the large inter-district variability. The authors note that Aedes breed mainly in a variety of water-holding containers and that the multivariate model—including public large wells—still failed to account for the very wide variation in incidence. Thus attributing the high variability primarily to public large wells is not supported by the presented evidence.

338.Option: The 'valley of death' in diagnostic development is primarily caused by insufficient funding for large-scale prospective studies, which prevents promising early-phase candidates from being validated in real-world settings.
Evidence: Thus 86 out of the 96 tests (89%) were evaluated in phase I and/or II only.
Seven tests (6 antigens) in phase III are evaluated on less than 600 specimens, while 3 antigens/tests are evaluated on 601 to 5000 specimens and no antigen is evaluated on >5000 specimens.
338.Rationale: The passage documents a pattern of many tests remaining in early-phase evaluations and few progressing to large-scale studies (e.g., "Thus 86 out of the 96 tests (89%) were evaluated in phase I and/or II only."). However, it does not provide any evidence attributing this pattern primarily to insufficient funding. The cited text describes the distribution of evaluation phases and sample sizes but contains no statements about funding, financial constraints, or resource allocation as the cause. Therefore the causal claim that the 'valley of death' is primarily due to insufficient funding cannot be derived from the Passage Body.

338.Option: The diagnostic accuracy of rK28-based immunochromatographic tests in East Africa is consistently high across multiple studies, suggesting that this antigen is a reliable candidate for widespread deployment in the region.
Evidence: In 2010, an RDT with rK28 as antigen has been developed to overcome the issue of low sensitivity of rK39-based tests in Eastern Africa.

RDTs with rK28 antigen have been evaluated in four studies in Sudan and Ethiopia with promising results for the test manufactured by CTK Biotech (sensitivities between 94 and 98%, and specificities between 95 and 98%)

With rk28 having been evaluated on a relatively small number of specimens (VL suspected patients: 285), there is a risk that its performance turns out to be variable based on the population it is evaluated on.

Seventeen of the tests in phase I, 11 in phase II and 1 test in phase III (rK28 immunochromatographic test [ICT] from CTK) consistently have sensitivities above 94% and specificities above 97% in all studies analysed (green in Fig 2 and grey in Table 1).

Only Indirect Fluorescent Antibody Technique (IFAT) and the rK28 ICT were shown to be >94% sensitive and >97% specific when evaluated in phase III studies on respectively 104 and 285 East African persons [15,108].
338.Rationale: The Passage Body provides evidence that rK28 ICT has shown high sensitivity and specificity in several studies (e.g., "sensitivities between 94 and 98%... specificities between 95 and 98%" and that rK28 ICT from CTK "consistently have sensitivities above 94% and specificities above 97% in all studies analysed"). It also notes that rK28 ICT was >94%/>97% in a phase III evaluation on 285 East African persons. However, the passage also explicitly cautions that rK28 was evaluated on a relatively small number of specimens and that "there is a risk that its performance turns out to be variable based on the population it is evaluated on." The option's additional inference that this implies the antigen is a reliable candidate for widespread deployment goes beyond the presented evidence, which highlights both promising results and the small sample size/risk of variable performance. Therefore the assertive conclusion about reliable widespread deployment is not fully supported by the passage.

338.Option: The diagnostic development pipeline for visceral leishmaniasis serodiagnostic tests is characterized by a high proportion of early-phase evaluations, with most candidates not advancing to large-scale prospective studies despite showing high diagnostic accuracy in initial assessments.
Evidence: Thus 86 out of the 96 tests (89%) were evaluated in phase I and/or II only.

Seventeen of the tests in phase I, 11 in phase II and 1 test in phase III (rK28 immunochromatographic test [ICT] from CTK) consistently have sensitivities above 94% and specificities above 97% in all studies analysed (green in Fig 2 and grey in Table 1).
Ten tests representing 9 antigens, were evaluated in phase III studies.
338.Rationale: The passage explicitly describes that the vast majority of tests remain in early-phase evaluation: "Thus 86 out of the 96 tests (89%) were evaluated in phase I and/or II only." It also shows that multiple tests in early phases already meet high accuracy thresholds (e.g., "Seventeen of the tests in phase I... consistently have sensitivities above 94% and specificities above 97%"), while only a small number reached phase III ("Ten tests representing 9 antigens, were evaluated in phase III studies"). Taken together, these statements support the inference that the pipeline is dominated by early-phase studies and that many promising high-performing candidates do not advance to large-scale prospective evaluation.

338.Option: The majority of serodiagnostic tests for visceral leishmaniasis are not advanced to phase III because they fail to meet minimum diagnostic accuracy thresholds in early-phase evaluations.
Evidence: Thus 86 out of the 96 tests (89%) were evaluated in phase I and/or II only.

Seventeen of the tests in phase I, 11 in phase II and 1 test in phase III (rK28 immunochromatographic test [ICT] from CTK) consistently have sensitivities above 94% and specificities above 97% in all studies analysed (green in Fig 2 and grey in Table 1).
The main reasons for exclusion in the full text screen were the following: ... the diagnostic sensitivity and/or specificity was <50% (n = 9).
338.Rationale: While the passage indicates that most tests remain in early phases ("Thus 86 out of the 96 tests (89%) were evaluated in phase I and/or II only."), it also documents that a substantive number of early-phase tests did achieve high accuracy (e.g., "Seventeen of the tests in phase I... consistently have sensitivities above 94% and specificities above 97%"). Only a small number of studies were excluded for very low accuracy ("diagnostic sensitivity and/or specificity was <50% (n = 9)"). Therefore, the claim that the primary reason most tests are not advanced to phase III is failure to meet accuracy thresholds in early phases is not supported by the passage; many early-phase candidates show high accuracy but still do not progress, implying other reasons beyond early-phase failure.

338.Option: The lack of standardized production methods for native antigens with carbohydrate and lipid moieties contributes to high batch-to-batch variation, which limits their potential for commercialization and widespread use in low-resource settings.
Evidence: Fifty-three antigens (59%) were expressed in E. coli or on phages or were synthetic peptides and thus do not contain eukaryotic glycosylated or lipid moieties.

The remaining 34 (38%) are divided in three categories. In 14, the nature of the antigen could not be deducted from its isolation procedure (parasite lysate run on sodium dodecyl sulfate polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (SDS-PAGE) and Western blot, antigen eluted form SDS-PAGE, acid extraction, isolation using a Lipidex-1000 column [34,41,44,48,63,104,118,121]) and no additional analysis has been performed to conclude on its nature.

All 20 antigens with a known carbohydrate and or lipid moiety are native antigens. Fourteen are antigen mixtures of undefined identity, such as the DAT [122] and soluble leishmanial antigen (SLA) (e.g. [62]) which consist of a mixture of parasite molecules including carbohydrates and lipids.
338.Rationale: The Passage Body documents that many native antigens contain carbohydrate and/or lipid moieties and that several are mixtures of undefined identity (e.g., "All 20 antigens with a known carbohydrate and or lipid moiety are native antigens... Fourteen are antigen mixtures of undefined identity"). This supports the observation that such antigens are compositionally complex and not always well-defined. However, the passage does not provide explicit evidence about "lack of standardized production methods," "high batch-to-batch variation," or direct consequences for commercialization and use in low-resource settings. Those are plausible concerns but are not stated or demonstrated in the text; thus the specific causal and commercial claims in the option cannot be directly derived from the Passage Body.

339.Option: Participants who reported trying to do as many physical and social activities as possible were more likely to experience a faster recovery and return to normal functioning, suggesting that active coping strategies are clinically effective.
Evidence: In contrast, people with lingering complaints while reporting less impact of chikungunya also showed different coping strategies, i.e. emotional coping strategies.
The participants using emotional coping strategies went less often to a physician, used less medications and reported trying to do as many physical/social activities as possible.
For example, they tried to mobilize by performing the physical exercises or movements they could do. With this in mind, people were more willing to accept that symptoms would eventually subside.

That it hurts and you see that it, there’s no eeeh, no eeeh, there’s no way to remove the pain. There’s no eh and then you hear ‘o they [people] don’t have it [chikungunya] anymore?’ But I still have it!
339.Rationale: The passage states that people using emotional (acceptance-focused) coping reported doing more physical/social activities and reported less perceived impact. However, it does not provide evidence of faster clinical recovery or objective return to normal functioning attributable to these active coping behaviors. The passage also emphasizes that there is no cure and persistent pain may remain, so inferring clinical effectiveness or faster recovery from the self-reported behaviors goes beyond the presented data. Therefore the specific claim that active coping leads to faster recovery is not supported by the passage.

339.Option: Since effective long-term treatments are not available yet for chronic chikungunya, the high uptake of (alternative) medicines and frequent physician visits will normally not lead to success, resulting in dissatisfaction and further emotional stress.
Evidence: that was the thing the thing that people kept stating: it is not curable and there is no treatment.

Many participants had used medicines to treat their physical complaints. Those included the medicines bought over the counter or prescribed by the physician, amongst which were mainly paracetamol or NSAID’s. Furthermore, natural or other alternative medicines were very popular since they had been prominently presented in advertisements and media by alternative healers.
Frequent physician visits were linked to desperation, a desire for solace, persistence of symptoms, and a desire to try new treatment.

Every week I was at the doctor, because I had pain here, pain at the feet, uh! (…) it didn’t disappear and according to me I say ‘yes, there should be something that I can use or so’ (…) I don’t know if 20 times, the first months for sure every day at the doctor told me ‘I can really not do anything for you’.
Frequent physician visits of health care providers as coping strategy led to unmet expectations for cure or treatment, resulting in stress (negative impact on QoL).
339.Rationale: The passage explicitly states there is no cure/treatment for chikungunya and documents widespread use of over-the-counter, prescribed and alternative medicines as well as frequent physician visits. It also explicitly links frequent physician visits and attempts to find treatments to desperation, unmet expectations and resulting stress. From these statements it is reasonable to infer that high uptake of medicines and physician consultations, in the context of absent effective long-term treatments, typically will not achieve clinical success and contributes to dissatisfaction and emotional stress. This conclusion is directly supported by the provided quotes.

339.Option: The universal health care program in Curaçao minimized out-of-pocket expenditures and reduced the financial burden on individuals, which may have contributed to higher healthcare utilization among chikungunya patients.
Evidence: Generally, all inhabitants of Curaçao are insured for most medical expenses through a state sponsored health insurance scheme (Social Insurance Bank), through their employer, or through a private health insurance.

The financial impact due to chikungunya on participant’s personal situation was generally perceived as limited. In Curaçao, chikungunya patients could rely on an insurance system, which covered health care costs, which in turn minimized out-of-pocket expenditures. These insurances covered also parts of the income loss of chikungunya patients who could not work.
Frequent physician visits were also performed because people needed to prove disease persistence in order to be reimbursed for the absence from their work.

Every week I was at the doctor, because I had pain here, pain at the feet, uh! (…) it didn’t disappear and according to me I say ‘yes, there should be something that I can use or so’ (…) I don’t know if 20 times, the first months for sure every day at the doctor told me ‘I can really not do anything for you’.
339.Rationale: The passage documents that most inhabitants have insurance covering medical costs and partial income loss, and that participants perceived limited personal financial impact due to those coverages. It also provides direct evidence that frequent physician visits were performed in part to obtain documentation for reimbursement of work absence. These statements support the inference that minimized out-of-pocket costs could facilitate or be associated with higher healthcare utilization among chikungunya patients. While the passage does not present formal utilization-rate analyses, linking lower financial barriers to increased use of services is a reasonable inference supported by the quoted material (hence the cautious phrasing 'may have contributed' is justified).

339.Option: Problem-focused coping strategies aimed at physical healing induced a high uptake of (alternative) medicines and consultation of physicians, whereas emotional coping strategies focused on acceptance of the situation were linked to lower perceived impacts on HRQoL and less uptake of medical care.
Evidence: Within the community, problem-focused coping was commonly performed by using (natural) medicines and by visiting a physician to cope with the physical impact of chikungunya.

Many participants had used medicines to treat their physical complaints. Those included the medicines bought over the counter or prescribed by the physician, amongst which were mainly paracetamol or NSAID’s. Furthermore, natural or other alternative medicines were very popular since they had been prominently presented in advertisements and media by alternative healers.
Frequent physician visits were linked to desperation, a desire for solace, persistence of symptoms, and a desire to try new treatment.
In contrast, people with lingering complaints while reporting less impact of chikungunya also showed different coping strategies, i.e. emotional coping strategies.
The participants using emotional coping strategies went less often to a physician, used less medications and reported trying to do as many physical/social activities as possible.
339.Rationale: The passage explicitly contrasts problem-focused and emotional coping. It documents that problem-focused coping commonly involved physician consultations and use of (including alternative) medicines, and that frequent doctor visits were common among those seeking cures. It also states that individuals employing emotional coping strategies went to physicians less often, used fewer medications, engaged in more activity/acceptance behaviors, and reported less perceived impact on HRQoL. This option is a direct restatement of the patterns described in the study and is therefore supported by the passage.

340.Option: The increase in the median age of hospitalized dengue cases over time indicates that younger patients are being under-treated or under-referred to hospitals.
Evidence: the median age among hospitalized cases increased from 38 years in 2007 to 43 years in 2017 (p < 0.0005) (Fig 3).
In the aftermath of the 2004–2005 dengue epidemic in Singapore, hospitals reviewed and refined their admission criteria for dengue cases [11–13].
The proportion of dengue cases hospitalized plummeted from 97.3% in 2003 to an all-time low of 25.6% in 2014 (p < 0.0005).
340.Rationale: The passage documents a rise in the median age of hospitalized cases and major changes in admission criteria with a large decline in the proportion hospitalized. However, there is no evidence in the passage that younger patients are being under-treated or under-referred. The observed age shift can be explained by changing admission practices and demographic shifts described in the text, but the specific causal claim that younger patients are under-treated is not supported by any verbatim data or statements in the passage.

340.Option: The reduction in hospitalization rates for dengue cases is primarily due to improved early diagnosis and management in primary care settings, which has led to fewer patients requiring hospital admission.
Evidence: In the aftermath of the 2004–2005 dengue epidemic in Singapore, hospitals reviewed and refined their admission criteria for dengue cases [11–13].
The proportion of dengue cases hospitalized plummeted from 97.3% in 2003 to an all-time low of 25.6% in 2014 (p < 0.0005).
340.Rationale: The passage attributes the decline in hospitalization to hospitals reviewing and refining admission criteria and describes changes in MOH guidance; it does not present evidence that improved early diagnosis or primary care management was the primary driver. The passage does not provide data on primary-care diagnostic or management changes or attribute the reduction primarily to those factors, so this causal inference cannot be derived from the provided text.

340.Option: The implementation of more selective admission criteria for dengue has not led to an increase in adverse outcomes, despite a significant reduction in the proportion of cases hospitalized.
Evidence: In the aftermath of the 2004–2005 dengue epidemic in Singapore, hospitals reviewed and refined their admission criteria for dengue cases [11–13].
The proportion of dengue cases hospitalized plummeted from 97.3% in 2003 to an all-time low of 25.6% in 2014 (p < 0.0005).
The CFR ranged from 0% in 2017 to 0.27% in 2006.
About 0.6% to 1.5% of dengue patients in hospital were admitted to the ICU during the study period (Table 1).
The LOS in hospital remained stable over the years; both the annual mean and median LOS were 3 to 4 days.
340.Rationale: The passage documents that admission criteria were reviewed and refined, and that the proportion hospitalized declined markedly. At the same time, indicators of adverse outcomes (case fatality rate, ICU admission proportions, and stable length of stay) do not show a worsening trend in the presented data. From these facts, it is epidemiologically reasonable to infer that more selective admission did not lead to an increase in adverse outcomes over the study period.

340.Option: The proportion of dengue cases hospitalized in the elderly aged ≥65 years has been highest since 2013, which may be attributed to both the MOH guideline to refer for hospital evaluation those aged ≥65 years as well as a shift in demographic profile of dengue cases towards older adults.
Evidence: In the majority of years after 2007, the hospitalization rate per 100,000 population was highest in the elderly ≥65 years of age.

older adults (aged ≥45 years) accounted for an increasingly higher proportion over the 15-year period: from 21.0% to 29.9% in the age group 45–64 years, and from 4.9% to 16.8% in elderly patients ≥65 years of age (all p < 0.0005) (S3 Table).

In 2007, a medical expert committee established the criteria for immediate referral to hospitals, which comprised objective criteria such as ... as well as subjective criteria such as severe vomiting or diarrhoea, severe abdominal pain, and elderly patients with medical co-morbidities who are unwell [15].
340.Rationale: The passage supports that hospitalization rates are highest in the elderly (≥65) in the years after 2007 and documents a demographic shift toward older age groups. It also records that MOH/hospital criteria included 'elderly patients with medical co-morbidities who are unwell' as a subjective referral criterion. However, the option asserts a MOH guideline to refer all persons aged ≥65 for hospital evaluation (an explicit age-based referral), which is not stated in the passage. Because the specific claim about an MOH guideline to refer all ≥65 is not supported, the combined attribution in the option cannot be fully derived from the passage.

340.Option: The implementation of more selective admission criteria by hospitals has led to a significant reduction in hospital bed utilization, even during large dengue epidemics.
Evidence: In the aftermath of the 2004–2005 dengue epidemic in Singapore, hospitals reviewed and refined their admission criteria for dengue cases [11–13].
The proportion of dengue cases hospitalized plummeted from 97.3% in 2003 to an all-time low of 25.6% in 2014 (p < 0.0005).
There were two large epidemics, each stretching over two years, in 2004–2005 and 2013–2014, both of which were associated with a switch in the predominant serotype from DENV-2 to DENV-1 (Table 1).
The proportion of dengue cases hospitalized during these three epidemic periods was 93.2% in 2004–2005, 58.1% in 2007 and 28.9% in 2013–2014.
The total number of hospital bed-years due to dengue halved from 136 in 2005 to 69 in 2013 (Fig 4).
340.Rationale: The passage links post-epidemic revisions of admission criteria with a marked fall in the proportion of cases hospitalized. It also shows that hospital bed-years due to dengue were halved between 2005 and 2013, and that hospitalization proportions were much lower in the 2013–2014 epidemic compared with 2004–2005. These data support the inference that more selective admission practices reduced hospital bed utilization, including during later large epidemics.

341.Option: The decline in anti-filarial IgG4 antibody prevalence after MDA occurred more slowly than the decline in microfilaria prevalence, indicating that antibody levels persist longer after infection clearance and may reflect past exposure rather than current transmission.
Evidence: MF rates fell from a baseline of 26% to less than 1% after round 4, and this decrease was maintained for the duration of the study including the period following cessation of MDA.
The antibody rate decreased from 80% at baseline to a rate of 6.4% in 2010.
The antibody to MF ratio was 3.1 at baseline, but increased dramatically after MDA. This means MF rates fell more rapidly after MDA than antibody rates.
Anti-filarial antibodies are a marker for past, present or exposure to filarial infections.
341.Rationale: The passage provides direct longitudinal data showing microfilaria (MF) prevalence dropped rapidly to <1% after several MDA rounds while antibody prevalence declined more slowly (from 80% to 6.4% by 2010). The authors explicitly state MF rates fell more rapidly than antibody rates and also state that anti-filarial antibodies indicate past, present or exposure. From these facts it follows that antibody levels can persist after clearance of active infection and therefore may reflect past exposure rather than ongoing transmission; this is exactly the inference in the option.

341.Option: The increase in the antibody-to-MF ratio after MDA reflects a higher sensitivity of the Brugia Rapid test in detecting past infections compared to the membrane filtration method used for microfilariae.
Evidence: Other studies have shown that BmR1 rapid antibody test [5], [6] is a sensitive marker for detecting brugian filariasis in populations.
We used the Brugia Rapid dipstick test as a marker for ongoing or prior infection, because there is no adult worm antigen detection test for Brugia spp.
The antibody to MF ratio was 3.1 at baseline, but increased dramatically after MDA. This means MF rates fell more rapidly after MDA than antibody rates.
For this reason, it is not possible to use antibody rates to estimate MF prevalence rates following MDA.
341.Rationale: The passage describes the Brugia Rapid antibody test as a 'sensitive marker' and explicitly states it is a marker for 'ongoing or prior infection'. The observed increase in antibody-to-MF ratio after MDA is explained in the text by the fact that MF (detected by membrane filtration) declines faster than antibody prevalence. Therefore the antibody test is relatively more likely to remain positive after infection clearance (i.e., detect past exposure) than the MF membrane filtration method detects active microfilaremia. The option's claim about higher sensitivity for past infections compared to membrane filtration is supported by these statements.

341.Option: The persistence of anti-filarial antibodies in children after MDA cessation indicates that transmission of B. timori was not fully interrupted in the study population.
Evidence: While the positive test rate for Brugia Rapid in children aged between 3 and 10 years dropped rapidly following MDA, the prevalence and the upper 95% confidence limit dropped below 5% and 10%, respectively, for the first time in 2009, 22 months after the last round of MDA.
Anti-filarial antibodies are a marker for past, present or exposure to filarial infections.
MF rates fell from a baseline of 26% to less than 1% after round 4, and this decrease was maintained for the duration of the study including the period following cessation of MDA.
341.Rationale: The passage notes that antibody rates in children dropped and even reached thresholds used for program decisions, and it explicitly states that anti-filarial antibodies indicate past as well as present infection. Persistence of antibodies therefore can reflect prior exposure rather than ongoing transmission. The passage does not present direct evidence that persistent antibodies in children after MDA imply continued transmission; in fact MF prevalence remained very low. Thus the claim that persistence of antibodies in children indicates transmission was not interrupted cannot be derived from the provided data.

341.Option: The rebound in STH infection rates after MDA cessation suggests that anti-filarial antibodies may also reappear in the population if transmission resumes.
Evidence: The lowest prevalence of 18% was detected in 2006 after 4 rounds of MDA. In 2010, 34 months after the last round of MDA, the prevalence (45%) was significantly higher than the baseline prevalence (34%) prior to MDA (p<0.01).
Hookworm rates rebounded to 7% at 34 months after the last MDA.
Anti-filarial antibodies are a marker for past, present or exposure to filarial infections.
The antibody to MF ratio was 3.1 at baseline, but increased dramatically after MDA. This means MF rates fell more rapidly after MDA than antibody rates.
341.Rationale: While the passage documents rebound in soil-transmitted helminth (STH) prevalence after stopping MDA, it contains no data linking STH trends to filarial antibody dynamics or demonstrating that antibody prevalence increased in response to resumed filarial transmission. The statement that anti-filarial antibodies 'may also reappear' if transmission resumes is a plausible hypothesis, but the passage does not provide empirical evidence of antibody reappearance following a documented resurgence of filarial transmission. Therefore this inference cannot be directly derived from the presented data.

341.Option: The rapid decline in microfilaria prevalence following MDA suggests that the Brugia Rapid test is a more sensitive marker for active infection than for past exposure.
Evidence: MF rates fell from a baseline of 26% to less than 1% after round 4, and this decrease was maintained for the duration of the study including the period following cessation of MDA.
We used the Brugia Rapid dipstick test as a marker for ongoing or prior infection, because there is no adult worm antigen detection test for Brugia spp.
The antibody to MF ratio was 3.1 at baseline, but increased dramatically after MDA. This means MF rates fell more rapidly after MDA than antibody rates.
For this reason, it is not possible to use antibody rates to estimate MF prevalence rates following MDA.
341.Rationale: The passage shows the opposite: microfilaria prevalence declined more rapidly than antibody prevalence, leading to an increased antibody-to-MF ratio. The authors describe the Brugia Rapid test as a marker for 'ongoing or prior infection' (i.e., it detects past exposure) and explicitly state antibody rates cannot be used to estimate MF prevalence after MDA. Thus the claim that the Brugia Rapid test is a more sensitive marker for active infection than for past exposure contradicts the provided evidence and is not supported.

342.Option: The high level of amino acid variation (36.4%) in mitochondrial protein-coding genes between human-Trichuris and pig-Trichuris is consistent with recent cross-infection events between the two host species.
Evidence: For individual genes, the nucleotide and amino acid sequence differences between human-Trichuris and pig-Trichuris vary from 25.4 to 37.4% and 13.6 to 62.5%, respectively (Table 5).
Sequence difference across the entire mt genome was 32.87%.
The phylogenetic analyses of amino acid sequence datasets using B. malayi as the outgroup reflected the clear genetic distinctiveness between human-Trichuris and pig-Trichuris...
342.Rationale: The passage reports substantial amino acid and nucleotide divergence (e.g., ~32.9% across the mt genome and per-gene amino acid differences up to 62.5%) and states that phylogenetic analyses show "clear genetic distinctiveness" between human- and pig-derived Trichuris. High genetic divergence and clear phylogenetic separation are inconsistent with the inference of recent cross-infection (which would predict low divergence and intermixing). Therefore the option's conclusion (that the high variation is consistent with recent cross-infection) cannot be derived from the presented data.

342.Option: The genetic similarity in the rrnL gene between human-Trichuris and pig-Trichuris suggests that they are closely related and likely represent a single, host-generalist species.
Evidence: Comparison of the nucleotide sequences of each mt gene and NCR, as well as the amino acid sequences, conceptually translated from all protein genes of the two Trichuris, is given in Tables 4 and 5.
The magnitude of sequence variation in each gene and NCR between human-Trichuris and pig-Trichuris ranged from 24.6–47.4%. Sequence difference across the entire mt genome was 32.87%.
The alignment of the partial rrnL sequences revealed that all individuals of human-Trichuris differed at 89 nucleotide positions when compared with pig-Trichuris.
Phylogenetic analyses of the rrnL sequence data revealed strong support for the separation of human-Trichuris from pig-Trichuris individuals into two distinct clades...
342.Rationale: Although the rrnL and rrnS were noted as the "two most conserved genes," the passage also reports substantial sequence differences (rrnL differences ~25.1% and overall mt divergence ~32.87%), 89 nucleotide differences in partial rrnL alignments, and phylogenetic analyses that strongly separate human- and pig-derived Trichuris into distinct clades. These findings support distinction rather than a single host-generalist species; thus the option's claim is not supported by the data.

342.Option: The substantial genetic divergence observed in mitochondrial protein-coding genes and ribosomal RNA genes between human-Trichuris and pig-Trichuris supports the hypothesis that they represent distinct species.
Evidence: For individual genes, the nucleotide and amino acid sequence differences between human-Trichuris and pig-Trichuris vary from 25.4 to 37.4% and 13.6 to 62.5%, respectively (Table 5).
Sequence difference across the entire mt genome was 32.87%.
There were 1299 amino acid substitutions (for an alignment length of 3559 and 3562 positions) in the 13 proteins...
The phylogenetic analyses of amino acid sequence datasets ... reflected the clear genetic distinctiveness between human-Trichuris and pig-Trichuris ...
The alignment of the partial rrnL sequences revealed that all individuals of human-Trichuris differed at 89 nucleotide positions when compared with pig-Trichuris.
Phylogenetic analyses of the rrnL sequence data revealed strong support for the separation of human-Trichuris from pig-Trichuris individuals into two distinct clades...
342.Rationale: The passage documents substantial sequence divergence across mitochondrial protein-coding genes and rRNA (including ~32.9% overall mt divergence, many amino acid substitutions, and 89 nucleotide differences in rrnL), and reports that phylogenetic analyses show clear genetic distinctiveness and strong clade separation between human- and pig-derived Trichuris. These molecular results support the inference that the two are distinct species.

342.Option: The high A+T content and AT-rich non-coding regions in the mitochondrial genomes of human-Trichuris and pig-Trichuris indicate that these parasites have evolved a shared mechanism for mitochondrial DNA replication.
Evidence: The nucleotide composition of the entire mt genome is biased toward A and T, with T being the most favoured nucleotide and G being least favoured...
Two AT-rich non-coding regions (NCRs) were inferred in the mt genomes of both human-Trichuris and pig-Trichuris.
The high A+T content and the predicted structure of the AT-rich NCRs suggest an involvement in the initiation of replication [41].
342.Rationale: The passage reports shared features (high A+T content and AT-rich NCRs) and states these features "suggest an involvement in the initiation of replication." However, it does not provide direct evidence of the specific replication mechanism nor demonstrate that the mechanism is shared and evolved as such. The leap to a definitive statement that they "have evolved a shared mechanism for mitochondrial DNA replication" goes beyond the presented evidence, which only suggests a possible role of the AT-rich regions in replication initiation.

342.Option: The use of mitochondrial DNA markers in this study provides definitive proof that Trichuris from humans and pigs are reproductively isolated and cannot interbreed.
Evidence: For individual genes, the nucleotide and amino acid sequence differences between human-Trichuris and pig-Trichuris vary from 25.4 to 37.4% and 13.6 to 62.5%, respectively (Table 5).
Sequence difference across the entire mt genome was 32.87%.
The phylogenetic analyses of amino acid sequence datasets ... reflected the clear genetic distinctiveness between human-Trichuris and pig-Trichuris ...
Phylogenetic analyses of the rrnL sequence data revealed strong support for the separation of human-Trichuris from pig-Trichuris individuals into two distinct clades...
342.Rationale: Mitochondrial DNA markers and phylogenetic separation provide strong molecular evidence of genetic distinctiveness between human- and pig-derived Trichuris, but they do not constitute definitive proof of reproductive isolation or inability to interbreed. The passage presents sequence divergence and phylogenetic clades but contains no direct data on reproductive compatibility, hybridization studies, or gene flow. Therefore the strong claim of definitive proof of reproductive isolation cannot be drawn solely from the presented mitochondrial data.

343.Option: The cumulative negative conversion rate of blood-cultured fungi is significantly influenced by the duration of antifungal therapy, with longer treatment leading to better clearance regardless of fungal susceptibility.
Evidence: Strains from the 190 patients were split into three groups according to the MIC values for voriconazole in vitro: lower (MIC ≤ 0.008), medium (0.008 < VOA ≤ 0.015), and higher (VOA > 0.015). Cumulative negative conversion rate of blood cultured fungi was assessed among three groups after 2, 4, 6 weeks and more than 6 weeks of antifungal treatment. P<0.05 is considered statistically significant.

The cumulative negative conversion rate of blood cultured fungi among three groups after 2, 4, 6 weeks and more than 6 weeks of antifungal treatment was significantly different (Shown in Fig 2), indicating that the MIC values of voriconazole significantly affects the clearance of T. marneffei in blood.
343.Rationale: The passage reports that cumulative negative conversion rates over time were compared across groups defined by voriconazole MIC and that these rates were significantly different, explicitly indicating that fungal susceptibility (voriconazole MIC) affects clearance. The option asserts that duration alone determines clearance 'regardless of fungal susceptibility,' which contradicts the reported finding that MIC influences clearance. The passage does not provide evidence that longer treatment leads to better clearance irrespective of susceptibility.

343.Option: The susceptibility of T. marneffei to voriconazole in vitro is an independent factor influencing the clearance of the fungus from blood culture after antifungal therapy in AIDS patients with talaromycosis.
Evidence: Significant differences were found in the overall distribution of MIC value between the two groups both for VOR (Z = 1.762, P < 0.001) and FLU (Z = 1.211, P = 0.006). The MICs of VOR and FLU in the Pos-group were significantly higher than those in the Neg-group (the proportion of isolates with MICs above 0.008 for VOR: 69/101 (68.3%) vs. 38/89 (42.7%), respectively, χ2 = 12.623, P < 0.001; ...)

Multivariate analysis using the logistic regression model involving the 2 significant factors (AST and CD4+T levels)determined by univariate analysis and the MIC values for VOR and FLU. ... The MIC value for VOR was identified as the prognostic variable derived from binary logistic regression analysis that influence the clearance of T. marneffei in blood culture after antifungal therapy among AIDS patients with talaromycosis (shown in Table 4).

The cumulative negative conversion rate of blood cultured fungi among three groups after 2, 4, 6 weeks and more than 6 weeks of antifungal treatment was significantly different (Shown in Fig 2), indicating that the MIC values of voriconazole significantly affects the clearance of T. marneffei in blood.
343.Rationale: The passage provides direct statistical evidence: MICs for voriconazole differed significantly between patients who cleared blood cultures and those who did not, and binary logistic regression identified voriconazole MIC as the prognostic (independent) variable influencing clearance. Thus the susceptibility of T. marneffei to voriconazole in vitro is supported as an independent factor affecting blood-culture clearance after therapy.

343.Option: The high mortality rate of talaromycosis in some regions is primarily due to delayed diagnosis and limited access to antifungal drugs, rather than poor antifungal efficacy.
Evidence: However, the mortality of talaromycosis remains as high as 30% in some regions, representing an important public health problem threatening patients with AIDS [8,9].
The underlying mechanism of poor therapeutic effects in patients with AIDS complicated with talaromycosis is still unclear.
343.Rationale: While the passage states that mortality can be high and that mechanisms of poor therapeutic effects are unclear, it does not provide evidence attributing high mortality primarily to delayed diagnosis or limited access to drugs. The study focuses on factors affecting clearance and in vitro susceptibilities, but does not present data on diagnostic delays, drug availability, or that these are the primary drivers of mortality. Therefore the causal statement in the option is not supported by the passage.

343.Option: The elevated AST levels observed in the delayed clearance group are a direct result of voriconazole-induced hepatotoxicity, rather than a marker of systemic disease severity.
Evidence: While the Pos-group had a higher baseline Aspartate aminotransferase (AST, 78.5 vs. 105 U/L; P = 0.073) and lower CD4+ T cells level (11 vs. 7 cells/μl; P = 0.061) (shown in Table 1).

Multivariate analysis using the logistic regression model involving the 2 significant factors (AST and CD4+T levels)determined by univariate analysis and the MIC values for VOR and FLU. The variable assignment was shown in Table 3. The MIC value for VOR was identified as the prognostic variable ... that influence the clearance ...
343.Rationale: The passage notes higher AST in the delayed-clearance group but does not state the cause of AST elevation. Voriconazole hepatotoxicity is not asserted or evidenced, and multivariate analysis ultimately identified voriconazole MIC (not AST) as the independent prognostic factor. Therefore there is no basis in the passage to conclude that elevated AST is directly due to voriconazole-induced hepatotoxicity rather than reflecting disease severity or other factors.

343.Option: The use of amphotericin B followed by itraconazole as first-line therapy is ineffective in patients with talaromycosis due to widespread resistance to these drugs.
Evidence: At present, amphotericin B (AMB) followed by itraconazole (ITRA) is the main treatment strategy for talaromycosis [5], and voriconazole (VOR) is regarded as an alternative in patients with AMB intolerance [6,7].
No differences were found in the distribution of MIC for POS, ITR, AMB, and echinocandins between the two groups.
The baseline MICs from low-to-high were as follows: POS and VOR, ≤.008−0.06 µg/mL; ITRA, ≤.015−0.03 µg/mL; AMB, ≤.25−1 µg/mL; ANI, 4−8 µg/mL; CAS, 2−8 µg/mL; MICA, >8 µg/mL; and FLU, 1−16 µg/mL.
343.Rationale: The passage describes AMB followed by itraconazole as the main treatment and presents MIC data showing low MIC ranges for ITRA and AMB, and reports no difference in MIC distributions for ITR and AMB between groups. There is no evidence of 'widespread resistance' or treatment ineffectiveness to support this strong claim. Thus the option's causal assertion is not supported by the study data.

344.Option: The Clone 13 vaccine is safe for use in pregnant ewes at any stage of gestation, as demonstrated by the absence of acute clinical signs in vaccinated animals.
Evidence: None of the ewes developed fever or clinical signs throughout the course of the study.
Remarkably, inoculation during gestation resulted in vertical transmission of the virus to the fetus, which was associated with malformed lambs, stillbirths and precolostral antibodies.

The proportion of stillborn lambs was 30% for the group inoculated at approximately 50 days after mating (including the foetus that was about to be aborted) and 11% for the group inoculated at approximately 120 days after mating.

As many as 7 lambs of 5 different ewes inoculated with Clone 13 at 50 days after mating showed malformations of the central nervous system ... No malformations were observed in lambs of ewes inoculated with Clone 13 at 120 days after mating.
344.Rationale: While the passage states that vaccinated ewes did not develop fever or observable clinical signs, it also clearly documents harmful fetal outcomes (vertical transmission, malformations, stillbirths) that vary by gestational age. The absence of acute maternal clinical signs does not equate to safety for the fetus at all gestational stages. Therefore the blanket statement that the vaccine is safe for pregnant ewes at any stage of gestation is not supported by the passage.

344.Option: The discrepancy in outcomes between this study and previous field studies is likely due to differences in sheep breed, not the vaccine dose.
Evidence: Sheep were Texel/Swifter crossbreds and purchased in The Netherlands.

In line with the regulations from the OIE and EP, all studies were performed with an overdose. This overdose was set at 107 TCID50 as we anticipated that the maximum virus titre likely to be contained in 1 dose of the vaccine is 106 TCID50.
These results indicate that Clone 13 is highly safe in lambs, even when a high dose is administered via four different inoculation routes.
344.Rationale: The passage notes the breed used and that an overdose was administered, but it contains no data or analyses comparing outcomes across different breeds or contrasting breed versus dose effects from field studies. There is no evidence presented that attributes discrepancies to breed rather than dose. Hence this causal attribution is not derivable from the study text.

344.Option: Caution should be taken when the vaccine is used in pregnant animals, especially during the first trimester of pregnancy, as Clone 13 may spread to the fetus and cause malformations and stillbirths.
Evidence: Remarkably, inoculation during gestation resulted in vertical transmission of the virus to the fetus, which was associated with malformed lambs, stillbirths and precolostral antibodies.
Untoward events were most prominent in ewes inoculated during the first trimester of gestation.
The proportion of stillborn lambs was 30% for the group inoculated at approximately 50 days after mating ... and 11% for the group inoculated at approximately 120 days after mating.
As many as 7 lambs of 5 different ewes inoculated with Clone 13 at 50 days after mating showed malformations of the central nervous system ...
344.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports vertical transmission with associated malformations and increased stillbirths, and states that untoward events were most prominent when ewes were inoculated in early gestation (first trimester). Therefore the epidemiological inference recommending caution—particularly during early pregnancy—is directly supported by the experimental outcomes.

344.Option: The teratogenic effects observed in the Clone 13 study are likely due to the NSs protein, which is known to mediate chromosome cohesion and segregation.
Evidence: The Clone 13 virus, isolated from a non-fatal human case in the Central African Republic, was shown to lack 69% of the gene encoding the nonstructural protein of the S genome segment (NSs).

Extensive studies have demonstrated that the NSs protein counteracts host innate immune responses at different levels, e.g. by suppression of type-I interferon responses, and is thereby considered the major virulence factor.
Remarkably, inoculation during gestation resulted in vertical transmission of the virus to the fetus, which was associated with malformed lambs, stillbirths and precolostral antibodies.
344.Rationale: The passage states that Clone 13 lacks most of the NSs gene and that NSs functions to counteract innate immune responses; it does not describe NSs as mediating chromosome cohesion or segregation. Given that Clone 13 (which lacks NSs) was associated with fetal malformations, attributing teratogenicity to NSs is inconsistent with the data and is not supported by any text in the passage.

344.Option: The Clone 13 vaccine does not cross the placental barrier, as no viral RNA was detected in the fetuses of ewes vaccinated at 120 days of gestation.
Evidence: Analysis of the lamb organs and plasma samples revealed that ten lambs were positive for Clone 13 RNA in the blood or one or more organ samples, six in the 50 days group and four in the 120 days group.
several precolostrum serum samples, one in the 50 days group and six in the 120 days group, tested positive for RVFV-specific antibodies.
344.Rationale: The passage reports that viral RNA was detected in lambs from both gestational-age groups, explicitly noting four positives in the 120 days group, and also reports precolostral antibodies in multiple lambs from the 120 days group. These findings indicate in utero exposure/vertical transmission at 120 days in some instances, so the statement that no viral RNA was detected in fetuses of ewes vaccinated at 120 days is contradicted by the study data.

345.Option: The high diagnostic performance of chimeric recombinant antigens in this study suggests that they can fully replace all existing confirmatory tests for chronic Chagas disease, including those based on native parasite antigens.
Evidence: The phase I study (proof-of-concept) was carried out employing a serological panel composed of samples from T. cruzi-positive and T. cruzi-negative individuals from both endemic and non-endemic settings.
Based on AUC values, all IBMP chimeric antigens were classified as having outstanding diagnostic potential (Fig 2).

IBMP-8.1, IBMP-8.2 and IBMP-8.4 produced a sensitivity of 100%, while IBMP-8.3 offered 95%. All IBMP chimeric antigens attained the maximum specificity value of 100%, and maximum accuracy was achieved when samples were assayed using either IBMP-8.1, IBMP-8.2 or IBMP-8.4.

Until 2015 and 2017, respectively, HBK 740 Immunoblot Linhas anti-T. cruzi and TESA-blot were routinely used for confirmatory testing [32]; however, commercial productions have since been discontinued, leaving a gap in the performance of confirmatory chronic CD diagnosis.
345.Rationale: Although the passage reports outstanding diagnostic metrics for IBMP chimeric antigens in this phase I proof-of-concept study (high sensitivity, specificity, and AUC), it is a limited early-stage evaluation. The study design (phase I, limited sample panel) does not provide sufficient evidence to conclude that these antigens can fully replace all existing confirmatory tests based on native parasite antigens. The passage itself frames the work as a proof-of-concept rather than definitive replacement of established tests, so the strong claim of full replacement is not supported by the provided text.

345.Option: The discontinuation of TESAcruzi production has created a critical gap in confirmatory testing for chronic Chagas disease, necessitating the development of alternative diagnostic platforms.
Evidence: Until 2015 and 2017, respectively, HBK 740 Immunoblot Linhas anti-T. cruzi and TESA-blot were routinely used for confirmatory testing [32]; however, commercial productions have since been discontinued, leaving a gap in the performance of confirmatory chronic CD diagnosis.

The high diagnostic performance offered by IBMP chimeric antigens and the need for confirmatory serological methods led us to perform a proof-of-concept study (phase I) to evaluate the capability of these antigens to detect anti-T. cruzi IgG using a Western blot platform in serum samples obtained from distinct clinical presentations, representative of diverse Brazilian geographical settings.
345.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that production of previously used confirmatory assays (including TESA-blot) has been discontinued and that this discontinuation has left a gap in confirmatory chronic Chagas diagnosis. The authors directly link this gap to the motivation for developing/evaluating alternative platforms (IBMP chimeric antigens in a Western blot). Therefore, the inference that discontinuation created a critical gap and necessitates alternative diagnostics follows from the text.

345.Option: The use of chimeric recombinant antigens in a Western blot platform offers a promising alternative to existing confirmatory tests for chronic Chagas disease, with high diagnostic performance and reduced risk of cross-reactivity.
Evidence: The high diagnostic performance offered by IBMP chimeric antigens and the need for confirmatory serological methods led us to perform a proof-of-concept study (phase I) to evaluate the capability of these antigens to detect anti-T. cruzi IgG using a Western blot platform in serum samples obtained from distinct clinical presentations, representative of diverse Brazilian geographical settings.
Based on AUC values, all IBMP chimeric antigens were classified as having outstanding diagnostic potential (Fig 2).

IBMP-8.1, IBMP-8.2 and IBMP-8.4 produced a sensitivity of 100%, while IBMP-8.3 offered 95%. All IBMP chimeric antigens attained the maximum specificity value of 100%, and maximum accuracy was achieved when samples were assayed using either IBMP-8.1, IBMP-8.2 or IBMP-8.4.
No cross-reactions were observed when assaying leishmaniasis-positive serum samples using all four chimeric proteins.
345.Rationale: The passage reports that IBMP chimeric antigens were evaluated in a Western blot (WB) format and demonstrated outstanding diagnostic metrics (high AUC, sensitivities near or at 100%, and 100% specificity for all antigens tested). Additionally, a small cross-reactivity panel with visceral leishmaniasis samples showed no cross-reactions. These findings support the inference that chimeric recombinant antigens in a WB platform are a promising alternative for confirmatory chronic Chagas testing, offering high diagnostic performance and an apparent low risk of cross-reactivity in this evaluation. The wording 'promising alternative' is consistent with the phase I proof-of-concept context presented in the passage.

345.Option: The absence of cross-reactivity with visceral leishmaniasis in this study implies that chimeric recombinant antigens are universally safe for use in all co-endemic regions, regardless of local parasite diversity.
Evidence: No cross-reactions were observed when assaying leishmaniasis-positive serum samples using all four chimeric proteins.
A panel composed of three serum samples was used to evaluate cross-reactivity with antibodies produced against visceral leishmaniasis.
extensive T. cruzi genetic and phenotypic intraspecific diversity
345.Rationale: While the passage reports no observed cross-reactions in a small panel of three visceral leishmaniasis sera, this is a very limited assessment and does not justify a universal claim of safety for all co-endemic regions. The authors also note extensive T. cruzi genetic and phenotypic diversity, which could affect assay performance across regions. Therefore, generalizing 'universally safe ... regardless of local parasite diversity' is not supported by the presented evidence.

345.Option: The Abbott ESA Chagas immunoblot, which uses chimeric recombinant antigens, has been shown to be superior to the TESAcruzi test in both sensitivity and specificity, making it the current gold standard for confirmatory testing.
Evidence: The Chagas blot and Abbott ESA Chagas, whose use is limited to Europe and the USA, respectively.

Until 2015 and 2017, respectively, HBK 740 Immunoblot Linhas anti-T. cruzi and TESA-blot were routinely used for confirmatory testing [32]; however, commercial productions have since been discontinued, leaving a gap in the performance of confirmatory chronic CD diagnosis.

RIPA, a highly complex technique, is labor-intensive, expensive and involves radioactivity, which limits its use [26,27]. INNO-LIA Chagas (Innogenetics N.V., Ghent, Belgium) is a commercially available immunoblot assay consisting of seven recombinant and synthetic antigens: CRA, FRA, Tc-24, SAPA, MAP, TcD and Ag39, which are coated at separate locations onto a single nylon membrane strip. While offering high diagnostic performance, results can be difficult to interpret, and this platform remains expensive [28–31].
345.Rationale: The passage mentions the existence and geographic availability of Abbott ESA Chagas but provides no data comparing its sensitivity or specificity to TESA-blot, nor any statement declaring it superior or the 'current gold standard.' TESA-blot production was discontinued, and other confirmatory assays have limitations, but no comparative performance evidence is presented in the text to support the strong claim in the option. Therefore, this conclusion is not supported by the passage.

346.Option: The lower case fatality in patients with gastrointestinal involvement is primarily due to the higher prevalence of hepatomegaly and splenomegaly, which are markers of immune activation and better host response.
Evidence: Table 1 shows there were significantly fewer deaths at one month in patients with a digestive presentation and positive identification of H. capsulatum than in patients without a gastrointestinal presentation.
A significant proportion of patients also had hepatomegaly and, to a lesser degree, splenomegaly.

Patients with abdominal pain were less likely to have hepato or splenomegaly (OR = 0.3, 95%CI = 0.2–0.6, P<0.001) and those with diarrhea were also less likely to have hepato or splenomegaly (OR = 0.3, 95%CI = 0.2–0.6, P<0.001).
346.Rationale: The passage documents lower one-month deaths among those with a digestive presentation and mentions hepatomegaly/splenomegaly occur in a significant proportion. However, it does not state that hepatomegaly or splenomegaly are more prevalent specifically among the subgroup with lower fatality, nor does it present any evidence that these findings are markers of immune activation or cause better outcomes. In fact, patients with common gastrointestinal symptoms (abdominal pain or diarrhea) were less likely to have hepato- or splenomegaly. Therefore the causal claim that lower case fatality is primarily due to higher prevalence of hepatomegaly/splenomegaly is not supported by the passage.

346.Option: Patients with gastrointestinal involvement and positive identification of H. capsulatum were more likely to have a shorter hospital stay because they presented with milder systemic disease and fewer comorbidities.
Evidence: Table 1 shows there were significantly fewer deaths at one month in patients with a digestive presentation and positive identification of H. capsulatum than in patients without a gastrointestinal presentation.
Biologically, those with gastrointestinal H. capsulatum had lower TGO, LDH, Ferritin, prothrombin time than those without digestive signs.
They had a shorter delay between symptoms onset and diagnosis and were more likely to be diagnosed through pathology than those without gastrointestinal manifestations.
346.Rationale: The passage provides evidence of lower one-month mortality, lower certain lab markers, and shorter delay to diagnosis among patients with gastrointestinal identification. It does not provide any data on hospital length of stay or on comorbidities in these groups. The specific causal statement that shorter hospital stay is due to milder systemic disease and fewer comorbidities is not supported by any quoted text. Therefore this option cannot be derived from the passage.

346.Option: A quicker diagnosis through endoscopy may explain why patients with gastrointestinal identification of H. capsulatum had lower severity and case fatality compared to those without gastrointestinal symptoms.
Evidence: Table 1 shows there were significantly fewer deaths at one month in patients with a digestive presentation and positive identification of H. capsulatum than in patients without a gastrointestinal presentation.
They had a shorter delay between symptoms onset and diagnosis and were more likely to be diagnosed through pathology than those without gastrointestinal manifestations.
Among those with diarrhea, 84% had colonoscopy and among those with abdominal pain 68.5% had a colonoscopy.

Biopsies performed during colonoscopy were more contributive than those performed during oesogastroduodenoscopy whether considering direct examination (respectively 56.4% vs 21.7% positive), fungal culture (respectively 74.1% vs 40.9% positive), or pathology (respectively 72.9% vs 28.6% positive).
346.Rationale: The passage shows an association: patients with gastrointestinal identification had lower one-month mortality and a shorter delay between symptom onset and diagnosis, and they were more often diagnosed by pathology (often obtained via endoscopic biopsy). High rates of colonoscopy among symptomatic patients and the high yield of colonoscopic biopsies support the plausible inference that endoscopic diagnosis enabled quicker identification of Histoplasma, which may help explain the observed lower severity and fatality. The option uses cautious language ('may explain'), which matches the associative evidence provided.

346.Option: The increasing prevalence of gastrointestinal manifestations over time is likely due to improved access to abdominal CT-scans and gastrointestinal biopsies, which enhances detection of subclinical cases.
Evidence: There was a linear trend for the odds of having gastrointestinal signs and symptoms which increased significantly with time (P<0.0001).

There was no such temporal trend for gastrointestinal endoscopies but there was a significant trend for an increase in the proportion of patients benefitting from and abdominal CT-scan, a gastrointestinal biopsy, and a decrease in abdominal ultrasonography.
346.Rationale: The passage documents a significant temporal increase in observed gastrointestinal presentations and, concurrently, a significant increase over time in the use of abdominal CT-scans and gastrointestinal biopsies. This temporal concordance supports the epidemiological inference that improved access to and use of cross-sectional imaging and biopsy could account, at least in part, for increased detection of gastrointestinal manifestations (including less overt or previously unrecognized cases). The option's phrasing ('likely due to' and 'enhances detection') aligns with the associative evidence in the passage; although causation is not definitively proven in the text, the trend data make this a reasonable derivation from the passage.

346.Option: The higher rate of positive identification via pathology in patients with gastrointestinal involvement suggests that histopathological examination is more sensitive than fungal culture or direct examination for detecting H. capsulatum in clinical samples.
Evidence: Overall, among those having benefitted from gastrointestinal biopsies 65/117 (55%) had a positive identification of Histoplasma on the sample; 32 (27.3%) by direct examination, 44 (37.6) by culture, and 45 (38.4%) by pathology.
They had a shorter delay between symptoms onset and diagnosis and were more likely to be diagnosed through pathology than those without gastrointestinal manifestations.

Biopsies performed during colonoscopy were more contributive than those performed during oesogastroduodenoscopy whether considering direct examination (respectively 56.4% vs 21.7% positive), fungal culture (respectively 74.1% vs 40.9% positive), or pathology (respectively 72.9% vs 28.6% positive).
346.Rationale: The passage gives the raw detection yields: pathology (38.4%) is numerically slightly higher than culture (37.6%) and higher than direct examination (27.3%) overall, but the differences are small and not presented as statistically significant. For colonoscopic biopsies culture (74.1%) was actually slightly higher than pathology (72.9%), and for oesogastroduodenoscopy pathology was lower than culture. The passage also states patients with GI involvement were more likely to be diagnosed via pathology, which could reflect diagnostic strategy/use rather than inherent superior sensitivity. The text does not provide definitive evidence that histopathology is more sensitive than culture or direct exam; therefore the categorical claim of greater sensitivity is not supported by the data presented.

347.Option: The presence of Anopheles mosquitoes in areas with high human population density is primarily driven by the availability of livestock blood rather than human blood, as human settlements are not a major attractant.
Evidence: An. sinensis is an outdoor resting and biting species, which mostly inhabits rice planting areas, and can be recorded between northeast to Liaoning Province and southwest to Yunnan Province [25].
An. lesteri is the main malaria vector at low altitudes in the 22°N to 33°N range [28,29].
An. minimus is active in southern China below 25°N and mainly inhabits marshes, ponds, and streams in jungle areas [31].
347.Rationale: The passage describes habitat types and geographic ranges for the three Anopheles species (rice planting areas, low-altitude ranges, marshes/streams/jungle areas) but includes no information about host preference (human vs livestock) or the role of livestock blood in driving presence in population centers. Because the claim asserts a specific driver (availability of livestock blood) and downplays human settlements as attractants, and the passage contains no data or statements about blood meal sources or attraction to humans versus livestock, this inference cannot be derived from the passage.

347.Option: The seasonal movement patterns of Anopheles mosquitoes in China are primarily determined by the availability of forested habitats, which serve as the main breeding grounds for all three species.
Evidence: An. sinensis is an outdoor resting and biting species, which mostly inhabits rice planting areas, and can be recorded between northeast to Liaoning Province and southwest to Yunnan Province [25].
An. minimus is active in southern China below 25°N and mainly inhabits marshes, ponds, and streams in jungle areas [31].
Monthly precipitation, construction, cropland, elevation, forest, and monthly mean temperature are considered to be important variables in An. lesteri model.
For the An. minimus model, Monthly precipitation, monthly mean temperature, and elevation are thought to have significant effects on the survival of An. minimus.
The important variables in the An. sinensis model are Monthly precipitation, construction, monthly mean temperature, and elevation.
347.Rationale: While forest habitat is noted as relevant for some vectors (and An. minimus is associated with 'jungle areas'), the passage shows that different species have different key environmental drivers (e.g., An. sinensis linked to cropland/rice areas, An. lesteri has forest among several variables, and An. minimus is influenced primarily by precipitation, temperature, and elevation). The claim that seasonal movement patterns are 'primarily determined by the availability of forested habitats' for all three species is not supported by the passage, which indicates heterogeneous habitat associations and multiple important climatic and land-cover variables.

347.Option: The ecological niche model predictions suggest that climate change has already caused a permanent northward shift in the distribution of An. sinensis, extending its range beyond historical limits.
Evidence: Studies have indicated that the habitat suitability areas of An. sinensis tend to expand gradually with climate change [26,27].
In March An. sinensis began to move northwards and by June it covered the areas between the Yangtze and Yellow Rivers, Yunnan Province, and Hainan Province.
The habitat suitability areas for An. sinensis began to shrink in October and declined even more in November when the predicted graphs were similar to those for March.
347.Rationale: The passage reports that studies indicate a tendency for An. sinensis habitat suitability to expand with climate change and describes seasonal northward movement during warmer months with contraction in cooler months. However, it does not present evidence that a permanent northward shift beyond historical limits has already occurred. The model outputs described reflect seasonal expansion and contraction, and the cited statement about gradual expansion with climate change is a general note of tendency, not direct evidence of an established, permanent range shift. Therefore the specific claim of an already established permanent northward shift cannot be derived from the passage.

347.Option: The seasonal variation in habitat suitability for Anopheles mosquitoes in China follows a pattern of gradual expansion from south to north during warmer months, followed by contraction from north to south in cooler months, with peak suitability occurring in summer for An. lesteri and An. minimus and in autumn for An. sinensis.
Evidence: The distribution range of An. sinensis in winter is very small, and it was only observed in Taiwan Province (December to February), Guangxi Province (February), and Guangdong Province (February). In March An. sinensis began to move northwards and by June it covered the areas between the Yangtze and Yellow Rivers, Yunnan Province, and Hainan Province. Over the following three months, the habitat suitability areas for An. sinensis gradually expanded, peaking in September with widespread distribution over much of the areas south of the Yellow River. The habitat suitability areas for An. sinensis began to shrink in October and declined even more in November when the predicted graphs were similar to those for March.

During the first month of winter (December), An. lesteri was only visible in Taiwan and Guangxi Provinces. As a result of changes in temperature and precipitation, the suitable areas for An. lesteri gradually expanded northwards from January to June, reached a yearly maximum in the summer (June to August), and covered more than 21 provinces between 18° and 43°N. After autumn (September to November), An. lesteri began to migrate southwards and the habitat suitability areas became progressively smaller.

The expansion of the distribution of An. minimus began in March when it could only be observed in parts of Guangxi Province, Guangdong Province, Jiangxi Province, Fujian Province, and Taiwan Province. After summer, the trend of increasing habitat suitability for An. minimus flattens out... By November, only the eastern part of Taiwan Province and the southern part of Hainan Province were suitable for the survival of An. minimus.
347.Rationale: The passage explicitly describes a seasonal pattern for each species characterized by limited winter range, northward expansion beginning in spring and peaking in warmer months, followed by shrinkage in autumn/winter. It states that An. lesteri reaches a yearly maximum in summer (June–August) and that An. sinensis peaks in September (autumn). An. minimus shows expansion beginning in March and a flattening after summer with contraction by November. These statements together support the inferred overarching ecological trend of seasonal south-to-north expansion in warm months and north-to-south contraction in cool months, with the specified timing of peak suitability for the three species.
