348.Option: The mild perivascular lymphocytic infiltrates observed in the rVSV-ZEBOV-GP and rVSV-MARV-GP groups indicate a low-level neuroinflammatory response that may compromise long-term neurological function in vaccinated individuals.
Evidence: The seven animals that received rVSV-ZEBOV-GP had a variety of mild histologic changes. One animal (363-09) lacked parenchymal lesions in the brain and spinal cord (Table 4), while 6 of the 7 animals (352-09, 354-09, 357-09, 358-09, 360-09, 361-09) had rare, focal areas of meningeal inflammation in various cerebral sections (Fig. 4, Table 4).

The macaques that received the rVSV-MARV-GP IT inoculation had varied histology. Two animals (57-09, 61-09) did not have any histologic lesions present in the brain and spinal cord sections examined (Table 4). Two different animals (52-09, 59-09) had mild lesions in the cortex consisting of small perivascular cuffs of lymphocytes and rare macrophages (Table 4).
No neurological deficits were observed in any of the macaques in the vehicle control, rVSV-ZEBOV-GP, or rVSV-MARV-GP groups.
From these data the rVSV filovirus GP vaccines appear to have no substantial NV in cynomolgus macaques.
348.Rationale: The passage documents mild, focal perivascular/meningeal inflammation in some animals given rVSV-ZEBOV-GP or rVSV-MARV-GP but also states that no neurological deficits were observed and that the vectors appear to have no substantial neurovirulence in macaques. There is no data on long-term follow-up or functional neurological outcomes in vaccinated individuals to support the claim that these mild histologic changes 'may compromise long-term neurological function.' That implication goes beyond the evidence presented and cannot be derived from the passage.

348.Option: The rVSV-ZEBOV-GP and rVSV-MARV-GP vectors lack the neurovirulence properties of the rVSV-wt vector, as evidenced by the absence of clinical neurological signs, significant histopathological lesions, and detectable viral replication in neural tissues in cynomolgus macaques following intracerebral inoculation.
Evidence: Clinically, two macaques from the rVSV-wt control group (67-01 and 56-09) developed progressive neurological signs including ataxia, proprioceptive deficits, and tremors, and were euthanized at either day 5 or 6 post inoculation (Table 1).

The two rVSV-wt macaques that showed clinical signs of neurologic disease (Table 1) also had rVSV and rVSV genome in the frontal cortex sections with one animal (56-09) having both rVSV and rVSV genome in the left and right hemisphere frontal cortex sections (Table 2).
No neurological deficits were observed in any of the macaques in the vehicle control, rVSV-ZEBOV-GP, or rVSV-MARV-GP groups.
None of the other macaques had detectable rVSV RNA in neural tissues, spleen, liver, heart, kidney, axillary LN, or adrenal gland (data not shown for non-neural negative tissues).
From these data the rVSV filovirus GP vaccines appear to have no substantial NV in cynomolgus macaques.
348.Rationale: The passage directly contrasts the outcomes between rVSV-wt and the filovirus GP-recombinants: rVSV-wt produced clinical neurologic disease, histopathologic lesions, and recoverable virus/genome in brain tissue, whereas animals given rVSV-ZEBOV-GP or rVSV-MARV-GP showed no clinical neurologic signs, only mild or absent histologic changes, and generally no detectable rVSV RNA or recoverable virus in neural tissues. The authors explicitly conclude the filovirus GP vectors 'appear to have no substantial NV' in this macaque model, supporting this inference.

348.Option: The rVSV-ZEBOV-GP and rVSV-MARV-GP vectors induce stronger immune responses than rVSV-wt in cynomolgus macaques, as evidenced by the absence of clinical signs and viral replication in neural tissues.
Evidence: No neurological deficits were observed in any of the macaques in the vehicle control, rVSV-ZEBOV-GP, or rVSV-MARV-GP groups.
None of the other macaques had detectable rVSV RNA in neural tissues, spleen, liver, heart, kidney, axillary LN, or adrenal gland (data not shown for non-neural negative tissues).

Initial studies showed that a single intramuscular (i.m.) vaccination of cynomolgus macaques with a rVSV vector expressing the ZEBOV GP or MARV GP induced strong humoral and/or cellular immune responses and elicited complete protection against a high dose (1000 plaque forming unit [PFU]) i.m. challenge of homologous ZEBOV or MARV given 28 days later [8].
348.Rationale: The NV study reports clinical and virologic safety outcomes after intracerebral inoculation but does not present data comparing immune responses elicited by rVSV-wt versus rVSV-ZEBOV-GP or rVSV-MARV-GP in this experiment. Absence of neurologic signs or viral RNA in neural tissue cannot be taken as direct evidence of 'stronger immune responses'—these are different endpoints. While prior vaccination studies (different routes and endpoints) showed immunogenicity of rVSV-GP vaccines, the current passage provides no data to substantiate the specific comparative immunogenicity claim, so the inference is not supported by the presented evidence.

348.Option: The detection of low levels of rVSV RNA in nasal and oral swabs from some rVSV-ZEBOV-GP animals suggests that the vaccine may be shed and could pose a risk of transmission to close contacts.
Evidence: Three animals from the rVSV-ZEBOV-GP group also had detectable rVSV RNA in nasal (357-09), oral (358-09), and rectal (352-09, 358-09) swabs at different times post inoculation with only 357-09 having virus isolated from the nasal swab at day 2 post inoculation (Table 3).

To assess if there was replicating virus in nasal, oral, and rectal swabs (day 0, 2, 4, 7, 14, and 21), blood (day 0, 2, 4, 7, 14, and 21), non-neural tissues... virus titers were determined by conventional plaque assay on Vero cell monolayers.
348.Rationale: The passage documents that some rVSV-ZEBOV-GP–inoculated animals had detectable rVSV RNA in nasal/oral/rectal swabs and that infectious virus was isolated from a nasal swab in one animal at day 2. Detection of viral RNA and isolation of infectious virus from mucosal swabs following intracerebral inoculation is direct evidence that the recombinant vector can be present in secretions, which implies potential for shedding and therefore a potential transmission risk to contacts. The passage does not provide transmission studies or quantify the transmission risk, but the detection of infectious virus in a nasal swab supports the inference of possible shedding and transmission potential.

348.Option: The rVSV filovirus GP vaccine vectors are completely attenuated for pathogenesis in the mouse model, similar to VSV vectors expressing influenza hemagglutinin (HA) protein.
Evidence: In the current study, we evaluated the NV potential of the rVSV-ZEBOV-GP and the rVSV-MARV-GP vectors in cynomolgus macaques.
To date, the rVSV filovirus GP vectors have been used in over 80 NHPs with no signs of toxicity as a result of vaccination [8], [9], [11], [12], [18], [35].

This is important considering that the vaccine is replication-competent and there is potential for VSV neurovirulence (NV), as has been reported in rodents [41], [42], [43], [44], [45], [46], [47], [48], macaques [49], cattle, sheep, and horses [50].
348.Rationale: The passage contains no data from mouse models or any direct comparison to VSV vectors expressing influenza HA. The experiments and conclusions reported are specific to cynomolgus macaques (NHPs). Furthermore, the text notes that VSV neurovirulence has been reported in rodents, which contradicts any blanket claim of complete attenuation in mouse models. Therefore the statement about being 'completely attenuated for pathogenesis in the mouse model' and being 'similar to VSV vectors expressing influenza HA' is not supported by the provided passage.

349.Option: Leishmania mexicana infection suppresses DC maturation regardless of the timing of immune activation signals, indicating that the parasite's inhibitory mechanisms are independent of host cell activation state.
Evidence: Under these conditions, L. mexicana infected-DCs showed similar levels of maturation markers compared to cells stimulated only with LPS.

Leishmania infection did not have any effect on LPS-induced T cell activation, as IL-2 production by T cells co-cultured with LPS-matured DCs and then infected for 24 hrs was similar to that of cultures of T cells with DCs stimulated only with LPS.

infection for 24 hr prior to LPS stimulation significantly reduced the levels of MHC I, MHC II, CD11b and B7.1 in comparison with LPS-matured cells, suggesting that addition of LPS after infection is not sufficient to reverse the suppressing effect of L. mexicana promastigotes on the expression of these molecules.
349.Rationale: The passage shows that the timing matters: if DCs are stimulated with LPS before infection, L. mexicana infection does not suppress maturation markers or LPS-induced T cell activation (quoting that infected-DCs showed similar levels to LPS-only). In contrast, infection prior to LPS does suppress maturation. Therefore the parasite's inhibitory effects are not independent of host cell activation state; they depend on timing of infection relative to activation signals. The provided quotes directly contradict the absolute statement in the option.

349.Option: Infection of dendritic cells with L. mexicana promastigotes prior to LPS stimulation inhibits the phosphorylation of ERK and p38, which are required for the expression of MHC and co-stimulatory molecules, thereby impairing antigen presentation and T cell activation.
Evidence: Figure 2B shows that infection with L. mexicana after LPS stimulation reduced phosphorylation of ERK, JNK and p38. The same reduction on ERK and p38 phosphorylation was observed when DCs were infected before LPS stimulation; however, JNK phosphorylation was not affected in this condition.
Protein phosphorylation though MAPKs plays an essential role in many cellular functions [24].

infection for 24 hr prior to LPS stimulation significantly reduced the levels of MHC I, MHC II, CD11b and B7.1 in comparison with LPS-matured cells, suggesting that addition of LPS after infection is not sufficient to reverse the suppressing effect of L. mexicana promastigotes on the expression of these molecules.

when T cells were co-cultured with L. mexicana-infected DCs prior to stimulation with LPS, they produced ∼85% less IL-2, suggesting that L. mexicana impairs the ability of DCs to stimulate T cell responses (Figure 7A).
349.Rationale: The passage provides experimental evidence that infection prior to LPS reduces phosphorylation of ERK and p38 (Figure 2B) and that MAPK phosphorylation is important for cellular functions. It also shows that infection before LPS significantly reduces surface expression of MHC and co-stimulatory molecules and markedly diminishes DC ability to stimulate T cells (IL-2 production ∼85% less). These statements together support the inference that pre-stimulation infection inhibits ERK/p38 phosphorylation and that this is associated with impaired maturation, antigen presentation and T cell activation.

349.Option: Infection of dendritic cells with L. mexicana promastigotes after LPS stimulation leads to a complete reversal of the parasite's inhibitory effects on DC maturation and antigen presentation.
Evidence: Under these conditions, L. mexicana infected-DCs showed similar levels of maturation markers compared to cells stimulated only with LPS.

Leishmania infection did not have any effect on LPS-induced T cell activation, as IL-2 production by T cells co-cultured with LPS-matured DCs and then infected for 24 hrs was similar to that of cultures of T cells with DCs stimulated only with LPS.

DC infection after LPS stimulation did not impair IL-12 mRNA levels; however, when DCs were first infected and then stimulated with LPS, IL-12 mRNA expression was reduced more than 30 fold, indicating that L. mexicana infection renders DCs refractory to further LPS stimulation, in line with the demonstration that LPS-signaling in infected cells was altered.
349.Rationale: The experimental data show that when DCs are matured with LPS before infection, subsequent infection does not reduce maturation marker expression, does not impair LPS-induced IL-2 production by T cells, and does not impair IL-12 mRNA levels. These findings indicate that LPS stimulation prior to infection effectively prevents the parasite's inhibitory effects on maturation and antigen-presentation functions measured in these experiments, which supports the option's claim that infection after LPS reverses (i.e., does not produce) the inhibitory phenotype.

349.Option: The activation of JNK phosphorylation in LPS-stimulated DCs following Leishmania infection suggests that the parasite promotes DC maturation and enhances T cell activation.
Evidence: On the other hand, infection with L. mexicana promastigotes inhibited basal phosphorylation of ERK, JNK and p38, without affecting total protein expression (Figure 2A right panels).
The same reduction on ERK and p38 phosphorylation was observed when DCs were infected before LPS stimulation; however, JNK phosphorylation was not affected in this condition.
IL-2 production by T cells was significantly reduced when cultured with Leishmania-infected DCs, compared to LPS-stimulated DCs (Figure 7A).

when T cells were co-cultured with L. mexicana-infected DCs prior to stimulation with LPS, they produced ∼85% less IL-2, suggesting that L. mexicana impairs the ability of DCs to stimulate T cell responses (Figure 7A).
349.Rationale: The passage does not report parasite-driven activation of JNK; rather it reports inhibition of basal phosphorylation of JNK (and ERK/p38), and that in the condition where infection precedes LPS, JNK phosphorylation was not affected (not activated). Moreover, functional outcomes show that infected DCs have reduced capacity to stimulate T cells (IL-2 strongly reduced). Therefore the claim that Leishmania promotes DC maturation and enhances T cell activation via JNK activation is not supported by the data and is contradicted by reported inhibitory effects.

349.Option: The increased expression of CD40 in Leishmania-infected DCs compensates for the loss of other co-stimulatory molecules, enabling effective T cell activation despite impaired antigen presentation.
Evidence: Interestingly, CD40 expression was increased in LPS-matured DCs, and its expression was not affected by L. mexicana.

infection for 24 hr prior to LPS stimulation significantly reduced the levels of MHC I, MHC II, CD11b and B7.1 in comparison with LPS-matured cells, suggesting that addition of LPS after infection is not sufficient to reverse the suppressing effect of L. mexicana promastigotes on the expression of these molecules.
IL-2 production by T cells was significantly reduced when cultured with Leishmania-infected DCs, compared to LPS-stimulated DCs (Figure 7A).

Together, our data suggest that alterations in DC maturation and cytokine production upon infection with L. mexicana promastigotes involve activation of PTPs that can dephosphorylate important MAP kinases and inhibit nuclear translocation of AP-1 and NF-κB, resulting in DCs with a suppressed activation phenotype, characterized by decreased expression of MHC and costimulatory molecules, decreased IL-12 production and increased production of IL-10
349.Rationale: The passage states that CD40 expression was increased by LPS and was not affected by L. mexicana (i.e., infection did not increase CD40). Importantly, despite CD40 being present, infected DCs showed decreased expression of other antigen-presentation and co-stimulatory molecules and had impaired ability to stimulate T cells (reduced IL-2). The data therefore do not support the claim that CD40 compensates to enable effective T cell activation; on the contrary, effective T cell activation was impaired.

350.Option: The differences in resistance mutation frequencies between H. contortus and T. circumcincta are due to differences in the mutation rates of their isotype-1 β-tubulin genes.
Evidence: We report a remarkably high diversity of benzimidazole resistance haplotypes with both hard and soft selective sweeps occurring in different parasite populations.
We propose a model for anthelmintic resistance in which multiple independent resistance mutations recurrently arise in parasite populations and are then spread by migration.
In contrast, for T. circumcincta 22 of the total number of isotype-1 β-tubulin haplotypes (43) were identified as possible recombinants resulting from just 5 unique recombination events.
The error rates calculated for H. contortus and T. circumcincta using this method were similar to each other (T. circumcinta: 7.09–9.46*10–6, H. contortus: 7.23–9.64*10–6).
350.Rationale: The passage does not present any data comparing intrinsic genomic mutation rates of the isotype-1 β-tubulin genes between the two species. Instead, the authors attribute observed patterns to multiple independent mutations, recombination and migration ("multiple independent resistance mutations recurrently arise ... and are then spread by migration") and document recombination contributing to diversity in T. circumcincta. The reported PCR error rates were similar between species, so technical artifacts are unlikely to explain species differences. Therefore the specific claim that differences in frequencies are due to underlying mutation-rate differences is not supported by the Passage Body.

350.Option: The high genetic diversity of resistance haplotypes in T. circumcincta is primarily due to historical recombination events that predate the onset of anthelmintic use.
Evidence: In contrast, for T. circumcincta 22 of the total number of isotype-1 β-tubulin haplotypes (43) were identified as possible recombinants resulting from just 5 unique recombination events.

The resistance haplotypes are polyphyletic, being distributed across the entire network consistent with a hypothesis that resistance mutations have appeared on different ancestral susceptible haplotypes (Fig. 4).
We propose a model for anthelmintic resistance in which multiple independent resistance mutations recurrently arise in parasite populations and are then spread by migration.
350.Rationale: While the passage documents that recombination has contributed substantially to the haplotypic diversity in T. circumcincta (22 of 43 haplotypes identified as possible recombinants), it does not provide evidence that these recombination events are historical and specifically predate the onset of anthelmintic use. The authors emphasize multiple independent (recurrent) mutation events and migration as contributors to diversity rather than attributing diversity primarily to pre-drug-era recombination. Therefore the stronger claim that historical recombination predating drug use is the primary cause is not supported by the provided text.

350.Option: The lack of significant genetic differentiation between farms for T. circumcincta indicates that resistance mutations are fixed in the population and no further evolution is expected.
Evidence: AMOVA analysis estimated that the percentage of variation that partitioned between farm populations was 10-fold less for T. circumcincta (0.24%) compared to H. contortus (2.84%) suggesting greater geographical sub-structuring of H. contortus than T. circumcincta.
There was a total of fifteen different isotype-1 β-tubulin haplotypes for H. contortus and forty three different haplotypes for T. circumcincta across the seven farms.
It was at fixation on farm 37 (Gloucester) and close to fixation on two other farms, farm 86 (Middlesex) and farm 110 (Kent).

The only farm of the seven that had a predominantly susceptible T. circumcincta population, farm 102 (Inverness), had the highest level of haplotype diversity and the least evidence of selection on the neutrality tests (Table 1).
350.Rationale: Although neutral-marker differentiation between farms is low for T. circumcincta (AMOVA 0.24%), the passage documents very high overall haplotype diversity (43 haplotypes) and variable local patterns: some farms show fixation or near-fixation of particular resistance polymorphisms while others retain predominantly susceptible and highly diverse haplotypes (e.g., farm 102). This indicates ongoing heterogeneity and the potential for further evolutionary change. The Passage Body therefore does not justify concluding that resistance is fixed everywhere or that no further evolution is expected.

350.Option: The high diversity of resistance haplotypes observed in both H. contortus and T. circumcincta, combined with the lack of genetic differentiation between farms at neutral loci, suggests that recurrent mutations and migration are key drivers of anthelmintic resistance emergence and spread.
Evidence: We report a remarkably high diversity of benzimidazole resistance haplotypes with both hard and soft selective sweeps occurring in different parasite populations.
We propose a model for anthelmintic resistance in which multiple independent resistance mutations recurrently arise in parasite populations and are then spread by migration.

AMOVA analysis estimated that the percentage of variation that partitioned between farm populations was 10-fold less for T. circumcincta (0.24%) compared to H. contortus (2.84%) suggesting greater geographical sub-structuring of H. contortus than T. circumcincta.

The resistance haplotypes are polyphyletic, being distributed across the entire network consistent with a hypothesis that resistance mutations have appeared on different ancestral susceptible haplotypes (Fig. 4).
There was a total of fifteen different isotype-1 β-tubulin haplotypes for H. contortus and forty three different haplotypes for T. circumcincta across the seven farms.
350.Rationale: This option restates the principal inference the authors themselves draw. The Passage Body documents high numbers of distinct resistance haplotypes, polyphyletic placement of resistance alleles (consistent with independent origins), low neutral-marker differentiation across farms (especially for T. circumcincta), and explicitly proposes a model of recurrent independent mutations followed by spread via migration. Therefore the epidemiological/evolutionary inference that recurrent mutation plus migration drives emergence and spread is directly supported by the passage.

350.Option: The presence of a single resistance haplotype at high frequency on multiple farms suggests that resistance mutations arose once and spread through the population via a hard selective sweep.
Evidence: We report a remarkably high diversity of benzimidazole resistance haplotypes with both hard and soft selective sweeps occurring in different parasite populations.

The resistance haplotypes are polyphyletic, being distributed across the entire network consistent with a hypothesis that resistance mutations have appeared on different ancestral susceptible haplotypes (Fig. 4).

Hr1 was the most frequent haplotype on five out of seven farms (farm 37 (Gloucester), farm 54 (Devon), farm 86 (Middlesex), farm 101 (E. Sussex) and farm 110 (Kent)) and Hr4 was identified on five farms (farm 95 (N. Yorkshire), farm 37 (Gloucester), farm 54 (Devon), Farm 86 (Middlesex) and farm 101 (E. Sussex)) (Fig. 3).
It was at fixation on farm 37 (Gloucester) and close to fixation on two other farms, farm 86 (Middlesex) and farm 110 (Kent).
350.Rationale: While the Passage Body provides examples where particular resistance haplotypes (e.g., Hr1, Hr4) are frequent on multiple farms and some farm-level near-fixations occur, the overall pattern reported is of both hard and soft selective sweeps and of polyphyletic resistance haplotypes arising on different ancestral backgrounds. The authors explicitly emphasize multiple independent origins and migration as explanations rather than a single-origin hard sweep universally explaining the data. Thus the statement that a single-origin hard sweep explains the observed multi-farm high-frequency haplotypes is overly general and not supported as the sole inference from the presented data.

351.Option: Anti-schistosome treatment with praziquantel results in partial, but often incomplete, restoration of Sh-associated changes in immune phenotype, gene expression, and microbiota.
Evidence: Overall, this study indicated multiple changes occurring on a molecular level during Sh infection, consistent with increased cell adhesion and type 2 immunity, which was only partly reversed by PZQ treatment.

analysis of the whole blood transcriptome identified 320 differentially expressed genes between 8 Gabonese schoolchildren with Sh infection and 9 without Sh infection, while after PZQ treatment only 4 genes remained differently expressed between the two groups.
These changes in the fecal microbiota appeared to be refractory to praziquantel treatment [216,217,220].

Taken together, most studies support a conclusion that a strong Th2 immune response is triggered by dying Sh worms. In the ensuing time after treatment, most studies indicate further immune changes including a continued increase of Th2 cytokines [71,84,85,88,93] and decrease in pro-inflammatory cytokines and chemokines including IFN-γ [88,93] and MIP-1α [89].
351.Rationale: The passage provides multiple lines of evidence that praziquantel (PZQ) modifies host biology but does not fully return it to the uninfected baseline. A whole-blood transcriptome study showed most transcriptional differences reverted after treatment but some remained, and the authors explicitly stated molecular changes were only "partly reversed by PZQ treatment." Microbiome studies noted that fecal microbiota changes "appeared to be refractory to praziquantel treatment." Cytokine and immune-cell studies similarly describe complex, time-dependent shifts after PZQ with some markers increasing or decreasing but with persistent alterations and uncertainty about duration. Together these statements support the inference that treatment yields partial but often incomplete restoration of immune, gene-expression, and microbiota perturbations.

351.Option: Praziquantel treatment leads to a complete normalization of immune function, as evidenced by the restoration of CD4+ T cell counts and T cell proliferation in individuals with Sh infection.
Evidence: individuals living in endemic areas frequently become reinfected with Sh after successful treatment with praziquantel (PZQ) [13] and can experience additional DALYs lost after reinfection.

PZQ treatment of those with Sh infection led to decreased CD4+ T cells and CD4+ memory T cells, increased proliferation of naïve thymic CD31+CD4+ T cells, and no changes in CD8+ T cell and CD8+ memory T cell frequencies at 6 weeks [126].

lymphocyte proliferation was similar among individuals with Sh infection and without Sh infection in response to stimulation by generic antigens [57,88,118] and by schistosome antigens AWA and SEA before and after PZQ chemotherapy [119].
In general, Sh appears to evoke aspects of both pro- and anti-inflammatory responses in the host.
351.Rationale: The passage does not support complete normalization of immune function after PZQ. Findings are heterogeneous: some studies report decreases in CD4+ and CD4+ memory T cells after treatment, others show limited or no change in lymphocyte proliferation before versus after PZQ. The review repeatedly emphasizes complex, partial, and time-dependent immune changes post-treatment rather than consistent full restoration to uninfected immune status. Therefore the strong claim of complete normalization (restoration of CD4+ counts and proliferation) is not supported by the presented evidence.

351.Option: The mixed immune response observed in Sh infection, including both pro- and anti-inflammatory cytokines, may explain the paradoxical findings of reduced disease severity in some chronic viral infections such as HIV, HTLV-1, and respiratory viruses.
Evidence: In general, Sh infection appears to result in systemic alterations in an array of cytokines and chemokines, with most studies describing an increase in the Th2 cytokines IL-4, IL-5, and IL-10, as well as Th1 cytokines IL-6 and IFN-γ (Fig 2). This mixed pro-inflammatory and anti-inflammatory response observed in Sh infection highlights the complexity of immune responses to this parasite.

increasing evidence suggesting that Sh may additionally contribute to DALYs lost through its association with increased susceptibility to secondary infections, including HIV, HPV, and other sexually transmitted infections [6–12].

women with FGS (defined as presence of sandy patches on colposcopy exam) were found to have increased expression of the HIV entry molecule CCR5 on both systemic circulating and local cervical CD4+ T cells and CD14+ monocytes, which decreased on both cell populations post-PZQ treatment in both compartments [107].
351.Rationale: Although the passage documents a mixed pro- and anti-inflammatory cytokine milieu in Sh infection, it does not provide evidence that this explains reduced severity of chronic viral infections. On the contrary, the review cites associations between Sh and increased susceptibility to viral infections (HIV, HPV) and mechanisms that could increase viral acquisition (e.g., elevated CCR5 expression). There is no discussion or data in the passage indicating a protective or disease-attenuating effect of Sh on chronic viral infections; therefore the proposed explanatory inference is not supported by the text.

351.Option: The persistence of altered gene expression and microbiome composition after praziquantel treatment suggests that Sh infection may induce irreversible epigenetic changes that increase long-term cancer risk.
Evidence: Methylated RASSF1A and TIMP3 genes, both tumor suppressor genes that have been documented to be elevated in bladder cancer and damage [199], were found to be increased in the urine of individuals with Sh infection who had ultrasound-confirmed bladder damage compared to individuals without Sh infection from the same endemic region in Ghana.
These methylated genes could serve as predictive biomarkers in individuals with Sh infection for genitourinary damage and cancer risk.
These changes in the fecal microbiota appeared to be refractory to praziquantel treatment [216,217,220].

Overall, this study indicated multiple changes occurring on a molecular level during Sh infection, consistent with increased cell adhesion and type 2 immunity, which was only partly reversed by PZQ treatment.
It remains to be determined how long this Th2 predominant immune phenotype persists and if it ever reverts to a more balanced Th1/ Th2 phenotype in the absence of further helminth infections.
351.Rationale: The passage documents persistence of some altered gene-expression markers (including DNA methylation of tumor-suppressor genes) and microbiome changes after PZQ, and it notes links between Sh and carcinogenic processes. However, it does not establish that these are irreversible epigenetic modifications nor definitively demonstrates that they causally increase long-term cancer risk. The wording in the option asserts irreversibility; the passage explicitly expresses uncertainty about duration and reversibility of phenotypes. Thus the stronger causal claim of irreversible epigenetic induction increasing long-term cancer risk is not fully supported by the presented evidence, although the passage raises concerns and suggests this as a hypothesis worth further study.

351.Option: Praziquantel treatment effectively prevents reinfection by restoring a robust Th2 immune response, as demonstrated by elevated IL-4 and IL-5 levels in individuals who remain uninfected after treatment.
Evidence: individuals living in endemic areas frequently become reinfected with Sh after successful treatment with praziquantel (PZQ) [13] and can experience additional DALYs lost after reinfection.

ratios increased towards a Th2 phenotype 12 and 18 months post-PZQ therapy in those who remained uninfected versus those who became reinfected in areas of both high and low endemicity. Specifically, those who became reinfected showed a more pronounced Th1 phenotype [88].
higher levels of Th2 cytokines have been linked to reduced likelihood of reinfection, while lower levels correlated with reinfection in two separate studies [75,91].
repeated treatment led to high IL-5 and low IFN-γ production in response to AWA in comparison to no or single treatment, but was not protective against Sh reinfection.
351.Rationale: While the passage documents associations between a Th2-skewed response (e.g., higher IL-4/IL-5) and lower likelihood of reinfection in some studies, it also states reinfection is common after PZQ and explicitly reports that repeated treatment was "not protective against Sh reinfection." Thus the available evidence does not support the claim that PZQ treatment effectively prevents reinfection by restoring Th2 immunity. The relationship described is associative and inconsistent; treatment does not reliably prevent reinfection according to the passage.

352.Option: The increasing dominance of CV-A6 and other non-EV-A71 serotypes in Guizhou Province indicates that the EV-A71 vaccine is no longer effective and should be discontinued in the region.
Evidence: CV-A6 emerged as the dominant serotype in 2019, 2022, and 2023, accounting for 36.94%, 41.21%, and 59.20%, respectively (Fig 4A).
Meanwhile, monovalent EV-A71 vaccines do not offer cross-protection against other serotypes and have low coverage rates in the province.

Phase III clinical trials demonstrated that the three inactivated EV-A71 vaccines showed over 90% efficacy against EV-A71-associated HFMD and nearly 100% efficacy in preventing severe manifestations of the disease
Notably, EV-A71 was responsible for 85.07% of the 154 fatal cases (Fig 4D).
352.Rationale: The passage documents the rise of CV-A6 and other non-EV-A71 serotypes, and it also documents that EV-A71 vaccines are monovalent (no cross-protection) and have low coverage. However, the passage also states that EV-A71 vaccines have high efficacy against EV-A71 and that EV-A71 accounted for the majority of fatal cases. There is no evidence in the passage that EV-A71 vaccines have become ineffective for preventing EV-A71 disease, nor any data supporting discontinuation. The observed serotype replacement and lack of cross-protection argue for broadened prevention strategies, not for stopping a vaccine that prevents severe EV-A71 disease.

352.Option: The emergence of CV-A6 as the predominant serotype in Guizhou Province since 2019, coupled with low vaccination coverage and lack of cross-protection from the EV-A71 vaccine, suggests that current prevention strategies are insufficient to control the overall burden of HFMD in the region.
Evidence: CV-A6 emerged as the dominant serotype in 2019, 2022, and 2023, accounting for 36.94%, 41.21%, and 59.20%, respectively (Fig 4A).
Meanwhile, monovalent EV-A71 vaccines do not offer cross-protection against other serotypes and have low coverage rates in the province.
The EV-A71 vaccine was introduced in Guizhou Province in 2016, and the vaccination rate ranged from 0.96% to 9.86% in the province between 2016 and 2023.
These factors may contribute to the persistent epidemic of HFMD in Guizhou and inform enhancements to pathogen surveillance for HFMD.
352.Rationale: The passage directly reports CV-A6’s emergence as predominant since 2019, states that EV-A71 vaccines are monovalent with no cross-protection, and documents very low EV-A71 vaccine uptake in Guizhou. The authors explicitly note these factors may contribute to persistent HFMD epidemics. Together, these facts support the inference that current prevention strategies (monovalent vaccination with low coverage and existing surveillance) are insufficient to control overall HFMD burden in the region.

352.Option: The phylogenetic similarity between Guizhou CV-A6 isolates and strains from Yunnan, Sichuan, and Hebei provinces indicates that regional travel and population movement are the primary drivers of HFMD transmission in Guizhou.
Evidence: The 20 Guizhou CV-A6 isolates were all found to be located on the D3a branch, and were closely related to the strains from Sichuan, Hebei, and Yunnan provinces of China.
This suggests that CV-A6 from Guizhou Province co-circulated with CV-A6 from other regions of China (Fig 8A).
352.Rationale: The passage shows phylogenetic relatedness and co-circulation of strains between Guizhou and other provinces, which is consistent with viral movement between regions. However, it does not provide direct evidence that regional travel or population movement are the primary drivers of transmission, nor does it quantify their relative contribution compared to local transmission dynamics, environmental factors, or other drivers. Inferring a single primary driver is not supported by the provided data.

352.Option: The biennial epidemic pattern of CV-A6 in Guizhou Province suggests that seasonal vaccination campaigns could effectively reduce transmission and prevent outbreaks.
Evidence: CV-A6 demonstrated a biennial epidemic pattern since 2019, with alternating peaks across geographic regions.
Meanwhile, monovalent EV-A71 vaccines do not offer cross-protection against other serotypes and have low coverage rates in the province.
The EV-A71 vaccine was introduced in Guizhou Province in 2016, and the vaccination rate ranged from 0.96% to 9.86% in the province between 2016 and 2023.
352.Rationale: While the passage documents a biennial pattern for CV-A6, it does not provide evidence that vaccination campaigns timed to seasonality would be effective. In addition, the only vaccine discussed is monovalent EV-A71, which lacks cross-protection against CV-A6, and vaccine coverage in the province is low. There is no data on efficacy of a CV-A6 vaccine, on the effects of seasonal vaccination timing, or on programmatic feasibility. Therefore the specific inference that seasonal vaccination campaigns would effectively reduce CV-A6 transmission is not supported by the passage.

352.Option: The high seroprevalence of neutralizing antibodies against CV-A6, CV-A10, CV-A16, and EV-A71 in healthy individuals suggests that natural immunity is effectively controlling the spread of HFMD in Guizhou Province.
Evidence: The seropositive rates of CV-A6, CV-A10, CV-A16, and EV-A71 were 62.04% (95% CI: 57.5–66.6%), 54.17% (95% CI: 49.5–58.9%), 54.63% (95% CI: 49.9–59.3%), and 64.35% (95% CI: 59.8–68.9%), respectively, which showed significant differences (χ2 = 14.3, P < 0.005).

Of these, more than 90% of healthy individuals carried positive NtAbs against at least one serotype of the four enteroviruses, and over 70% of the participants had positive NtAbs against at least two types of these enteroviruses (Table 1).
A total of 513,143 HFMD cases were reported based on clinical symptoms in Guizhou Province between 2008 and 2023.

children aged 0–5 years accounted for 98.12% of the reported cases, with an average annual incidence rate of 979.62 per 100,000. The highest incidence was observed in the 0–2–year age group (1347.70 per 100,000 population annually).
The seroprevalence rates of CV-A6, CV-A10, and CV-A16 were lowest in the 0–2 year age group, reaching the highest levels in the 9–17 age group.
The GMTs of CV-A6, CV-A10, and CV-A16 were lowest in the 0–2–year–old age group and increased with age;
352.Rationale: Although overall seroprevalence among sampled healthy individuals is substantial (many have NtAbs against at least one or multiple serotypes), the passage also documents a very high burden of HFMD (over half a million cases) concentrated in young children, and notes that the youngest age group (0–2 years) has the lowest seroprevalence and lowest GMTs. These facts show that population-level natural immunity is uneven and that susceptible young children sustain transmission and disease burden. The data do not support the stronger claim that natural immunity is effectively controlling HFMD spread across the population.

353.Option: The high genetic admixture observed in Iquitos B and Barrio Florida indicates that these populations are self-sustaining and do not rely on external mosquito introductions for persistence.
Evidence: Structure results showed a clear pattern of genetic admixture from all the sample populations, suggesting steady gene flow within this region (Fig 3).

dominant haplotype membership (> 0.8) to the same group (depicted in blue in Fig 3) was observed among 84.4% of mosquitoes collected from Indiana/Mazan, 87.5% of mosquitos from Iquitos B, 75% of mosquitoes from Aucayo, and 64.5% of individuals from Barrio Florida.

While the dominance of one genetic group (depicted in blue) in Iquitos B (IQTB), Indiana/Mazan (INDM), Aucayo (AUC), and Barrio Florida (BFL) indicates some genetic isolation at these sites, other sampling locations contain individuals from various genetic backgrounds.
353.Rationale: The option misstates the genetic pattern. The passage does not report "high genetic admixture" in Iquitos B; instead it reports high dominance of a single haplotype in Iquitos B (87.5%) and a majority-dominant haplotype in Barrio Florida (64.5%), which the authors interpret as indicating some genetic isolation at those sites. Moreover, the passage overall describes "steady gene flow within this region," not definitive evidence that those populations are entirely self-sustaining without external introductions. Thus the claim that high admixture indicates self-sustaining populations independent of introductions is not supported by the presented data.

353.Option: The Propagule Pressure Index (PrPI) demonstrates that terrestrial transportation routes, such as the Iquitos-Nauta Highway, are the primary mechanism for Aedes aegypti dispersal into smaller communities.
Evidence: Accessible only by plane or boat, Iquitos is surrounded by other, smaller settlements that are primarily connected to one another via river networks.
The only significant source of terrestrial transit is the 95km Iquitos-Nauta Highway, connecting Iquitos (population, pop henceforth: 406,340) to the smaller city of Nauta (pop: 13,983) [41].
Our results indicated that some vehicle types were consistently infested with Ae. aegypti across multiple months (71% of large barges, 35% of medium-sized barges, and 12.5% of buses).

Values of PrPI were plotted against FST to visually explore the relationship between these two variables. However, no statistical tests were conducted due to low sample sizes—pairwise calculations of PrPI were only available between Iquitos and other towns, leaving a total of five observations.
353.Rationale: The passage emphasizes riverine transport (boats, barges) as the dominant transit mode and reports much higher infestation rates on large and medium barges than on buses. The authors also state they could not statistically evaluate the relationship between PrPI and genetic distances due to low sample size and limited transportation data. Therefore the passage does not demonstrate that terrestrial routes (the highway) are the primary dispersal mechanism; in fact the evidence points toward fluvial transport and indicates insufficient data to conclude primacy of any particular route.

353.Option: The lack of isolation by distance in the study area suggests that genetic differentiation among Aedes aegypti populations is primarily driven by local ecological factors such as water storage practices and microclimate, rather than by human transportation networks.
Evidence: Our analysis of isolation by distance showed no clear relationship between genetic distance (FST) and geographic distance for the three standard distance models (Fig 2).

Ae. aegypti genetic differentiation across various spatial scales is likely due to its limited flight range (rarely exceeding 100m under natural conditions [35–37]), heterogeneities in insecticide application, human population densities, and water storage habits [33, 38, 39].

At coarser scales (i.e., between nations, or regions within nations), human transportation networks have been implicated as a driver of Ae. aegypti gene flow; that is, relatedness between populations has been shown to correlate with major highways and waterways [25, 32].
353.Rationale: Although the passage notes there is no clear isolation by distance and mentions local ecological factors that can influence genetic differentiation, it also explicitly acknowledges that human transportation networks have been implicated as drivers of gene flow at broader scales and provides evidence of vehicle-mediated infestation and steady gene flow in the region. The statement in the option asserts a primary causal role for local ecological factors over human transportation networks; this stronger causal claim is not directly supported by the data presented. The passage does not provide evidence that rules out transportation-driven dispersal or that quantifies the relative contribution of local ecological factors versus human transport, so the option cannot be derived solely from the passage.

353.Option: The spread of Aedes aegypti to smaller surrounding communities is likely driven by repeated introductions from larger urban centers such as Iquitos, rather than by local establishment alone.
Evidence: Ae. aegypti dispersal can occur in one of two ways: the slower, active dispersal of flying adult females in search of bloodmeals or oviposition sites, or the faster, passive human-mediated dispersal, by which humans unintentionally transport mosquitoes via vehicle traffic (boats, cars, planes, etc). The latter can involve transport of eggs, larvae, pupae, or adults, all of which have been documented in vehicles, particularly in boats [10, 19–22].

Our previous research in the Peruvian Amazon demonstrated Ae. aegypti infestation on different vehicles commonly used for trade and transportation, including large barges, medium-sized barges, and buses [23, 24].
Iquitos A (Puerto Masusa) serves as a major hub of fluvial transit and predominantly harbors large barges, which carry cargo and passengers throughout the Peruvian Amazon distances up to ~500km.
Our results indicated that some vehicle types were consistently infested with Ae. aegypti across multiple months (71% of large barges, 35% of medium-sized barges, and 12.5% of buses).

Iquitos samples generally showed a lower degree genetic differentiation both when comparing sites within Iquitos and sites outside of Iquitos. Notably, mosquitoes collected from Iquitos A and B (the major and secondary port for fluvial transit) had lower FST values than mosquitoes collected from the interior of Iquitos.
Structure results showed a clear pattern of genetic admixture from all the sample populations, suggesting steady gene flow within this region (Fig 3).
353.Rationale: The passage documents human-mediated, vehicle-associated dispersal (particularly via river barges), high infestation rates on barges, major fluvial hubs in Iquitos with long-distance connectivity, lower genetic differentiation associated with port sites, and an overall pattern of genetic admixture and steady gene flow. Together these lines of evidence support the epidemiological inference that repeated introductions from the larger urban center (Iquitos) via human transportation are a plausible driver of Ae. aegypti presence in surrounding smaller communities, rather than purely local, independent establishment.

353.Option: The genetic isolation of Barrio Florida is due to its small population size and limited human mobility, which restricts both mosquito dispersal and gene flow.
Evidence: Mosquitoes were collected from the city of Iquitos, and the neighboring towns of Nauta (pop: 13,983), Indiana-Mazan (pop: 6,594), Barrio Florida (pop: 728), Tamshiaco (pop: 4,583), and Aucayo (pop: 806).

Pairwise FST values demonstrated low to moderate differentiation for the majority of site pairs (Table 3). Mosquitoes from Barrio Florida and Nauta were significantly differentiated from all other sampling locations, with FST values ranging from 0.022 to 0.11. Barrio Florida mosquitoes had the highest FST values observed for all study sites (FST > 0.05 in 4 out of 8 pairwise comparisons).

While the dominance of one genetic group (depicted in blue) in Iquitos B (IQTB), Indiana/Mazan (INDM), Aucayo (AUC), and Barrio Florida (BFL) indicates some genetic isolation at these sites, other sampling locations contain individuals from various genetic backgrounds.
353.Rationale: While the passage documents that Barrio Florida is small (pop: 728) and that its mosquitoes show relatively high genetic differentiation (highest FST values), it does not provide direct data on human mobility specific to Barrio Florida or demonstrate causation linking small human population size and limited mobility to genetic isolation. The authors do not measure or report local human movement patterns for Barrio Florida, so attributing the observed genetic isolation specifically to limited human mobility and population size is speculative and not directly supported by the presented data.

354.Option: The intermediate-chain lectin genes (igl) in E. histolytica and E. dispar are not orthologous due to extensive gene duplication and loss, which explains the high dS values observed.
Evidence: Visual inspection of aligned scaffolds of E. histolytica and E. dispar in amoebaDB identified six pairs of genes which could be identified as orthologous by surrounding synteny (Figures 1, 2 and Figure S1). These orthologous pairs were: hgl genes EHI_012270:EDI_213670 and EHI_046650:EDI_123980; igl genes EHI_006980:EDI_276450 and EHI_065330:EDI_244250; and lgl genes EHI_049690:EDI_071530 and EHI_159870:EDI_325130.

The pattern we observed for the igl genes EHI_006980:EDI_276450 and EHI_065330:EDI_244250 and lgl genes EHI_049690:EDI_071530, where dS was notably higher than for neighbouring genes, suggested a mechanism generating diversity other than positive selection on nonsynonymous mutations.
The pattern indicated gene conversion, in which gene regions are changed to match a paralogue that may be more divergent than a gene's true orthologue, hence the elevated dS.
354.Rationale: The passage explicitly identifies the two igl pairs as positional orthologues based on synteny. The authors interpret the unusually high dS between these identified orthologue pairs as evidence for gene conversion (a mechanism altering a gene to match a paralogue) rather than as evidence that the genes are non-orthologous due to duplication/loss. Therefore the statement that they are not orthologous (and that duplication/loss explains the high dS) is not supported by the text.

354.Option: Gene conversion, a process of non-reciprocal homologous recombination, likely contributes to genetic diversity in the Gal/GalNAc lectin complex of Entamoeba histolytica, potentially enabling immune evasion by generating novel antigenic variants.
Evidence: we found evidence that gene conversion had occurred among members of the Gal/GalNAc lectin gene families.
The pattern indicated gene conversion, in which gene regions are changed to match a paralogue that may be more divergent than a gene's true orthologue, hence the elevated dS.
Immune mediated selection can be a powerful driver of diversity in parasite surface proteins [9]–.
The lectin complex binds galactose and the N-acetyl-D-galactosamine on mucin glycoproteins and on host cell surfaces and mediates both colonisation and contact-dependent cytotoxicity [2].
Anti-lectin immunoglobulin A is associated with protection from amoebiasis [3] and the Gal/GalNAc lectin heavy-chain subunit is a leading vaccine candidate [4].
354.Rationale: The passage directly reports evidence for gene conversion among Gal/GalNAc lectin genes. It also states that immune-mediated selection can drive diversity in parasite surface proteins and that anti-lectin immune responses are protective; the lectin complex is a surface adhesin. Combining these statements, it is epidemiologically and evolutionarily reasonable, and directly supported by the text, to infer that gene conversion contributes to lectin diversity and could thereby facilitate immune evasion by generating novel antigenic variants (the option uses hedging terms 'likely' and 'potentially', which match the level of evidence presented).

354.Option: The presence of gene conversion in the Gal/GalNAc lectin complex suggests that Entamoeba histolytica undergoes sexual reproduction, as gene conversion is a hallmark of meiotic recombination.
Evidence: we found evidence that gene conversion had occurred among members of the Gal/GalNAc lectin gene families.
The pattern indicated gene conversion, in which gene regions are changed to match a paralogue that may be more divergent than a gene's true orthologue, hence the elevated dS.
354.Rationale: While the passage documents gene conversion among lectin genes and defines gene conversion as regions changed to match a paralogue, it does not link this process to sexual reproduction or meiosis. The authors interpret high dS as evidence of gene conversion without claiming it reflects meiotic recombination or sexual reproduction. Gene conversion can occur by several mechanisms (including mitotic recombination), and the passage provides no evidence to infer sexual reproduction; therefore the option's causal claim is not supported by the text.

354.Option: The high sequence similarity between paralogous igl genes, particularly at the 3′ end and between bases 1000 and 1500, suggests that these regions are under strong purifying selection to maintain functional integrity.
Evidence: Sequence similarity between paralogues is particularly notable at the 3′ end and between bases 1000 and 1500 (Figure 5), covering two of several putative growth factor receptor domains predicted in the genes.
To further explore and locate possible gene conversion among the igl genes, we assessed sequence similarity among members of the gene family across the length of the genes.
The pattern indicated gene conversion, in which gene regions are changed to match a paralogue that may be more divergent than a gene's true orthologue, hence the elevated dS.
354.Rationale: The passage documents high sequence similarity in specific regions of igl paralogues but explicitly investigates and attributes such similarity to gene conversion rather than interpreting it as evidence of purifying selection. The authors do not present dN/dS or other analyses showing purifying selection in those regions. Because the passage assigns the observed similarity to gene conversion, the inference that it is primarily due to strong purifying selection is not supported by the study's data as presented.

354.Option: The Gal/GalNAc lectin complex in E. histolytica has evolved under positive selection, as evidenced by elevated dN/dS ratios in the igl and lgl genes.
Evidence: We would expect, under positive selection, that the number of nonsynonymous differences per nonsynonymous site (dN) would be high, but that dS would not differ from dS in neighbouring genes. The pattern we observed for the igl genes EHI_006980:EDI_276450 and EHI_065330:EDI_244250 and lgl genes EHI_049690:EDI_071530, where dS was notably higher than for neighbouring genes, suggested a mechanism generating diversity other than positive selection on nonsynonymous mutations.
The number of synonymous differences per synonymous site (dS) between orthologous genes was particularly high (Figure 1, Figure 2), with dS>1 for two of the genes.
354.Rationale: The passage explicitly contrasts the expectations under positive selection (elevated dN) with the observed pattern (elevated dS). The authors conclude that the unusually high synonymous divergence indicates gene conversion rather than positive selection acting on amino-acid changing mutations. Therefore there is no evidence in the passage of elevated dN/dS supporting positive selection for the igl and lgl genes; the data argue against that interpretation.

355.Option: The ability of pathogenic Leptospira spp. to degrade DNA suggests that they possess a virulence factor that enables escape from neutrophil extracellular traps, potentially contributing to their persistence in the host.
Evidence: According to our results, some Leptospira were able to evade NETs.

Because some microorganisms evade NETs due to nuclease activity, we then studied whether Leptospira exerts this enzyme activity by incubating plasmid DNA with pathogenic and saprophyte Leptospira, or with exogenous DNase as a positive control.
Remarkably, while the treatment of DNA with either DNase or the pathogenic LIC resulted in marked DNA degradation, the saprophyte Patoc had no effect (Fig 5).
NETs reduced the viability of LIC by more than 90%. The degradation of LIC-induced NETs using DNase treatment at the beginning of the assay increased the number of live bacteria (Fig 4A).

Collectively, these data show that intravascular NET formation not only promotes bacteria snare and prevent extensively dissemination, but also protects the host from additional kidney bacterial burden.
355.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports that pathogenic Leptospira interrogans (LIC) degrades DNA in the same way as DNase (Fig 5) and that some Leptospira can evade NETs. The experiments showing that NET degradation by DNase increases bacterial survival (Fig 4A) support the functional importance of DNA-degrading activity for escape from NET-mediated killing. The in vivo data showing NETs limit dissemination and kidney burden further supports that escape from NETs would contribute to persistence. From these facts, it is a reasonable epidemiological/biological inference that pathogenic Leptospira possess a DNA-degrading virulence factor enabling escape from NETs and thereby potentially promoting persistence in the host.

355.Option: The formation of NETs in response to Leptospira interrogans is primarily driven by bacterial motility, as immotile mutants failed to induce significant NETosis in human neutrophils.
Evidence: Considering that Leptospira spp. are highly motile bacteria, we explored whether this property was important for the induction of NETs.

As shown in Fig 3A and 3B, bacteria motility does not appear to be relevant for NET formation since there were no significant differences in the formation of NETs triggered by the wild-type or the mutant strains.
355.Rationale: The passage directly tests and rejects the role of motility: experiments comparing motile wild-type strains and non-motile fla mutants showed no significant differences in NET formation. Therefore the assertion that NET formation is primarily driven by bacterial motility is contrary to the presented data and cannot be derived from the passage.

355.Option: Intravascular formation of NETs occurs in vivo upon Leptospira interrogans infection and appears to be a key mechanism by which the host attempts to control bacterial dissemination.
Evidence: After depletion of more than 90% of neutrophils (Fig 6A), we analyzed the formation of NETs in blood from mice inoculated with LIC during the first 3 days post-infection (dpi), by measuring the levels of nucleosomes in the plasma of infected animals. From these reported results, it is epidemiologically and experimentally supported that intravascular NET formation is a host mechanism to control Leptospira dissemination.

Therefore the claim that depletion led to severe interstitial nephritis is not supported, nor is there evidence that NETs directly protect renal tissue from inflammation; the data support NETs limiting bacterial burden but not reducing renal inflammation in this model. Thus the overall option is not derivable from the passage.

Evidence: After depletion of more than 90% of neutrophils (Fig 6A), we analyzed the formation of NETs in blood from mice inoculated with LIC during the first 3 days post-infection (dpi), by measuring the levels of nucleosomes in the plasma of infected animals. 355.Rationale: The passage documents detection of circulating nucleosomes (NET markers) in a murine model of infection, but it does not present any data on diagnostic performance (sensitivity, specificity, predictive value), nor does it provide data from human patients. Taken together, these elements from the passage support an inference that population aging, congregate/communal living among older adults, and potential declines in treatment efficacy are likely contributors to rising scabies burden in adults in certain regions.

357.Option: The upregulation of quorum-sensing protein potA and ABC transporter AapJMQ genes in response to rifampicin exposure suggests that these genes are directly responsible for rifampicin efflux and resistance in B. melitensis.

No functional knockout or direct efflux assays for potA/AapJMQ are presented. Because the study provides only correlative transcriptomic data and speculative wording ("may be"), the stronger claim that these genes are directly responsible for rifampicin efflux and resistance is not supported by the presented evidence.

357.Option: The T4SS is a multi-protein complex that plays a critical role in conjugation and DNA uptake, and its disruption leads to increased susceptibility to rifampicin in B. melitensis.

After 3 d, the MIC values of the gene deletion strains were higher than those of the parent strain (Fig 6B).
These changes may lead to less rifampicin intake.
357.Rationale: The passage documents downregulation of T4SS (VirB genes) under rifampicin and reports that deletion of VirB7-11 produced higher MICs (i.e., increased resistance), not increased susceptibility. The option's functional assertion that T4SS "plays a critical role in conjugation and DNA uptake" is not stated in the passage. Moreover, the experimental data contradict the claim that disruption increases susceptibility; instead, disruption of certain VirB genes was associated with higher MICs. From this, it is reasonable to infer that the early adaptive resistance observed is not driven by RNA polymerase (rpoB) mutations, which appeared later (at 60 h). Thus the inference that early adaptive resistance is independent of rpoB mutations is directly supported by the presented time-course data.

357.Option: The downregulation of the VirB operon in response to rifampicin exposure is a key mechanism for reducing rifampicin uptake and enhancing resistance in B. melitensis.

the expression of the T4SS regulation factor vjbR [26], HutC [27], BvrR [28] and RpoH-like heat shock sigma factor [29] were down-regulated, while the expression of the VirB operon was also downregulated, which led to a reduction in the secretory system.

The authors conclude that "VirB7-11 played important roles in rifampicin adaptive resistance." 359.Option: The clustering of Hansen's disease cases within families with up to eight cases suggests that genetic factors are the primary driver of disease transmission in hyperendemic areas.

The evidence supports multiple contributors to clustering (household exposure, proximity to infectious multibacillary cases, socioeconomic factors, and genetics) but does not support the stronger claim that genetic factors are the primary driver. Therefore the option overstates what can be derived from the passage and is not supported as the primary explanation.

359.Option: The lack of significant difference in detection rates between household and neighbor contacts suggests that spatial clustering is not a meaningful factor in determining Hansen's disease risk in hyperendemic areas.
Evidence: Of these new Hansen's disease cases, 6 (40.0%) were household contacts and 9 (60.0%) were neighbor contacts, with no difference in the rate of new cases found in household (2.9/100) or neighbor (2.1/100) contacts (p = 0.555) (Table 2).

The hypothesis that the new case household locations were independent from the previous multibacillary cases' households was rejected, as shown in Figure 2A, since the observed Gcross curve is found above the theoretical curve.
The newly diagnosed Hansen's disease case distribution was not random; rather it was clustered, as shown in Figure 2D, and was dependent on the presence of multibacillary cases (Figure 2A).

Thus spatial clustering is meaningful and supported by the data; the lack of difference in crude household vs neighbor rates does not negate spatial dependence shown by the Gcross analysis. The option incorrectly equates the nonsignificant household-vs-neighbor comparison with absence of spatial clustering.

359.Option: Since the distribution of new cases was not random and depended on the presence of multibacillary cases, the risk of Hansen's disease is primarily determined by the number of multibacillary cases in a neighborhood rather than by proximity to a single case.
Evidence: The hypothesis that the new case household locations were independent from the previous multibacillary cases' households was rejected, as shown in Figure 2A, since the observed Gcross curve is found above the theoretical curve.
The newly diagnosed Hansen's disease case distribution was not random; rather it was clustered, as shown in Figure 2D, and was dependent on the presence of multibacillary cases (Figure 2A).
Hoeven et al (2008) identified an area with radius of 10 meters from the index case as being the highest risk for development of Hansen's disease. [20]

Indeed, prior work cited in the passage highlights very local (10 meter) increased risk around an index case. The provided data show dependence on multibacillary cases but do not justify the specific claim that neighborhood multibacillary case count is the primary determinant over proximity to single cases; therefore the option over-interprets the evidence.

359.Option: In hyperendemic areas, the risk of Hansen's disease extends beyond household contacts to include residents in neighboring homes, particularly those living close to multibacillary cases, suggesting that expanding contact investigations to include neighborhood contacts is essential for effective case detection.
Evidence: Of these new Hansen's disease cases, 6 (40.0%) were household contacts and 9 (60.0%) were neighbor contacts, with no difference in the rate of new cases found in household (2.9/100) or neighbor (2.1/100) contacts (p = 0.555) (Table 2).

The hypothesis that the new case household locations were independent from the previous multibacillary cases' households was rejected, as shown in Figure 2A, since the observed Gcross curve is found above the theoretical curve.
The newly diagnosed Hansen's disease case distribution was not random; rather it was clustered, as shown in Figure 2D, and was dependent on the presence of multibacillary cases (Figure 2A).

and it is believed that disease development is associated with close and prolonged contact with untreated people with multibacillary disease, [12]; [13] as well as genetic [14]–[16] and socioeconomic factors.
359.Rationale: The study found substantial numbers of new cases among neighbor contacts (60% of new cases) and no significant difference in detection rates between household and neighbor contacts, indicating risk extends beyond the household. Spatial analysis showed clustering of new cases that depended on presence of multibacillary cases, and the passage states that disease development is associated with close and prolonged contact with untreated multibacillary cases. Together these findings support the inference that expanding contact investigations to include neighboring homes—especially those near multibacillary cases—can enhance case detection in hyperendemic settings.

359.Option: The mean age of previously diagnosed Hansen's disease cases was significantly higher than that of household and neighbor contacts, indicating that older individuals are more likely to develop the disease due to cumulative exposure over time.
Evidence: The mean age of previously diagnosed Hansen's disease cases (46.4± SD 18.5 years) was significantly higher than household contacts (30.3±21.2 years) (p<0.0001) and neighbor contacts (31.5±21.3 years) (p<0.0001).

In endemic areas, the majority of individuals infected with M. leprae do not develop disease, [10]; [11] and it is believed that disease development is associated with close and prolonged contact with untreated people with multibacillary disease, [12]; [13] as well as genetic [14]–[16] and socioeconomic factors. [17]; [18]
359.Rationale: While the passage documents that previously diagnosed cases were older on average than the contacted populations, it does not provide data demonstrating that older age per se increases likelihood of disease due to cumulative exposure. Newly diagnosed cases in this study were younger than previously diagnosed cases, and the passage emphasizes multiple determinants of disease development (close contact, genetics, socioeconomic factors). Inferring that older individuals are more likely to develop disease because of cumulative exposure goes beyond the evidence presented and invokes a causal mechanism not directly supported by the data in the passage.

360.Option: The increasing trend in anthrax incidence in wildlife along the Zambezi River basin could be representing a shared disease across an international boundary with neighboring Zambia, where the disease has long been documented as endemic.
Evidence: From the 2004–2005 first massive outbreak in wildlife in Malilangwe Wildlife Reserve and Save Valley Conservancy in the south-eastern Lowveld of Zimbabwe, anthrax has been occurring almost every year especially along the Zambezi River basin from Zambezi National Park (Kazungula, west of Victoria Falls) to Mana Pools National Park in the mid-Zambezi area (Table 7).
Anthrax is endemic in much of sub-Saharan Africa where it occurs naturally in wildlife and livestock, with herbivores being particularly susceptible [3–6].
Zimbabwe, alongside other southern African countries, falls under anthrax endemic regions [7–11].
360.Rationale: The passage documents increased incidence along the Zambezi River basin and states that anthrax is endemic in sub-Saharan Africa and in southern African countries, but it does not provide any direct data or statements about cross-border transmission with Zambia or about the endemicity specifically in Zambia. The option asserts a cross-border shared-disease interpretation that is not supported by any explicit evidence in the passage, so this inference cannot be derived solely from the provided text.

360.Option: The lack of carcass disposal and environmental decontamination following anthrax outbreaks, particularly in wildlife, is likely contributing to the expansion in the range and frequency of the disease in animals.
Evidence: Anthrax, caused by Bacillus anthracis, an endospore-forming Gram positive bacterium [1,2] is a cosmopolitan disease that exists since antiquity and has become endemic in some regions of the world.

For all anthrax outbreaks noted in wildlife in this study (n = 10 documented and cross referenced), only Malilangwe Wildlife Reserve had a history of carcass disposals and environmental decontamination efforts such as burning or burying carcasses during an outbreak in 2004–2005. For all other outbreaks of anthrax in wildlife, only disease confirmation was done as already described, but with no containment measures undertaken.

the sequence of response to outbreaks of anthrax by communities was: initially salvaging carcasses of dead or hastily slaughtered sick livestock for human consumption, followed by burial or burning of some carcasses and, finally efforts at disposal and environmental decontamination abandoned and carcasses left to dissipate into the environment as carcass numbers increased.

The sub-optimal surveillance, under-reporting and the consequent under-estimation of the disease impact collectively undermine the basis for informed disease containment measures including policy formulation for safeguarding animal and human health, communication and awareness of local extension services and human communities at risk.
360.Rationale: The passage states that B. anthracis is an endospore-forming bacterium (so spores persist in the environment) and documents that except for one reserve, outbreaks in wildlife had no carcass disposal or decontamination. It also reports community practices leaving carcasses to dissipate into the environment and that poor surveillance and lack of containment undermine control. Epidemiologically, persistence of spores in soil and failure to remove or decontaminate infectious carcasses are plausible drivers for environmental contamination, which would contribute to expanding range and frequency of animal anthrax. These conclusions follow from the combination of the biological characteristic of the organism and the documented gap in carcass management and containment.

360.Option: The lack of anthrax strain typing data in this study limits the ability to confirm whether the disease is spreading through direct transmission between wildlife and livestock, but the observed patterns suggest that such transmission is unlikely.
Evidence: However, it is not known if livestock/ wildlife interactions at wildlife-livestock-human interfaces (further referred to here as ‘interface’) has any influence on the transmission dynamics of the disease in either species [12,20,21].
no comprehensive epidemiological studies have been undertaken to understand the incidence, propagation and impact of anthrax in animals at the wildlife-livestock-human interface.
The outbreaks and cases described in this study were all laboratory confirmed.
360.Rationale: It is correct that the study does not resolve transmission dynamics at the interface and that comprehensive epidemiological work is lacking; this implies limited ability to confirm transmission pathways (which would indeed be aided by strain typing). However, the option's claim that the observed patterns suggest transmission is unlikely is not supported. The passage explicitly states uncertainty about whether wildlife-livestock interactions influence transmission, and it documents outbreaks in both wildlife and livestock and examples of spillover to humans from livestock. There is no evidence in the text indicating that transmission between wildlife and livestock is unlikely; on the contrary, the lack of data prevents firm conclusions either way. Therefore the statement as a whole is not derivable from the passage.

360.Option: The occurrence of anthrax in isolated cases or small numbers of animals, rather than mass mortalities, suggests that the disease is becoming less virulent and less likely to cause widespread outbreaks in wildlife populations.
Evidence: Clegg et al. (2007) [14] reported on a massive outbreak of anthrax in Malilangwe Wildlife Reserve in Chiredzi District of Zimbabwe in 2004 where more than 1500 wild herbivores, and mostly greater kudu (Tragelaphus strepsiceros) died.

From the 2004–2005 first massive outbreak in wildlife in Malilangwe Wildlife Reserve and Save Valley Conservancy in the south-eastern Lowveld of Zimbabwe, anthrax has been occurring almost every year especially along the Zambezi River basin from Zambezi National Park (Kazungula, west of Victoria Falls) to Mana Pools National Park in the mid-Zambezi area (Table 7).
A total of 3516 anthrax cases were recorded in animals at the study sites, with wildlife (86.7%, 3050/3516) contributing a higher percentage of the cases than livestock (Tables 5 & 6).
360.Rationale: The passage documents large historic mass mortalities (e.g., >1500 animals) and ongoing annual occurrences with wildlife accounting for the majority of cases in the study sites. There is no evidence presented that virulence has decreased or that outbreaks have become limited to isolated small numbers; indeed the data show substantial wildlife impact and recurring outbreaks. The claim that disease is becoming less virulent is not supported by the passage and cannot be inferred.

360.Option: The seasonal patterns of anthrax outbreaks in wildlife have shifted to the rainy season, indicating that environmental conditions such as increased moisture may be promoting spore germination and disease transmission.
Evidence: From the 2004–2005 first massive outbreak in wildlife in Malilangwe Wildlife Reserve and Save Valley Conservancy in the south-eastern Lowveld of Zimbabwe, anthrax has been occurring almost every year especially along the Zambezi River basin from Zambezi National Park (Kazungula, west of Victoria Falls) to Mana Pools National Park in the mid-Zambezi area (Table 7).
Anthrax is endemic in much of sub-Saharan Africa where it occurs naturally in wildlife and livestock, with herbivores being particularly susceptible [3–6].
360.Rationale: The passage describes geographic and temporal occurrence (years, locations) and documents recurring outbreaks, but it does not present information on seasonality or a shift of outbreaks to the rainy season. No data or statements in the text link outbreak timing to rainy-season conditions or to changes in seasonal patterns. Therefore the specific inference about a seasonal shift to the rainy season and moisture-driven spore germination cannot be derived from the provided material.

361.Option: The high genetic diversity of parasites in human males compared to females and non-human primates suggests that male patients are more likely to be re-infected with genetically distinct schistosome strains due to greater exposure to contaminated water sources.
Evidence: We can notice a general trend, with the highest genetic diversity index in the parasite infrapopulations coming from human males, followed by human females and lastly, by NHP.
This last study showed higher prevalence in male compare to female patients.
The people in the village use the lake water for irrigation, fishing and other domestic activities. Numerous vervet monkeys range in the environment and share the water source with humans.
The age of male patients was positively correlated to He (n = 36, r = 0.333, p<0.05), Ar (n = 36, r = 0.331, p<0.05), and the number of breeders (n = 32, r = 0.403, p<0.05)
361.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence that parasite genetic diversity (He, Ar) is highest in infrapopulations from human males and that males have higher prevalence. The study area descriptions indicate shared water contact activities (irrigation, fishing, domestic use) which are plausible exposure routes. Positive correlations between male age and diversity metrics further support accumulation of diverse infections over time. From an epidemiological standpoint, higher within-host parasite genetic diversity and higher prevalence are consistent with greater or repeated exposure and thus an increased likelihood of re-infection by genetically distinct strains. While the passage does not directly measure re-infection events, the observed pattern of higher diversity in males combined with behavioral context reasonably supports the inference that male patients are more likely to acquire multiple genetically distinct infections due to greater exposure to contaminated water.

361.Option: Because non-human primates are genetically similar to humans and share the same habitat, schistosomiasis control programs in Ethiopia should prioritize the treatment of both human and non-human primate populations to prevent zoonotic spillover.
Evidence: With close geographical and genetic proximity of many species of non-human primates (NHP) to humans, and given sufficient epidemiological opportunity, NHP can become infected with S. mansoni and may potentially exhibit similar clinical signs and symptoms [5] and would act as fully competent definitive hosts [6, 7].
