The specific claim that the primary role is immune evasion is not established by the provided data and thus cannot be confidently derived from the passage.

390.Option: The high frequency of HLA-A0201 expression in Latin American populations enables the identification of a broad range of T. cruzi-specific CD8+ T cell responses, making it the most effective strategy for studying immune responses in chronic Chagas disease.
Evidence: Altogether, a hierarchy of antigenicity was apparent with the A02 supertype peptides being the most frequently recognized in the Chagas disease population followed by the A03 and the A24 supertype peptides.
This analysis also allowed the identification of previously unknown ts epitopes that may be useful for the monitoring CD8+ T cell responses in Chagas disease subjects regardless of their HLA type.
The six most common HLA supertypes cover one or more HLA allele in >95 of a human population, irrespective of ethnicity [26],[27].
It is noteworthy that responses to HLA-A02 and HLA03 supertype-restricted epitopes covered 77% of the total responses detected, consistent with the high worldwide frequency of these alleles [26].
390.Rationale: The passage shows that A02-supertype peptides were the most frequently recognized in this Chagas cohort and that A02/A03 responses covered 77% of detected responses, noting the worldwide frequency of these alleles. However, the passage does not state that HLA-A0201 specifically is highly frequent in Latin American populations nor does it claim that targeting A0201 alone is the "most effective strategy" for studying immune responses. The text emphasizes using multiple supertypes to cover a broad population and explicitly notes utility "regardless of their HLA type." Therefore the stronger claim in the option (A0201 frequency in Latin America and it being the single most effective strategy) is not supported by the passage.

390.Option: The broad and multispecific CD8+ T cell response to T. cruzi in humans, compared to the focused response in mice, is primarily due to the greater genetic diversity of HLA alleles in human populations.
Evidence: Contrasting with the mouse system, a lower and more diffuse pattern of CD8+ T cell responses specific for HLA-A0201-restricted ts peptides in human subjects was found [25].

An additional complication of translating the results of studies in mice to the assay of human T cell responses is the large degree of polymorphism of human HLA alleles and the requirement to HLA-type each individual subject prior to attempting to measure T cell responses.

A number of parameters have been implicated in determining which pathogen epitopes are primarily targeted by T cell responses, including the nature of the restricting MHC allele, efficiency of epitope processing and translocation into the endoplasmatic reticulum, the degree of sequence variability in epitopes derived from highly variable pathogens and antigen availability by either cross-presentation of exogenous antigen or processing of intracellular proteins [9]–[11]. In addition, interactions among different T cell populations and cross-reactivity between “self” and/or “other” pathogen antigens, as well as the presence of antagonistic epitopes, may further impact immunodominance patterns [12].
390.Rationale: The passage indicates humans display a lower/more diffuse CD8+ T cell response compared to mice and notes that the large degree of polymorphism of human HLA alleles complicates translation from mice to humans. However, it explicitly lists multiple factors that influence epitope targeting (processing efficiency, antigen availability, cross-reactivity, antagonistic epitopes, etc.). The option claims the broader human response is "primarily" due to HLA genetic diversity; the passage does not provide evidence that HLA diversity is the primary cause rather than one of multiple contributing factors. Thus the asserted primary causal attribution is not supported by the text.

390.Option: The low prevalence of IL-2-secreting T cells specific for T. cruzi, despite the presence of functional IFN-γ-secreting T cells, suggests that chronic antigen persistence drives T cells toward a monofunctional, exhausted phenotype with impaired proliferative capacity.
Evidence: The simultaneous assessment of IFN-γ and IL-2 production after stimulation with ts-derived peptides, demonstrates that the CD8+ T cell compartment specific for T. cruzi displays a very low level of polyfunctional cytokine responses with a predominance of T cells secreting IFN-γ alone and very low prevalence of single IL-2-secreting or dual IFN-γ/IL-2 secreting T cells.

Positive IL-2 responses were very low irrespective of the clinical status of the subjects, and was essentially undetectable in the G1 group upon stimulation with ts peptides (mean net SFCs±SD/106 PBMC in subjects with positive IL-2 ELISPOT responses = 28±8, range = 15–55; mean SFCs±SD/106 PBMC in media alone = 6±5), [Fig. 2A]. Less than 30% of the individuals evaluated displayed positive IL-2 and IFN-γ ELISPOT responses while IL-2 responses alone was not detected (Fig. 2A).

This functional pattern of cytokine production in response to HLA-class I-restricted ts epitopes in conjunction with the negative correlation between disease severity and IFN-γ-secreting T cells, found in the present and former studies [7], as well the increased levels of fully differentiated T cells in these patients [8] supports the notion that the long-term persistence of T. cruzi infection may lead to a degradation of parasite-specific T cell responses.

This apparent impairment in CD8+ T cell responses was associated with an increased frequency of fully differentiated memory (CD27−CD28−CD45RA−) CD8+ T cells and a high rate of apoptosis, possibly reflecting exhaustion in the CD8+ T cell compartment [8].
390.Rationale: The passage documents a predominance of IFN-γ-only (monofunctional) responses with very low IL-2 or dual IFN-γ/IL-2 secretion, and explicitly links this functional pattern, together with increased fully differentiated memory phenotype and apoptosis, to the notion that long-term persistence of T. cruzi leads to degradation/exhaustion of parasite-specific T cells. Impaired IL-2 production is consistent with reduced proliferative capacity. Therefore the inference that chronic antigen persistence drives T cells toward a monofunctional, exhausted phenotype with impaired proliferative capacity is supported by the presented data and interpretations.

390.Option: The impaired IL-2 production in T. cruzi-specific T cells is a direct result of heart failure in advanced Chagas disease, rather than a consequence of chronic immune activation.
Evidence: Positive IL-2 responses were very low irrespective of the clinical status of the subjects, and was essentially undetectable in the G1 group upon stimulation with ts peptides (mean net SFCs±SD/106 PBMC in subjects with positive IL-2 ELISPOT responses = 28±8, range = 15–55; mean SFCs±SD/106 PBMC in media alone = 6±5), [Fig. 2A].
Group 3 (G3, n = 4) seropositive patients had ECG abnormalities, heart enlargement and clinical or radiologic evidence of heart failure.

This functional pattern of cytokine production in response to HLA-class I-restricted ts epitopes in conjunction with the negative correlation between disease severity and IFN-γ-secreting T cells, found in the present and former studies [7], as well the increased levels of fully differentiated T cells in these patients [8] supports the notion that the long-term persistence of T. cruzi infection may lead to a degradation of parasite-specific T cell responses.
390.Rationale: The passage reports low IL-2 responses "irrespective of the clinical status of the subjects," indicating impaired IL-2 secretion is not confined to or caused only by patients with advanced cardiac disease. The authors instead interpret the pattern as linked to long-term antigen persistence and degradation/exhaustion of parasite-specific T cells. There is no direct evidence in the passage that heart failure causally produces impaired IL-2; thus the option's claim that IL-2 impairment is a direct result of heart failure rather than chronic immune activation is not supported.

390.Option: The presence of IFN-γ-secreting T cells in chronic Chagas disease patients indicates that the immune system is effectively controlling parasite replication, preventing further tissue damage.
Evidence: Even in cases in which such responses are stimulated sufficiently to control the acute infection, T. cruzi is not completely cleared but instead, persists in infected hosts for decades. The inflammatory responses that such persistence induces may eventually result in the tissue damage that is associated with chronic Chagas disease [5],[6].

we have shown that chronic chagasic subjects with no or mild clinical disease have significantly higher frequency of interferon-gamma (IFN-γ) producing T cells specific for T. cruzi than do individuals with more severe disease [7],[8].

This functional pattern of cytokine production in response to HLA-class I-restricted ts epitopes in conjunction with the negative correlation between disease severity and IFN-γ-secreting T cells, found in the present and former studies [7], as well the increased levels of fully differentiated T cells in these patients [8] supports the notion that the long-term persistence of T. cruzi infection may lead to a degradation of parasite-specific T cell responses.
390.Rationale: While the passage notes IFN-γ-producing T cells are present and are more frequent in subjects with less severe disease, it also clearly states that even with immune responses sufficient to control acute infection, T. cruzi persists for decades and persistence can induce inflammatory tissue damage. The presence of IFN-γ responses does not equate to effective clearance or guarantee prevention of further tissue damage. Therefore concluding that IFN-γ-secreting T cells indicate effective control preventing further damage is not supported by the passage.

391.Option: The temporal pattern of dengue in Port Sudan showed a bi-cyclic trend, with two transmission peaks: a short peak in winter (November–December) and a longer peak in summer (June–August), both preceded by peaks in mosquito density.
Evidence: The M-shaped curves have two peaks. The first peak of P/P (0.51) occured in November followed by a serological peak of IgM in December (7.89%).
The second and largest peak of IgM was in July (8.14%). It was preceded by a higher peak of P/P two months earlier in May (0.66) and by the highest peak of larval ration in April (1.81).
Both the very low P/P in August (0.04) and disappearance of dengue vector in September resulted in zero IgM seropositivity in September and October.
391.Rationale: The passage clearly supports a bi-phasic (M-shaped) pattern with two serological peaks (December and July) and indicates that each serological peak was preceded by increases in pupal/demographic (P/P) indices (November preceding December, May preceding July). Therefore the core claim of two peaks both preceded by mosquito-density peaks is supported. However, the option's assertion that the summer peak is a "longer peak in summer (June–August)" is not supported by the passage: the documented serological peak is concentrated in July, P/P is very low in August, and the vector disappears in September. The passage does not provide evidence that the summer peak spans June–August or is longer in duration than the winter peak, so the specific characterization of duration (June–August and "longer") cannot be derived from the text.

391.Option: The low incidence rate in lower and middle class neighborhoods suggests that these areas have higher herd immunity due to earlier exposure to dengue.
Evidence: BI, HI and P/P indices were lower in the upper class neighborhoods (65, 51 and .24), as compared to the other two strata (91, 66 and .38, respectively).
A significant difference between the high stratum and the other two strata was shown on incidence rates (F = 73.97, d.f. = 2, p<0.001), 0.0031 versus 0.0036 each, respectively.
391.Rationale: The passage does not report a low incidence rate in lower and middle class neighborhoods; in fact it states that incidence rates were significantly different between the high stratum and the other two strata, with the high stratum having an incidence of 0.0031 versus 0.0036 in the other strata (i.e. the lower/middle strata had equal or slightly higher incidence). The option's inference that lower/middle areas have lower incidence and that this reflects higher herd immunity from earlier exposure is not supported by the provided data. The passage does not present serological evidence of higher past exposure (e.g., IgG) in these neighborhoods nor any direct measure of herd immunity, so the causal claim about herd immunity cannot be derived from the text.

391.Option: The disappearance of the dengue vector (Ae. aegypti) in September demands further research to define whether there is a true disappearance and if so how and from where the vector is reintroduced.
Evidence: The dengue vector was found throughout the year in Port Sudan, except in September when no larvae or pupae Ae. aegypti was found in the inspected water containers.
It is possible that Ae. aegypti dwells in different breeding containers during September and/or there are desiccated eggs of the mosquito in the breeding containers.
Both the very low P/P in August (0.04) and disappearance of dengue vector in September resulted in zero IgM seropositivity in September and October.
391.Rationale: The passage documents an absence of detectable Ae. aegypti larvae/pupae in September and offers possible (but unconfirmed) explanations such as dormancy in different containers or desiccated eggs. This observation and the stated uncertainty justify an inference that further research is warranted to determine whether there is a true disappearance and, if so, the mechanisms or source(s) of reintroduction. The option's call for further study is therefore directly supported by the authors' own observations and uncertainty.

391.Option: The bi-cyclic transmission pattern in Port Sudan is driven primarily by seasonal monsoon winds, which facilitate the dispersal of Ae. aegypti from neighboring countries.
Evidence: Meteorological parameters (minimum and maximum temperature, relative humidity, precipitation and, evaporation rates, wind speed and direction) were recorded on daily basis in two stations in Port Sudan.
There is a significant correlation between P/P index and IgM sero-prevalence in the next month (Pearson r = 0.71, n = 11, p = 0.015).

The negative association between the minimum temperature and P/P index was significant (Pearson correlation = −0.83, n = 11, p = 0.027). Similarly, the maximum temperature had a negative correlation with HI (Pearson correlation = −0.65, n = 11, p = 0.032).
391.Rationale: The passage does not present any evidence that seasonal monsoon winds drive the bi-cyclic pattern or that wind-mediated dispersal from neighboring countries is responsible. Instead, the authors present correlations implicating mosquito density (P/P) and associations with temperature and humidity in explaining temporal patterns. Wind speed/direction were recorded as meteorological parameters, but no results or analyses are provided linking monsoon winds to transmission peaks or to cross-border dispersal of Ae. aegypti. Therefore the specific causal claim that monsoon winds are the primary driver is not supported by the presented data.

391.Option: The high seroprevalence of IgM in July (8.14%) was likely due to secondary dengue infection in adults aged 18–39, who are more vulnerable to severe disease.
Evidence: The second and largest peak of IgM was in July (8.14%).
Among these age groups, higher dengue attack rates were shown in adults aged 18–39 and above 40 years, 2.14% (17/791) and 1.77% (14/791), respectively.
Kits of enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) were used to detect dengue-specific IgM antibodies in all samples according to the manufacturer's instructions (PanBio, Brisbane, Australia).

During surveys, residents with symptoms of dengue (i.e. high fever and two of the following criteria: rash, severe headache, severe eye pain, joint pain, and muscle and/or bone pain) were referred to the hospital but excluded from the sample size.
391.Rationale: While the passage documents a July IgM peak and notes higher attack rates in adults aged 18–39, it does not provide any data to identify infections as secondary (which typically requires IgG or seroconversion paired samples or specific serological testing) nor does it present evidence that adults 18–39 had more secondary infections or greater vulnerability to severe disease. The study measured IgM only, and symptomatic individuals were referred and excluded from the serosurvey, limiting inference about severity. Therefore the specific claim that the July IgM peak was likely due to secondary infections in 18–39-year-olds and linked to greater severity is not supported by the passage.

392.Option: Visiting workers in Al Ahsa exhibit a stronger immune response to sandfly saliva because they are more likely to have been previously infected with L. major.
Evidence: In Al Ahsa, we found that non-local patients (visiting labour) had significantly higher levels (P<0.001) of anti-PpSP32 compared to the local residents (Fig. 5A).
Interestingly, nearly three quarters of the non-local patients developed more than three lesions compared to only 40% in the local group (Fig. 5B).
The presence of IgG antibodies against Ph. papatasi saliva has been associated with a higher risk of being infected with L. major [4,6].
392.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that visiting workers have higher anti-PpSP32 antibody levels and more lesions, and that anti-saliva IgG is associated with higher infection risk. However, it does not state or provide evidence that the visiting workers' stronger antibody responses are due to prior (pre-arrival) infections with L. major. The data show higher antibody levels (indicating greater exposure) and more lesions among non-locals, but do not establish that these reflect previous infections acquired before arrival rather than recent exposure after arrival. Therefore the causal claim (because they are more likely to have been previously infected) is not supported by the passage.

392.Option: The transient nature of the antibody response to sandfly bites allows for the detection of recent exposure but does not reflect long-term risk of leishmaniasis infection.
Evidence: Repeated exposure to sandfly bites produces antibodies against its salivary components in the host, providing an indirect measure of exposure to vectors [5].
The transient nature of the antibody response to sandfly bites [6–10] allows for the study of temporal changes in transmission risk and the efficacy of vector control programmes [11].
The presence of IgG antibodies against Ph. papatasi saliva has been associated with a higher risk of being infected with L. major [4,6].
392.Rationale: The passage explicitly describes anti-saliva antibodies as an indirect measure of exposure and states their response is transient, which allows study of temporal (recent) changes in transmission. From these statements it is epidemiologically reasonable to infer that the marker detects recent exposure rather than serving as an indicator of long-term exposure or long-term risk. While the passage also notes an association between saliva antibodies and infection risk, the transient nature implies limited utility for reflecting long-term risk.

392.Option: The higher antibody response to sandfly saliva in visiting workers compared to long-term residents in Al Ahsa suggests that the migrant population is highly exposed to sandfly bites and less immune to cutaneous leishmaniasis, while residents may have developed tolerance due to long-term exposure.
Evidence: In Al Ahsa, we found that non-local patients (visiting labour) had significantly higher levels (P<0.001) of anti-PpSP32 compared to the local residents (Fig. 5A).
Interestingly, nearly three quarters of the non-local patients developed more than three lesions compared to only 40% in the local group (Fig. 5B).

Although such differences did not correlate with the levels of anti-PpSP32 (S3 Fig.), patients from the visiting labour group presented in general a higher number of lesions compared to the residents (S2 Table).
Repeated exposure to sandfly bites produces antibodies against its salivary components in the host, providing an indirect measure of exposure to vectors [5].
392.Rationale: The passage supports that visiting workers have higher anti-PpSP32 levels (indicating higher exposure) and have more lesions. However, the additional claims that migrants are 'less immune' and that residents have developed 'tolerance' from long-term exposure are not supported by the presented data. The authors explicitly note the difference in lesion number did not correlate with anti-PpSP32 levels, and no immunologic or longitudinal evidence of tolerance or relative immunity by residents is provided. Thus the suggested mechanism (less immunity in migrants and tolerance in residents) is speculative and not derivable from the passage.

392.Option: The significantly higher number of lesions in visiting workers compared to local residents in Al Ahsa is primarily due to genetic susceptibility differences among the migrant population.
Evidence: Interestingly, nearly three quarters of the non-local patients developed more than three lesions compared to only 40% in the local group (Fig. 5B).

Although such differences did not correlate with the levels of anti-PpSP32 (S3 Fig.), patients from the visiting labour group presented in general a higher number of lesions compared to the residents (S2 Table).
392.Rationale: While the passage documents a higher lesion count among visiting workers, it provides no data or discussion regarding genetic susceptibility or any genetic analyses. The attribution of the lesion difference 'primarily' to genetic differences is not supported by any evidence in the passage and is speculative. Other explanations (e.g., differential exposure, timing of infection, access to care) are possible but not tested here; therefore the genetic causation claim cannot be derived from the passage.

393.Option: Elevated levels of IL-6 in severe visceral leishmaniasis are primarily driven by concurrent bacterial infections, as indicated by high serum levels of LPS and FABP2.
Evidence: IL-6 levels were more significantly elevated in the patients with severe VL (Fig 3A; p = 0.03).
Severe VL patients that died had IL-6 serum levels of 11,443 ± 15,388 pg/ml, whereas the surviving severe VL patients had 2,848 ± 7,512 pg/ml (Fig 3B; p = 0.01).
All of the patients that died had circulating IL-6 levels ≥ 200 pg/ml, while only 2 patients that survived (1 SVL, 1 classical VL) presented with IL-6 levels above this threshold.
LPS and FABP2 levels were similar in the VL D0, DTH+ and HC groups and were not changed after treatment (VL D30) (Fig 4A and 4B).
393.Rationale: The passage shows IL-6 is elevated in severe VL and in those who died, but it explicitly states that LPS and FABP2 levels were similar across groups and did not change with treatment. Because LPS and FABP2 (markers of bacterial translocation/injury) are not elevated, the passage does not support the claim that elevated IL-6 in severe VL is primarily driven by concurrent bacterial infections indicated by high LPS/FABP2. Therefore the causal statement is not supported by the provided data.

393.Option: IL-6 levels are elevated in severe visceral leishmaniasis due to a failure in the regulation of IFN-γ production, which leads to uncontrolled inflammation and tissue damage.
Evidence: classical VL patients present an impairment of IL-2, IFN-γ and IL-12 production from T cells specific to leishmania antigens in vitro, that is restored after successful treatment [6–14].
patients with active VL have high levels of IFN-γ and IL-12 in their serum, which theoretically could help to control the infection.
High levels of IFN-γ, IL-10, IL-6, IL-27 and TNF-α cytokines were observed in sera of VL patients before treatment when compared with healthy control and DTH+ individuals (Fig 1).
393.Rationale: The passage documents both high serum IFN-γ in active VL and impaired antigen-specific T cell production of IFN-γ in vitro, and it documents elevated IL-6 in severe disease. However, it does not provide evidence that elevated IL-6 results from a failure to regulate IFN-γ production, nor that such a mechanism causes uncontrolled inflammation and tissue damage. The authors do not present causal data linking dysregulated IFN-γ to IL-6 elevation; therefore this causal inference cannot be derived from the passage.

393.Option: The presence of sCD14 in serum is a reliable biomarker for bacterial co-infection in visceral leishmaniasis patients, as it is elevated during active disease and decreases after treatment.
Evidence: sCD14 and MIF, molecules associated with TLR recognition, were higher in the active disease group (2,882 ng/ml and 39 ng/ml, respectively) than DTH+ group (1,348 ng/ml and 19 ng/ml, respectively). Furthermore, circulating levels of sCD14 and MIF decreased with treatment (1,820 ng/ml and 17 ng/ml, respectively) (Fig 4C and 4D).
LPS and FABP2 levels were similar in the VL D0, DTH+ and HC groups and were not changed after treatment (VL D30) (Fig 4A and 4B).
These data suggest that sCD14 could directly participate in modulating the immune response against L. infantum.
sCD14 levels were not, however, associated with disease severity/ mortality.
393.Rationale: While sCD14 is elevated during active disease and declines after treatment, the passage does not establish sCD14 as a reliable biomarker of bacterial co-infection. LPS (a direct marker of bacterial translocation) and FABP2 were not elevated, and the authors propose sCD14 may participate in modulating the immune response to L. infantum. Additionally, sCD14 was not associated with disease severity or mortality. Therefore the statement that sCD14 is a reliable biomarker for bacterial co-infection is not supported by the presented data.

393.Option: The systemic release of cytokines, particularly IL-6, is associated with disease severity and mortality in visceral leishmaniasis, independent of bacterial co-infection.
Evidence: IL-6 levels were more significantly elevated in the patients with severe VL (Fig 3A; p = 0.03).
Severe VL patients that died had IL-6 serum levels of 11,443 ± 15,388 pg/ml, whereas the surviving severe VL patients had 2,848 ± 7,512 pg/ml (Fig 3B; p = 0.01).
All of the patients that died had circulating IL-6 levels ≥ 200 pg/ml, while only 2 patients that survived (1 SVL, 1 classical VL) presented with IL-6 levels above this threshold.
LPS and FABP2 levels were similar in the VL D0, DTH+ and HC groups and were not changed after treatment (VL D30) (Fig 4A and 4B).
sCD14 levels were not, however, associated with disease severity/ mortality.
393.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence that IL-6 levels are higher in severe VL and markedly higher among patients who died, indicating an association between systemic IL-6 release and severity/mortality. At the same time, commonly used serum markers of bacterial translocation/injury (LPS and FABP2) were not elevated, and sCD14—though elevated in active disease—was not associated with severity or mortality. These findings support the inference that cytokine release, particularly IL-6, is associated with severity and mortality in VL independent of evidence for bacterial co-infection.

393.Option: The strong correlation between IL-27, IL-10, IL-6, and TNF-α suggests that these cytokines act in a synergistic cascade to drive the progression of visceral leishmaniasis.
Evidence: We observed a strong correlation between IL-27 and IL-6 (r = 0.69), IL-10 (r = 0.85) and TNF-α (r = 0.86) (Fig 2).
The levels of particular circulating cytokines during the inflammatory processes observed in active VL disease could be a determinant, or arise as a consequence, of disease severity.
393.Rationale: The passage documents strong correlations among IL-27, IL-10, IL-6, and TNF-α, but correlation does not establish causation or a mechanistic 'synergistic cascade' driving disease progression. The authors explicitly note that these cytokine levels "could be a determinant, or arise as a consequence, of disease severity," indicating uncertainty about causal direction. Therefore asserting they act in a synergistic cascade to drive progression is not supported by the data presented.

394.Option: The reproduction number in Chile fluctuated around 1.0 from early August 2020 onward, indicating that the epidemic was in a state of equilibrium with no net growth or decline in transmission.
Evidence: This was followed by a decline in the reproduction number to less than 1.0 between June 19th-August 9th, 2020.
Since then, the reproduction number has fluctuated around 1.0 with the most recent estimate of R ~ 0.96 (95% CrI: 0.95, 0.98) (Fig 5).
The most recent estimate of R~ 0.96 (95% CrI: 0.95, 0.98) as of November 2nd, 2020.
Estimates of effective R indicate if the disease transmission continues (R>1) or if the active disease transmission ceases (R<1). Therefore, in order to contain an outbreak, we need to maintain R<1.
394.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that after a period with R<1 (June 19-August 9) "since then" R "has fluctuated around 1.0" with a most recent estimate ~0.96. Epidemiologically, R near 1.0 indicates roughly stable transmission (no sustained exponential growth or decline). The passage also defines the interpretation of R>1 and R<1, so interpreting R fluctuating around 1.0 as a state of near-equilibrium (no net growth or decline) is supported by the provided text.

394.Option: The reopening of society in late April 2020 under the 'Safe Return' plan led to a sustained increase in case incidence, which was not mitigated by subsequent localized lockdowns in Santiago and Valparaíso.
Evidence: the government started to ease the COVID-19 restrictions in late April by reopening the economy under the “Safe Return” plan, including the televised opening of some businesses and stores, as new infections had reduced between 350–500 per day by the end of April, implying an only apparent flattening of the COVID-19 curve [33–35].
imposing and lifting lockdowns in small geographical areas (municipalities) proved unsuccessful in areas with high interdependencies such as the Greater Santiago [36].

This strategy resulted in a new wave of infections; with the virus spreading from more affluent areas of Chile to more impoverished, crowded communities, forcing the government to reimpose lockdown measures in Santiago in mid-May (Fig 1) [23,37,38].
The case counts continued to increase, averaging ~4943 cases per day in June 2020, and started to decline thereafter.
394.Rationale: The passage supports that reopening under the 'Safe Return' plan was followed by a new wave of infections and that localized municipal-level lockdowns were initially unsuccessful in highly interconnected areas (e.g., Greater Santiago). However, the statement asserts that the increase was "sustained" and "not mitigated" by subsequent localized lockdowns in Santiago and Valparaíso. The passage reports that case counts "started to decline" after the June peak and that larger-scale measures (e.g., greater Santiago lockdown, later measures) were implemented; thus the claim that localized lockdowns never mitigated the increase is too strong and not fully supported by the text. The Passage shows initial localized measures were insufficient, but later measures coincided with declines.

394.Option: The initial sub-exponential growth pattern in Chile (p ~0.8) was primarily due to the country’s high population density and urbanization, which limited the effectiveness of early interventions.
Evidence: The incidence curve displays sub-exponential growth dynamics with the scaling of growth parameter (deceleration of growth parameter), p, estimated at 0.77 (95% CI: 0.73, 0.81) and the intrinsic growth rate, r, estimated at 0.81 (95% CI: 0.67, 1.00).
These initial containment strategies kept the COVID-19 case counts lower than regional peers; Brazil, Peru, and Ecuador until the end of April 2020.
Several socioeconomic, demographic, and political factors make control of the pandemic in Latin America particularly challenging [22-25].
394.Rationale: While the passage documents a sub-exponential growth pattern (p ~0.77) and mentions socioeconomic, demographic and political challenges in the region, it does not attribute the sub-exponential growth primarily to high population density or urbanization, nor does it state that these factors limited early intervention effectiveness. The causal attribution to population density/urbanization is not present in the Passage and therefore cannot be derived solely from the provided text.

394.Option: The implementation of lockdowns and apt social distancing interventions have indeed slowed the spread of the virus.
Evidence: The 20-day ahead forecast suggested that the early social distancing measures significantly slowed down the early spread of the virus in Chile, whose effect is noticeable about two weeks after implementing an intervention, as shown in Fig 6.

We generated a 20-day ahead forecast based on the daily incidence curve from May 9th-July 7th, 2020.... The 20-day ahead average forecast utilizing the GLM model showed that Chile could accumulate ~45,160 cases (95% CI: 27,934–67,600) between July 8th-July 27th, 2020 (Fig 7). Our forecast results approximate closely the ~46,798 cases reported between July 8th-July 27th, 2020 by the Ministry of Health, Chile.
The reproduction number was estimated at 0.87 (95% CI: 0.84, 0.89) as of July 7th, 2020.
394.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports modeling results and forecasts concluding that early social distancing measures "significantly slowed down the early spread of the virus" with effects noticeable about two weeks after interventions. Additionally, model-based estimates show the reproduction number decreased (e.g., R = 0.87 by July 7), supporting the inference that implemented interventions and lockdowns reduced transmission. These statements directly support the option.

394.Option: The high positivity rate at the beginning of the epidemic indicates that the government failed to cast a wide enough net to test the masses early in the pandemic, and there were probably many more active cases than those detected by epidemiological surveillance, underestimating the epidemic growth curve.
Evidence: Daily testing and positivity rates for the time period April 9th–November 2nd, 2020, by the reporting date are shown in Fig 8.
The total number of tests performed for this time period were 4,325,617, amongst which 10.9% (47,597) had positive results.
The average number of daily tests was estimated at ~5,460 for April 2020 and ~12,959 for May 2020, a 137% increase.

The testing rate in Chile further increased in June 2020, testing on average ~17,578 individuals per day,... The positivity rate (percentage of positive tests among the total number of tests) has fluctuated from a monthly average of ~9.07% (SD: 2.3) in April 2020 to a monthly average of ~4.87% (SD: 0.65) in October 2020.
394.Rationale: The passage provides empirical testing and positivity data (higher positivity in April that declined over time, and expanding testing capacity). However, it does not explicitly state that the government "failed to cast a wide enough net" early on or quantify the degree of under-ascertainment. While high positivity rates can be consistent with limited testing and under-detection, the Passage does not make that causal claim directly. Therefore the stronger assertion about government failure and probable many more active cases than detected is not strictly derivable from the provided text alone.

395.Option: The level of knowledge about rabies and its control measures is directly associated with the uptake of the 2012 vaccination campaign, indicating that educational campaigns should be prioritized to improve vaccination rates.
Evidence: The vaccination uptake was significantly associated with the knowledge of the dog owners about rabies (p<0.001) and its control measures (p<0.05) (Table 6).
The proportion of dog owners who had vaccinated dogs was higher for those who considered rabies a fatal disease (54%; 225/415) than for those who did not (26%; 9/35).
Similarly, dog owners with a high level of knowledge of rabies control measures (59%; 161/272) tended to vaccinate their dogs compared to their counterparts (46%; 60/131).

Of the 13 independent variables that had an association (p-value less than 0.25) with the uptake of the 2012 vaccination campaign in the univariable analyses (Table 1 and Table 6), only five variables were retained in the final multivariable model. Regency, having female dogs for breeding, economic value of dogs, income of dog owners, and accessibility of the village were significantly associated with the uptake of vaccination (Table 7).
395.Rationale: While the passage clearly shows a univariable association between knowledge and vaccination uptake (significant p-values and higher proportions among knowledgeable owners), the multivariable analysis did not retain knowledge as an independent predictor of 2012 vaccination uptake. Instead, regency, dog sex/use, dog economic value, owner income, and village accessibility were the independent predictors in the final model. Thus, the statement asserting that knowledge "most strongly" influences uptake and therefore educational campaigns should be prioritized as the primary strategy is not fully supported by the multivariable evidence in the passage. The passage supports that knowledge is associated with uptake but does not support that it is the principal or strongest determinant after adjustment for other factors.

395.Option: Geographical accessibility is one of the important predictors of vaccination uptake among dog owners, and targeted interventions in villages with poor accessibility may increase vaccination uptake in the future.
Evidence: The odds of having a high level of knowledge of rabies control measures was higher in the following situations: ... for dog owners who lived in villages with good accessibility (OR = 2.14; 95% CI = 1.07–4.27) compared to poor accessibility...

The odds... of vaccination uptake was higher if the dog owners ... lived in a village with a good accessibility (OR = 3.84; 95%CI = 1.92–7.67) compared with those ... who lived in a village with poor accessibility.

Villages located along the provincial road connecting West to East Flores Island were categorized as villages with ‘good accessibility' ... Villages located more than 3 km from the main road ... were categorized as villages with ‘average accessibility'. Villages that can only be reached by foot or motorcycle ... were categorized as villages with ‘poor accessibility'.
395.Rationale: The passage provides direct multivariable evidence that living in a village with good accessibility substantially increases the odds of vaccinating dogs (OR = 3.84). The definition of accessibility categories is provided, and accessibility remained an independent predictor in the final model. From an epidemiological perspective, it is therefore reasonable to infer that targeted efforts to reach villages with poor accessibility (e.g., improved outreach, adapted logistics) could increase vaccination uptake in those areas.

395.Option: The low vaccination coverage in Flores Island is primarily due to the lack of trust in the vaccine's efficacy, as many dog owners expressed skepticism about its effectiveness.
Evidence: For those dog owners who had not vaccinated their dogs (216 of 450 dog owners), the most important reasons for not joining were the lack of information about the schedule of the vaccination campaign (40%) and the difficulty to catch their dogs during the vaccination campaign (37%).
Other reasons, of minor importance, included the lack of belief in the vaccine efficacy (13%), and the young age of the dog at the time of the vaccination campaign (6%).
The uptake rate of the dog vaccination measure has been low, with an average vaccination coverage of around 53% of the registered dogs during the 2000–2011 vaccination campaigns.
395.Rationale: The passage lists the main reasons for nonparticipation, with lack of information (40%) and inability to catch dogs (37%) being the top reasons. Lack of belief in vaccine efficacy accounted for only 13% and is explicitly described as a reason of minor importance. Therefore, the claim that skepticism about vaccine efficacy is the primary cause of low coverage is not supported by the survey data in the passage.

395.Option: The high uptake of post-exposure treatment among dog-bite victims indicates that the public health system in Flores Island is well-equipped to manage rabies exposure and prevent fatalities.
Evidence: Of these 89 bite cases, 75 (84%) cleaned the wound, and 50 (56%) received vaccination.
Of the 450 respondents interviewed, 89 (20%) reported that at least one of their family members had been bitten by a suspected rabid dog during the last fourteen years.
Until 2012, 96 human cases of rabies were officially registered by the Public Health Department...
395.Rationale: The passage provides specific uptake proportions for post-exposure measures: wound cleaning was performed in 84% of reported bite cases, but only 56% received vaccination. The statement overgeneralizes from these figures to conclude the public health system is well-equipped to manage exposure and prevent fatalities. The passage does not provide data on timeliness, completeness of post-exposure prophylaxis, coverage of rabies immunoglobulin, clinical outcomes, or capacity to prevent fatalities. Moreover, continued occurrence of human rabies cases (96 registered until 2012, with likely underreporting) argues against concluding the system is fully well-equipped. Thus, this policy-consequence inference is not supported by the available data.

395.Option: The culling of roaming dogs in Manggarai Regency has been effective in reducing rabies transmission and should be expanded to other regencies as a primary control strategy.
Evidence: Manggarai has been applying additional control measures (such as culling) alongside the nationally recommended vaccination control campaign.

Given the number of dogs registered during this vaccination campaign, the number of dogs is estimated at 37,000 in Sikka and 6,675 in Manggarai. The difference in the number of dogs per regency is a result of the culling measures that were implemented in Manggarai.
In total, 33% of the dog owners reported that at least one of their dogs had been culled during the last fourteen years.
The proportion of dog owners that had culled their dogs was significantly higher in Manggarai (45%) than in Sikka (27%) (p<0.001).
However, this campaign has not yet been successful in eliminating rabies from Flores Island.
395.Rationale: While the passage documents that culling was practiced more in Manggarai and that its dog population is smaller (attributed to culling), it provides no evidence that culling reduced rabies transmission or human cases. The passage explicitly notes that the overall campaign has not yet eliminated rabies from Flores Island. There is therefore no basis in the presented data to conclude that culling has been effective and should be expanded as a primary control strategy.

396.Option: The spatial distribution of dengue cases in Peru is primarily driven by human movement and population density, with weather patterns playing a secondary role in determining transmission intensity.
Evidence: We conclude that a coupling of temperature-humidity is an important and sensitive determinant of DENV transmission-potential regulating the location, timing and intensity of risk.

Out of more than 2 trillion classification tests performed to fully explore Peruvian weather-space, only one set of weather inputs achieved a classification of districts as dengue-positive or negative with 95% sensitivity and specificity.

With 30 million people in Peru, the largest population centers are mostly clustered along the western coast. Highest dengue incidence rates developed in a different spatial pattern across northeastern Peru.
396.Rationale: The passage explicitly identifies the temperature-humidity coupling as the primary determinant of transmission potential, timing, and intensity, and demonstrates that specific weather thresholds classify transmission presence/absence with high sensitivity and specificity. The text also notes that population centers are clustered on the coast while highest incidence occurred in a different (northeastern) spatial pattern, which contradicts the claim that population density and human movement are the primary drivers. Thus the statement that spatial distribution is primarily driven by human movement/population density with weather secondary is not supported by the passage.

396.Option: The coupling of temperature and humidity is a critical determinant of dengue transmission potential, with higher humidity levels amplifying transmission magnitude when temperature is optimal, and transmission intensity increasing exponentially with longer duration in optimal weather-space.
Evidence: We conclude that a coupling of temperature-humidity is an important and sensitive determinant of DENV transmission-potential regulating the location, timing and intensity of risk.
Mean humidity in conjunction with temperature was necessary for differentiating groups by transmission intensity, indicating a coupling of temperature-humidity in the dynamics of large epidemics.

High dengue impact was concentrated in a small area of weather-space above 25°C mean temperature and above 75% mean humidity. Impact was highest as humidity increased above 80% when mean temperature was 25–29°C.
For mean temperature >25°C, as humidity rises from 60% to 80%, incidence rates accelerate in highest transmission areas, p<0.00001.

The natural log of cumulative incidence rate was used because the increase in incidence as temperatures rise within the optimal range and as humidity rises amidst temperatures optimal for transmission is exponential in nature.
Annual duration above 22°C was the best predictor of incidence magnitude in lower incidence districts; duration above 25°C was the best predictor in higher incidence districts.
396.Rationale: The passage repeatedly states that temperature-humidity coupling is a key regulator of transmission potential and intensity. It provides empirical findings that high impact concentrates where mean temperature >25°C and mean humidity >75–80%, that mean humidity plus temperature was necessary to distinguish high transmission groups, and that longer annual duration in optimal conditions predicts exponentially higher incidence (log-scale used because the increase is exponential). These statements directly support the option.

396.Option: The relationship between weather and dengue transmission is linear and predictable, with each degree increase in temperature directly proportional to a corresponding increase in case counts.
Evidence: Weather-dengue dynamics are complex, multi-factorial, and non-linear and traditional statistical methods have produced diverse and conflicting perspectives regarding the weather-disease connection [3–5,7,8,11–18].

The natural log of cumulative incidence rate was used because the increase in incidence as temperatures rise within the optimal range and as humidity rises amidst temperatures optimal for transmission is exponential in nature.
A slight temperature change, for example, can impact life cycle dynamics, adult vector survival, or extrinsic incubation period in different ways [21–37].
396.Rationale: The passage explicitly characterizes the weather–dengue relationship as non-linear and complex, and notes increases in incidence with temperature/humidity are exponential in optimal ranges rather than linear proportionality. Therefore the claim of a simple linear one-degree-to-case-count proportional relationship is contradicted by the text and cannot be derived from the passage.

396.Option: Rainfall is a more significant predictor of dengue transmission in Peru than temperature and humidity, as it directly influences mosquito breeding sites and seasonal onset of epidemics.
Evidence: Temperature and humidity are emerging as weather components with greatest impact on DENV transmission, yet the separate and combined effects of this coupling across a range of ecological conditions remain ill-defined [3,4,7,11].

Out of more than 2 trillion classification tests performed to fully explore Peruvian weather-space, only one set of weather inputs achieved a classification of districts as dengue-positive or negative with 95% sensitivity and specificity. The combined criteria: mean temperature >22°C for 7 or more weeks per year and minimum temperature >14°C for 33 or more weeks per year provided a classification of districts with 95% sensitivity and 95% specificity, and overall 5% misclassification rate.
Mean humidity in conjunction with temperature was necessary for differentiating groups by transmission intensity, indicating a coupling of temperature-humidity in the dynamics of large epidemics.
396.Rationale: The passage focuses on temperature and humidity as the primary weather components influencing transmission and provides empirical classification results based on temperature (and humidity for intensity). Rainfall is not presented or analyzed as a more significant predictor in this study. Thus the claim that rainfall is more significant than temperature/humidity is not supported by the passage.

396.Option: The largest dengue epidemics in Peru occurred in areas with the highest population density, indicating that human population size is the primary determinant of epidemic intensity regardless of weather conditions.
Evidence: With 30 million people in Peru, the largest population centers are mostly clustered along the western coast. Highest dengue incidence rates developed in a different spatial pattern across northeastern Peru.
Together these areas represent 11% of the Peruvian population and 70% of cases reported during 1994–2012.
We conclude that a coupling of temperature-humidity is an important and sensitive determinant of DENV transmission-potential regulating the location, timing and intensity of risk.
High dengue impact was concentrated in a small area of weather-space above 25°C mean temperature and above 75% mean humidity.
396.Rationale: Although some high-incidence areas have sizeable populations and the passage notes that 11% of the population accounted for 70% of cases, the authors emphasize weather (temperature-humidity coupling and duration in optimal weather-space) as the determinant of transmission potential and intensity. They also note that largest population centers are on the coast while highest incidence developed in a different (northeastern) spatial pattern, undermining the claim that population size alone is the primary determinant regardless of weather. Therefore the option is not supported by the passage.

397.Option: The structural similarity of Ov-TSP-1 to Sm-TSP-2 from S. mansoni implies that Ov-tsp-1 is essential for tegument formation in both species due to conserved function.
Evidence: Sm-TSP-2 is a tetraspanin from the tegument of S. mansoni [15], [16] that is essential for proper tegument formation, and silencing of the Sm-tsp-2 gene proved lethal for schistosomula in vivo [17].

Ov-TSP-1 showed the conserved characteristics of the TSP family, notably the signature cysteine–cysteine-glycine (CCG) motif in the LEL, which is the location for the formation of three disulfide bonds within the LEL and influences interactions with other molecules (Figure 1).

Ov-TSP-1 shares 97% identity with a TSP of its close relative, Clonorchis sinensis (GAA49954.1) calculated from the alignment over 229 amino acid (amino acid position 19–247 of Ov-TSP-1 to 87–315 of C. sinensis), and 74% identity with Sm-TSP-1 of Schistosoma mansoni (XP_002580456.1) (Figure 1).

Suppression of Ov-tsp-1 in adult flukes impacts on proper tegument formation and results in increased vacuolation, implying that this protein is essential for fluke development and survival and is therefore worthy of consideration as a vaccine and/or drug target.

The tegument of Ov-tsp-1 dsRNA treated worms (Figure 7C, E and F) was more highly vacuolated than firefly luciferease dsRNA and non dsRNA controls (Figure 7B, A and D), with extensive and enlarged vacuoles throughout the surface layer.
397.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence that a tetraspanin (Sm-TSP-2) is essential for tegument formation in S. mansoni and that Ov-TSP-1 suppression causes tegument defects in O. viverrini. However, the specific sequence identity reported is with Sm-TSP-1 (74%), not Sm-TSP-2, and the passage does not state a direct structural similarity between Ov-TSP-1 and Sm-TSP-2. Therefore the claim that structural similarity to Sm-TSP-2 implies conserved essential function across both species is not supported by the passage's explicit sequence comparisons. While related tetraspanins in both species have essential roles, the specific inference based on structural similarity to Sm-TSP-2 is not derivable from the provided text.

397.Option: Suppression of Ov-tsp-1 expression in adult O. viverrini results in tegument malformation, including increased vacuolation and a thinner tegument, indicating that Ov-TSP-1 is essential for proper tegument formation and structural integrity.
Evidence: Suppression of Ov-tsp-1 in adult flukes impacts on proper tegument formation and results in increased vacuolation, implying that this protein is essential for fluke development and survival and is therefore worthy of consideration as a vaccine and/or drug target.

This RNAi procedure knocked down expression of Ov-tsp-1 by 67% (p = 0.06), 72% (p = 0.01) and 55% (p = 0.02) on days 6, 10 and 16 in vitro, respectively, compared to the negative control group that received luciferase dsRNA (Figure 6).

The tegument of Ov-tsp-1 dsRNA treated worms (Figure 7C, E and F) was more highly vacuolated than firefly luciferease dsRNA and non dsRNA controls (Figure 7B, A and D), with extensive and enlarged vacuoles throughout the surface layer.
The tegument of knocked down tsp-1 worm is more highly vacuolated (indicated by arrows) and thinner compared with controls.
397.Rationale: The passage provides direct experimental evidence: RNAi-mediated suppression of Ov-tsp-1 reduced transcript levels and produced clear ultrastructural tegument abnormalities on TEM, including increased vacuolation and thinning compared with controls. The authors explicitly interpret these findings as indicating that Ov-TSP-1 is essential for proper tegument formation and parasite development/survival. Thus this inference follows directly from the reported results.

397.Option: The recognition of Ov-TSP-1 by sera from infected humans and hamsters indicates that the protein is a promising candidate for serodiagnosis of opisthorchiasis.
Evidence: Immunoblot analysis showed that sera from humans and hamsters infected with O. viverrini both reacted with rOv-TSP-1 (Figure 5).
Strips were incubated with sera from patients and hamsters. Non-infected hamsters served as negative controls. Sera from mice immunized with recombinant Ov-TSP-1 were used as positive controls.
397.Rationale: The passage documents that recombinant Ov-TSP-1 is recognized by antibodies in sera from naturally infected humans and experimentally infected hamsters, demonstrating that the protein is immunogenic and detectable by host antibodies. From an epidemiological and diagnostic perspective, an antigen that is consistently recognized by infected hosts' sera is a reasonable candidate for serodiagnosis. While the passage does not present sensitivity/specificity data or validation, the evidence that infected sera react with rOv-TSP-1 supports the inference that it is a promising serodiagnostic candidate.

397.Option: The expression of Ov-tsp-1 in all developmental stages of O. viverrini suggests that it plays a role in the initial excystation of metacercariae from fish hosts.
Evidence: Ov-tsp-1 is expressed in all stages; expression was highest in metacercariae followed by two-week old juveniles, adults and eggs in descending order (Figure 3).
Metacercariae of O. viverrini were collected from infected fishes obtained from reservoirs in Khon Kaen province, Thailand.
397.Rationale: The passage shows that Ov-tsp-1 is expressed across developmental stages and is highest in metacercariae, but it provides no experimental data linking Ov-tsp-1 to excystation from fish or to the excystation process in the definitive host. Inferring a specific functional role in initial excystation is speculative and not directly supported by the presented evidence. Therefore this specific causal inference cannot be derived from the passage.

397.Option: The high sequence identity between Ov-TSP-1 and C. sinensis TSP suggests that Ov-tsp-1 may function as a receptor for host bile components, facilitating parasite survival in the biliary tree.
Evidence: Ov-TSP-1 shares 97% identity with a TSP of its close relative, Clonorchis sinensis (GAA49954.1) calculated from the alignment over 229 amino acid (amino acid position 19–247 of Ov-TSP-1 to 87–315 of C. sinensis), and 74% identity with Sm-TSP-1 of Schistosoma mansoni (XP_002580456.1) (Figure 1).

Tetraspanins contain 4 transmembrane domains and are frequently expressed at the cell surface in association with each other and with other molecules, such as integrins, where they function to regulate cell adhesion, migration, proliferation, and differentiation [12], [13].
The tegument of the adult fluke forms an intimate contact with the host biliary epithelium [20], resulting in chronic cell proliferation, immunopathology and ultimately tumorigenesis [21], [22].
397.Rationale: While the passage documents very high sequence identity between Ov-TSP-1 and a C. sinensis tetraspanin and describes tetraspanins as cell-surface proteins involved in interactions with other molecules, it does not provide any direct evidence that Ov-TSP-1 functions as a receptor for host bile components or that it specifically mediates parasite survival via binding bile constituents. The close contact of the tegument with biliary epithelium makes such a role plausible, but the claim that Ov-TSP-1 acts as a bile-component receptor is speculative and not directly supported by the experimental data presented.

398.Option: Since FZT infections involve multiple reservoir hosts including domestic animals and the transmission cycle includes environmental contamination, preventive chemotherapy targeting only humans may not be sufficient to reduce long-term transmission.
Evidence: Studies from the same area have revealed high prevalence of different MIF-species, especially Haplorchis spp., in dogs, cats and pigs, suggesting that domestic animals could play an important role as reservoir hosts and in the transmission of FZT [9], [10].

Nissen et al. found that half of the North Vietnamese farm dogs that had been given a single dose of praziquantel were reinfected with FZT within three months [24]. Hence they could not recommend anthelmintic treatment as the only intervention to control FZT in dogs.
However, 87.7% of those negative after two weeks became reinfected within a year [23].

As seen in Figure 2, the prevalence of small trematode eggs in stool before preventive chemotherapy for the two sites combined were 40.2% (95% confidence interval (CI) of 35.3–45.0). The prevalence two, 16, 29 and 60 weeks after preventive chemotherapy were 2.3% (CI 0.8–3.7), 10.9% (CI 7.8–13.9), 18.2% (CI 14.4–22.0) and 29.8% (CI 25.3–34.3), respectively.
398.Rationale: The passage explicitly documents that domestic animals (dogs, cats, pigs) have high prevalence and may act as reservoir hosts and that animals can be rapidly reinfected after treatment, undermining animal-only anthelmintic approaches. The study also demonstrates rapid reinfection in humans after preventive chemotherapy, with prevalence rising again toward baseline by 60 weeks. Together these facts support the epidemiological inference that interventions directed only at humans are unlikely to sustainably reduce transmission given reservoir hosts and environmental/foodborne transmission pathways.

398.Option: The high reinfection rate observed within one year after preventive chemotherapy suggests that the current strategy of selective treatment based on self-reported raw fish consumption is ineffective and should be replaced with universal treatment in all endemic areas.
Evidence: preventive chemotherapy is here given as selective chemotherapy to people confirming that they eat raw fish.
However, 87.7% of those negative after two weeks became reinfected within a year [23].

As seen in Figure 2, the prevalence of small trematode eggs in stool before preventive chemotherapy for the two sites combined were 40.2% ... The prevalence ... 60 weeks after preventive chemotherapy were ... 29.8% (CI 25.3–34.3).
Despite the large number of treatments, the effectiveness of preventive chemotherapy in Vietnam has not been thoroughly assessed.
398.Rationale: While the passage shows that selective treatment (self-reported raw fish consumers) was the strategy used and documents substantial reinfection within a year, it does not provide direct evidence comparing selective versus universal treatment effectiveness in these settings or data demonstrating that universal treatment would be superior in practice. The WHO guidance on universal versus selective treatment is described elsewhere in the passage as conditional on district prevalence (>20%), but the study does not provide empirical evidence to conclude that selective treatment is ineffective in all endemic areas nor that it should be universally replaced. Thus the prescriptive recommendation to replace selective with universal treatment cannot be derived solely from the presented data.

398.Option: The fact that C. sinensis infections are less common in fish than in humans suggests that human-to-human transmission of this species is more likely than transmission via contaminated fish.
Evidence: FZT infects animals and humans after consumption of raw or insufficiently cooked freshwater fish, in the case of humans often as a traditional dish [2].
The morphological characteristics of eggs belonging to different species are difficult to differentiate by microscopy, and this may lead to unreliable data of their epidemiological distribution [6].
In Northern Vietnam, there is a mix of C. sinensis and MIF, especially Haplorchis spp., in humans [7], [8].
398.Rationale: The passage states FZT transmission to humans occurs via consumption of raw/undercooked freshwater fish and does not present any data claiming C. sinensis is less common in fish than humans. There is no evidence of human-to-human transmission of C. sinensis presented. Instead, the text emphasizes a foodborne (fish-mediated) lifecycle and notes limitations in species identification by egg morphology. Therefore the specific claim that lower fish prevalence implies human-to-human transmission is not supported by the passage.

398.Option: Because MIF infections are less serious and not carcinogenic, preventive chemotherapy should be prioritized for liver fluke infections rather than for minute intestinal fluke infections.
Evidence: The MIF infections have been less studied, but the clinical manifestations may be less serious and MIF are not considered carcinogenic [5].
The morbidity of the liver trematodes, especially the predisposition for cholangiocarcinoma, is the main reason for establishing control measures [11].
WHO recommends preventive chemotherapy, that is large-scale distribution of anthelminthic drugs to populations at risk, as the main intervention strategy against liver flukes [12], [13].
398.Rationale: The passage explicitly contrasts MIF as generally less serious and non-carcinogenic with liver flukes whose morbidity, notably increased risk of cholangiocarcinoma, motivates control programs. WHO recommendations cited in the text specifically target liver flukes with preventive chemotherapy. From an epidemiological prioritization standpoint based on morbidity and carcinogenic risk described in the passage, prioritizing preventive chemotherapy toward liver fluke infections is a supportable inference.

398.Option: The use of praziquantel in dogs and cats has been shown to be highly effective in reducing FZT transmission, making animal treatment a viable alternative to human-targeted interventions.
Evidence: Nissen et al. found that half of the North Vietnamese farm dogs that had been given a single dose of praziquantel were reinfected with FZT within three months [24]. Hence they could not recommend anthelmintic treatment as the only intervention to control FZT in dogs.
Hence they could not recommend anthelmintic treatment as the only intervention to control FZT in dogs.
398.Rationale: The passage reports rapid reinfection of treated dogs and explicitly states the authors could not recommend anthelmintic treatment as the only intervention for dogs, which contradicts the claim that praziquantel in animals has been shown to be highly effective for reducing transmission. Therefore the assertion that animal treatment is a viable alternative to human-targeted interventions is not supported by the study's findings.

399.Option: The presence of specific refractory genes in Ae. aegypti populations from northern Cameroon may explain the lower transmission rates observed in these populations, suggesting that genetic resistance could limit ZIKV spread in certain regions.
Evidence: TR varied significantly (Fisher’s exact test: P = 0.0014) according to the population with lowest TR reported in two populations from northern part of Cameroon, Bénoué (56%) and Maroua (43%) (Fig 3B).

The vector competence of natural Aedes populations from Central Africa has remained unclear. It has been demonstrated that the level of vector competence varies according to mosquito populations and ZIKV strains [28–31].
399.Rationale: The passage documents lower transmission rates in specific northern Ae. aegypti populations and states that vector competence varies by population and viral strain. However, it contains no data or statements about genetic mechanisms (e.g., specific refractory genes) underlying those differences. Inferring the presence of refractory genes as the explanation for lower TR requires genetic evidence not provided in the passage, so this causal genetic claim cannot be derived from the presented data.

399.Option: The invasive species Ae. albopictus, which has replaced Ae. aegypti in many areas of Central Africa, is likely to be the primary driver of future ZIKV outbreaks due to its higher population density and broader geographic distribution.
Evidence: it was reported for the first time in Central Africa in early 2000 in Cameroon [21] and has progressively colonized almost all countries in the region where it tends to supplant the resident species Ae. aegypti [22–25].
When all populations of each species were analysed together, Ae. aegypti (56%) exhibited higher TEs than Ae. albopictus (42%) (Fisher’s exact test: P = 0.02).
Overall, ZIKV titres were significantly higher in Ae. aegypti compared to Ae. albopictus (Chi-squared test: χ2 = 6.4527, df = 1, P = 0.01).
399.Rationale: While the passage describes Ae. albopictus as an invasive species that has colonized much of the region, the experimental data show that Ae. aegypti had higher transmission efficiency and higher ZIKV titres. The option's claim that Ae. albopictus will be the primary driver because of higher density/distribution is not supported by the transmission data presented and additionally invokes population density (not measured here). Therefore the assertion cannot be derived from the passage.

399.Option: The higher transmission efficiency of Ae. aegypti in Central Africa is primarily due to its greater preference for human blood meals, which enhances its role in sustaining human-to-human transmission cycles.
Evidence: When all populations of each species were analysed together, Ae. aegypti (56%) exhibited higher TEs than Ae. albopictus (42%) (Fisher’s exact test: P = 0.02).

The blood meal was supplemented with adenosine 5’-triphosphate (ATP) as a phagostimulant at a final concentration of 5 mM. The titre of infectious blood-meals provided to mosquitoes was 107 pfu/mL using a Hemotek membrane feeding system (Hemotek Ltd., Blackburn, UK).
For each population, six batches of 60, 7–10 day-old females were exposed to an infectious blood meal containing 1.4 mL of washed rabbit erythrocytes and 700 μL of viral suspension.
399.Rationale: The experiment demonstrates higher transmission efficiency for Ae. aegypti, but the study did not measure or report host blood preference or human biting rates. The laboratory blood meals used washed rabbit erythrocytes, not human blood, so there is no experimental evidence in the passage linking higher TE to greater anthropophily. Thus asserting human-blood preference as the primary cause is not supported by the presented data.

399.Option: Despite the invasive nature of Ae. albopictus and its dominance in sympatric areas, Ae. aegypti exhibits higher transmission efficiency and viral titres in saliva compared to Ae. albopictus, suggesting a greater potential for ZIKV transmission in Central Africa.
Evidence: it was reported for the first time in Central Africa in early 2000 in Cameroon [21] and has progressively colonized almost all countries in the region where it tends to supplant the resident species Ae. aegypti [22–25].
When all populations of each species were analysed together, Ae. aegypti (56%) exhibited higher TEs than Ae. albopictus (42%) (Fisher’s exact test: P = 0.02).
Overall, ZIKV titres were significantly higher in Ae. aegypti compared to Ae. albopictus (Chi-squared test: χ2 = 6.4527, df = 1, P = 0.01).
399.Rationale: The passage documents both the invasive spread and local dominance of Ae. albopictus and experimental findings that, when aggregated, Ae. aegypti populations had significantly higher transmission efficiency and higher ZIKV titres than Ae. albopictus. From an epidemiological perspective, higher transmission efficiency and viral loads in saliva imply greater potential to sustain human transmission cycles. Therefore this inference is supported by the data presented.

400.Option: The lack of further titer increase after a third vaccination in adult pigs suggests that mRNA vaccines are less effective than traditional vaccines in boosting long-term immunity.
Evidence: The first two vaccinations with RABV-G mRNA led to seroconversion of all animals with antibody titers >0.5 IU/ml. The third vaccination at day 49 did not further increase RABV-G specific neutralizing antibody levels.
For comparison with a licensed vaccine, one group of piglets received a full human dose of Rabipur that was injected intramuscularly as recommended.

These data demonstrate that RABV-G vaccine is immunogenic in newborn piglets, leading to rapid seroconversion and indicating comparable VN titer kinetics for mRNA vaccine compared to the licensed Rabipur vaccine.
400.Rationale: The passage does state explicitly that a third vaccination in adult pigs did not further increase neutralizing antibody levels, but it does not provide any direct comparison in adult animals between a third-dose boost of mRNA vaccine and a third-dose boost of a traditional vaccine. The cited text about comparability refers to newborn piglets and VN titer kinetics, not to adult boosting or long-term immunity comparisons. Therefore the claim that mRNA vaccines are less effective than traditional vaccines in boosting long-term immunity is not supported by the passage.

400.Option: The fact that some mice with VN titers above 0.5 IU/ml still died after challenge suggests that the 0.5 IU/ml threshold is invalid for all forms of rabies infection, including natural exposure.
Evidence: After two vaccinations, all RABV-G immunized mice developed relevant virus neutralizing (VN) titers of ≥0.5 IU/ml (range: 5.9 to 70.2 IU/ml), considered as protective in humans, dogs and cats [34].
All RABV-G mRNA and HDC vaccinated mice were protected against lethal rabies virus challenge (Fig 3A).

In groups with an intact CD4+ T cell compartment during prophylactic immunization, more than 87% of mRNA-vaccinated mice survived infection (one out of eight mRNA-immunized mice without detectable virus neutralizing antibodies after immunization had to be sacrificed on day ten due to pronounced weight loss) (Fig 5B).
400.Rationale: The passage does not provide evidence that mice with VN titers above 0.5 IU/ml died after challenge. On the contrary, it reports that vaccinated mice (which had VN titers ≥0.5 IU/ml) were protected against intracerebral challenge. The single death described among mRNA-immunized mice was explicitly associated with a mouse that lacked detectable neutralizing antibodies. Thus there is no support in the passage for the sweeping claim that the 0.5 IU/ml threshold is invalid for all forms of rabies infection or natural exposures.

400.Option: The observed reduction in TNFα mRNA expression in vaccinated mice indicates that the mRNA vaccine prevents rabies virus replication by suppressing neuroinflammation rather than by direct antiviral mechanisms.
Evidence: In mRNA and Rabipur vaccinated mice TNFα mRNA transcript level was reduced compared to mock vaccinated control group at both analysis time points and different brain sections (Fig 4B).

In contrast, all but one of the analyzed brains of mice immunized with RABV-G mRNA or Rabipur were negative for N-protein mRNA at both time points, demonstrating a strong suppression of viral replication below the detection limit after vaccination (Fig 4A).

This may require immediate neutralization by antibodies directed against the viral G protein upon entry of rabies virus into uninfected tissue and/or early elimination of infected cells by virus-specific cytotoxic T cells, when limited replication may take place in non-nervous tissue at the site of entry.
400.Rationale: While the passage documents reduced TNFα mRNA (a marker of neuroinflammation) in vaccinated mice and also shows suppression of viral replication, it does not claim that suppression of neuroinflammation is the mechanism by which the vaccine prevents replication. In fact, the text describes immune mechanisms (neutralizing antibodies and virus-specific T cells) that are expected to intercept virus before neuronal infection. Thus the data are consistent with reduced inflammation as a consequence of effective antiviral immunity, but do not support the specific causal inference that prevention of replication occurs primarily by suppression of neuroinflammation rather than by direct antiviral immune mechanisms.

400.Option: The mRNA vaccine's ability to induce strong CD4+ T cell responses suggests that cellular immunity is more critical than humoral immunity for protection against rabies virus in experimental models.
Evidence: These results indicate efficient RABV-G-specific T cell induction by RABV-G mRNA and better induction of CD4+ T cells by the mRNA vaccine compared to Rabipur.

In the CD4-depleted, mRNA immunized group VN titers were strongly reduced: 3 out of 8 mice showed VN titers below 0.5 IU/ml and the mean titer of the groups was about 3 IU/ml, i.e. more than 15-fold reduced compared to the CD4-intact, mRNA-vaccinated group.

In the CD4-depleted, mRNA vaccinated group, all mice had to be sacrificed within 11 days after infection with similar kinetics as in the negative control group (Fig 5C) in line with the strongly reduced VN titers in this group.
400.Rationale: The passage shows that the mRNA vaccine induces strong CD4+ T cell responses, but it also shows that CD4+ T cell depletion markedly reduced virus-neutralizing (VN) antibody titers and that CD4-depleted, mRNA-vaccinated mice lost protection and died. This indicates that CD4+ T cells are important largely because they support humoral (antibody) responses. The data therefore do not support the conclusion that cellular immunity is more critical than humoral immunity; rather they indicate that both arms, and particularly the antibody response (which depends on CD4 help), are essential for protection.

400.Option: The titer of 0.5 IU/ml, while considered a correlate of protection for natural rabies infection, may not reliably predict protection against intracerebral challenge in experimental settings.
Evidence: After two vaccinations, all RABV-G immunized mice developed relevant virus neutralizing (VN) titers of ≥0.5 IU/ml (range: 5.9 to 70.2 IU/ml), considered as protective in humans, dogs and cats [34].
The dotted line at 0.5 IU/ml is considered as a correlate of protection.
Intracerebral infection poses an extremely harsh challenge that is artificial, but accepted by the scientific community as a stringent experimental test.

In contrast, all but one of the analyzed brains of mice immunized with RABV-G mRNA or Rabipur were negative for N-protein mRNA at both time points, demonstrating a strong suppression of viral replication below the detection limit after vaccination (Fig 4A). The brain of one mRNA vaccinated mouse was positive for viral N-protein RNA at day 6 after infection (telencephalon: ct-value of 25.4) indicating a non-protective response to the mRNA vaccine in one case.
400.Rationale: The passage states that 0.5 IU/ml is considered a correlate of protection for natural infection, but it also emphasizes that the intracerebral challenge is an artificial, extremely harsh, and stringent experimental test. Although most vaccinated animals with VN titers well above 0.5 IU/ml were protected and showed suppression of viral replication, the passage documents at least one instance of a vaccinated (mRNA) animal with detectable viral N-protein RNA in the brain ('indicating a non-protective response in one case'). Taken together, these points justify the cautious epidemiological inference that the 0.5 IU/ml threshold—validated as a correlate for natural exposures—may not always reliably predict protection in the particular and extreme conditions of intracerebral experimental challenge.

401.Option: The presence of N. mikurensis in bat-associated ticks that fed on domestic dogs, bats, and other wildlife suggests that these ticks may serve as bridge vectors in the transmission of this zoonotic pathogen between different host species.
Evidence: We detected Neoehrlichia mikurensis in both I. simplex (n = 1, EW16, Romania) associated with M. schreibersii and I. vespertilionis (n = 3, EW54, Romania; EW75, EW159, Hungary), previously fed on C. lupus familiaris, M. schreibersii, and M. myotis (Fig 5).

I. vespertilionis indicated the broadest spectrum of host feeding, including M. schreibersii (n = 13), M. bechsteinii (n = 1), M. myotis (n = 6), Rhinolophus euryale (n = 1), R. ferrumequinum (n = 18), R. hipposideros (n = 3), Rhinolophus sp. (n = 6), and non-bat hosts of C. lupus familiaris (n = 6), Equus caballus (n = 1), and Sus scrofa (n = 1)
